```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:F58jj.44473$L27.14435@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> world, and wouldn't know holiness if it bit you in the armpit.

I don't consider the world I live in to be debased and immoral. Certainly it 
contains a very few such individuals, but there is nobilty, decency, and 
kindness as well.

Maybe one's view of God is coloured by one's view of the World.


>If the
> Bible is correct, then God is pure and holy in a way that we can't
> begin to understand.

It isn't, so the predicate doesn't follow.


>Would you have the criminals set the rules for
> the judicial system?

If there were no criminals we wouldn't NEED a judicial system. If every one 
of us was responsible and considerate, we wouldn't need to have laws; people 
would just do "the right thing" by everybody else.

So, why didn't God create us perfect?

Seems like He screwed up and we have been carrying that guilt for ever.

Or else He INTENDED strife and conflict between good and evil and we are 
just puppets playing out a cosmic morality play for His amusement.

Doesn't matter how you slice it, there is no way I can see that would make 
me want to be part of what you currently perceive as God.

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> So, why didn't God create us perfect?

He did, but we chose to sin. Pete, has anyone ever forced you to lie? Yet
I would bet my life that you have. Is that from your perfections, or from
your flaws? Every time you ever lied, or did any other bad thing, you had
a choice. You did (like me and everyone else) what you did because you
*chose* to do so. God gave us free will; it is we who choose to sin.
Don't blame God for that; blame us. The real question is why God didn't
blast us all into oblivion a long time ago.
```

- **References:** `<5uns9tF1j30lfU1@mid.individual.net> <dqOhj.51297$N67.44049@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <5uqg38F1jhl04U1@mid.individual.net> <378go3tnhko793u45ebi4snptotcr90tik@4ax.com> <d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <h41qo3h290f2ku9d7p773m6iccekot5cfo@4ax.com> <F58jj.44473$L27.14435@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4pdgF1jrq9pU1@mid.individual.net> <wlajj.127$1f.26@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:wlajj.127$1f.26@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
>> So, why didn't God create us perfect?
>
> He did, but we chose to sin.

If we were capable of sin then we weren't perfect, so He didn't create us 
that way...


>Pete, has anyone ever forced you to lie?

No. Not that I can recall. In fact, people have a pretty hard time forcing 
me to do anything :-)

I wasn't bullied at school (the bullies went for easier targets), never 
bullied other kids (or had any desire to do so), and on several occasions 
prevented or stopped bullying that was going on.

On the rare occasions when I have lied, it has been in full knowledge of 
what I was doing and why I was doing it.

I am not easily coerced.:-)


>Yet
> I would bet my life that you have. Is that from your perfections, or from
> your flaws?

You'd certainly win the bet. However, it is not necessarily in self interest 
that I have done this. I don't see it as coming from flaws OR perfections... 
Humans are complex creatures.


>Every time you ever lied, or did any other bad thing, you had
> a choice.

That's a sweeping statement. Sometimes there was no other acceptable choice. 
As for doing "bad" things, I learned long ago that this is non-survival 
behaviour (there are always consequences) and best avoided. I derive no 
pleasure out of being bad, so I stopped it. It is just as easy to be "good" 
as to be "bad" and being "good" makes the world a better place. Simple 
common sense really...


>You did (like me and everyone else) what you did because you
> *chose* to do so.

Please don't project your character onto me. We are not alike.


>God gave us free will; it is we who choose to sin.

And yet the Church teaches that even a new-born baby is born in sin, as a 
consequence of what Adam and Eve did in disobeying God's command.

I don't buy it.

I believe people are basically good. I further believe we get fucked up by 
the interactions we experience in living and growing. How we deal with those 
experiences determines whether we can sleep easily or not, and whether we 
enjoy our time in this place. I try to learn from bad stuff and put it 
behind me; I don't blame Satan or God for what happens to me. I believe all 
of us are responsible for the condition we are in; the sooner we start 
taking that responsibility for ourselves, the sooner our lives can be 
restored, rehabilitated, and continued pleasurably and happily.


> Don't blame God for that; blame us.

In work and in life, I try not to be judgemental and I don't "blame" 
anybody.  Shit happens. We deal with it and learn from it. Blame and 
punishment are equally counter productive, in my view. Someone screws up on 
my watch, they take responsibility for that screw-up and, as a team we 
assist them to make it right. Nothing is achieved by blame, except 
undermining of confidence and consequent demotivation.

Because I hold the above belief, I am immediately not predisposed to any 
religion that pivots on guilt and blame.


>The real question is why God didn't
> blast us all into oblivion a long time ago.

Where's the fun in that? :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> If we were capable of sin then we weren't perfect, so He didn't create us that way...

Capable means free, not flawed. How could God create a being with
completely free will who *could not* sin? If they *could not* sin,
they wouldn't have true free will. When you open the door to freedom,
you open the door; it's as simple as that.

>>Yet
>> I would bet my life that you have. Is that from your perfections, or from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> flaws OR perfections... Humans are complex creatures.

That's rationalization. Deceipt is deceipt.

>>Every time you ever lied, or did any other bad thing, you had
>> a choice.
>
> That's a sweeping statement. Sometimes there was no other acceptable choice.

Ah! Acceptable to you. I.e. you chose what you preferred, even at the
cost of deceipt. A murderer chooses to kill because it is their preference.
Both break the "rules" of "good conduct" because it is their "acceptable"
choice. It is only a difference in magnitude, not kind.

>>You did (like me and everyone else) what you did because you
>> *chose* to do so.
>
> Please don't project your character onto me. We are not alike.

I didn't say that. I said that you make your choices, and everybody else
makes theirs, me included. In that, we're all alike; not in the particular
choices we individually make.

>>God gave us free will; it is we who choose to sin.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't buy it.

There's that free will again. :-)
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Humans are just as evil as they ever were.

And they are just as nopble and just as decent as they ever were.

Evil?

If someone believes that he is doing good by strapping dynamite to himself 
and exploding it in a crowded marketplace, in the certain knowledge that his 
subsistence family will receive vast wealth ($US20,000), and he himself will 
be rewarded with 70 virgins in Paradise, how is that evil?

It is not the person who is evil. It is the indoctrinated belief system, and 
the economic circumstances in which he lives, which gave him that viewpoint. 
That is what is "evil"..



>Have the events of the last 100
> years (or 10 years) not made that clear? People today are commining 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> never met.

I don't dispute that. I dispute whether it is evil or not, because this 
depends on your viewpoint. It is certainly misguided.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-15T18:21:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<13ocasvqbc8ak39@corp.supernews.com> <3Fqhj.30190$L27.1835@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5uns9tF1j30lfU1@mid.individual.net> <dqOhj.51297$N67.44049@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <5uqg38F1jhl04U1@mid.individual.net> <378go3tnhko793u45ebi4snptotcr90tik@4ax.com> <d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4or9F1kopo3U1@mid.individual.net> <heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4sfhF1kcru0U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> And they are just as nopble and just as decent as they ever were.

True, which doesn't say much.

> Evil?
>
> If someone believes that he is doing good by strapping dynamite to himself and exploding it in a crowded marketplace, in the 
> certain knowledge that his subsistence family will receive vast wealth ($US20,000), and he himself will be rewarded with 70 
> virgins in Paradise, how is that evil?

He does not have the right to the lives of those other people; they do not
belong to him to do with them as he wishes.

> It is not the person who is evil. It is the indoctrinated belief system, and the economic circumstances in which he lives, which 
> gave him that viewpoint. That is what is "evil".

People choose. There are examples of children being reared to the
highest standards of behavior who rejected it and did very bad
things. There are also examples of the reverse; of people being reared
by brutes, who overcame that to be model citizens. Training and
environment are only influences; though they can be very powerful,
they are not deterministic. Anyone who has reared children knows
this. You can't make your children come out well, you can only be
as positive as you can. You don't have to teach children to lie or
to be selfish; they do that naturally. You have to teach them *not* to
do those things, and it's an uphill struggle.

The bottom line is, people choose their way. It's all about free will,
and what the human heart chooses to do, and the human heart has a
very strong propensity toward bad, not good. Even "good" people
struggle against their nature (and that includes Christians). Few
people have never wanted to punch someone out, even if they've
never done it. The human heart is by nature selfish, prideful and
deceitful. Humans have to struggle to achieve true generosity, humility
and honesty. That's why so relatively few get there.
```

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> and honesty. That's why so relatively few get there.

Speak for yourself.

I believe the majority "get there".

I see examples of kindness, goodness, decency, nobility and even heroism and 
self-sacrifice every day in ordinary familes, with ordinary people, living 
ordinary lives.

In my travels I have never ceased to be amazed by the kindness of strangers 
and I have recirocated it to the best of my ability.

I am not blinded to the evil in the world, but I don't wallow in it either.

We obviously live in different worlds.

You say God created us perfect, but gave us free will, so we are not, but 
that doesn't alter the fact that we were created perfect, and it is only our 
innate badness that makes us choose to be bad and so need the salvation of 
God, but God knew when He created us what the outcome would be because He's 
omniscient, but he can't be because he doesn't know what choice we'll make 
with the free will which He gave us... and so on ad nauseum.

It is a totally incoherent argument that defies all rules of logic.

It's like the old Russian riddle: "Is God so powerful He could create a 
stone that even HE cannot move?"

Pete.
```

```
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

I love my grandchildren, too. But the fact is they have a human nature

are the same, as is every other human being on the planet. We *learn*
to do better; it's not part of our natures to do so on our own. Some
children become good or bad more easily than others. That is a trait
that varies with the individual human heart, though it's present in all
of them.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4or9F1kopo3U1@mid.individual.net> <heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4sfhF1kcru0U1@mid.individual.net> <Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com>`

```


"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>> On Tue, 15 Jan 2008 18:21:17 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> of that?

I think that's kind of dirty pool, Daniel.

Kids don't necessarily do these things because of an innately sinful or 
wicked nature. It is part of a growth process where limits must be explored. 
Do you consider your kids to be wicked and liable to burn in Hell because 
they occasionally try and buy you off with a lie, or hit a sibling when 
fighting over toys, etc.?

Of course you don't. And they are not.

This whole doctrine of "Original Sin" and all of us being born in it is long 
overdue for review.

I can't see sin in a newborn baby; I see a blank page waiting to be written.

Parents have much to do with what will be written there, and if the parents' 
outlook is negative, how can you expect the children to be otherwise?

Sin (like God) is an invention of man.

As if that weren't bad enough, are we now to saddle our children with it 
from the moment they draw breath?

I don't think so.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4or9F1kopo3U1@mid.individual.net> <heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4sfhF1kcru0U1@mid.individual.net> <Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> overdue for review.

But, for those of us that believe it, it certainly explains the behavior 
described above.  Pushing limits is one thing, but lying and direct 
disobedience is quite another.

> I can't see sin in a newborn baby; I see a blank page waiting to be written.
> 
> Parents have much to do with what will be written there, and if the parents' 
> outlook is negative, how can you expect the children to be otherwise?

It's not negative at all.  How can I fault my child for being born with 
the same sinful nature that his mother and I were also born with?  :) 
It's just something we have to deal with.  We train their behavior until 
they grow old enough for us to also train their hearts.  Hopefully, by 
the time the heart is trained, we don't have to be training anymore - we 
give guidance on how to view situations based on principle, and let them 
make decisions.  If we feel that it's the wrong one, we'll explain to 
them why we feel they could have made a better one, and they learn.

There's also a concept in Scripture called the "age of accountability." 
  It's not a magic number, per se, but it's the age at which they have 
matured enough to understand the concept that they are sinners, that 
Jesus has paid the debt for that sin, and that they can accept this 
payment.  Babies that die aren't condemned to hell.
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
> I was always under the impression that unbaptised babies go to hell.

The Bible clearly teaches that a person must be aware of right and
wrong, and to choose wrong, to have guilt. Babies are innocent
because they have not reached what is sometimes called "the age of
accountability" when they become aware of such things.

The reason to baptize babies is to come before the Church and publicly
commit them to God, not for salvation. It is what happens in a person's
own heart that condemns or excuses them, not what others may do for
you or to you. Other people can influence you, but they can't determine
your fate.
```

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> Sorry I was not clear.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> broken relationship with God and that is what I call sin.

Uh, not exactly. The Ten Commandments came WAY after Adam & Eve. Besides 
which, there was no penalty for violating any of the Ten.

That's not to say there wern't commandments before Moses, there were, such 
as circumcising on the 8th day and not eating meat*. Jewish scholars say 
there are 613 direct commandments, 365 positive one ("thou shalt do") and 
248 negative ones ("thou shalt not").

As a test of Biblical knowledge, what was the first commandment God gave to 
mankind? Do you know? Answer below:

------
* Only Noah and his decendants were granted permission to eat meat. Think on 
it. Everybody up to the time of the flood was a vegetarian! And the world 
was so hoplessly corrupt, degenerate, and wicked that God had to destroy it 
and start over. There is some possibility that residual debauchery in the 
world comes from eating plants. After all, plants are not food; plants are 
what food eats.








--- Answer to quiz
Be fruitful and multiply.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:apUlj.63805$N67.14101@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> obeying. :-)

Well, OF COURSE, we're gonna do the EASY ones... :-)

Story I heard was that a year after the ark landed on Mount Ararat, Noah was 
going around to make sure that all the animals had complied with God's 
commandment.

There were Mr. and Mrs. Lion with a fine brood of cubs, the dogs had pups, 
the birds had chicks, and everybody seemed to have been busy doing what God 
required.

Except the snake.

Noah asked him the reason he had ignored God's command.

Said the snake:"I'm an adder and I can't multiply."

Tenderly, Noah picked up Mr. and Mrs. Snake and carried then into his own 
log cabin, where he placed them on the kitchen table.

Some time later they produced several offspring and Noah went to 
congratulate the snake.

"I thought you were an adder and couldn't multiply....", said Noah.

To which the snake replied: "Even an adder can multiply if he has a log 
table...".

Boom! Boom!

Pete.
```

```
On Wed, 23 Jan 2008 21:11:27 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>* Only Noah and his decendants were granted permission to eat meat. Think on 
>it. Everybody up to the time of the flood was a vegetarian! And the world 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>what food eats.

It proves that carnivores are all in-bred. 

The great flood was probably the Permian-Triassic Extinction Event, 250 mya, which killed
off 96% of marine species and 70% of land animals. Animal development WAS in big trouble
all through the Permian era. 

>As a test of Biblical knowledge, what was the first commandment God gave to 
>mankind?
>Be fruitful and multiply. 

How fitting. That's the ESSENTIAL commandment, because the purpose of life is evolution.
Other commandments are nice-to-haves. God issued the first commandment to prokaryotes.
```

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> commandments.

You said "e.g" (for example...) Does this imply you admit other rules?

>If Adam and Eve had followed all the rules we would all be happier today.

For me to agree with that, I'd have to believe that events described in 
Genesis are true and actual fact, and that there really WERE two individuals 
named Adam and Eve, created directly by God from dust and a rib bone. To 
believe that, I would have to accept that the Earth is less than 6000 years 
old (calculated from the genealogies in the Pentateuch). Obviously, it is 
impossible for me to do that, as the evidence refutes it. So here we must 
part company. (No hard feelings... :-))



>They did not and that resulted in a broken relationship with God and that 
>is what I call sin.

I understand.

Thanks for the clarification.

Pete.
```

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-24T08:43:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> before days, it must be a different definition than literalists claim.

You're right Howard, many people do that. Unfortunately, it doesn't
work. Genesis is too specific (evening and morning, etc.) of a "day"
being one rotation of the earth. There are also other problems with that
interpretation. But there are much deeper problems than that. If one
takes the Bible as simply a figurative account, they have destroyed any
benefit of the Bible. If it's not reliable, then who is to say what part
is reliable and what part isn't? The situation devolves to a simple human
relative value system, no more, no less. Worthless and pointless for
knowing God and obtaining eternal life. If Jesus was not Who He claimed
to be (the Messiah and Savior, the Son of God) then he was a liar or
insane; certainly no one that should be admired or followed. The logical
bottom line is that if the Bible isn't what it claims to be, the reliable
and true Word of God, then it is a lie, and what nitwit would stake their
faith and eternal destiny on a known lie?
```

- **References:** `<0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6uahp3pt8algufod3m7j6qoc723fi8p22t@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> actually count?

All the believers I know accept all of the Bible as true.

> But nobody believes that Jesus is a Greek letter.   How much of the
> Bible symbolic Truths and how much is literal Truths has been debated
> since it was written.

There is symbology in the Bible, without doubt. But I have never seen
a significant point that could not be resolved by simply considering
the local and Bible contexts. God is not so stupid as to be unable to
commuincate what He wants. The problem was never that. The problem is
people not wanting to believe what the Bible clearly says, like eternal
punishment for unrepentant sinners. You just won't believe it; not
because the Bible doesn't say it, but because you don't want to believe
it. In essence, you let your own judgment overrule the Word of God.
That is the central problem of humanity: humans want things their way,
not God's way. We can accept and submit to God on His terms, or reject
Him. We do not get to set the terms, any more than we can determine
natural law. What incredible arrogance, to presume to dictate to God
what His terms are or should be!

How does God respond to such human arrogance? Consider the following
passages from both Old and New Testaments:

Psalm 2 (NKJV)
  Why do the nations rage,
  And the people plot a vain thing?
  The kings of the earth set themselves,
  And the rulers take counsel together,
  Against the Lord and against His Anointed, saying,
  "Let us break Their bonds in pieces
  And cast away Their cords from us."
  He who sits in the heavens shall laugh;
  The Lord shall hold them in derision.
  Then He shall speak to them in His wrath,
  And distress them in His deep displeasure:
  "Yet I have set My King
  On My holy hill of Zion."
  "I will declare the decree:
  The Lord has said to Me,
  'You are My Son,
  Today I have begotten You.
  Ask of Me, and I will give You
  The nations for Your inheritance,
  And the ends of the earth for Your possession.
  You shall break them with a rod of iron;
  You shall dash them to pieces like a potter's vessel.' "
  Now therefore, be wise, O kings;
  Be instructed, you judges of the earth.
  Serve the Lord with fear,
  And rejoice with trembling.
  Kiss the Son, lest He be angry,
  And you perish in the way,
  When His wrath is kindled but a little.
  Blessed are all those who put their trust in Him.

John 3:16-19 (NKJV)
  "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten
  Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but
  have everlasting life. For God did not send His Son into
  the world to condemn the world, but that the world through
  Him might be saved. He who believes in Him is not condemned;
  but he who does not believe is condemned already, because he
  has not believed in the name of the only begotten Son of God.
  And this is the condemnation, that the light has come into
  the world, and men loved darkness rather than light, because
  their deeds were evil."
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:hS2mj.57509$Mu4.17465@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> it.

Or because it is inconsistent with the concept of a loving God.


>In essence, you let your own judgment overrule the Word of God.

As my own judgement has served me very well for the extent of my lifetime, I 
see no good reason to abandon it now.

If God wants to overrule my judgement, then, as Supreme Being, He resumably 
has that power. So far, He hasn't exercised it. I don't expect Him to, any 
time soon...

In the absence of any indication from God, I'll continue to reason things 
out for myself, as I have found this to be more successful than the 
alternative. (Letting other people reason things out for me...)

> That is the central problem of humanity: humans want things their way,
> not God's way. We can accept and submit to God on His terms, or reject
> Him.

It's a no-brainer. And it isn't personal. I don't reject God because I'm 
"anti-God" or wicked or evil; I do so because the "evidence" fails to 
persuade me.

>We do not get to set the terms, any more than we can determine
> natural law. What incredible arrogance, to presume to dictate to God
> what His terms are or should be!

What incredible arrogance on the part of a petty, petulant, bullying, object 
of worship to decide that there is no need to justify Himself to us.

We did a very bad job on creating this entity and it's time we fixed it.

In fact, it's time we dropped the imaginary friend altogether.

<tedious (although quite beautifully written) quotes snipped>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-24T17:14:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13pi6ufal6ur97d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> You're right Howard, many people do that. Unfortunately, it doesn't
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> destiny on a known lie?

Uh, Jesus never said he was the Messiah. He never said he was the Son of God 
(meaning biological rather than tribal). The Council of Nicea decided all 
that, along with the concept of the Trinity, in AD 325.

As for the Bible: it is almost irrelevant what it says. It is almost 
irrelevant what God said! What is important, or what SHOULD be important, is 
what God meant.

People have devoted their lives to studying Biblical texts. Here are some 
obvious things that one should not take seriously:
1. Exaggeration. Solomon had "treasures without end." Obviously they were 
not infinite.
2. Attribution: The Bible may accurately report the words of an actor, but 
could the actor have been lying? Sure he could. Heck, even God told a little 
fib to Abraham.
```

- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> knowing God and obtaining eternal life.

Very good. We agree... :-)


> If Jesus was not Who He claimed
> to be (the Messiah and Savior, the Son of God) then he was a liar or
> insane; certainly no one that should be admired or followed.

Too black and white.

Did he actually say he was the Saviour or that his sacrifice was to save 
Mankind? I can't find it.

Seems to me other people said that after he was dead (and risen, if you 
accept the Resurrection).

Please don't quote more scripture, but perhaps some references I can check 
in context, might shed more light on this. Any verbatim sources you know of, 
including the Gospels, where he is reported to have actually made these 
claims (rather than the writer presenting his own opinion) would be very 
acceptable.

If you are right and Jesus DID make these claims, it is also possible that 
he may have been mistaken, not wickedly seeking to deceive...

There was certainly SOMETHING special about this man; I would not dismiss 
all of the things attributed to him in the Gospels. I just don't accept it 
was supernatural, and I also believe much has been exaggerated and spun over 
centuries.

>The logical
> bottom line is that if the Bible isn't what it claims to be, the reliable
> and true Word of God,

Again, does the Bible itself claim to be the true Word of God?  Didn't the 
people who wrote it and the Council of Nicaea decide that?

As you know, it is a collection of ancient writings, describing events in 
the ancient world which can be independently verified in many cases. 
However, there are just as many ancient texts that were EXCLUDED from the 
Bible (and had the same credentials as what was INCLUDED) that we have to 
ask: "So, WHO decided what was the "reliable and true Word of God"?" (And, 
if you're anything like me, you then follow it with: "What agendas did these 
people have? What was their angle...?")

Is it unreasonable to suppose that if it WERE "the reliable and true Word of 
God", God would have put His imprimatur on it somewhere?... maybe it would 
light up every time you opened it :-), or there would be some other 
manifestation that this is a divine book, representing the will of a Supreme 
Being who is Lord of All...

No, it is a work of Men. Written by Men across centuries, in answer to the 
needs of Men.

> then it is a lie, and what nitwit would stake their
> faith and eternal destiny on a known lie?

Again, too black and white. It isn't necessarily a lie in the sense of a 
deliberate deceit. The nature of Truth changes. What might be an acceptable 
explanation to a primitive people 3500 years ago, may not be seen as "truth" 
today.

I believe there is much of value in the Scriptures of most of the World's 
great religions. It isn't about a two-valued Logic system: True or False, 
Black or White, it is about what is useful and helpful relative to other 
alternatives; a many-valued Logic system.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** roger.pearse@googlemail.com
- **Date:** 2008-01-26T04:27:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Jan 25, 12:26 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> > The logical bottom line is that if the Bible isn't what it claims
> > to be, the reliable and true Word of God,
>
> Again, does the Bible itself claim to be the true Word of God?  

Um, something very like it, as I think you know.

Phrasing a statement as a question is an old debating trick, which you
employ here in order to reach your desired conclusion, that the bible
is not inspired by God.  Now I have no opinion on that, but I am
somewhat nervous that you are simply playing with your correspondant
here, by starting any hares that are around in order to obscure
whatever the issue is. I didn't pay any attention to *that*, so have
no view on it; but I recognise the tricks of the trade.

> Didn't the people who wrote it and the Council of Nicaea decide that?

Decide what?

The idea that the Council of Nicaea determined the canon of scripture
-- which is what I think you are saying here -- is actually a myth.
No such events took place.

http://www.tertullian.org/rpearse/nicaea.html

Your other insinuation (no offence, but what else do I call a
statement which you don't actually make explicit but is clearly
there?) -- that if human beings wrote down a book then it cannot be
divinely inspired, well, that appears to be a theological proposition
about God.  Unless you can find an authority for it, you need to
demonstrate holiness of life and ability to work miracles in order for
your claim to authority to be taken seriously, surely?

At least, that was the medieval requirement, for such claims on behalf
of God, and if it was more widely applied today it would certainly cut
down on the number of L. Ron Hubbards around!

> However, there are just as many ancient texts that were EXCLUDED from the
> Bible (and had the same credentials as what was INCLUDED)

This is not correct.  All texts with any respectable credentials made
it into the NT.

> "So, WHO decided what was the "reliable and true Word of God"?"

A reasonable question.  I would only ask whether you have gone and
found out, or whether you are merely in "propellor mode" trying to
divert the question?  I think we know which is the case....  <grin>

Sorry, but you're playing a very traditional game here, raising any
objections or problems that you can find on the basis that you can
then get your opponent to buy into the idea that he can't win unless
he can rebut every one of your objections, while you win unless he
does.

> Is it unreasonable to suppose that if it WERE "the reliable and true Word of
> God", God would have put His imprimatur on it somewhere?... maybe it would
> light up every time you opened it :-)...

Still waiting for that evidence of holiness of life and ability to
work miracles from you so we can evaluate your theological
assertions...  <evil grin>

Man that asceticism hurts...

> No, it is a work of Men. Written by Men across centuries, in answer to the
> needs of Men.

Interesting theological assertion (see above for required evidence
prior to evaluation), but if this is supposed to be a conclusion from
what you wrote (or worse, insinuated) above, it is not.  It is merely
an assertion, and a rather tired one.

What you need to make clear is what your alternative is, surely.  I
think we would find that it was some form of conformity to societal
values, and that really won't do.  Surely?

> Again, too black and white. It isn't necessarily a lie in the sense of a
> deliberate deceit. The nature of Truth changes. What might be an acceptable
> explanation to a primitive people 3500 years ago, may not be seen as "truth"
> today.

I'm afraid that this sounds like the sort of evasion that that French
banker who lost $7bn might be trying on his employers at the
moment...  It isn't true, anyway.

> I believe there is much of value in the Scriptures of most of the World's
> great religions. It isn't about a two-valued Logic system: True or False,
> Black or White, it is about what is useful and helpful relative to other
> alternatives; a many-valued Logic system.

I would agree that there is little of value in the scriptures of the
religion of most people, including yourself, since these 'scriptures'
consist mainly of whatever the people who control the media agenda of
the time in which we live happen to want to make orthodoxy.  We need
to think for ourselves, and evaluate what we are told.  Nice to see
their tired old cliche about "the World's great religions" -- to
suggest fake equivalence -- as a distraction from a straight debate,
tho.  That's what they made it for.  But we should ask instead just
why our masters are trustworthy.  If we have money in the stockmarket,
doubly so!

I only popped in because I saw the ref. to Nicaea, but I hope this
helps.  I'm not attacking *you* -- you may not even realise how the
polemic you are repeating is structured -- but it *is* bogus polemic,
is put out by the Great and the Good of our day, and involves a fraud
being practised on us all.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-27T02:40:43+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
<roger.pearse@googlemail.com> wrote in message 
news:506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
On Jan 25, 12:26 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> > The logical bottom line is that if the Bible isn't what it claims
> > to be, the reliable and true Word of God,
>
> Again, does the Bible itself claim to be the true Word of God?

Um, something very like it, as I think you know.

Phrasing a statement as a question is an old debating trick, which you
employ here in order to reach your desired conclusion, that the bible
is not inspired by God.

[Pete:]==============

Not at all. I have no desired conclusion whatsoever. I don't personally 
believe the Bible was inspired by God, because I don't believe in God, but 
that wasn't the reason for my question. Having done some quite extensive 
research on the subject, I don't recall at any time seeing where the Bible 
itself (meaning the writings of which it is composed) carries the imprimatur 
of the Almighty. It is a fair question and not intended as a "debating 
trick".
==================

Now I have no opinion on that, but I am
somewhat nervous that you are simply playing with your correspondant
here, by starting any hares that are around in order to obscure
whatever the issue is.

[Pete:]==============

Please, don't be nervous. There are no hidden agendas here. I call 'em like 
I see 'em... Don't read anything more than what is actually written.
==================

I didn't pay any attention to *that*, so have
no view on it; but I recognise the tricks of the trade.



> Didn't the people who wrote it and the Council of Nicaea decide that?

Decide what?

[Pete:]===============

Decide what would be the contents of the Bible, as approved by the Christian 
Church.
===================

The idea that the Council of Nicaea determined the canon of scripture
-- which is what I think you are saying here -- is actually a myth.
No such events took place.
http://www.tertullian.org/rpearse/nicaea.html

[Pete:]===============

I accessed your page and found it very interesting. However, you cannot 
definitively state that "No such events took place". While I agree there is 
evidence to suggest that canonization of Scripture may not have been a major 
item on the agenda, I'm not persuaded that there was no discussion of it at 
all. I believe it is "arguable"...
====================

Your other insinuation (no offence, but what else do I call a
statement which you don't actually make explicit but is clearly
there?) -- that if human beings wrote down a book then it cannot be
divinely inspired, well, that appears to be a theological proposition
about God.

[Pete:]================

No such implication was intended, there is no such insinuation, and you have 
simply inferred incorrectly.

I can accept that people inspired by God may well have wrtten scripture. 
(Given that I could accept the existence of God). My problem with it is that 
had that been the case, there would be some evidence of divine inspiration, 
and the work would not be as flawed as it is. If God wrote it (directly or 
indirectly) why isn't His mark on it?
=====================

Unless you can find an authority for it, you need to
demonstrate holiness of life and ability to work miracles in order for
your claim to authority to be taken seriously, surely?

[Pete:]=================

I make no claim to Authority, other than Logic and the ability to reason. 
I'm not sure exactly what you think I'm claiming...
======================

At least, that was the medieval requirement, for such claims on behalf
of God, and if it was more widely applied today it would certainly cut
down on the number of L. Ron Hubbards around!

[Pete:]=================

I have a number of friends who are Scientologists. They don't strike me as 
any loonier than the Christian friends I have. If memory serves, I believe 
LRH is no longer around, so the number has been definitely cut by one...
======================

> However, there are just as many ancient texts that were EXCLUDED from the
> Bible (and had the same credentials as what was INCLUDED)

This is not correct.  All texts with any respectable credentials made
it into the NT.

[Pete:]==================
OH no they didn't... OH yes they did.... Look out behind you...!
=======================

> "So, WHO decided what was the "reliable and true Word of God"?"

A reasonable question.  I would only ask whether you have gone and
found out, or whether you are merely in "propellor mode" trying to
divert the question?  I think we know which is the case....  <grin>

[Pete:]===================

I don't know who you are referring to by "we". If I was trying to divert the 
question I wouldn't have asked it. As noted above, there is no more or less 
in what I write than what you see. As for "Going and finding out" I spent 30 
years doing exactly that... I still ask the question in the hope that 
someone else may have an aswer that is definitive.
========================

Sorry, but you're playing a very traditional game here, raising any
objections or problems that you can find on the basis that you can
then get your opponent to buy into the idea that he can't win unless
he can rebut every one of your objections, while you win unless he
does.

[Pete:]====================

I'm really nowhere near that complex.

=========================

> Is it unreasonable to suppose that if it WERE "the reliable and true Word 
> of
> God", God would have put His imprimatur on it somewhere?... maybe it would
> light up every time you opened it :-)...

Still waiting for that evidence of holiness of life and ability to
work miracles from you so we can evaluate your theological
assertions...  <evil grin>

[Pete:]======================

But even if it were forthcoming, you wouldn't accept it... :-)
===========================

Man that asceticism hurts...

[Pete:]=======================

There is balm in Gilead...
============================

> No, it is a work of Men. Written by Men across centuries, in answer to the
> needs of Men.

Interesting theological assertion (see above for required evidence
prior to evaluation), but if this is supposed to be a conclusion from
what you wrote (or worse, insinuated) above, it is not.  It is merely
an assertion, and a rather tired one.

[Pete:]========================

It is indeed an assertion. I never said otherwise and the rest of it was all 
in your head.

Whether it is tired or not, I really can't say. It is the first time I have 
expressed that idea in quite that way so it isn't tired to me. If others 
have done so already, then it is possible they came to the same conclusion I 
did, and this would end to support my claim, ratyher than refute it.
=============================

What you need to make clear is what your alternative is, surely.  I
think we would find that it was some form of conformity to societal
values, and that really won't do.  Surely?

[Pete:]=========================

I have no idea what you're talking about... :-)
==============================

> Again, too black and white. It isn't necessarily a lie in the sense of a
> deliberate deceit. The nature of Truth changes. What might be an 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> today.

I'm afraid that this sounds like the sort of evasion that that French
banker who lost $7bn might be trying on his employers at the
moment...  It isn't true, anyway.

[Pete:]=======================

You certainly show propensity to see evasion and dishonest argument where 
none is intended. I don't see the connection to the French fraud, but then, 
as I mentioned, I have no idea what you are talking about...

===========================

> I believe there is much of value in the Scriptures of most of the World's
> great religions. It isn't about a two-valued Logic system: True or False,
> Black or White, it is about what is useful and helpful relative to other
> alternatives; a many-valued Logic system.

I would agree that there is little of value in the scriptures of the
religion of most people, including yourself, since these 'scriptures'
consist mainly of whatever the people who control the media agenda of
the time in which we live happen to want to make orthodoxy.

{Pete:]========================

Ah, now THAT I understand. I reference the Scriptures of most of the World's 
great religions and you decide I am referring to the Media of the time, who 
have an agenda that becomes "orthodoxy".  If you read such scriptures, you 
might change your opinion (or not).

We need
to think for ourselves, and evaluate what we are told.

[Pete:]=========================

Yes, that works pretty well for me...
==============================


Nice to see
their tired old cliche about "the World's great religions" -- to
suggest fake equivalence -- as a distraction from a straight debate,
tho.  That's what they made it for.  But we should ask instead just
why our masters are trustworthy.  If we have money in the stockmarket,
doubly so!

[Pete:]=========================

"fake equivalence" to what?  You lost me again...
==============================

I only popped in because I saw the ref. to Nicaea, but I hope this
helps.  I'm not attacking *you* -- you may not even realise how the
polemic you are repeating is structured -- but it *is* bogus polemic,
is put out by the Great and the Good of our day, and involves a fraud
being practised on us all.

[Pete:]==========================

Just as well there are a few astute people who can see through it... right? 
:-)
===============================

All the best,

Same to you... get that paranoia seen to, though...:-)

Pete.
```

```
On Jan 26, 1:40 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> <roger.pea...@googlemail.com> wrote in message
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not at all. I have no desired conclusion whatsoever.

Would that were true.

> I don't personally believe the Bible was inspired by God, because
> I don't believe in God,

Seems reasonable.

> but that wasn't the reason for my question. Having done some quite extensive
> research on the subject, I don't recall at any time seeing where the Bible
> itself (meaning the writings of which it is composed) carries the imprimatur
> of the Almighty. It is a fair question and not intended as a "debating
> trick".

Well I'm sure people with bibles at the ready will tell you.  But if
it did, wouldn't the question be begged anyway?  It seems a bit of a
bogus issue to me.

> > Didn't the people who wrote it and the Council of Nicaea decide that?
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Church.

Ah.  Thanks.  No they didn't, is the answer.

The apostolic circle made no pronouncement on a canon of a NT, not
least because the issue was probably simply irrelevant in their time
-- who needs a book when you have a living voice.

> The idea that the Council of Nicaea determined the canon of scripture
> -- which is what I think you are saying here -- is actually a myth.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> definitively state that "No such events took place".

I think that we can.  If there is no evidence of it whatever, and the
other evidence indicates a process occurring rather differently, then
surely that is final.

Anyone can make up stories about antiquity.  But we ignore them unless
something in the data calls for it, surely?

> While I agree there is
> evidence to suggest that canonization of Scripture may not have been a major
> item on the agenda, I'm not persuaded that there was no discussion of it at
> all. I believe it is "arguable"...

Based on what piece of data?

> Your other insinuation (no offence, but what else do I call a
> statement which you don't actually make explicit but is clearly
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> simply inferred incorrectly.

Would that were true.

> I can accept that people inspired by God may well have wrtten scripture.
> (Given that I could accept the existence of God). My problem with it is that
> had that been the case, there would be some evidence of divine inspiration,
> and the work would not be as flawed as it is. If God wrote it (directly or
> indirectly) why isn't His mark on it?

I wonder how you would test for divine inspiration?  <chuckle>

And you deny making 'theological statements'??

> > However, there are just as many ancient texts that were EXCLUDED from the
> > Bible (and had the same credentials as what was INCLUDED)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> OH no they didn't... OH yes they did.... Look out behind you...!

I wonder why you didn't simply either back up your claim or withdraw
it.  Instead you choose to mock my comment.  Do you have problems with
honest debate?

> > "So, WHO decided what was the "reliable and true Word of God"?"
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> someone else may have an aswer that is definitive.

??? Anyone who chooses to know the history of the NT canon may do so
in an afternoon in any research library.  If you spent 30 years, why
on earth didn't you look at Metzger on the canon of the NT?  The
standard, undergraduate text?  Who did you ask, what did you read, if
you never got that far?

I am having great difficulty with believing in your sincerity, when
you write stuff like this, containing no data, assertions of "it's all
too difficult" followed by the conclusion "Christianity can't be
true".

> ========================
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm (evasion snipped)

Be honest.

> > No, it is a work of Men. Written by Men across centuries, in answer to the
> > needs of Men.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is indeed an assertion. I never said otherwise

Where did you label it an assertion?

> and the rest of it was all in your head.

Would that were true.

> Whether it is tired or not, I really can't say...(etc)

I think you've been caught red-handed, buster.  But if bashing the
bible requires these sorts of tricks and evasions, then I infer that
you must be rather afraid that it might all be true!  Quite why that
possibility should make you quake I do not know -- the idea doesn't
make me tremble one bit.

> =========================
> What you need to make clear is what your alternative is, surely.  I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (evasion snipped)

Hum.

> > Again, too black and white. It isn't necessarily a lie in the sense of a
> > deliberate deceit. The nature of Truth changes. What might be an
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> You certainly (snip) ...

The more he talked about integrity, the faster we counted our spoons.

> > I believe there is much of value in the Scriptures of most of the World's
> > great religions. It isn't about a two-valued Logic system: True or False,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> might change your opinion (or not).

No answer?  I thought not.

It's terribly easy to throw the rocks that the establishment proffer
at their selected targets, if we are dumb and gullible.  But it should
be a clue to us all, when we can't even discuss our alternative view.

> =========================
> Nice to see their tired old cliche about "the World's great religions"
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> ==============================

Fake equivalence between...

> I only popped in because I saw the ref. to Nicaea, but I hope this
> helps.  I'm not attacking *you* -- you may not even realise how the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Oh dear.  Funny how failures of integrity seem to be all part and
parcel of bashing the bible.  I can never see why they should be, but
they are.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

```
<roger.pearse@googlemail.com> wrote in message
news:506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
On Jan 25, 12:26 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> > The logical bottom line is that if the Bible isn't what it claims
> > to be, the reliable and true Word of God,
>>
>> Again, does the Bible itself claim to be the true Word of God?

>> However, there are just as many ancient texts that were EXCLUDED from the
>> Bible (and had the same credentials as what was INCLUDED)

>This is not correct.  All texts with any respectable credentials made
>it into the NT.

So there must have been texts with un-respectable credentials or else you
couldn't make that statement.  So trexts WERE excluded.  Do you really think
the Council was a free and democratic deabte?

>Sorry, but you're playing a very traditional game here, raising any
>objections or problems that you can find on the basis that you can
>then get your opponent to buy into the idea that he can't win unless
>he can rebut every one of your objections, while you win unless he
>does.

You're playing an equally ancient game of evading answers while appearing to
give them.


>I only popped in because I saw the ref. to Nicaea, but I hope this
>helps.  I'm not attacking *you* -- you may not even realise how the
>polemic you are repeating is structured -- but it *is* bogus polemic,
>is put out by the Great and the Good of our day, and involves a fraud
>being practised on us all.

You'll have to be a great deal more convincing than that.  A far greater
polemic is the one you're seeming to defend.

PL

Roger Pearse
```

```
On 26 Jan, 18:45, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> <roger.pea...@googlemail.com> wrote in message
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Council was a free and democratic deabte?

You know, wouldn't it better to find out the facts BEFORE dogmatising
like this?

1.  The Council of Nicaea made no statements or decisions relating to
the canon.  None, zero, zip.  OK?

2.  You talk about texts being excluded.  Naturally the church did not
accept into its services books forged by outsiders much later in order
to propagate paganism, or novels, or whatever.  Why should it?  You
seem to insinuate in some way this is unreasonable.  It isn't.

> >Sorry, but you're playing a very traditional game here, raising any
> >objections or problems that you can find on the basis that you can
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> You're ... (change of subject snipped)

Tu quoque noted.

> >I only popped in because I saw the ref. to Nicaea, but I hope this
> >helps.  I'm not attacking *you* -- you may not even realise how the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> You'll have to be a great deal more convincing than that.  (etc)

I fear that your own beliefs are merely based on convenience, and
often merely the convenience of those who know how to sucker *you*.
So I'm sorry that the truth is inconvenient, and I wish you well with
your second-hand day dream.  I would suggest we be more sceptical of
what we think you know, and rather more open-minded.

If you must throw stones, please at least be original.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

```
<roger.pearse@googlemail.com> wrote in message
news:d49a7111-2dbf-497d-817c-208d0881253d@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com...
On 26 Jan, 18:45, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> <roger.pea...@googlemail.com> wrote in message
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> However, there are just as many ancient texts that were EXCLUDED from
the
> >> Bible (and had the same credentials as what was INCLUDED)
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Council was a free and democratic deabte?

You know, wouldn't it better to find out the facts BEFORE dogmatising
like this?

1.  The Council of Nicaea made no statements or decisions relating to
the canon.  None, zero, zip.  OK?

>>PL  Ah, brain cramp.  Nicea was about Arianism, not about the Canon.  We
should be speaking about the  Council of Carthage.    I am at fault.  Why
not make the correction instead of being smug?

2.  You talk about texts being excluded.  Naturally the church did not
accept into its services books forged by outsiders much later in order
to propagate paganism, or novels, or whatever.  Why should it?  You
seem to insinuate in some way this is unreasonable.  It isn't.

>>PL Much later than what?  We're talking ~ 400 CE.  There is no possible
doubt that books were excluded.  Forgeries or novels?  How do you know?

> >Sorry, but you're playing a very traditional game here, raising any
> >objections or problems that you can find on the basis that you can
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> You're ... (change of subject snipped)

Tu quoque noted.

>
> You'll have to be a great deal more convincing than that. (etc)

I fear that your own beliefs are merely based on convenience, and
often merely the convenience of those who know how to sucker *you*.
So I'm sorry that the truth is inconvenient, and I wish you well with
your second-hand day dream.  I would suggest we be more sceptical of
what we think you know, and rather more open-minded.

If you must throw stones, please at least be original.

>>PL Pot calling the kettle black, I fear.  Since you came into this thread
late, you either don't know or have ignored my stated position, which is
agnostic.  That being said, I can read things for myself and decide if they
make sense.  The bible is clearly written by men  - the prophets of or
speakers for God, perhaps ruled by the spirit of God - which I can't deny is
possible, being agnostic; but men nonetheless, with all of man's failings.
I see no reason at all that God, as he seems to be defined, would ever
inspire such a muddle as to inspire life-and-death struggles over trivial
things written therein.

I strongly doubt if anyone can say anything original about this.  Sorry
about that.  I'm glad we're not living in Spain about 500 years ago: you
would make me very, very frightened.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

```
On Jan 26, 10:11 pm, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> <roger.pea...@googlemail.com> wrote in message
> news:d49a7111-2dbf-497d-817c-208d0881253d@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL:  Ah, brain cramp.  Nicea was about Arianism, not about the Canon.  

Glad that it went in second time around.  Indeed it was.

> 2.  You talk about texts being excluded.  Naturally the church did not
> accept into its services books forged by outsiders much later in order
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL Much later than what?  

Much later than the first century.

> We're talking ~ 400 CE.  

Are we?  The canon of the NT was settled for most practical purposes
by the 2nd century.  The extent of it wasn't a subject of controversy
in the 4th century.

> There is no possible doubt that books were excluded.  

I'm sorry that you are reiterating here; it's one of the easiest ways
to pick a fight.  But perhaps you didn't understand the point made
earlier.

There is no possible doubt that books that had been considered
canonical were not  excluded.   The "Da Vinci Code" is *not* a
reliable source of historical information, you know.

> Forgeries or novels?  How do you know?

By the usual means by which forgeries and novels are distinguished
from historical accounts.   You find out the dates of composition and
the contents and these tell you.

I realise that you're just repeating some standard ideas, but they are
not sound, if we think about them for five minutes.  If someone says
something is a forgery, the idea that we can't find out is absurd.
The police do it every day.

No, this particular story/idea/meme is circulated to us all by people
who want to confuse and obscure things, and aren't very scrupulous as
to how.  I would suggest that it might be people who don't care for
"Thou shalt not commit adultery..." and the like (which would sort of
cover most of our politicians!)

> > >Sorry, but you're playing a very traditional game here, raising any
> > >objections or problems that you can find on the basis that you can
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> have ignored my stated position, which is agnostic.  

Actually I couldn't care less if you were an Aztec and cut the heart
out of an actuary every Friday night (which would probably be a
socially worthy thing to do right now).  But I'm not sure that I see
how this relates to what I wrote.  Can you justify your belief
system?  Can you even state it?  If not, how "convincing" can you be
for it?

NB: the standard evasion at that point is to say "I don't have one" or
"I don't need to be".  But people engaging in throwing stones at the
religions of others automatically fail both statements, IMHO.

> That being said, I can read things for myself and decide if they
> make sense.  

I hope so.

> The bible is clearly written by men  ...(reiteration snipped)

We discussed this, and you were mistaken.

> I strongly doubt if anyone can say anything original about this.  Sorry
> about that.  

What I think we need to avoid are the mindless repetitions of slogans,
tho.

> I'm glad we're not living in Spain about 500 years ago: you
> would make me very, very frightened.

As far as I know it isn't agnostics who are being harassed by the
police today, so this is a particularly nasty accusation.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-29T12:02:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GTJnj.10886$q36.4454@newsfe14.lga>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net> <506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <beLmj.170$5M1.129@newsfe23.lga> <d49a7111-2dbf-497d-817c-208d0881253d@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <kfOmj.27166$R55.10071@newsfe13.lga> <c9fea0a7-5d7b-47be-9455-6b68a5d07352@1g2000hsl.googlegroups.com>`

```
Well, Roger, since your positions are so strongly defined and your opinions
so immoveable, perhaps you're confident enough to answer  a simple question.
Judson McClendon posed a challenge to the group: if irrefutable evidence for
the existence of God should be brought forward, would group members accept
it and change their lives accordingly: yes or no.  Unfortunately he didn't
state that it was a purely hypothetical situation: he merely wanted people
to demonstrate their objectivity (or otherwise); but everyone seems to have
taken it that he has such evidence available and has challenged him to
present it.

So, Roger Pearse, I challenge you to answer this question: in the event that
someone brought forward irrefutable evidence that God does NOT exist, would
you accept it and change your life accordingly?  (Such evidence will never
be available, so the question is purely hypothetical and you can safely
answer without compromising your beliefs).

The way you answer will reveal all.  If you answer yes or no, you're an
honest man and an intelligent one; if you won't answer, or obfuscate by
saying something like "But God does exist, so the question's not worth
answering" you have revealed yourself as having a closed mind, clinging to
beliefs for emotional reasons.

You ask about my belief systems: I accept as truth anything that can be
proved, whether I want it to be true or not.  I have beliefs which are not
supported by proof, to be sure, but I don't assert them as facts.  One thing
I do not accept as truth or belief is that I'm a hopelessly brainwashed
dupe, victim of whatever "our masters" have chosen to to publish.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-29T13:30:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<V8Lnj.64986$_m.14200@bignews4.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net> <506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <beLmj.170$5M1.129@newsfe23.lga> <d49a7111-2dbf-497d-817c-208d0881253d@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <kfOmj.27166$R55.10071@newsfe13.lga> <c9fea0a7-5d7b-47be-9455-6b68a5d07352@1g2000hsl.googlegroups.com> <GTJnj.10886$q36.4454@newsfe14.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Well, Roger, since your positions are so strongly defined and your opinions
> so immoveable, perhaps you're confident enough to answer  a simple question.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> present it. ...

I don't mean to interfere with your discussion, but I do want to correct a
small but critical error in your paragraph above, concerning my challenge.
Here is my actual challenge, copied from the original post:

Judson McClendon:
> Now, prove me wrong. Just honestly go on record right here that, if you could
> see real and unquestionable proof that the Bible is absolutely true and reliable,
> you would be willing to accept Jesus and submit your life to God.

The correction is that I did not say "proof of God's existence," I said "real
and unquestionable proof that the Bible is absolutely true and reliable." And
I did not say "accept God" I said "accept Jesus". I realize most non believers
won't understand, and think this is just a mere quibble. Let me explain why
the specific name of Jesus is critically important to this issue, and also
answer a possible objection related to my other comments in the thread.

I will only quote one Scripture here, but there are many others that could
be used to establish that it is faith in Jesus Christ specifically that God
requires for salvation. If anyone doubts this, I'll gladly supply more (e.g.
John 6:29). As you can see, the following passage is as clear and specific
as one could want.

(Acts 4:10-12)
  "let it be known to you all, and to all the people of Israel, that by the
  name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified, whom God raised from
  the dead, by Him this man stands here before you whole. "This is the 'stone
  which was rejected by you builders, which has become the chief cornerstone.'
  "Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under
  heaven given among men by which we must be saved."

I'll resist the urge to give more detail about why this is critical, but I
can if anyone wants. The point is that we aren't saved by believing in God,
or even by submitting to some form of 'god' in our actions. We are *only*
saved by having faith in the atoning sacrifice of Jesus Christ. Nothing
else.

I let it pass because everyone was getting tired of the issue (and see
below), but if you read Pete's, or any of the others', responses, you will
see they *did not* say they would "accept Jesus" under those conditions.
Pete also said he would reserve final judgment about submitting, even if
the proof was accepted. The essence and picture of the nature of rebellion
in the human heart: "I reserve to myself the right to decide when, how and
to whom I will submit, no matter the proof, and even to my God and Creator."
Any wonder why, when the this is the typical human position, that so many
seek and cling to completely secular belief systems? :-)

Now, to answer a possible objection. Another reason I didn't protest Pete's
or the others' failure to respond specifically to my challenge is that I
was somewhat to blame, in that I gave them a small "out" by failing to be
specific about the name of Jesus every time I mentioned the challenge. I
knew better, and quoted the challenge verbatim several times, but in the
heat of the debate I was not explicit about the name of Jesus every single
time I mentioned the challenge, and they wiggled through. :-)  Sorry.
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> Judson,
>  Probably a silly/moot point, but I assume that I am correct in assuming <G> that when you state,
>    "proof that the Bible is absolutely true and reliable"
>
> that you are referring to (what is commonly called) the "Old and new Testaments - without the Apocrypha".

Yes. By "Old Testament" I mean the same books that the Jews refer to
as the Tanakh. I bought my English translation Tanakh at a synagogue.
Not wanting to offend, I asked a Jewish friend to go with me to his
synagogue bookstore. I was surprised that they use the same chapter
and verse divisions and numbering as the Christian Old Testament, at
least in the version I have. The order of the books is different, but
the original language source texts are the same.

> My point being that defining what is meant by "the Bible" before deciding what beliefs would follow.  (Matters of translation 
> could impact "details" but not the thrust of your question.)
```

```
> can if anyone wants. The point is that we aren't saved by believing in 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the proof was accepted.

Absolutely. Nevertheless, YOUR point was that my mind was closed; MY point 
is that it isn't.

I responded honestly to your challenge, both in its original form and in the 
amended form which you later posted. Instead of acknowledging that, and 
apologizing for your presumption (as you said you would) you now say that I 
"wiggled out" on a technicality that was due to an ommision/amendment on 
YOUR part.

That is neither fair, honest, nor sporting. Maybe I was wrong to expect 
those behaviours?


> The essence and picture of the nature of rebellion
> in the human heart: "I reserve to myself the right to decide when, how and
> to whom I will submit, no matter the proof, and even to my God and 
> Creator."

Yes. I don't see it as rebellion; I see it as common sense.

When Human hearts offer only blind obedience (to ANYTHING) we are done 
for...

(And, if you are right, and God actually created us in His own image, why 
would he be proud of a kiss-arse, blindly obedient, creation? Might as well 
have built automatons...)

An ever-increasing number, thankfully...

> Now, to answer a possible objection. Another reason I didn't protest 
> Pete's
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> time I mentioned the challenge, and they wiggled through. :-)  Sorry.

I'm really quite appalled by this Judson.

What "heat of debate"? I noticed increasing savagery in your posts as you 
felt that I was not responding, and I saw no reason for it. In your zeal to 
get the answer you wanted, you ignored the answers you actually got. Even if 
I had responded as you wished, would it really have proved anything? Would 
you have changed any minds or done anything other than feel you won a 
debating point? Suppose you had actually been right, and my mind WAS closed 
to the possibility of real and substantial evidence that vindicates the 
existence of GOD, truth of the Bible, or any other platform you want to 
promote (and I re-iterate again and finally that it is NOT...)? So, what?

Since when did the Universe require my approval in order to persist?

I've re-evaluated why I would spend time here and realised that it is 
completely pointless. Fundmentalist, racist bigots like Robert Pearse, and 
Evangelical Christian zealots like yourself are NEVER going to even look at 
(never mind accept or evaluate) ANYTHING from ANYONE who is NOT one of you, 
and who does not adhere to the party line as perceived by you.

There is really no point in debating it, and least of all in this particular 
forum.

I thought it might be possible; I should have known better...

Pete.
```

```
religious upbringing, as an Anglican, I was always taught about the
Trinity -God the father, God the son, and God the Holy Ghost.  I was always
taught that they were one but three.  You are probably aware of the ancient
controversy about whether Jesus came "through" or "of" God.  It makes no
difference to what I'm suggesting to Pearse.

(And please, I don't want you to elucidate this.  I am not in the least bit
interested).

PL
```

```
On Jan 29, 7:30 pm, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> The correction is that I did not say "proof of God's existence,"
> I said "real and unquestionable proof that the Bible is absolutely
> true and reliable."

I don't believe such proof exists.  Do people really become Christians
by coming to believe this about the bible, while not being believers
(admittedly a possible position; the devil holds it, no doubt), and
then draw the conclusion that perhaps they had better convert?

I fear that you have got hold of a debased form of Christian
apologetics here.  Any system of thought must start from facts about
which there is no possible doubt, and work from there.  I don't
believe that this position is one of these -- that position is a
conclusion from a great many other things, surely?

The basic fact of human existence is sod's law.  As far as I know this
is generally acknowledged but ignored by the system of beliefs in
which we all grow up -- societal values.  But the bible tells us that
this is so, and is because the world is screwed up, because we're all
screwed up too.

Discuss.  (But not with me; theology makes my mind hurt.  One
substantia, two ousias, ouch!)

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```
