- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net> <506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <beLmj.170$5M1.129@newsfe23.lga> <d49a7111-2dbf-497d-817c-208d0881253d@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <kfOmj.27166$R55.10071@newsfe13.lga> <c9fea0a7-5d7b-47be-9455-6b68a5d07352@1g2000hsl.googlegroups.com> <GTJnj.10886$q36.4454@newsfe14.lga>`

```
On Jan 29, 6:02 pm, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> Well, Roger, since your positions are so strongly defined and your opinions
> so immoveable, ...

Projection noted.

> perhaps you're confident enough to answer  a simple question.

Only if you pay money; otherwise I have other things to do.
Intelligent people don't let other people set the agenda in terms
which make only one answer possible.

> You ask about my belief systems: I accept as truth anything that can be
> proved, whether I want it to be true or not.  

So do I.  Few people would say otherwise, which makes this a rather
empty comment.

But since you make it, start with showing how living in conformity to
societal values can be proved then, since that is precisely the belief
system of most people, no doubt including yourself.

> I have beliefs which are not supported by proof, to be sure, but
> I don't assert them as facts.  

The question is whether you live by them or not.

> One thing I do not accept as truth or belief is that I'm a
> hopelessly brainwashed dupe, victim of whatever "our masters"
> have chosen to to publish.

But... can you even state the system of beliefs by which we live?  If
not, consider where the values we live by are liable to come from.
Don't most of us just create a subset of the environment in which he
happen to have been born and grown up?

Fish find it hard to see water.  But we can't discuss the merits of
breathing oxygen while we take water as an undiscussed default.  IMHO,
anyway.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

"roger.pearse@googlemail.com" <roger.pearse@googlemail.com> wrote:

Roger Pearse

On Jan 30, 5:20 pm, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> <roger.pea...@googlemail.com> wrote in message
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>PL.  So you don't have the confidence to ...

Two things.

1.  Kindly indent your posts in a standard way.

2.  Improve your grasp of logic.

The remainder of your post seems to consist of demands and bluster,
with a consistent refusal to address the points that I am making.
Well that is your right; but I'm not sure why you bothered. The
bluster of those who refuse to put their own belief-systems on the
table isn't impressive to those of us who assess things rationally and
sceptically.  Until you try to do this, in a sensible and
intellectually coherent manner, nothing that you say can be taken as
anything but a rationalisation of self-servingness (I mean no insult;
but unless it is the outworking of a rational system, what else can it
be?)  That isn't something which words will help you with.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

On Jan 30, 5:20 pm, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> <roger.pea...@googlemail.com> wrote in message
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>PL. So you don't have the confidence to ...

Two things.

1.  Kindly indent your posts in a standard way.

>>PL If you've been reading these posts you'll be aware that the indenting
and adding of >>'s isn't happening with all messages.  Rather than adding
all the >'s I'm using this method.  Very sorry if it offends you.

2.  Improve your grasp of logic.

The remainder of your post seems to consist of demands and bluster,
with a consistent refusal to address the points that I am making.
Well that is your right; but I'm not sure why you bothered. The
bluster of those who refuse to put their own belief-systems on the
table isn't impressive to those of us who assess things rationally and
sceptically.  Until you try to do this, in a sensible and
intellectually coherent manner, nothing that you say can be taken as
anything but a rationalisation of self-servingness (I mean no insult;
but unless it is the outworking of a rational system, what else can it
be?)  That isn't something which words will help you with.

All the best,

Roger Pearse

>>PL Pot calling the kettle black, again, only more poisonously.

There are people on this group who have been taking the same side of the
debate as you.  All of them are reasonably courteous and usually attempt to
answer the points made.  Your replies seem to come from some rarified height
of smugness and illogic which enables you to evade answers with complete
conviction that you've actually responded.  You exhibit almost a Jehovah's
Witness' certainty that opposition proves you right.  I have come to the
conclusion that treating with you further will accomplish nothing at all.

Therefore, I will not respond to or comment on any of your posts forthwith.
If you want to look upon that as a victory over me, feel free.

I urge the rest of the group to do likewise.

PL

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 71)_

```
On Jan 31, 12:28 am, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> <roger.pea...@googlemail.com> wrote in message
> news:373dfe56-e3d7-4725-bf8d-76b8df96c8ba@n20g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well that is your right; but I'm not sure why you bothered....

> >>PL (complaints snipped).

    I wonder hesitantly if this is actually born of hurt feelings?  If
so, I apologise.  I certainly wouldn't want to inflict injury on
anyone.  I post to inform.  (You have been pretty rude to me, you
know; hence the kind of response you got).

    I can certainly be at fault in the way that I post.  Something
that I notice is that, having been online for many years, one gets
hardened.  You can only deal with so many trolls before developing a
reflex technique of slapping them.  The trouble is that it can be
misapplied.

    I'm afraid that like most long-term usenet posters, I respond best
to courtesy and reason, and rather firmly to jeering and deceit.  The
dishonest will always meet courtesy with attempts to pick a fight;
these days I don't fight that.  The tricks of the troll I recognise at
once, although they can be unconscious in some people.

    But the consequence can be a certain unfortunate hardness. It can
mean a failure to recognise that poster K, to whom I respond today, is
not in fact the hardened villain poster J whom I flayed last week,
despite the identity of their arguments, but a kid who has read one
book and is repeating the silliness in perfect honesty and enthusiasm
and good faith.

    I hope that all of us would tolerate a certain amount of insults
and jeering so long as there is content.  I try to make allowances for
mere clumsiness, although of course I get cross occasionally like
everyone else.  The medium itself tends to make commonplace remarks
sound like insults, even when this is not intended.

    Anything dodgy I just bat straight back.  Abuse I don't even
notice any more.  I've been abused by experts, in the past, after
all.  All abuse tends to be the same.  I ought to give it numbers...

> I have come to the conclusion that treating with you further
> will accomplish nothing at all.

Probably a wise decision.

> Therefore, I will not respond to or comment on any of your posts forthwith.
> If you want to look upon that as a victory over me, feel free.

    As a matter of fact I have no interest in illusory 'victories' or
personalities; only in the subjects on which I post.  Any troll can
wear people down to silence.  I would hardly post under my own name if
I sought to do this.

    Good luck to you, all the same.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** "roger.pearse@googlemail.com" <roger.pearse@googlemail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-30T06:53:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88056498-1c19-4e92-8278-b7da788d18a6@m34g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net> <506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <beLmj.170$5M1.129@newsfe23.lga> <d49a7111-2dbf-497d-817c-208d0881253d@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <kfOmj.27166$R55.10071@newsfe13.lga> <c9fea0a7-5d7b-47be-9455-6b68a5d07352@1g2000hsl.googlegroups.com> <rrmvp395jb38nj5d41jd9delfps8ukk0tq@4ax.com>`

```
On Jan 30, 2:10 am, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> >Are we?  The canon of the NT was settled for most practical purposes
> >by the 2nd century.  The extent of it wasn't a subject of controversy
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> basis.  

This is actually a separate discussion to the general subject of "was
the NT invented in the 4th century", and I don't want to confuse that,
to which the answer is an unequivocal "no, don't be silly".

But I think there is confusion here.  The extent of it was not
*agreed* (one could argue that it isn't agreed even today, given that
the RCC have the OT apocrypha and no-one else does); but it was not a
subject of controversy.  By this I mean that the arguments of the 4th
century are about Arianism, not about the canon, and that party lines
form around the former, not the latter.  No-one seems to be able to
make any definitive decision, and it seems as if quietly people end up
with a canon that includes everything that anyone has any real grounds
to suppose apostolic.  The truth is that no-one actually knows how it
all came into being (as far as I can tell).  People mutter about
councils, but all the councils that discuss canon have no ecumenical
authority, and merely issue a list of genuine books (indicating only
that they were being pestered with fakes, of the kind that St.
Augustine somewhere remniscences about finding in the bookstalls on
the quays of Carthage, rather than any decision process).

The canon of the Syriac world is a version of that prevalent in the
Greek east, with the same uncertainties about the Catholic epistles
and Revelation.  The special issues of whether the Peshitta should
stay closer to a Jewish targum or the Greek, with consequent
retranslations (e.g. by Philoxenus) are, of course, part of the
history of the development of the Syriac NT, but not really the point
here.

Sorry, long answer to a brief point, but I don't want to write an
essay on the development of the canon, but merely to rebut common
canards.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net> <506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <beLmj.170$5M1.129@newsfe23.lga> <d49a7111-2dbf-497d-817c-208d0881253d@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <kfOmj.27166$R55.10071@newsfe13.lga> <c9fea0a7-5d7b-47be-9455-6b68a5d07352@1g2000hsl.googlegroups.com> <ccabcf9b-7729-4284-b617-16ae82dc5226@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Jan 30, 7:16 pm, Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> On 29 Jan, 16:07, "roger.pea...@googlemail.com" >
> > There is no possible doubt that books that had been considered
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> were excluded.

Heh.  Yes, that got away from me, in my desire to push nonsense right
back again.  I'll try again:

Those texts which are included in the list known in modern times as
'New Testament apocrypha' are not found listed as canonical in any
early source.  There is no doubt whatever about that; anyone who
disagrees gets to produce the source which gives the text in question
as canonical.  Anyone who claims that texts considered canonical were
excluded gets to produce the minutes of who did this and when.  (The
whole canard does not reflect what we actually know about how the
canon came into existence).

The reason that nothing much was excluded is pretty simple.  Pretty
much all the texts that had any reasonable claim to being apostolic
were included in the NT.

But forgery of pseudo-gospels by outsiders to try to confuse things
has been a cottage industry from the 2nd century to our own day (there
are several 'gospels' supposedly by Thomas, for instance, of varying
dates).  I am always amused when people try to pretend that obvious
frauds (which they tend not to have read) are in some way genuine
products of the apostolic circle.  Usually it's just the false
equivalence trick; a standard technique to confuse the dumb, put out
by our Masters.

But all these other texts reveal their later date by their dependence
on the canonical gospels (at best) -- they can hardly have been
written before documents that they quote verbatim.  They also reveal
their spurious authorship because they peddle ideas which are not
documented in the apostolic age, nor among the organisation that those
apostles founded, but are instead clearly identifiable as products of
later movements (of various complexions).  Fakes have to have these
fingerprints; they're created to peddle some novel idea, and so a
novel idea *must* be present in them.  And so indeed is the case.

Note that there were some texts that do have genuine claims to emanate
from the apostolic circle.  The gospel of the Hebrews is one.  But
none of these have survived.  Those that have are crude frauds.

> I have no doubt that the gospel according to mary was excluded.

That is because you are mis-informed (and why this one of out the
hundreds of apocrypha?  Is this the DVC again?).  The pseudo-gospel of
Mary was never considered canonical in any of the apostolic churches.
A look at its contents indicates that it emanates from some part of
the 2nd century gnostic movement.

> > > Forgeries or novels?  How do you know?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> then look it up on wikipedia.

I do indeed know about Q, which of course is not a manuscript... <g>

What we today suppose to be issues of style is not a reliable guide to
authorship or construction, unfortunately, although always
interesting.  Pardon me; I know that you are not likely to believe me,
not least as you aren't educated enough (sorry, but you DID make this
plain just now with your reference to Q) to do more than repeat
whatever you read somewhere.  I do happen to know about this.  You
will appreciate that I have little interest in shouting "DID...DIDN'T"
to and fro.

Instead, your best method to learn about this is to find a non-
controversial text (NOT the bible) and do some stylistic analysis on
it, and read about what scholars say on the subject.

> > No, this particular story/idea/meme is circulated to us all by people
> > who want to confuse and obscure things, and aren't very scrupulous as
> > to how.
>
> Like bible scholars? Interesting that you threw in the word meme.

Oh dear.  Now I rather regret having taken the time to respond
substantively.  Now I wonder if anything else you say deserves my
time.  Let me look.

> >  I would suggest that it might be people who don't care for
> > "Thou shalt not commit adultery..." and the like (which would sort of
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> equally capable.

Tellingly, you don't attempt it.  Somehow they never do.

The remainder of the post doesn't relate to mine in any substantive
way, and doesn't deserve a response.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net> <506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <beLmj.170$5M1.129@newsfe23.lga> <d49a7111-2dbf-497d-817c-208d0881253d@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <kfOmj.27166$R55.10071@newsfe13.lga> <c9fea0a7-5d7b-47be-9455-6b68a5d07352@1g2000hsl.googlegroups.com> <ccabcf9b-7729-4284-b617-16ae82dc5226@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <76a1e6f1-0be4-4c54-8fda-4b1266985c76@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <8b9fcd87-128a-4b6d-a237-93fce032bd08@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Jan 30, 8:23 pm, Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> On 30 Jan, 20:00, "roger.pea...@googlemail.com"
> <roger.pea...@googlemail.com> wrote:
…[82 more quoted lines elided]…
> texts in those gospels.

It's a bit more complex than that, but that's more or less correct.
(I ought to indicate at least one of the possible complexities: it is
not in fact *necessarily* the case that it ever existed or is in the
fact the reason why the two texts have portions which are not from
Mark and are identical verbatim, but it's a useful placeholder for the
'common source material').

Be wary of hearsay online, incidentally.  There seem to be little men
around somewhere who are actually *inventing* nonsense about all these
things, and we get to see their productions at fifteenth hand.  On
matters of controversy, political or religious, always ask to see the
ancient source texts that say whatever is being alleged (they tend to
be online these days anyway).  It can be pure fiction.  This is from
experience with stories about Mithras, for instance; or the Council of
Nicaea, which is where I came in.

Disclaimer: I have no special knowledge or interest in the various
modern theories around the process of composition of the canonical
gospel texts.  This is because the material that I *have* read
suggests to me that these theories don't seem to be derived as closely
as they might from solid ancient data; and fiction and speculation
bores me.  (I *have* read all the ancient evidence on the subject
which exists, which is little enough.  On the other hand it's splendid
that we have any; most ancient literary texts just appear, without any
real indications as to how they came to be composed.  Some of Cicero's
have details of the process, tho, in his letters).

None of this stuff has any real bearing on whether Christianity is
true, IMHO, or why people do or don't believe in it.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

- **Message-ID:** `<59s3q39rshh64m1mlr1lfn5stobmp8op59@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c9fea0a7-5d7b-47be-9455-6b68a5d07352@1g2000hsl.googlegroups.com> <ccabcf9b-7729-4284-b617-16ae82dc5226@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <76a1e6f1-0be4-4c54-8fda-4b1266985c76@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <8b9fcd87-128a-4b6d-a237-93fce032bd08@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <ef40d913-e555-4898-af8a-7049d7ed200c@y5g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <r0p3q31tkn8guq4a263tj0fkrqvp7npi5f@4ax.com> <af21ef6b-01dd-42f0-a0c8-e9abbe4ffd77@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 31 Jan 2008 07:56:42 -0800 (PST),
"roger.pearse@googlemail.com" <roger.pearse@googlemail.com> wrote:

>> When ancient data don't support my beliefs, I need to work more at
>> interpreting which parts really matter (so that my beliefs get the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>false belief system.

Surely.

But I see all over people who know their way is right (it works for
them) who if they analyze it at all, it's to justify their belief. An
obvious example is how they search hard to "prove" that homosexuals
are dammed, while ignoring commandments from God that don't fit modern
life styles.     Another example is when someone denounces someone
with a different political view than theirs as being "unpatriotic".

That doesn't bother me nearly as much as those who expand that to say
that other ways are wrong - and that those who have other ways will be
punished by them, if not in this world, in the next.     Quite often
they aren't content to wait for the next world and seek to punish them
now.    

While this is common with various forms of '-ism" (Patriotism,
Communism, Islam, etc.), Christianity has a hope for those who believe
in emulating WWJD (What would Jesus do), who should note that Jesus'
example was to do good, even if it disagreed with the scriptures'
"right".
```

On Jan 31, 4:13 pm, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> life styles.    

The condemnation of sodomy is not a novelty, tho; it is the
*endorsement* of it to fit modern life styles that is.

> Another example is when someone denounces someone
> with a different political view than theirs as being "unpatriotic".

I agree that is sickening, but it's worse on the PC side.  If I were
to express views held by everyone 20 years ago, I would risk prison.
There is no right of free speech in the UK.

Intimidation as a technique to stifle debate is always awful, IMHO.

> That doesn't bother me nearly as much as those who expand that to say
> that other ways are wrong - and that those who have other ways will be
> punished by them, if not in this world, in the next.     Quite often
> they aren't content to wait for the next world and seek to punish them
> now.    

I'm content to be punished by anyone young, blonde and female, and
dressed in something low-cut and tight-fitting.  I'd almost pay the
fines willingly!  But I insist on high-heels. (for her)

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

On Sat, 26 Jan 2008 04:27:24 -0800 (PST), roger.pearse@googlemail.com
wrote:

>> "So, WHO decided what was the "reliable and true Word of God"?"
>
>A reasonable question.  I would only ask whether you have gone and

>divert the question?  I think we know which is the case....  <grin>

Looking at history we see lots of changes in how people interpret the
Word of God.   And lots of changes in what people have for most of
their values worth fighting for.     But each time, people are sure
that this time their values are what God intended when He created the
universe.   Those other generations, churches, and cultures had it
wrong, but This Time we have it right.     Too bad the new kids are
getting it wrong again.

- **References:** `<XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net> <506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8kvrp3ph1kbi5bb4rt77csn53qcqrsdstb@4ax.com>`

```
On Jan 28, 4:13 pm, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Sat, 26 Jan 2008 04:27:24 -0800 (PST), roger.pea...@googlemail.com
> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Word of God.  

We see some, certainly, although I'm not sure what you have in mind.

But can't we relate most of the ones that I can think of to the
'clamour of the times' -- the ideas then in fashion in contemporary
society?  That people have a tendency to read into the bible what
those who control the media agenda of that society want to be
normative?  In other words, the written text is not a 100% guarantee
that people will not follow the mores of their times.

All the same, this sounds like saying that people change their minds,
therefore we can't know what the text says.  With respect, this isn't
so (whichever text we have in mind!).

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-28T13:32:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0cesp31babvbob72snm619frv2d76k9o3q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net> <506de0df-d205-4c06-ba1a-37a08770bfa2@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8kvrp3ph1kbi5bb4rt77csn53qcqrsdstb@4ax.com> <89c66546-f3b3-496c-b1c0-0ac9814168d6@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Mon, 28 Jan 2008 12:15:02 -0800 (PST),
"roger.pearse@googlemail.com" <roger.pearse@googlemail.com> wrote:

>> Looking at history we see lots of changes in how people interpret the
>> Word of God. ï¿½
>
>We see some, certainly, although I'm not sure what you have in mind.

Over and over again people have been willing to use God's name to
demand that others accept their values.   They use the Bible to
justify attitudes and evils done.   When values change, the new
generation understands that the previous generation had important
things wrong.    It's not always so obvious as when the LDS profit
tells their church that blacks can now be saved - but it happens.
...


>All the same, this sounds like saying that people change their minds,
>therefore we can't know what the text says.  With respect, this isn't
>so (whichever text we have in mind!).

I'm in favor of "flip flopping" as we learn more and mature.
Politicians who are unwilling to be convinced are politicians who
won't change from wrong paths.

But back to your statement:

The important thing to remember is that people in the past have
studied the scriptures with much vigor and came up with support for
many different values they were willing to war for.    We have every
reason to believe that this process did not end with our generation.

Maybe being so Righteous about this generation's values isn't
important enough to war over.   Maybe Jesus' example of preferring to
be good (healing on the Sabbath), is a better model.
```

On Jan 28, 8:32 pm, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Mon, 28 Jan 2008 12:15:02 -0800 (PST),
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> justify attitudes and evils done.  

They do if the bible is important in their society, I agree.  But is
this the case today?

You see, I live in a society where any appeal to the bible would
automatically discredit whoever did it.  Indeed the Prime Minister
said as much when he said that to talk about religion would make him
sound like a "complete nutcase".

What I see today is the politically correct demanding that people
accept their values, hate whom they hate, and accept whom they
accept.  There's no biblical origin to this.  So unless this is a
historical excursus, I'm not sure that I see the relevance? (Sorry)

> >All the same, this sounds like saying that people change their minds,
> >therefore we can't know what the text says.  With respect, this isn't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> won't change from wrong paths.

Maybe, but isn't the problem with the b*gg*rs that they are usually
complete tarts -- anyone's if it looks advantageous?

> Maybe being so Righteous about this generation's values isn't
> important enough to war over.  

I agree.  But then I don't share any of this generation's values; I
didn't vote for them, don't respect them, and know that they will pass
away in a generation as all temporary values do.  So undue respect for
the Selfish Generation and its mores is not something that I am
tempted to.

I do agree that people decorating purely societal values with biblical
phraseology is revolting.  You see this with the current fad, climate
change -- people hunting around in the bible for stuff to justify it,
which is frankly irrelevant and unnecessary to the real (secular)
urgency behind that campaign, and evidently not what any previous
generation ever saw in it.  I hate people who get all religious about
a party political programme.  They're different things (which is not
to say that religious imperatives should never take a political form).

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:0731a576-2cd4-4d27-8ab2-b25060eabecb@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com...
On 25 Jan, 01:31, "Charles Hottel" <chot...@earthlink.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
>
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> Friedman.

Is that a reference to the Q manuscript (preceding two of the gospels)?

IIRC it has to do with the idea that the J manuscript and the Court History 
of Davis may have be written by the same author. Page 12 of the book lists 
the verses that the author believes make up one coherent complete story. 
There are words and phrases that occur throughout that do not occur 
elsewhere. It is a flowing, continuous story. There are quotations, 
allusions, puns and a variety of literary devices that show they originally 
went together.  It was the Bible's editors who broke the unity and 
continuity of this work by mixing other works into it.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-25T10:53:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3uomj.60016$vt2.37057@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13pieuh6qdoqqb5@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
>
> Do you believe the Bible was literally dictated by God?

I believe that Bible was truly inspired by God. God obviously worked
using the personalities and abilities of the 40 or so individual
authors. But I believe what got written on the page was precisely
what God wanted to get there. And I do not believe human attitudes
of "perfection" are the same as God's. Our ideas of "perfection"
are far too simplistic. What God means by "perfect" appears far more
rich and subtle than what humans mean by such terms. The gap between
our intellect and God makes the difference between a man's intellect
and a worm seem insignificant. God sees everything, every detail of
every single issue. If one of the original authors made a spelling
error, for example, God would know exactly what the result would be,
throughout history. Though I believe the original documents were
"perfect" in that they were what God wanted, and would accomplish
precisely what God intended them to accomplish, a human would be
incapable of determining that from observation of the original
document. You or I might see a spelling error and think "Oh, oh!
There's a flaw." But God might see that the spelling error actually
achieved some good purpose 500 years later when a scribe found it.
We know for a fact that many wonderful Scriptural "gems of truth"
have emerged from the digging Bible scholars have done while seeking
to reconcile two apparently contradictory passages. It seems clear
to me that God could easily have placed such passages for just this
purpose, for God's subtlety, wisdom and knowledge are far beyond
human comprehension. Jesus said that not even the tiny marks of the
letters would "pass from the law" until heaven and earth pass away
and all things are fulfilled. Obviously, God is well aware of such
details.

There is an old expression "killing two birds with one stone." But
God can "kill an infinity of birds with one stone" if He wanted.
Jesus said that all the hair in our heads are numbered. I believe
that is a one of the greatest understatements in history. I believe
that God could give you the name of every sub-atomic particle in the
universe, and that He not only knows where it is and what it's doing
all the time, but that it's doing what it is because God constantly
wills it. I believe the physical universe we see is a direct and
constant manifestation of the power and will of God, specifically of
the power of Jesus, the Second Person of the Trinity. The original
Greek in Colossians 1:17 expresses that all things exist through the
constant exercise of His power. God would not have to destroy, He
would only have to "turn loose" and it would cease to exist.

> As humans were involved perfection does not seem possible.  It does seem that the Bibile has been edited i.e. changed. For a 
> reference see "The Hidden Book in the Bible, The Discovery of the First Prose Masterpiece" by Richard Elliott Friedman. I confess 
> that I read this quite a while ago and the details escape me now.  My point is why would it need editing?  Did God get it wrong 
> the first time?

God *always* gets it right. Humans plan and do their thing, but God is
sovereign, and His plans will not fail. :-)

It is undeniable that scribes down through the ages have knowingly or
accidentally introduced small changes in the manuscripts. On the plus
side, the number of ancient Bible manuscripts is very large, literally
in the tans of thousands. Many are fragments, of course. When the
Dead Sea Scrolls were found, and the older manuscripts were compared
with the best previously known manuscripts, they were in extremely
close agreement. I believe what we have today is so close to the
original documents that there are no significant issues of doctrine
involved. How many people were killed when a particular wall fell is
not going to endanger the doctrines of the Trinity or Original Sin or
Jesus Christ as the Son of God. :-)

To my mind, one of the most convincing evidences for the authenticity
and accuracy of the Bible is that the number of things in the Bible
that are "challenged" by contemporary historical and archeological
convention is ever decreasing, not increasing. If the Bible was merely
a human creation, there would be many inevitable flaws in such a large
book, and over time more and more of them would be revealed. What we
actually see is the reverse. Over time the Bible has been verified as
correct again and again by subsequent historical and geographical
discoveries. My favorite is Sargon II, which I've listed here before.
The only known reference to him was in Isaiah 20:1, and many bible
critics cited that as a Bible error, until Circa 1930, when they dug
up his palace and 100,000 volume library, in which was found an
account of the very event mentioned in Isaiah 20:1. Just because our
very limited secular knowledge does not always support the Bible,
does not mean the Bible is wrong. :-)

The Bible is far more than simply a bunch of words, even divinely
inspired words. The Bible is the written Word of God, and it is alive
and powerful (Hebrews 4:12). After all, God spoke the universe into
existence. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

and mud walls. So where are they?

> Jesus Christ as the Son of God. :-)
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

know that the bible is wrong about so many things. The only way you
can justify your statement is if you start with a plateau of disbelief
and then corroborate the odd statement or word.


> If the Bible was merely
> a human creation, there would be many inevitable flaws in such a large
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> On 25 Jan, 16:53, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> When the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> and mud walls. So where are they?

Where was Sargon II's palace before 1930? Just because they
haven't found it, doesn't mean it never existed. Duh!

>> Jesus Christ as the Son of God. :-)
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> and then corroborate the odd statement or word.

You're confusing the decline of faith in a secular population with
actual proof. In this particular case, I'm speaking of verification
of Biblical historial accuracy and authenticity, not whether or not
you actually believe what it says about God. You do not believe
that God created the workd in six days, but you have no proof
that He did not. For example, in the Biblical account of the Exodus,
the Bible says the Jews had to make brick without straw. They have
found archeological evidence of that. This is the kind of verification
I'm referring to here.

>> If the Bible was merely
>> a human creation, there would be many inevitable flaws in such a large
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not much of the bible has been verified.

Incredibly broad, absolutely false statement. Such a pitifully false
statement implies you're either joking, abysmally mis-informed
and uninformed, or obtuse.

>> My favorite is Sargon II, which I've listed here before.
>> The only known reference to him was in Isaiah 20:1, and many bible
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> you would say corroborate the bible?

Names are wrong by whose taxonomy?

>> The Bible is far more than simply a bunch of words, even divinely
>> inspired words. The Bible is the written Word of God, and it is alive
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the true word of god but that the Koran is not?

Mohammed is dead, and the location of his remains are known. Jesus
Christ rose from the dead, and over 500 people saw Him at once. You
should read "The Testimony of the Evangelists" by Simon Greenleaf.
Greenleaf, who did, indeed write "The Book" on legal evidence (every
law student in the Western Hemisphere knows Simon Greenleaf was
and may have had to study his "A Treatise on the Law of Evidence"),
insists that the Gospel accounts of Jesus could be proven in a court
of law. Check out Greenleaf's credentials as an authority on legal
evidence, then read the book. ISBN 0-8254-2747-9
```

"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> > Not much of the bible has been verified.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> At the moment, then, your comment applies to yourself on this matter.

I said the documentation for the events of Christ's life were vastly more
than any other comparably ancient event. The documents are vastly more
numerous, and much closer to the actual events. For example, there are
only nine ancient copies of Caesar's Gallic Wars, and the earliest dates
from over 900 years after the events. Yet no one seriously questions
them. However, there are thousands of copies of the New testament
manuscripts, some dating from about 30 years after the events, and that
was in living memory of them. It is uncommon for even hostile secular
critics who are knowledgeable of this evidence to question that the Gospel
accounts of Jesus' life are accurate. If you don't believe that, then there is
nothing I can say that would change your mind.
```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:GS_nj.62829$vt2.45599@bignews8.bellsouth.net...
> "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> > Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > best-documented events of the time.  You've yet to justify that
statement.
> > At the moment, then, your comment applies to yourself on this matter.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> accounts of Jesus' life are accurate. If you don't believe that, then
there is
> nothing I can say that would change your mind.
> --
> Judson McClendon       judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)


Well, come on.  You've mentioned ONE document so far (I am lumping together
for the purposes of brevity all the various "copies of New Testament
manuscripts"), the Bible, so far.  Any others?  The entry in Josephus is
usually held to be a lter interpolation; there are two (or three) one-line
mentions of Jesus in cotemporary historians.  If the New York Times makes a
statement, the fact that there are millions of copies of it doesn't make it
any more authoritative.  Come on, then: what else is there?

In one of the Gospels, Jesus blasts a fig tree because it wasn't bearing
fruit, as an example of what you can do by faith.  The text also says it was
out of season.  If this is true, then it makes you wonder about Jesus' state
of mind.  (And it isn't a parable).

As for Caesar: five minutes' research enabled me to find four contemporary
works mentioning him; there are lots of published epigrams (and I can't find
the title of the book); there are statues etc. still in existence; and the
Gallic Wars.  I'm sure there's an authoritative listing of all the sources
on Caesar.

I'm sure you get the point.

PL
```

```
tlmfru wrote:
>
> Well, come on.  You've mentioned ONE document so far (I am lumping
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are correct in that the paragraph in Josephus mentioning Jesus is 
universally accepted to be a gloss.

A more interesting question is, out of the thousands of pages in the 
Josephus' work, why did the author of that paragraph, either Josephus or 
someone else, put the paragraph where it is? Immediately after the paragraph 
mentioning Jesus, is the scandal involving the Temple of Isis. In a nutshell 
(short version):

A dude named Mundis was smitten by a married woman named Paulina. He offered 
her 200,000 Attic drachmas for one night's play. Mundis was refused. As a 
result of this rejection, Mundis determined to kill himself, but his 
housekeeper, an evil woman named Ide, devised a plan. She conspired with the 
priests of the Temple of Isis.

The High Priest of the Temple approached Paulina and told her the God 
Annubis wished to sup with her! Paulina quickly assented. The appointed 
night came and Paulina went to the temple and dined alone. The lights were 
put out, the priests retired, and the great doors locked.

Thereupon Mundis, disguised as the god Annubis, lept from behind the 
curtains and had his way with the willing Paulina all the night through.

On the third day thereafter, Mundis met Paulina in the market and said: 
"Nay, Paulina, thou hast saved me two hundred thousand drachmas, which sum 
thou mightest have added to thy own family; yet hast thou not failed to be 
at my service in the manner I invited thee... I rejoice in the pleasure I 
reaped by what I did when I took unto myself the name Annubis."

The mortified Paulina told her husband. Her husband told Tiberius.

Tiberus ordered the priests crucified, as well as the housekeeper Ide, the 
temple torn down and the statue of the god thrown in the Tiber.

Mundis was only banished inasmuch as what he did was done out of love.

I have my own thoughts about the placement of this story; you may have 
yours. At least one thing is clear - human nature hasn't changed much.
```

On 30 Jan, 13:22, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> > Judson McClendon <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> nothing I can say that would change your mind.

As a secular individual, I dispute the veracity and contents of the
gospels. They are contradictory and at least two appear to have
substantial amounts incorporated into them from a preceding document.
The gospels only document about three years of JCs life and only, to
my knowledge, have one external (to the bible) contemporaneous
reference to JC. Not much to go on. And something which would probably
fail in a court of law. (and yes I do recall the nonsence written
earlier about how it could prove his existence - I don't believe it).
```

- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13pieuh6qdoqqb5@corp.supernews.com> <3uomj.60016$vt2.37057@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <fb6cd888-dae4-4116-ae76-a4e842156b6d@q39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <vROnj.68395$N67.7431@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <EjSnj.23248$E01.2704@newsfe22.lga> <GS_nj.62829$vt2.45599@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Jan 30, 1:22 pm, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> > Judson McClendon <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> than any other comparably ancient event.

I think this needs to be nuanced rather more.  It's broadly true.  But
there are several different things mixed up in this, which I think I
should clarify.

Firstly the life of Jesus of Nazareth is indeed very well documented,
because of the existence of 4 contemporary biographies.  It is better
documented than any *comparable* figure of the same period; i.e.
relatively obscure provincial figures.  It has better *literary*
attestation than some emperors! But... it is not better documented
than some of the major figures of the immediately preceding period.
And figures who left traces such as coins and inscriptions are of
course not comparable; we have nothing like that for Jesus (or indeed
for most important figures apart from emperors and officials).

If we ignore archaeology, consider the emperor under whom Jesus
flourished; Tiberius.  Our major sources for all the events of his
reign are Tacitus, Suetonius, Cassius Dio and Josephus.  All these
write ca. 100 AD or later, but are perfectly good sources, and we get
a far better picture of his reign and policies from these than from
contemporary sources written in fear of Sejanus' praetorian thugs.  In
addition we do have contemporary literature of various sorts.  So
there is good documentation, of sorts.

But surely we are not comparing an obscure Jewish peasant with the
master of the world?  If so, surely we commit a canard?

> The documents are vastly more numerous, and much closer to the
> actual events. For example, there are
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> was in living memory of them.

This is a truly common confusion (on all sides, I might add), between
the *texts* that were written in antiquity, and the physical
handwritten copies (=manuscripts)that exist today of them.

The above paragraph is taken from some literature discussing the
evidence that the words written in the bible in ancient times have
reached us, responding to the objection that since all copying
involves error then we can't actually possess a copy of the bible word
for word anyway (which objection involves obscurantism).  And it is
absolutely correct; there are far more and earlier biblical
manuscripts than of any other Greek or Latin text.  There is no
mystery to this; the scribes of the succeeding period needed bibles.
The next largest group of manuscripts is of copies of texts by the
major Church Fathers, and for the same reason.

But this has nothing to do with the number of *texts* that describe
events.  Do I make the distinction clear?

> It is uncommon for even hostile secular critics who are knowledgeable
> of this evidence to question that the Gospel accounts of Jesus' life
> are accurate.

I can assure you that there is no detail of Christian origins that has
not been denied, misrepresented, lied about or distorted over the last
200 years.  Trust me on this!

But what I think you mean is that educated people of all religions,
including atheists who hate Christianity, do not try to assert that
the texts are so corrupt that we don't even know what they said.  I
think this is true, although one atheist bible scholar is currently
trying very hard to put this idea out.

In short I think that the above contains overstatements.  What we can
certainly say is :

1.  that the text of the New Testament that we have is a good copy of
the original -- better, anyway, than any copy of any other ancient
*literary* text -- and good enough for all practical purposes (whether
that includes theological or divine purposes is for people to whom God
speaks to say, not us :-) ).

2.  that the number of literary texts is more than adequate to detail
the life, career and teaching of this obscure but remarkable man, and
is even corroborated by stray mentions in surviving classical
literature, as far as they go.

I must admit that I don't quite understand why people get into
difficulty with all this.  The same sort of thing could be said of
Alexander of Abuteichnos, who invented a fake deity Glycon in the 2nd
century (documented by Lucian, and with coins of the cult), although
the evidence for him is much less.  The question is whether the claims
of this person are true.  On that, there might legitimately be more
than one opinion.

All the same, I weary of the silent presumption that all of us can
continue to live by whatever system we currently do, but only the
Christians have to prove their position.  Rationally that must be
nonsense.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>> So, Judson, you've stated that the birth of Christ is one of the
>> best-documented events of the time.  You've yet to justify that
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> that, then there is nothing I can say that would change your mind.

Uh, no. The earliest surviving manuscript of the New Testament is a two-inch 
square from the Gospel of John, dated to mid-fourth century. That the 
Gospels themselves originated earlier - Mark is dated about 65 CE - they 
probably were transmitted orally for several centuries. Of course Mark never 
saw Jesus, so he was not a witness himself. And no early manuscript of the 
Gospels has ever been found in Aramaic, Hebrew, or Latin.

That there are no manuscript fragments from earlier times may be the result 
of the Council of Nicea creating an establishment church and that 
organization's zeal to vacuum up any non-conforming texts.
```

```
On Jan 31, 1:47 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >> So, Judson, you've stated that the birth of Christ is one of the
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> square from the Gospel of John, dated to mid-fourth century.

Um, by the mid fourth century we have complete parchment bibles such
as Sinaiticus!

From the third century we have more or less complete manuscripts of
most NT texts, including gospels.

The earliest surviving fragment, to which you refer, was dated to 125
AD by all the paleographers who found it, plus or minus 25 years.
That dating still holds, despite intermittent efforts by odd scholars
to try to date it later.

> That the Gospels themselves originated earlier - Mark is dated about
> 65 CE - they probably were transmitted orally for several centuries.

No, that can't be so.

> Of course Mark never saw Jesus, so he was not a witness himself.

He recorded the testimony of Peter, who was.

> And no early manuscript of the Gospels has ever been found in
> Aramaic, Hebrew, or Latin.

Not least since they were composed in Greek... :-)

> That there are no manuscript fragments from earlier times may be the result
> of the Council of Nicea creating an establishment church

No such thing occurred at the Council of Nicaea.  Christianity did not
become the state religion until 50 years later, in the time of
Theodosius I.

> and that organization's zeal to vacuum up any non-conforming texts.

This story unfortunately is not recorded in any contemporary text,
despite the "Da Vinci Code", and doesn't reflect how things actually
happened on the ground.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

```
On Jan 31, 4:59 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> roger.pea...@googlemail.com wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> you remember something?

A police siren?  :-)

> >> And no early manuscript of the Gospels has ever been found in
> >> Aramaic, Hebrew, or Latin.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> And Peter or Mark spoke Greek? Oh well.

An awful lot of people out in the region speak English at the moment,
as well as their native Arabic.  In Hellenistic times everyone who
wanted to sell a camel or a postcard probably needed to know some
Greek.

Josephus knew Greek, but not to rely on, so hired assistants to polish
his composition.  These are sufficiently distinctive, in terms of the
older authors whom they mimic, as to be identifiable by modern
editors.  It's notable in that context that Peter co-writes his
epistle with Silas, whose Greek name of Silvanus suggests that he was
pretty Hellenised.

> >> That there are no manuscript fragments from earlier times may be the
> >> result of the Council of Nicea creating an establishment church
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> dogma as heretics.

Probably all a bit anachronistic; the Spanish Inquisition wasn't to be
thought of for a 1000 years.  At that date the Fathers had just come
through a bestial persecution, and some of them had come straight from
the prison-camps etc.  Many of them were simply glad to be alive!
Others couldn't get over the unimaginable idea of a Christian
emperor.

The later ideas of authoritarianism aren't what is going on there, or
not seriously.  Every movement has the right to self-definition,
without this being sinister. The use of accusations of heresy as part
of a power struggle really starts ca. 400 in the Origenist
controversies, when money and power come into it.  (The accounts of
the Council of Ephesus in 433 don't make pretty reading)  Constantine
was somewhat responsible for this, in that around the time of the
Council of Nicaea he gave the clergy wealth and legal position.  The
rest was inevitable, as people scrambled to join the new privileged
class.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

On 29 Jan, 23:42, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

> > When the wall of Jericho fell might imperil your faith. Where are
> > Sodom and Gomorrah? Fire and Brimstone would not destroy stone, brick
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is only one mention of Sargon in the bible. Hardly a glowing
commendation; Isaiah 20:1, which records the Assyrian capture of
Ashdod in 711 BC.

For the record, I do not doubt that Sodom and Gommorah existed and
were destroyed by natural processes (slipping either under sea or in
to liquid mud).

>
> >> To my mind, one of the most convincing evidences for the authenticity
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> actual proof.

I suspect that the number of non-believers or atheists in the UK
during the early 1900s would not constitute a secular population but
rather a non-religious minority. Faith in the bible in the early 20th
century was high with most people believing in the bible 100%.

> In this particular case, I'm speaking of verification
> of Biblical historial accuracy and authenticity, not whether or not
> you actually believe what it says about God.

I didn't say whether I believed in god. In the 1900s virtually
everyone in the UK believed in god and the bible so faith would have
been absolute. Since then the readiness of people to believe in the
word of the bible has declined and some of the blame for that belongs
to the various religious groups espousing different doctrines and
interpretations of the bible.

The bible is not historically accurate.

> You do not believe
> that God created the workd in six days, but you have no proof
> that He did not.

Outside of the bible all proof points to a duration of 4.5 - 5 billion
years. Only the bible says 6 days and some groups argue that a day can
be as long as you want it to be. With faith so malleable, what price
is truth?

> For example, in the Biblical account of the Exodus,
> the Bible says the Jews had to make brick without straw. They have
> found archeological evidence of that. This is the kind of verification
> I'm referring to here.

Couldn't your god have put those bricks there just to provide
corroboration for a poorly written book?

>
> >> If the Bible was merely
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> and uninformed, or obtuse.

Please list all parts of the bible which have been irrefutably proven
to be true. I would be particularly interested in knowing where Lot's
wife, in the form of a pillar of salt, can be found.


>
> >> My favorite is Sargon II, which I've listed here before.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Names are wrong by whose taxonomy?

By the only means of verifying the truth of the bible: outside
sources. If a number of disparate sources disagree with the bible and
have some corroboration within themselves then the bible must, in the
absence of any verifying source, be deemed to be wrong.

>
> >> The Bible is far more than simply a bunch of words, even divinely
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> evidence, then read the book. ISBN 0-8254-2747-9

Greenleaf's standard of proof leaves a lot to be desired. Only a court
of canonical law would find in favour of the accounts of the gospels.
Two of the gospels would be thrown out for being blatant plagiarism of
another manuscript and no gospel would be admitted as evidence because
they have multiple authors and were not contemporary to the incidents
that they purport to be eye-witness to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-30T13:05:16-07:00

lick the salt, the wind does blow quite a bit and the sand and salt abrades 
everything.

Interestingly, God told Lot not to look back, but Lot didn't relay that 
interesting tid-bit to his wife.

It's possible that Lot came up with a God-given opportunity to off the old 
lady.

```
Alistair wrote:
>
> What about the chronologies of the pharoahs (names are wrong) and the
> genealogies mentioned in this thread that differ from the texts that
> you would say corroborate the bible?
>

It's getting worked out. One of the more interesting endeavors was a British 
Serving Officer in India who, as an amateur, puzzled over the contradictions 
in the geneologies of the Hebrew Kings. Until he worked it out, scholars had 
dismissed the chronologies as scribal error or mistake.

Here's what this officer (whose name escapes me) figured out:
1. The beginnings of the year were different in the kingdoms of Judea and 
Israel. One group started their year on Rosh Hashannah, the other on 
Passover.
2. Some kingships started their reign with a year zero, others started 
numbering at one, some didn't start either number until the new year 
(whichever calendar was in use).
3. Sometimes there was a co-regency - a son and father ruled concurrently, 
so the twelfth year of the father may have been the third year of the son.

Anyway, applying all these fudge-factors (almost all with sufficient 
justification), the linage of the Hebrew Kings made sense.

Here's another story you might find interesting.

In the late 1920s, a chap (call him "Smith" because I forgot his name) 
graduates from Oxford with a degree in linguistics. In time, he was 
conscripted by the British War Ministry and sent to code-breaking school. He 
then spent the war years involved in de-cyphering enemy messages. After the 
war, Smith went on to become a reader in Norse mythology and ancient 
languages at Cambridge.

Shift gears

In the early 1970s, a farmer in Minnesota dug up a small boulder with Runic 
inscriptions. He loaded it into his truck and carried it to a history 
teacher at the local community college. The instructor, had no idea, but 
took some Polaroids and sent them to a professor he knew at CCNY.

Professor Brown (?) immediately mailed back: "Whatever you do, don't give 
Olaf Svenson any money! These are runes, but they don't make any sense. 
Somebody just copied random letters from an encyclopedia." When Svenson (or 
whatever his name was) was told, he took the rock back home and dumped it 
behind the barn and said "Fuck 'em all" (probably in Swedish).

Time passes.

In the early '80's, an annual convention of Norse scolars was held in New 
York (there are not a LOT of Norse scholars in the world). Professor Brown 
was the host, and invited a few (all? both?) of the attendees to his home 
for cocktails. Sufficiently lubricated, Brown pulls out his bullshit file 
and passes around the Polaroids from several years before.

Smith looks. Click-click-click! IT'S IN CODE! A simple substitution code 
that translated into something like: "Here, on the 214th day of the voyage 
of Bundy the Long Knife, I, Faustine the Brave, claim all this land in the 
name of our king...."

Serendipity. What are the chances of an English scholar trained in ancient 
Norse languages AND cryptography coming across a Runic surveyor's mark from 
Minnesota while visiting in New York?

Anyway, Smith hies himself back to England and founds a new science: 
Archeological Cryptology, perhaps a sub-discipline of deciphering ancient 
texts (hieroglyphs, Minoan B, etc.).

Also know the British Museum never throws anything away. Over the centuries, 
the museum has accumulated a lot of "junk" manuscripts, mainly from folks 
wanting money. Smith began studying these, and, sure enough, many were in 
code.

(Aside: I may have a few details wrong: The names, the sequence, the dates, 
places, and times. The facts may be fuzzy, but the narrative is correct. I 
make no apologies; I suffer from Some-heimers)

One of the more interesting decodings, to bring this all back to the 
discussion at hand, is a confusing verse in 2 Kings. Right in the middle of 
a geneology, there's a verse which says "Bananas are cheaper by the dozen," 
or somesuch. Religious scholars, both Jewish and Christian, have, for two 
thousand years, tried to deduce the holy substance and divine message behind 
this puzzling verse. Turns out this verse, too, is a simple substitution 
code. It translates as "This King is a fink."

Point is, discoveries are being made all the time, and not always from 
digging stuff up.
```

Massachusetts in 1926.

This description doesn't ring a bell, but it has resemblances to the
Kensington Stone story: farmer finds stone under a tree with what appear to
be runes, detailing a visit of a Nordic party nearby.  Much controversy.
But the inscription has now been pretty well proved to be a fake,
particularly because some of the runes were found only in a popular
scientific & historical publication of the same era.

Pity.  I liked the story!

On Wed, 30 Jan 2008 19:12:11 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>Alistair wrote:
>>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That was the Kensington Rune Stone.  The stone was found by a
Minnesota farmer named Olaf Ohman in November of 1898 (way before your
1970 date) while a digging up a poplar tree stump on the southern
slope of a 50-foot high knoll. The stone was buried face down about
six inches below the surface, with the tree roots wrapped around it. 

The stone was sent to the University of Minnesota and then to Chicago.
It was studied by runic scholars, who interpreted the inscription to
be an account of Norse explorers in the 14th Century. Many authorities
who have since examined the stone have claimed it a forgery, but
others are equally certain of its authenticity.

The transliteration of the text is generally accepted as:

"Eight Goths and 22 Norwegians on a journey of exploration from
Vinland very far west. We had camp by 2 rocky islands one day's
journey north from this stone. We were out fishing one day. After we
came home we found 10 men red with blood and dead. AVM [Ave Maria]
save us from evil." 

The inscription along the edge of the stone says:

"Have 10 men by the sea to look after our ships 14 days' journey from
this island. Year 1362."

The stone is now in the Runestone Museum in Alexandria, Minnesota,
near where the stone was found.

You can read a complete account of the Kensington Rune Stone and the
Heavener Rune Stone and other rune discoveries in America at:

http://www.sunnyway.com/runes/americanstones.html


>Professor Brown (?) immediately mailed back: "Whatever you do, don't give 
>Olaf Svenson any money! These are runes, but they don't make any sense. 
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,

         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

In view of this, I just spent half an hour scouring my library and actually
found my authority!

"The Kensington Stone, a mystery solved" by Erik Wahlgren: U. of Wisconsin
Press, Madison, 1958: LCC 58-6281.  I don't know if it has been assigned an
ISBN.

As I say, the peculiar runestones are the definitive proof pro or can, and
this book pretty well establishes the CON case.

PL

> >discussion at hand, is a confusing verse in 2 Kings. Right in the middle
of
> >a geneology, there's a verse which says "Bananas are cheaper by the
dozen,"
> >or somesuch. Religious scholars, both Jewish and Christian, have, for two
> >thousand years, tried to deduce the holy substance and divine message
behind
> >this puzzling verse. Turns out this verse, too, is a simple substitution
> >code. It translates as "This King is a fink."
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve

On Thu, 31 Jan 2008 11:54:52 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>In view of this, I just spent half an hour scouring my library and actually
>found my authority!
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>PL

The book you mention is rhetorically excellent, factually poor. Filled
with numerous errors, mistatements and outright lies - a good looking
but hollow condemnation of the Kensington Stone.

Better to read these books by Robert Hall:
---The Kensington Rune-Stone Is Genuine, Columbia SC Hornbeam Press,
1982.  The first book I read on the subject, presents several
excellent arguments for authenticity. Or...
---The Kensington Rune-Stone, Authentic and Important, Lake Bluff IL,
Jupiter Press, 1994
A follow up to the original containing much of the same material, but
adds some additional evidence, including a strong criticism of
Whalgren's work.


>
>
…[101 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

--Baseball Radio Announcer Frankie Frisch
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Steve

SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:smv3q3lsofiaa78voc78f4ejb0derqahfk@4ax.com...
> >
> >Smith looks. Click-click-click! IT'S IN CODE! A simple substitution code
> >that translated into something like: "Here, on the 214th day of the
voyage
> >of Bundy the Long Knife, I, Faustine the Brave, claim all this land in
the
> >name of our king...."
> >
> >Serendipity. What are the chances of an English scholar trained in
ancient
> >Norse languages AND cryptography coming across a Runic surveyor's mark
from
> >Minnesota while visiting in New York?
> >

This sort of thing raises very grave doubts.  I find it just about
impossible to believe that a set of runes that, translated, come out with a
coherent message, can ALSO be a substitution code.  There have been similar
claims for the scrap of manuscript (parchment) that was found in the Oak
Island pit.  A very short message might work - three or four characters -
but not anything longer.

PL
```

On Jan 25, 1:31 am, "Charles Hottel" <chot...@earthlink.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
>
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do you believe the Bible was literally dictated by God?

Who knows?

> As humans were involved perfection does not seem possible.  

Quite a lot of theological assumptions in there!  But it's fairly
obvious that I can give you a message; quite why a God should not be
able to do the same is not clear to me.

> It does seem that the Bibile has been edited i.e. changed.

Not in any meaningful sense.  But I sense a perfect storm of strawmen
about to be deployed.  The question that we should ask, surely, is why
our masters want our minds to be populated with some many excuses as
to why "Do not steal, do not commit adultery, do not lie" is not
binding.  While they keep our minds off this question, and while we
internalise what they feed us, we are unlikely to reach *any* valid
conclusions.  Surely?

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

ok" attitude for a "I'm ok you're ok one".  Then with practice you may be

- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com>`

```
I have a book somewhere called something like "Antiquity and Unity of the
Human Race", put out by some Christian theologian in the late 1800's,
discussing the writings of the Greeks and others, where they give figures of
thousands of years for this or that event or lifespan.  He harmonizes this
sort of thing with Bishop Usher's 4004 AD chronology by stating that the
Greeks etc. often wrote "years" when they meant "months".

If this sort of thinking is permissible then there's nothing that can't be
proved.

PL

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-25T12:57:27+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5vsmv9F1nvpvnU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 24 Jan 2008 22:07:12 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> before days, it must be a different definition than literalists claim.

Nevertheless, if you add up the lifespans of the genealogies described in 
the Pentateuch, starting from Adam, and add in the same for the descendants 
of Noah, then add in the 3500 years since it was written, you get around 
6000 years. It has nothing to do with the literal or allegorical meaning of 
"Days".

Even if we assumed that EVERYBODY lived several hundred years, it still 
comes out with the creation of the planet as less than 20,000 years ago.

I can't buy that, and, even if I could, there are too many other 
inconsistencies to make it acceptable. If Adam and Eve were the first people 
on Earth, how could Adam's son depart to the land of Nod and take a wife, 
after killing his brother?

The only options are:

1. It is an allegory not meant to be taken literally.
2. It is simply wrong.

Either way, I can't subscribe to it.

Pete.
```

Perhaps you should re-read chapters 4 and 5.

It is quite clear that Adam and Eve produced Cain, Abel, and Seth BEFORE 
they produced other offspring. At the time Cain murdered Abel, Seth was not 
born, and neither were their other offspring.

Just one of many inconsistencies in the "reliable and true Holy Word of 
God".

Pete.

```
On 25 Jan, 00:14, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:


> > I can't buy that, and, even if I could, there are too many other inconsistencies to make it acceptable. If Adam and Eve were the
> > first people on Earth, how could Adam's son depart to the land of Nod and take a wife, after killing his brother?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3. Adam and Eve had other sons and daughters (Gen 5:4), and Cain married one. Duh!

Incest? Sick.
KJV 12:
004:016 And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in
        the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.

004:017 And Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch:
        and he builded a city, and called the name of the city,
        after the name of his son, Enoch.

The bible does not explicitly state that Cain's wife was his sister
nor does it say that she wasn't. However, if she were his sister then
it would read "And Cain took his wife and went out from the presence
of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.

Interestingly, Cain founds a city (which presumably was populated by
more of his brothers and sisters who were enjoined by god to slay Cain
upon sight).
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.

Not if she was already there, or went there later. The Bible does not
record the timeframe, only the sequence of the birth of the sons.
These people lived almost a thousand years; what difference if Cain's
wife was born 50 or 100 years after him? Many people marry with
10 or 15 year difference in age, equivalent to more than 150 years
difference in those days. The Bible says Adam and Eve had other
sons and daughters, it doesn't say how many, or how many their
children had, or their grandchildren. Cain's wife could have been his
neice, or grand neice. Lack of detail does not imply a contradiction.

> Interestingly, Cain founds a city (which presumably was populated by
> more of his brothers and sisters who were enjoined by god to slay Cain
> upon sight).

(Genesis 4:15)
  And the Lord said to him, "Therefore, whoever kills Cain, vengeance
  shall be taken on him sevenfold." And the Lord set a mark on Cain,
  lest anyone finding him should kill him.
```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> *been* around ~6,000 years.

True. Assuming God did create the universe in 6 days, which is more
logical?

A. God created everything without the "implied prehistory" you suggest,
and Adam couldn't see a single star for 4 years, etc.

B. God created everything with an "implied prehistory", with everything
already in balance "as if" it had been there. There would be chickens,
eggs, and everything in between. Trees would have rings. Adam could see
all stars bright enough to be visible.

To my mind, assumption A is illogical, and criticizing the Creation
account on that basis is asinine. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-04T15:51:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> will be punished as part of a grand plan.

Duh! God *told* us what He did in the Bible. No "fooling" there.

> A better alternative is to ask what is important to learn from the
> scriptures.   Where should one's efforts be best used - to prove
> ourselves Right?   Or to follow Jesus' example?

What is important to learn from the Scriptures is what the Scriptures
actually say. The reason Jesus came into the world was to die in
payment for our sins (1 Timothy 1:15), not to be an example. Jesus
lived a perfect life *because* He did everything *right*. He followed
perfectly every principle and law of God, the Father. There is no
"follow Jesus example" apart from "being right"; that's an oxymoron.

Even so, no one is saved by their actions. Everyone has "fallen short"
of what God demands (Romans 3:21-23), and nobody is acceptable to God
except through faith in Jesus (Acts 4:10-12). Jesus is the only Man
Who ever lived a life acceptable to God, and no other human will ever
see heaven, apart from accepting God's grace through believing in the
atoning work of Jesus Christ. Our work is to accept God's plan of
salvation (John 6:29). We obey God to please Him *because* of what He
has done for us, and also because of Who He is.
```

The question isn't "who died" but "who died, rose again and ascended
bodily into Heaven?" Only been one of those. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T08:27:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

>
> The question isn't "who died" but "who died, rose again and ascended
> bodily into Heaven?" Only been one of those. :-)

Uh, not exactly. Of the ~34 miracles attributed to Jesus (including - sort 
of - resurrection), He was not the first. All had predecessors or one kind 
or another.

Some, admittedly, are only similar. Jesus walked on water, Moses moved the 
water out of the way so the Israelites could walk on the mud. Heck, even 
mere mortals can match some of Jesus' spectaculars (such as reanimating the 
dead or restoring sight).

Regarding "ascending bodily into Heaven," I refer you to Enoch, who didn't 
even die before making the trip ("... and Enoch was no more for God took 
him...")

Anyway, that stuff is not terribly important. To the believer, Jesus was who 
He was because of who He was - not because of "miracles." God does not 
expect us to differentiate between miracles and magic. If "miracles" were 
THE test of divinity, we'd all be worshiping Siegfreud and Roy.
```
