```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:5G0sj.30067$1f.17922@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wroteL
>>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>>>
<snip>>>>
>>> Either the Bible is completely true and trustworthy, or it is not. If it 
>>> is
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

I have snipped your long rationalization of faith although I take no issue 
with it.

Given YOUR definition, based on quoted scripture, your concern seems to be, 
not with what God would find "acceptable",  but rather with the depth and 
strength of a believer's faith when it is tested in times of adversity.

Summarising your argument, the Bible is necessary so that faith can be 
reinforced. That certainly works for you. It may not work for people who 
cannot read, and they may have other (perfectly valid) ways of reinforcing 
their faith. (Like attending Church, discussing things with their Pastor and 
fellows, or anything at all that works for them...watching sunsets on a 
mountain top can be just as real for some people as your reading scripture 
is for you...)

I believe your fanatical focus on one RIGHT way (faith based on the 
inerrancy of scripture) does you no credit, and it doesn't help others to 
find faith either, if that is your purpose. It is divisive and alienating, 
just as insistence on ANY SINGLE road to faith is.

Over many days and thousands of words here you have really failed to 
persuade anyone (including committed Christians) here, that the Bible IS 
inerrant. You have dismissed any argument that refutes your own arguments 
(polonium in rocks, effects of the flood, evolution, etc.) and simply droned 
on about the inerrancy of the Bible. (When most of us can see that it isn't 
inerrant, by even the most casual inspection).

Personally, I have no problem with you finding faith on this basis (or on 
any other basis...); my problem is when you promote this as being the ONLY 
way, and in doing so dismiss everyone who also has faith but arrived at it 
via a different route.

You have indicated that your faith has served you well and helped you 
through hard times. Good. You see your faith as unshakeable and "deep and 
reliable, and trustworthy". All good.  (I feel the same way about what I 
believe, although I accept there are some areas that are not 100% and so 
subject to ongoing review...:-))

So there is no reason why you as a faithful Christian, and me as an atheist, 
shouldn't be able to accept that we each have something working for us and 
leave it at that. I can do this, why can't you? Why are you so driven to 
propound your view? Are you really trying to convince yourself?

I have no desire to undermine or shake your faith (I know you believe that 
nothing could do that, anyway...), so where does the problem arise?

It arises when you are not content to simply have what you've got, but find 
it necessary to promote it as the ONLY way. Your zeal to give to others what 
you have found, leads you to sow on stony ground. (Even Jesus warned against 
doing that...)

Your arguments have been rejected consistently here, yet you persist. The 
persistence, rather than wearing people down, simply makes them more 
resistant and inflames anti-religious feeling by restimulating past bad 
experiences for them.

Ask yourself what is being achieved by all the effort and time you have 
invested.  Can you see even a single poster whose mind has been changed? Is 
there likely to be one?

>>
>>> This is logically
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> believer himself.

OK, then is it any less "reliable" to the believer himself, than your own? 
Can you not accept that people can find (and lose...) faith in diverse ways?

I have met and spoken at length with people who are every bit as ardent in 
their faith as you are (some of them came to it by amazing twists of 
circumstance). They would be offended by your suggestion that there is only 
ONE way. (Especially when  that way, to a dispassionate observer, is 
patently nonsensical. You have failed miserably to make your case for Bible 
inerrancy, despite thousands of words and many hours labouring it)

<snip>

> So, for a person to have "real faith" in anything, it has to be something
> they truly believe, and trust.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> an inerrant, totally dependable Word of God to believe and trust.

That's fine, and it is the way that YOU refresh your faith.

But it ISN'T the ONLY way.


> They
> vest their faith in God's character as described in the Bible to assure
> that His Word is absolutely reliable. Believers have literally been burned
> alive without disavowing belief. Most of the Apostles were willing to be
> martyred rather than rejecting Jesus because they *knew* Who He was.

They weren't martyred for their belief in Bible inerrancy...

>
> But once you depart from Biblical inerrancy, you have destroyed any
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Word of God, because you, not God, picked and chose them.

Suppose you were inspired by God at the time? Is it anymore unlikely than 
claiming that the people who wrote it were inspired by God?

>You
> also rejected other parts of the Bible *through your own judgment*,

And you were the one who was telling us that Free Will was given to us so we 
could exercise it.

Now, it's a bad thing?

(I warned you previously that there are logical repercussions from this 
"Free Will" thing... you still persist, unable or unwilling to see that if 
we have free will, then God doesn't know what the outcome will be, (if He 
did, there is no point in giving us free will).
If there is something that God doesn't know, then He isn't omniscient. If He 
isn't omniscient, why should we accept any of the other attributes He is 
supposed to have?)

Make up your mind: Is the exercise of Free Will by us  a good or bad action? 
Did God give us free will so He could test us, or because He didn't know 
what the choice made by each individual would be?

If it was to test us (He seems pretty fond of testing people...), and He 
already knew what the choices would be, why not just snuff out the ones who 
will choose "wrong" before they even choose (maybe at birth...)? Why make 
the "right" ones have to suffer living with the "wrong" ones?

Better still, why even have Free Will at all? Why not just have everybody be 
"right" and full churches every Sabbath?

What about the ones who vacillate? Find and lose faith several times in 
their lives (or even every other year...)? Does God get confused as to what 
their status is :-)?

> and have lost any claim to any kind of *totally reliable* Word of
> God. As long as everything is running smoothly, people continue like
> this indefinitely. But let their faith be greviously challenged; let them
> have to choose  between imprisonment or execution and denying
> God, and what "absolute" will they cling to then?

Presumably, the same criteria that brought them to faith in the first place.


> They will have no
> absolute, for they rejected that possibility when they rejected inerrancy.

Perhaps the "absolute" is the tiny inner voice that says: "I'm here..."  Who 
knows why people believe what they do? The fact is that they do, and it is 
as real for them as your faith is for you.

It is certain that there are many paths to truth (and "truth" is not 
absolute). To adopt tunnel vision in this regard is simply denying the 
validity of anyone's faith except your own. That's a pretty arrogant 
position to maintain.

> All they will have left is a human made patchwork, they *know* is a
> human made patchwork. Why do you think some believers appear to
> be Christians for years, until they lose a beloved spouse or child, then
> reject God?

Perhaps awful reality causes the scales to fall from their eyes; perhaps 
they find that the mercy they expected was not forthcoming, perhaps this 
crisis of faith makes them question, and the answers turn them from faith... 
Who knows? It really doesn't matter. If someone is going to lose faith, the 
inerrancy or infallibility of the Bible won't change it. Faith is a lot more 
than believing the Bible is true. Faith defines your relationship with God.
(Do you really need an atheist to remind you of this...?)

>They may have been true Christians, but they chose to
> put their faith in a human patchwork of beliefs instead of the infallable
> Word of God.

Not necessarily. How they came to faith is a matter for them. Why would you 
choose the "infallible word of God" if God is whispering in your ear every 
day?

>
> I don't say these things as a person whose faith has not been tested
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Word of God to cling to, or you will be in serious trouble. :-)

A passionate plea based on unfortunate experience. Every single person in 
this forum (posters and lurkers) has met with disaster and misfortune.

Whether you believe it or not your own "long and difficult personal 
experience" is not unique. There are people here who have had to endure 
almost unendurable events, people have lost loved ones, seen their lives 
wrecked, buried their children, suffered anguish of all kinds.  And yet, 
they don't emerge through it convinced that all hearts are wicked and babies 
are born into sin because of some 3500 year old mythology.

How many of us are in "serious trouble"?

What doesn't kill us makes us stronger. We use whatever works for us to get 
through hard times. Belief in the inerrancy of the Bible (for most of us) is 
the last thing on our minds when trouble comes. Kindness and support from 
our friends and families are often found to be helpful (despite the 
wickedness and deceit that they are born with, according to your view...)

I have never needed to see a Pyschiatrist (I mention it in passing; I don't 
think there is anything wrong with people getting help...) and I don't take 
anything that affects the nervous system except alcohol, in moderate doses, 
(not uppers, downers, anti-depressants, painkillers, not even Aspro...I've 
never had a headache in my life.) I sleep well and I enjoy my life. Serious 
trouble? I think not...

Now, your view of "serious trouble" is burning forever in a lake of fire. 
That is real for you; it isn't for me.

"Sufficient unto the morrow the evils thereof."

I'll take each day as it comes and if, having shuffled off this mortal coil, 
I find there is continued existence, I'll deal with that then, not waste the 
life I have now worrying about it.

The mistake you make is in projecting your own belief onto everyone else. In 
a way, I think you even revel in it.

You kind of like the idea that you have joined the select few who will pass 
the final judgement (even though Revelation says it is limited to a few 
hundred thousand, and I think the LDS have first dibs, so you may find 
yourself at the back of a very long line :-)...)

It makes me wonder how you can hold such a belief, knowing that with every 
day that passes more people are going to burn. If Judgement occurred 
tomorrow, there would be around 8 billion people (everyone who has ever 
lived and is living now), of whom less than 400,000 will get into Heaven. 
That's an awful lot of furnace fodder, and, as time goes by, the number 
increases... Your inerrant Book of Revelation has the figures... (I reckon 
it was the postprandial afternoon dream of a guy living in a cave in the 
first century, who had no TV or other diversion, but for you, it is the Holy 
Word of God...I sincerely hope you're wrong; not particularly for myself; 
for all of Humanity.)

As for what happens next, we'll have to wait and see...(Although, speaking 
for myself, I'm not in any particular hurry... :-))

I'm really OK with whatever outcome happens...but, I know which is the most 
likely.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 78)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> be just as real for some people as your reading scripture is for you...)

Pete, there is a vast difference between only knowing part of the truth
(e.g. someone who has only heard enough about Jesus to be saved) and
actively rejecting part of the truth (discounting part or all of the Bible as
untrue). No one but God knows the whole truth. No human can even
completely know and understand all that is in God's Word, the Bible,
even if they memorized every word. My point is that you cannot have
true and solid faith, while at the same time attack the very foundations
of that faith. Jesus is the living Word of God; the Bible is the written
Word of God (yes, many Scriptures to back that up). The situation of
a believer who rejects part of the Bible is similar to Jesus' statement
that "A house divided against itself cannot stand." This is not some
opinion I just pulled out of the air, the principles are clearly defined in
Scripture. The fact that a large number of believers don't realize this
is simply a sign of their ignorance, just as the fact that a large number
of believers live lives that are in contradiction to the teachings of
Jesus is also a sign of their ignorance. Jesus said "If you love Me,
follow my commandments." If they haven't gotten that down, why in
the world would we think they "have down" more subtle points of
Scripture, even when such are clearly defined?

Pete, you yourself have frequently pointed out some of the bad, even
horrible, things that have been done by believers, or in the name of God.
Surely it would be obvious, even to you as a non believer, that there
must be a reason for this? When people who claim the name of Christ
do not act as Christ says they should act, then they have obviously
departed from the teachings of Jesus. Why? It is because they are not
believers, or they are believers who are ignorant of the principles taught
by Jesus, or they don't care what Jesus says, or they are weak and
succumbed to temptation, or some other similar explanation. If they
aren't believers or don't care, then there is no remedy other than repent
and change. But for ignorance, the remedy is to know the truth. The
truth is found in the Bible, the Word of God. Every Christian on the
planet should know or learn this, for it is a fundamental tenet and truth
of Christianity. For believers to claim they don't know this is alarming,
and clearly part of the reason the Church (true believers) are doing
such a poor job these days.

> I believe your fanatical focus on one RIGHT way (faith based on the inerrancy of scripture) does you no credit, and it doesn't 
> help others to find faith either, if that is your purpose. It is divisive and alienating, just as insistence on ANY SINGLE road to 
> faith is.

If there is any truth taught in the Bible, is is that there is *one way*, and
that is God's way. Individual Christians experience many different ways
of coming to Jesus, but there is only one Jesus, and one salvation. There
is only one way to be acceptable to God, and that is through faith in
Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches this as clearly as it teaches anything.

> Over many days and thousands of words here you have really failed to persuade anyone (including committed Christians) here, that 
> the Bible IS inerrant. You have dismissed any argument that refutes your own arguments (polonium in rocks, effects of the flood, 
> evolution, etc.) and simply droned on about the inerrancy of the Bible. (When most of us can see that it isn't inerrant, by even 
> the most casual inspection).

You "can see?" You see God? You know Him? You observed the
universe being formed? You are certain of these things, you have
absolutely unrefutable evidence?

I didn't see the universe being formed any more than you or anyone else.
But I do know God. I don't know everything about God, and I don't
understand everything I do know about God. But I do know God. I talk
to Him, He talks to me. I can feel His presence inside me day to day. I
know these things as surely as I know anything. And I know that the
Holy Spirit who gives me assurence that God loves me also gives me
assurance that the Bible is God's Word. Jesus called Him the Spirit of
Truth (John 14:15-17). Every true believer has this Spirit of Truth, but
not all believers listen to Him, just as not all believers listen to Jesus.
There is clear evidence in the disobedience of many believers that they
do not always listen to and obey Jesus. Why is it strange to say they
also do not always listen to the Holy Spirit? It would be stranger still
if they did the one and not the other.

> Personally, I have no problem with you finding faith on this basis (or on any other basis...); my problem is when you promote this 
> as being the ONLY way, and in doing so dismiss everyone who also has faith but arrived at it via a different route.

I'm not talking about how people come to faith in Jesus. I'm talking
about how they treat the authority for that faith, the Bible.

> You have indicated that your faith has served you well and helped you through hard times. Good. You see your faith as unshakeable 
> and "deep and reliable, and trustworthy". All good.  (I feel the same way about what I believe, although I accept there are some 
> areas that are not 100% and so subject to ongoing review...:-))

Believe me, I would hardly characterize my faith as "unshakable." It
has been pushed to the breaking point (and seemingly beyond) more
times than I can count.

> So there is no reason why you as a faithful Christian, and me as an atheist, shouldn't be able to accept that we each have 
> something working for us and leave it at that. I can do this, why can't you? Why are you so driven to propound your view? Are you 
> really trying to convince yourself?

Because if you are right, you and I will cease to exist at death, and it
doesn't matter in the long run what I believe. But if I am right, you will
spend an eternity in torment unless you accept Jesus before you die.
I do not want that for anyone, and I don't want to stand before God
and hear Him say "Jud, if you had persevered longer, Pete Dashwood
(or someone else reading these threads) would have accepted Jesus
and be standing beside you now."

> It arises when you are not content to simply have what you've got, but find it necessary to promote it as the ONLY way. Your zeal 
> to give to others what you have found, leads you to sow on stony ground. (Even Jesus warned against doing that...)

Not so. The parable was about how different soil (people) received
the seed (Gospel), not about whether or not the sewer should have
sewn seed there. Jesus also said:

(Mark 16:15,16)
  And He said to them, "Go into all the world and preach the gospel
  to every creature. He who believes and is baptized will be saved;
  but he who does not believe will be condemned.

>>>> This is logically
>>>> inescapable, and a formal logical proof would be trivial. Believers who
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> thousands of words and many hours labouring it)

Again, I'm not talking about how one comes to faith in Jesus. That
happens in a vast variety of different ways. :-)

>> So, for a person to have "real faith" in anything, it has to be something
>> they truly believe, and trust.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> But it ISN'T the ONLY way.

No, one is free to attack the foundation of their faith, if they choose
to do so, or they can cling to the foundation of their faith. As in
most things, they will experience the consequences of that decision.

>> They
>> vest their faith in God's character as described in the Bible to assure
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> They weren't martyred for their belief in Bible inerrancy...

Doesn't matter what they were martyred for. To face martyrdom
without failing requires faith in something solid. Which is why
believers should not attack the foundations of their faith.

>> But once you depart from Biblical inerrancy, you have destroyed any
>> solid foundation for such belief. Because you have your own self
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> God?

Inspired by God to deny His Word that Jesus zealously supported?
I don't think so. :-)

>>You
>> also rejected other parts of the Bible *through your own judgment*,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now, it's a bad thing?

Free will, no. Making bad decisions, yes. :-)

> (I warned you previously that there are logical repercussions from this "Free Will" thing... you still persist, unable or 
> unwilling to see that if we have free will, then God doesn't know what the outcome will be, (if He did, there is no point in 
> giving us free will).
> If there is something that God doesn't know, then He isn't omniscient. If He isn't omniscient, why should we accept any of the 
> other attributes He is supposed to have?)

Warn away. :-)

I did not suggest that God was unaware of what would happen when
He gave man free will. On the contrary, God knew precisely what
would happen, every step of the way, down through history. But
knowing and permitting is not the same as causing. You may know
if you permit your children freedom they will abuse it, but that does
not mean you caused the abuse. God permitted it because it served
His purposes. I do not claim to know what all of God's purposes are,
though the Bible reveals some of them.

> Make up your mind: Is the exercise of Free Will by us  a good or bad action? Did God give us free will so He could test us, or 
> because He didn't know what the choice made by each individual would be?

Nothing God does is "bad." :-)

> If it was to test us (He seems pretty fond of testing people...), and He already knew what the choices would be, why not just 
> snuff out the ones who will choose "wrong" before they even choose (maybe at birth...)? Why make the "right" ones have to suffer 
> living with the "wrong" ones?

It's not all about us, and dealing with us. There are a vast number of
other beings whom God created who are observing this. Part of what
God is doing is revealing characteristics of His nature to His creation,
of which we are only a part. For example, the angels who were
subverted by Satan to rebel could not be saved. I don't know why,
but they weren't. Perhaps it was because they had clearer and more
definite knowledge of God than we do. God showed His justice in
dealing with them, but He apparently could not (or perhaps just did
not) show His mercy there. But God is revealing is mercy and grace
and forgiveness in dealing with mankind. God has a purpose in
everything, and those purposes will be revealed in His time.

> Better still, why even have Free Will at all? Why not just have everybody be "right" and full churches every Sabbath?

I can only surmise that God wanted people who *chose* to believe
and love Him, even when there were apparent reasons not to. This
would explain many things which you see as reasons not to believe.

> What about the ones who vacillate? Find and lose faith several times in their lives (or even every other year...)? Does God get 
> confused as to what their status is :-)?

We might, but God doesn't, of course. :-) Only God can say what
a person's precise state is.

>> and have lost any claim to any kind of *totally reliable* Word of
>> God. As long as everything is running smoothly, people continue like
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Presumably, the same criteria that brought them to faith in the first place.

Most people come to faith only knowing a little about God. That little
gave them enough faith to accept Jesus and be saved. But who would
want to stay there and never grow in knowledge and faith?

>> They will have no
>> absolute, for they rejected that possibility when they rejected inerrancy.
>
> Perhaps the "absolute" is the tiny inner voice that says: "I'm here..."  Who knows why people believe what they do? The fact is 
> that they do, and it is as real for them as your faith is for you.

"Real" doesn't equate to "mature" and "full." A baby is born with
only a few abilities. It is in the nature of babies and Christians to
grow into maturity with a multitude of abilities. A baby who doesn't
grow is stunted in some way, which is a problem. The same goes
for a Christian. A person isn't "dogmatic" to say such things, or
to be able to recognize a baby who isn't mature, or a Christian who
isn't mature.

> It is certain that there are many paths to truth (and "truth" is not absolute). To adopt tunnel vision in this regard is simply 
> denying the validity of anyone's faith except your own. That's a pretty arrogant position to maintain.

Paths vary. Absolute truth doesn't. God is truth. God's Word is Truth.
Every Christian should know this.
```

```
I believe you are very wrong about other people's faith.


>Jesus is the living Word of God; the Bible is the written
> Word of God (yes, many Scriptures to back that up). The situation of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Scripture.

That isn't what I'm arguing. You have jumped onto a hobby horse and missed 
the point entirely.


>The fact that a large number of believers don't realize this
> is simply a sign of their ignorance, just as the fact that a large number
> of believers live lives that are in contradiction to the teachings of
> Jesus is also a sign of their ignorance.

So there are lots of "believers" whose belief is flawed because of

No wonder attendance is declining.

>Jesus said "If you love Me,
> follow my commandments." If they haven't gotten that down, why in
> the world would we think they "have down" more subtle points of
> Scripture, even when such are clearly defined?

Presumably, most of them believe they ARE following Jesus' commandments. It 
is you who thinks they aren't...
>
> Pete, you yourself have frequently pointed out some of the bad, even
> horrible, things that have been done by believers, or in the name of God.
> Surely it would be obvious, even to you as a non believer, that there
> must be a reason for this?

I am aware that Human Beings are imperfect, complex creatures, yes.


>When people who claim the name of Christ
> do not act as Christ says they should act, then they have obviously
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> succumbed to temptation, or some other similar explanation.

OK.

> If they
> aren't believers or don't care, then there is no remedy other than repent
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> such a poor job these days.

So, what about my illiterates then? They go to Church, they discuss the 
Bible with their Pastor and their fellows, but they have no choice other 
than to accept the interpretation of others.

On the other hand, we have the educated ones (like yourself) who CAN read 
and study it for themselves, BUT they also are forced to accept the 
translator's interpretations. (Unless they can read Scripture in the 
original language which few today can do...)

Where's the difference?

You will build your faith based on what someone else thought the Scriptures 
said, and claim it as the inerrant inspired word of God, yet you won't 
accept that someone else could arrive at the same faith you have, by a 
different route entirely.

>
>> I believe your fanatical focus on one RIGHT way (faith based on the 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> that is God's way.

Unfortunately, there are many possible interpretations of what is God's way, 
all of which can claim to be backed by Scripture.


> Individual Christians experience many different ways
> of coming to Jesus, but there is only one Jesus, and one salvation. There
> is only one way to be acceptable to God, and that is through faith in
> Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches this as clearly as it teaches anything.

So most of Humanity (which doesn't happen to be Christian) is doomed...

A word of advice: don't get involved in any Ecumenical movements...
>
>> Over many days and thousands of words here you have really failed to 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You "can see?"

Yep. I can read and I can think. Had a lot of practise at it during this 
lifetime. It isn't hard to spot the errors in Genesis.

You see God?

NOBODY "sees God", not even the faithful. He's invisible, remember... ?

You know Him?

Had the odd chat a long time ago. I used to talk to my Teddy Bear as well. 
But you grow... (if you're lucky).


> You observed the
> universe being formed?

Yep. I have the NOVA PBS "Origins" videos... quite excellent.

>You are certain of these things, you have
> absolutely unrefutable evidence?
>

Unlike some people, I don't need "absolute" evidence. I can go with a decent 
probability as a working hypothesis. If something turns up that contradicts 
that hypothesis it can be re-evaluated and the probability adjusted. If the 
probability gets to be less than is workable, the hypothesis has to be 
discarded. It's called "keeping an open mind"... may be an unfamiliar 
concept for people who require absolute guarantees...

> I didn't see the universe being formed any more than you or anyone else.

You should order the "Origins" videos from Amazon...

> But I do know God.

Name dropper... :-)

> I don't know everything about God, and I don't
> understand everything I do know about God. But I do know God. I talk
> to Him, He talks to me. I can feel His presence inside me day to day.

Good. Next time you're chatting, ask Him about the sin of pride... Give Him 
my regards and tell Him we could use a bit of help with cancer down here... 
(I lost three good friends recently, nearly lost a couple more and had lunch 
with someone yesterday whose mother has just been diagnosed as incurable. 
It's getting past a joke...)

Many people feel the presence of God.  Even people in other faiths report 
the same phenomena. I used to chat with a Buddhist monk up in the Himalayas 
who reported the same awareness during meditation. He didn't call it the 
presence of God; it was a serenity and clarity that brought peace and a 
realization of the Oneness of all thngs. He had never read the Holy Bible. 
Why am I not convinced? Actually, I am... I realize that these spirtual 
experiences are very real for the people who have them. But there are people 
who swear they were abducted by aliens or spoke with Elvis (recently), or 
saw ghost ships on the ocean, or collapse in rapture during religious 
meetings. We are complex creatures.

> I
> know these things as surely as I know anything. And I know that the
> Holy Spirit who gives me assurence that God loves me also gives me
> assurance that the Bible is God's Word.

How do you know it isn't Satan winding you up, so you'll indulge in 
unwinnable arguments on Usenet? :-)

>> Personally, I have no problem with you finding faith on this basis (or on 
>> any other basis...); my problem is when you promote this as being the 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> about how they treat the authority for that faith, the Bible.

Faith, by definition, does not need an authority. If you need a logical 
basis for it, it isn't faith.

>
>> You have indicated that your faith has served you well and helped you 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> times than I can count.

And returns even stronger each time. All part of God's plan to make you 
better... :-)

>
>> So there is no reason why you as a faithful Christian, and me as an 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> spend an eternity in torment unless you accept Jesus before you die.

You said that before. But these are not the only two alternatives. What if 
neither of us is right? What if we are partially right?

It really doesn't matter. Should I waste my life in fear and trepidation of 
what will happen after it? Or should I embrace religion and get myself a 
guarantee I'll be OK, then waste my life persuading others how right I am?

It's a glorious day here. I'm off to the beach after I finish this and I 
intend to enjoy the rest of the day. I don't see a dismal world full of evil 
wicked people with deceit in their hearts; I actually enjoy the company of 
most people and I don't need for them to share my religious belief (or lack 
of it) before I can do that.


> I do not want that for anyone, and I don't want to stand before God
> and hear Him say "Jud, if you had persevered longer, Pete Dashwood
> (or someone else reading these threads) would have accepted Jesus
> and be standing beside you now."

That would be in the long line at the back of the LDS, right? :-)

Please don't take responsibility for my mortal soul. I won't let Jesus do 
that so there's no way I'll let you do it. I appreciate the thought, but "no 
thanks". :-)

>
>> It arises when you are not content to simply have what you've got, but 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> sewn seed there.

But in the time you wasted preaching to the unconvertable, you could have 
been sowing on more fertile ground...:-)


Jesus also said:
>
> (Mark 16:15,16)
>  And He said to them, "Go into all the world and preach the gospel
>  to every creature. He who believes and is baptized will be saved;
>  but he who does not believe will be condemned.

OK, so at what point do you give up and let them choose "condemnation"?
>
>>>>> This is logically
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> happens in a vast variety of different ways. :-)

Yet, having arrived there you seem to ascribe a "scale" of "reliability" to 
the faith of individuals, as if you have any right to do that.
>
>>> So, for a person to have "real faith" in anything, it has to be 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> most things, they will experience the consequences of that decision.

But faith has no required logical foundation. When it does, it isn't faith; 
it is conviction. (We are convinced by a logical argument; we believe 
through faith and there is no requirement for a logical basis for it. It is 
an experience, rather than a persuasion by logic.)
>
>>> They
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Doesn't matter what they were martyred for.

Er... I think you'll find it mattered a great deal to the people who had to 
undergo it.

> To face martyrdom
> without failing requires faith in something solid. Which is why
> believers should not attack the foundations of their faith.

Again, you are misrepresenting my argument. It wasn't about people 
"attacking the foundation of their faith". It was about faith not REQUIRING 
a foundation, and about some faithful coming to the faith without having 
read the Bible, and this, in my opinion, not rendering their faith any less 
real.

>
>>> But once you depart from Biblical inerrancy, you have destroyed any
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't think so. :-)

How is selecting pertinent passages from the Bible "denying" the word of 
God? Ministers, Pastors, and Priests do it every Sunday as the basis for 
their sermons.
>
>>>You
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Free will, no. Making bad decisions, yes. :-)

But Free Will gives us the right to make "bad" decisions. That is the whole 
point of it.
>
>> (I warned you previously that there are logical repercussions from this 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> though the Bible reveals some of them.

Ah, so when God behaves illogically, it is because He is an Unknowable 
Mystery and His motives are not given to us. I love the way, right 
throughout the Bible, God exhibits the imperfect behaviours of Man at the 
time it was written (this more than anything supports my proposition that 
God was invented by Man) and when the behaviours cause inconsistency, it is 
because it is all an unknowable mystery. If it is an unknowable mystery, why 
should we accept any of it?
>
>> Make up your mind: Is the exercise of Free Will by us  a good or bad 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nothing God does is "bad." :-)

Demonstrably untrue. You don't want a list... :-)
>
>> If it was to test us (He seems pretty fond of testing people...), and He 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> other beings whom God created who are observing this.

Now you have my attention. :-)

> Part of what
> God is doing is revealing characteristics of His nature to His creation,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> definite knowledge of God than we do.

Ah, so gaining knowledge is punishable by death and exile. No wonder the 
tree of Knowledge was forbidden. I guess Eve picked the wrong tree...

> God showed His justice in
> dealing with them, but He apparently could not (or perhaps just did
> not) show His mercy there.

One man's "justice" could be another man's "petulant revenge"...

> But God is revealing is mercy and grace
> and forgiveness in dealing with mankind.

Forgive me for asking: "How?"  I'm not feeling the love just at the 
moment... It looks to me far more like a random, amoral, Universe 
manifesting some really nasty stuff, than a Merciful creator showering us 
with love and compassion.


> God has a purpose in
> everything, and those purposes will be revealed in His time.

But He is timeless, so they may not...
>
>> Better still, why even have Free Will at all? Why not just have everybody 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> would explain many things which you see as reasons not to believe.

Yes, that is a possibility. But it is less likely than the fact that there 
are reasons not to believe, whether God exists or not.
>
<snip>>>
>> Presumably, the same criteria that brought them to faith in the first 
>> place.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> want to stay there and never grow in knowledge and faith?

Depends how you define "grow". I was interested in your definition of "Baby 
Christians"... never realised there were "Classes" of faithful.
>
>>> They will have no
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Every Christian should know this.

Good discussion. So why didn't you comment on the harder stuff? I reproduce 
it again for your perusal. Is your failure to comment to be taken as 
surrender? (You have no answer?):

<Repost of previously ignored arguments>

The mistake you make is in projecting your own belief onto everyone else. In
a way, I think you even revel in it.

You kind of like the idea that you have joined the select few who will pass
the final judgement (even though Revelation says it is limited to a few
hundred thousand, and I think the LDS have first dibs, so you may find
yourself at the back of a very long line :-)...)

It makes me wonder how you can hold such a belief, knowing that with every
day that passes more people are going to burn. If Judgement occurred
tomorrow, there would be around 8 billion people (everyone who has ever
lived and is living now), of whom less than 400,000 will get into Heaven.
That's an awful lot of furnace fodder, and, as time goes by, the number
increases... Your inerrant Book of Revelation has the figures... (I reckon
it was the postprandial afternoon dream of a guy living in a cave in the
first century, who had no TV or other diversion, but for you, it is the Holy
Word of God...I sincerely hope you're wrong; not particularly for myself;
for all of Humanity.)

</repost of previously ignored arguments>

Pete.
```

```
the authority of God's Word, the Bible. Not knowing this does not
change the reality, any more than not knowing the law changes the
reality of the law (man's law or God's law).

>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

best to do that. Should I simply stop responding? A unilateral cease
fire seems a bit one-sided, don't you think? :-)

>>> amazing twists of circumstance). They would be offended by your suggestion that there is only ONE way. (Especially when  that 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> do that.

That's not what I'm doing at all. I'm talking about the reliabilty of
someone's source (authority) for their belief. A person who accepts
a specific belief system does so in some context. For belief in Jesus,
the context is the Biblical record of who Jesus is and what he did.
That's not an opinion, that's a simple statement of fact. It is not
logical to claim belief in a system defined by the Bible, then reject
the Bible, or part of it, as an authority. It is oxymoronic - a house
divided against itself. Failure to realize or believe that doesn't alter
the reality of it. As I stated before, it is logically inescapable. But
humans have a boundless ability to ignore logic.

>>>> So, for a person to have "real faith" in anything, it has to be something they truly believe, and trust.
>>>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> persuasion by logic.)

Faith does require a framework under which the faith is defined.

About what? Concerning what? Doesn't make sense.

>>>> They
>>>> vest their faith in God's character as described in the Bible to assure
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Er... I think you'll find it mattered a great deal to the people who had to undergo it.

My point is that, when someone suffers martyrdom rather than
renounce their faith, that faith must be rock solid. You don't build
a rock solid reliable construction on ambiguity, you must build it
on something that is definite and solid (Matthew 7:26). A faith
based on a patchwork of beliefs selected arbitrarily from a menu
is the an of definite and solid.

>
>> To face martyrdom
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> not rendering their faith any less real.

Didn't say it wasn't real. I said it was based on a shaky foundation which
will crumble under extreme pressure. A structure is no more secure than
its foundation. The Bible claims to be the Word of God, living and
powerful (Hebrews 4:12). Can anyone claim such for their patchwork
collection of beliefs, when they reject part of God's Word? Remember,
we're talking about Christians here.

>>>> But once you depart from Biblical inerrancy, you have destroyed any
>>>> solid foundation for such belief. Because you have your own self
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> as the basis for their sermons.

Selection implies rejection of that not selected. God's Word is a whole,
not a menu. Jesus said we live by "every word of God" not "some words
of God" (Matthew 4:4). And Jesus is the object of faith of anyone claiming
to be Christian. Say you have faith in Jesus, yet deny His own words?
What kind of faith is that? As I keep saying - a house divided against itself.

>>>>You
>>>> also rejected other parts of the Bible *through your own judgment*,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> But Free Will gives us the right to make "bad" decisions. That is the whole point of it.

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

Without knowing what God is trying to do, how in the world can you
claim He's "being illogical?" That would be true even for a human, whose
goals we could expect to understand. But God, who may have purposes
beyond our comprehension? I don't think so. :-)

>>> Make up your mind: Is the exercise of Free Will by us  a good or bad action? Did God give us free will so He could test us, or 
>>> because He didn't know what the choice made by each individual would be?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Demonstrably untrue. You don't want a list... :-)

Again, by whose definition? In God's eyes, we're all criminals who
have broken God's laws. What kind of definitions for "good" and
"bad" would you get from a bunch of criminals? Would they be
suitable to definie a law abiding society's laws? I don't think so. :-)

>>> If it was to test us (He seems pretty fond of testing people...), and He already knew what the choices would be, why not just 
>>> snuff out the ones who will choose "wrong" before they even choose (maybe at birth...)? Why make the "right" ones have to suffer 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> wrong tree...

No, *ignoring* knowledge of God's laws is punishable by death.

Adam and Eve knowingly and willingly disobeyed God's clear
and express command. The nature of the tree is irrelevant.

>> God showed His justice in
>> dealing with them, but He apparently could not (or perhaps just did
>> not) show His mercy there.
>
> One man's "justice" could be another man's "petulant revenge"...

God has the right to set the rules because he created us and the
universe for His own purposes. Creators have that right, even
by human law.

>> But God is revealing is mercy and grace
>> and forgiveness in dealing with mankind.
>
> Forgive me for asking: "How?"  I'm not feeling the love just at the moment... It looks to me far more like a random, amoral, 
> Universe manifesting some really nasty stuff, than a Merciful creator showering us with love and compassion.

God, through Jesus, suffered death to pay for our sins against Him
(the penalty for sin is death - Romans 6:23). Whether we accept that
offer of free pardon is up to each of us.

>> God has a purpose in
>> everything, and those purposes will be revealed in His time.
>
> But He is timeless, so they may not...

We know that because God told us in the Bible.

>>> Better still, why even have Free Will at all? Why not just have everybody be "right" and full churches every Sabbath?
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> faithful.

the same degree of obedience to God. Obedience pleases God, it
glorifies God, and brings reward for the obedient Christian. We are
rewarded for obedience, but salvation comes only as a free gift we
obtain through faith in Jesus. :-)

>>>> They will have no
>>>> absolute, for they rejected that possibility when they rejected inerrancy.
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> yourself at the back of a very long line :-)...)

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANY language.

Pete, you keep ignoring stuff I have already said. :-) Remember when I
stated that it is impossible to understand the Scriptures without help
from the Holy Spirit? The Author of the Bible, the One Who inspired
the men who penned the Bible in the first place, lives within all true
believers in Christ and teaches them what they need to know, if they
will listen to Him (John 14:15-17,26). But, just as Christians can fail
to obey God in their conduct, they can also ignore the guidance of
the Holy Spirit. God does not force obedience, or override our free
will.

> And yet, despite this stumbling block, people still come to faith. You already allowed that it happens, but you say they cannot 
> grow in faith and are like babies unless they do as you do and study the Bible and take it as the literal Word of God.

Pete, I don't see why you find this so difficult, unless you are simply
trying to find areas to disagree. Do you have a problem understanding
the fact that not all things people eat or do are healthy for them? Why
would spiritual growth and health be any different? There is a lot of
Scripture that talks about things that are healthy and unhealthy for
spiritual growth. There are things that are bad, things that are not so
bad, but not particularly good, and things that are great; just as in
health for the physical budy, or the mind and emotions. Why do you
have such a problem with this?

> My point is that you have no right to say this  because YOUR way is no better. Reading an English translation is NOT necessarily 
> giving you the exact original meaning.

Duh! Do you ignore experts on nutrition and insist they are simply
demanding THEIR WAY? I'm not talking about *my way* here,
I'm talking about what God says in Hos Word. If you don't accept
the Bible as God's Word, then fine; but don't call it it *my way*.

> I speak four languages and understand (can read) 7; I know very well how essential nuances can be lost in even the best available 
> translation. Language can be translated, but culture and idiom very often can't. When you are dealing with a culture and idioms 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> content and debate that.

When the author of what you are reading lives inside you to tell you
how to understand what you are reading, don't you think that can help
you to understand what the author actually meant? Assuming you listen
to the author, of course. :-)

>>>> I don't know everything about God, and I don't
>>>> understand everything I do know about God. But I do know God. I talk
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> ascribe demonic or Satanic possession to them. Then again, you being you, perhaps you would...:-)

Ah, Pete. Have you ever met a con man? If you have, then you should
have no problem understanding what is meant by:

(2 Corinthians 11:14-15)
  And no wonder! For Satan himself transforms himself into an angel
  of light. Therefore it is no great thing if his ministers also transform
  themselves into ministers of righteousness, whose end will be
  according to their works.

> Tell you what, Judson, if I had to choose, I'd take his "demonic possession" against your "belief system as described here", in a 
> heartbeat.

That's what free will is all about. We make our choices, and then live
with the consequences. :-)

>>>> I
>>>> know these things as surely as I know anything. And I know that the
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> relevant.

The Bible says it's critically relevant (1 Corinthians 12:3). I know you don't
accept the Bible, but I do, which is why I believe that. So, give it a test.
Ask any of these people of Jesus is Lord (God) and see what they say. :-)

>>>>> Personally, I have no problem with you finding faith on this basis (or on any other basis...); my problem is when you promote 
>>>>> this as being the ONLY way, and in doing so dismiss everyone who also has faith but arrived at it via a different route.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Holy Bible (which I don't have, and you have dismally failed to persuade me otherwise...).

Rather than "proof" I would say the Bible is "evidence." But more
particularly to our discussion, I would say it is the "definition" of
Who Jesus is, what it means to be a Christian. As you yourself have
pointed out, if you aren't going to get a definition of what a Christian
is from the Bible, then where will you get one?

>>> It really doesn't matter. Should I waste my life in fear and trepidation of what will happen after it? Or should I embrace 
>>> religion and get myself a guarantee I'll be OK, then waste my life persuading others how right I am?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> So what are you doing here :-)?

(Mark 16:15,16)
  And He said to them, "Go into all the world and preach the gospel to
  every creature. He who believes and is baptized will be saved; but he
  who does not believe will be condemned."

>> I've been thinking about how best to disengage. Considering that from
>> the beginning of these threads, I've been essentially only replying to
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> CLC completely; your experience has earned your place here.

Thank you. :-)  No, I'm not planning on leaving CLC. Personally, I don't
mind continuing these threads, but I know some don't like them and I
have no desire to provoke people.

I think people should be able to explain why they believe what they do.
Not necessarily for the purpose of explaining ther beliefs, but for their
own understanding of exactly what their beliefs are, and why they
believe them. Discussions such as this, where multiple viewpoints are
articulated and defended, perhaps again and again from slightly different
perspectives, can be helpful.

>>>>> I have met and spoken at length with people who are every bit as ardent in their faith as you are (some of them came to it by 
>>>>> amazing twists of circumstance). They would be offended by your suggestion that there is only ONE way. (Especially when  that 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Especially religious humans... :-)

There have been a lot of very knowledgable, intelligent Christians, Pete.
Isaac Newton was a devout Christian, as well as many brilliant modern
scientists and scholars. History is replete with such people.

>>> But faith has no required logical foundation. When it does, it isn't faith; it is conviction. (We are convinced by a logical 
>>> argument; we believe through faith and there is no requirement for a logical basis for it. It is an experience, rather than a 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I am suggesting that people acquire faith in diverse ways. Once they have it, they can define it; until then, they probably can't.

No problem there. My point is that Christianity, even Jesus, the very
Object of Christianity, is defined in and by the Bible. How can one
put faith in what is only defined in the Bible, yet not jave faith in the
Bible?

>> Faith in what?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> without the other; you say they are wrong, so which is most important to you?)

I'm saying that those who have faith in Jesus, but not the Bible, are
deceiving themselves, in that to the extent they are rejecting the
authority of the Bible, they are also rejecting the foundation of their
faith. I'm not saying they don't have faith, or that they aren't "real"
Christians.

> It isn't a fair question because there are a number of possible answers and none of them is really satisfactory. You cannot answer 
> it unless you choose, and to choose is fatal because you consider both things critical. A logical dilemma. (You had no such qualms 
> about your "challenge" which was also a completely unfair question, for much the same reasons...:-))

There's no question about which is "most critical." Faith in Jesus is
the *only* thing that will satisfy God and bring forgiveness of sin.
This can be accomplished by simply accepting the word of someone
who has accepted Christ, or hearing about it, or God directly speaking
to someone, or any number of other ways. However, the Bible is the
foundation authority from which all this springs, because it is the
living Word of God. A person does not have to know this to accept
Jesus.

Let me give an anology. You can believe that the volume of a sphere
can be calculated by the formula v = 4/3 * pi * r ** 3 without knowing
the calculus used to derive it. You don't have to know where it came
from to believe it. But you are certainly not going to progress very far
in understanding math by denying the principles upon which math is
based, either.

>>God's Word is a whole,
>> not a menu. Jesus said we live by "every word of God" not "some words
>> of God" (Matthew 4:4).
>
> But he didn't say we have to live by all of them at once...

You aren't going to live by any of the words you choose to reject,
now, are you? That's my point.

>>> Depends how you define "grow". I was interested in your definition of "Baby Christians"... never realised there were "Classes" 
>>> of faithful.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why would a non-Believer care?

Didn't say they would care. I said they could see. :-)

>>> It makes me wonder how you can hold such a belief, knowing that with every
>>> day that passes more people are going to burn. If Judgement occurred
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes it does.  Revelation 7:1 - 8, amplified further in Revelation 14: 1 - 5

Revelation 7: Pete, those are 144,000 Jews chosen to evangelize the
Jewish people during the Tribulation Period, and have *nothing* do
with the total number of people who will be in Heaven!

Revelation 14: Same 144,000 as above, *after* the bodily return of
Jesus Christ to assume his throne here on Earth during the Millenial
Period. Again, has *nothing* to do with the total number of people
who will be in Heaven.

All of those 144,000 people are Jews, yet the Old Testament and
the New Testaments say the Messiah will save both Jews and
Gentiles. The Bible also says nothing about those 144,000 being
the only ones to enter Heaven. It only says they are the only ones
who can learn that particular song; apparently created just for them
by God.

>>Where did you get such a number?
>
> From your inerrant, inspired by God, Holy Writ. See above.

Clearly Pete isn't inerrent, because the Bible says no such thing. :-)
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:45Zsj.73459$Mu4.20127@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
<snip>
>> And yet, despite this stumbling block, people still come to faith. You 
>> already allowed that it happens, but you say they cannot grow in faith and 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> trying to find areas to disagree.

Judson,
  THIS is what others of us view as "circular reasoning".

You have a faith (assumption in "logic" terms) that places authority in the 
Bible. THEN you say that reading theBible, it requires you to "take it as the 
literal Word of God" (Pete's words).  Then you use the "literal words" of the 
Bible to justify the authority that you place in the Bible.

There is certainly nothing wrong with reaching your conclussions from the 
assumptions you make.  However, you can't state that the conclussion "proves" 
the original assumption, when they are based on that assumption - unless you 
admit that logic is curcular.
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> You have a faith (assumption in "logic" terms) that places authority in the Bible.

True

> THEN you say that reading theBible, it requires you to "take it as the literal Word of God" (Pete's words).

True, with this proviso: Well, it is literally "the Word of God" though
not every passage in the Bible is a literal statement. Not every statement
in the Bible is even true. The Bible sometimes records statements made
by people who are lying. Such things are understood through their
context, as in all human language commuincation.

>  Then you use the "literal words" of the Bible to justify the authority that you place in the Bible.

Not true. Such would, indeed, be circular reasoning. I use the text
of the Bible to *define* what I believe, and use the text of the Bible
to defend it against criticism of it's internal consistency and so
forth, and to show what the Bible actually says, when someone
questions or challenges it. I may even use the text of the Bible to
show that how I use or interpret the Bible is consistent with the
Bible itself. But I do not use the text of the Bible to justify its
authority, *except* to people who already profess beliefs which
are, by definition, defined by the Bible. They, or others, may not
realize or acknowledge this, but that doesn't change the fact.

> There is certainly nothing wrong with reaching your conclussions from the assumptions you make.  However, you can't state that the 
> conclussion "proves" the original assumption, when they are based on that assumption - unless you admit that logic is curcular.

Yes, I understand circular reasoning. :-)
```

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> will.

So the Bible cannot be correctly interpreted without access to the correct
guiding spirit. Which, as it turns out, is the one that Judson and those
who agree with him have a direct line to. Any disagreement is futile
because Jason simply "knows" the truth via his access to this guiding
spirit.

If the Bible is factually incorrect, or contradicts itself, or says things
that are abhorrent or that Judson disagrees with, this is not a problem.

How do we know if someone is a true believer, guided by the right kind of
spirit? Easy - ask if they agree with Judson.

I don't think the term "circular reasoning" comes close to describing this
kind of logic.

Tim
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:45Zsj.73459$Mu4.20127@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete, you keep ignoring stuff I have already said. :-)

If you say something new or different, I won't ignore it... (sorry, that's 
not true; I will... see below)

> Remember when I
> stated that it is impossible to understand the Scriptures without help
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> trying to find areas to disagree.

Obviously I don't need to find areas to disagree; I disagree with all of it.

After reading this (yet another...) regurgitation of the same tired circular 
arguments, the "broken record" repetition of the same meaningless litany, I 
thought pehaps I better take pause.

I have been trying to explore ideas of religion and belief with someone who 
HAS no ideas of his own on religion and belief.

You have accepted ONE idea, which you got from a book. You have not explored 
other ideas and you won't.

What have you ever experienced or evaluated for yourself?

You live in a world where hearts are wicked and deceitful (because the Bible 
says so), good, decent people, are immoral (if they don't believe in God), 
gentle, kind,  compassionate people are possessed by wicked demons who have 
fooled them into ruin (if they happen to be of another faith), and most of 
humanity is gleefully sentenced to an afterlife of eternal torture.

A world that requires its inhabitants to ignore the evidence of their own 
eyes, accept a planet that is 6000 years old, accept mediaeval superstition 
of ghosts and ghouls and demons and things that go bump in the night, and 
offer total obedience to a petulant, spiteful, "Supreme Being", who has the 
mental age of a 12 year old (or a person living 3500 years ago).

Your world is irrelevant in the 21st century, and the wonder is not that you 
choose to live in it, but that you are compelled to persuade others to live 
in it too.

It is only the fact I have some time that has kept me here, nothing is being 
achieved and it is foolish of me to waste the time I have.

May the God that you took from a printed book be with you...

I wish you well, but I wouldn't want to be you.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 86)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-18T09:14:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ghhuj.85817$N67.30559@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Lnqqj.67823$Mu4.58818@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ifgmq3la10u9mbjlj93p8dkatnh9rn1919@4ax.com> <611jrlF1tf590U1@mid.individual.net> <%%Oqj.82048$rc2.49212@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <612uioF1s0b69U1@mid.individual.net> <5G0sj.30067$1f.17922@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <61dc97F1tdsc3U1@mid.individual.net> <hpisj.70651$vt2.57879@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <61eqk9F1u9rojU1@mid.individual.net> <DZIsj.71421$vt2.11245@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <61hv82F1vd8bjU1@mid.individual.net> <45Zsj.73459$Mu4.20127@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <e6ae07df-0fe3-40f2-a486-0be80872b0cc@p43g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <SbDtj.74644$Mu4.54071@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <tOFtj.260843$X56.185604@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> <snip>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Please, PLEASE, tell me how this is not ciruclar reasoning.

Bill, I appreciate your post, and will try to make my position clear.
I hope so, anyway. :-)

> You state that the Bible must be read "in the manner" that you do.

That isn't what I'm saying (see below).

>  When asked why "it is conemened" not to read the Bible as you do, you respond
>   "because the Bible says to do so"

That isn't what I'm saying either.

> As I say (and have said), you can certainly believe what you want and you can read the Bible as you think it should be.  But when
> you use that way of reading the Bible to "justify" that way of reading the Bible, it just HAS to be considered circular.

I'm not defending "my way of reading the Bible". My argument is not based
on any doctrine.

> Obviously, I *DO* need a crayon drawing to figure out what you are trying to convey.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> If you are saying something else, then I just can't follow it.

Sorry I haven't been clear enough. I will make this as short as I can
and still be clear. I'm apologize if the length makes it more difficult
for you. :-)

First, let me make it very clear that this is a logic based argument,
not a doctrine based argument, vis-a-vis a narrow aspect of how true
believers in Christ "should" view the Bible. This necessitates that
the argument be framed within the context of a person who already has
faith in the atoning sacrifice of Jesus Christ for forgiveness of
their sins before God, which is the definition of "Christian".

People choose to believe or place faith in anything they want. But in
here, we're talking about a very specific faith that only has meaning
within a clearly defined context. Essentially, that there is a God, that
God has a moral code, that God judges and punishes those who violate
His moral code, that the believer at least has violated that moral code,
that God has made a way for such violations to be forgiven, that such
forgiveness can come through a substitutionary sacrifice, that Jesus
died to be that sacrifice for us, and that we appropriate forgiveness
through our faith Jesus' sacrifice. Without that context, there is no
meaning to the concept of having faith in Jesus for the forgiveness of
sins. Every Christian faith has these common beliefs at least, and any
religion that does not hold those beliefs is, by definition, not part
of Christianity. The above is a "minimal" definition of Christianity,
less than which Christianity becomes meaningless.

Obviously a person must come to some knowledge of that context just to
understand the concept of who Jesus is, or that they need forgiveness.
That knowledge may come through any number of ways; personal testimony
of another Christian, hearing an evangelist, direct revelation from God,
etc. But the issue pertinent to this discussion is: what is the source
of that knowledge? The source is without question the Bible. Nowhere
else can a Christian find the account of who God is, what sin is, why
man sins, how God judges sin, who Jesus is, how Jesus made atonement
for our sins, how faith in that atonement can bring forgiveness, and
all the other context that defines Christianity. Except for direct
divine revelation, there is no other source. This is not an opinion or
a statement of faith in the Bible, it is simply a fact that our only
knowledge of the context defining Christianity comes from the Bible
account. Note carefully that this does not depend on whether or not the
Bible or Christianity are true, or on Scriptural inerrancy or any other
method of Biblical interpretation. It is a historical fact that the
actual events, and the Bible account of them, are *the* source from
which Christianity springs. We no longer have the actual events to see,
nor is anyone alive who did see them. All that remains is the Bible
account for Christianity's definition and basis. (We have the testimony
of Christians as evidence that Christianity is valid, but that's another
issue; this argument does not address, and is not based on, Christianity
being valid.) From simple deduction it is obvious that, if the Bible
account relative to Christianity is true, then there is a valid basis
for Christianity, but if the Bible account relative to Christianity is
false, then there is no valid basis for Christianity. This should be
obvious to a Christian, an atheist, a Buddhist or anyone else.

Since the Bible is clearly *the* source and definition of Christianity,
how should that affect the way Christians view the Bible?

First, what about knowledge of the Bible? Since the Bible is the source
for all the context of what Christianity is and how it works, if "being
a Christian" is important to the believer (is anything more important
than whether one spends eternity in joy or torment?) logic suggests that
a Christian should want to have more knowledge of what Christianity is
than less. Since the Bible is the source for such knowledge, that means
knowledge of the Bible. In all human endeavors it is generally believed
that more knowledge is better than less knowledge. This is why societies
fund schools and universities, libraries, etc. I submit that it is only
logical that Christians should want to know what the Bible says about
their faith, and that not wanting to know is illogical.

Now to the crux of the argument; how should a Christian, who is placing
their faith for the security of their eternal soul in a Christianity
that is defined only in the Bible, view the Bible as to truth, accuracy,
reliability, etc.? We can break the possible ways to view the Bible into
three broad categories: the Bible is totally reliable, the Bible is
totally unreliable, or the Bible is partly reliable. Let's examine the
logical ramifications for the Christian of each:

1. The Bible is totally reliable: If this is the case, then the basis
for Christianity is also entirely reliable, and the Christian can study
the Bible, believe it, place their faith in what it says, and be
confident they are placing their faith in completely reliable truth.

2. The Bible is totally unreliable: If the Bible is totally false, a
fiction created by men, then there is no valid basis for believing in
anything it says, including Christianity. The Biblical account of God,
sin, judgment, forgiveness and Jesus is false, and any sort of faith
placed in Jesus is futile.

3. The Bible is partly reliable: With a partly reliable Bible, the
believer is presented with two critical difficulties.

3a. First, if the Bible is only partly reliable, how do we know which
parts are reliable? If the Bible is totally true, then it does not
depend on the proven fallibility of men for it's reliability. But if
it isn't, there is no other basis than one's fallible human judgment
as to which parts are reliable, or on one's own fallible judgment
about other humans' fallible judgment as to which parts are reliable,
if one takes someone else's opinion. This is an insoluble problem,
because the whole point of faith in Jesus is to obtain forgiveness
for sins which the believer's fallible human judgment led them into
in the first place. The fact that one's judgment got them there is
proof that it is unreliable in matters of divine truth and sin.

3b. Second, even if the believer decides on some subset of the Bible
in which to place their faith, how much confidence can they have on
their particular choices? To borrow an example from Jesus, a house
with an insecure foundation (e.g. sand) is not secure. We know this
to be true from human experience. The foundation of Christianity is
the truth of the events recorded in the Bible. If the Bible account
is not reliable, how much faith (confidence) does it warrant? If one
is asked to cross over a high chasm on a bridge that one is already
convinced is unreliable to support the weight, can that crossing be
made with strong faith? Logically not, because it would require
simultaneous belief in two conflicting things: that the bridge is
unreliable to support the weight, and that the bridge will support
the weight. The same conflicting beliefs apply to the Christian who
views the Bible as unreliable: that the object faith is reliable,
but that the source of their knowledge of that object is not
reliable. It's one thing to believe in the object of faith when
other evidence indicates otherwise. It's quite another to maintain
faith in something but at the same time doubt the source of the
basis of that faith. That's climbing a tree while chopping at it's
roots.

3c. Ramifications of view 3: We're talking about the eternal
destination of the believer here, not some trivial exercise for which
the stakes are small. If we do have eternal souls, and our eternal
future is dependent on faith in Jesus (which is the Christian belief),
then there could not be a more vital or important issue. Every single
Christian is faced with trials of their faith on a day to day basis,
and some of these trials are severe. Many Christians, down to this day,
have been confronted with either renouncing their faith in Christ, or
facing imprisonment or martyrdom, sometimes by torture. I submit that
it is only logical that a Christian who chooses this view of the Bible
cannot be as secure in their own faith as they would if they chose to
believe the Bible is totally reliable. The reverse, or that it doesn't
make a difference, is illogical and irrational. Remember, we *choose*
what to place our faith in. This is not about things that are
objectively provable with no possible shadow of a doubt. That day
passed with the Shekina Glory. This is about things in which we choose
to place our faith, even when it may appear there is reason to doubt.
I submit that it is illogical and irrational to choose to place one's
faith in *anything*, and at the same time choose to believe the
definitive source of that thing is unreliable.

Conclusion: Any Christian who maintains the Bible is not entirely true
has directly attacked the basis of their own faith, even if they do
not realize or accept it. Once one accepts that there is some degree
of unreliability in the Scriptures, there is no stopping point. Bit by
bit, the basis of their faith can be chipped away until nothing is
left. As I said from the beginning, this conclusion is logically
inescapable, whether one likes it, believes it, knows it, or not.

Whether or not anyone chooses to accept my argument above, it should
be crystal clear that there *is no* "circular reasoning" here. The
above is a straightforward logical argument to the issue of Christian
faith in the Bible, based only on historical fact and the ubiquitous
definition of Christianity. So, can we please put this "circular
reasoning" nonsense to rest? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 87)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2008-02-18T12:36:36-06:00

```
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:Ghhuj.85817$N67.30559@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> >  When asked why "it is conemened" not to read the Bible as you do, you
respond
> >   "because the Bible says to do so"
>
> That isn't what I'm saying either.
>
> > As I say (and have said), you can certainly believe what you want and
you can read the Bible as you think it should be.  But when
> > you use that way of reading the Bible to "justify" that way of reading
the Bible, it just HAS to be considered circular.
>
> I'm not defending "my way of reading the Bible". My argument is not based
> on any doctrine.
>
> > Obviously, I *DO* need a crayon drawing to figure out what you are
trying to convey.
> >
> > I understand it *IF* what you are saying is
> >  "If you read the Bible as I do, then you will determine that you must
read the Bible as I do."
> >
> > If you are saying something else, then I just can't follow it.
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> divine revelation, there is no other source.


I'd say, given a choice, that direct revelation from God is infinitely more
reliable than the Bible.  In fact, the possibility of direct revelation in
principle obviates the need for a Bible at all.  After all, such information
comes direct from God (by definition) and is not filtered through any human
mind, no matter how divinely inspired and directed.  God is capable of being
in full-time, direct communication with every spiritual entity that exists,
anywhere, anywhen, so no intermediaries are needed.  If you took the
position that the Bible is a good introduction to the subject, to be used
cautiously and honestly in the effort to achieve divine revelation, then I'd
bet you'd encounter a great deal less opposition to your beliefs.

PL

PL
```

```
Judson,
  I had delayed reading and thinking about this (useful to me) response to my 
question.  I *think* I can now (better) understand your position.

There is (to me) a "simple and obvious" fallacy in your logic (which  is 
important to me - but is probably irrelevant to you).

No matter when you think the New Testament (for example) was actually written 
down, there was SOME amount of time between the events occurring and their being 
recorded.  (Many place this time lapse greater than I think you would, but that 
doesn't matter for me).  Therefore, if you (as I do) believe that God was (and 
is) truly loving, then He/She would have provided for methods for providing 
guidance (Truth) during that time.  For me, this was (is) the purpose of
 A) the apostolic succession
 B) the presence of the Holy Spirit in the world
 C) the human mind

All of these were active in humanity during the time between the resurrection 
and the codification of the New Testament (however, long that was).  And to me, 
they have REMAINED active and important since that codification.  Therefore, 
when you state

. But the issue pertinent to this discussion is: what is the source
> of that knowledge? The source is without question the Bible. Nowhere
> else can a Christian find the account of who God is, what sin is, why
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> divine revelation, there is no other source.

To me, you are ignoring the other ways in which God did (and to me continues) to 
provide "Truth" to humanity.  If "the Church" tells me that parts of the Bible 
(including those parts that tell me "not to") should be "read in context", then 
that dichotomy must be resolved by me via my understanding (my mind) and what I 
understand the Holy Sprit to direct me to do.  Again, to me, there is no 
fundamental contradiction, because it is my understanding that the REASON that 
God provided these other gifts to humanity was that He/She *knew* that we would 
need ongoing assistance in understanding the Word of God - and placing it in 
context of our own lives (and times).

   ***
Again, I think you for your reply and it does help me to understand (if not 
agree with) your perspective.
```

```
christian nor can I pass judgement on your fitness as a christian. In
your religion, the only judge is god. Whether I, or anyone else, reads

is the first, final and only judge. You can not know (despite

in the narrow concept of christianity being the only way.

The bible would say that and so would any author of those texts,
propogandist espousing those texts or any person willing to suspend
```

```
cultus of Peter, but the original grave is unlikely ever to be found.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

>
> Either the Bible is completely true and trustworthy, or it is not. If
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> when their faith becomes seriously challenged.

Nobody accepts the literal truth of the Bible. There are exaggerations 
("Solomon had treasure without end..."). There are liars - that is the Bible 
could accurately report what someone said, but who's to say whether that 
person was lying? Heck, even God lied when He told Abraham how Sarah reacted 
to the news that she was to become pregnant. There are mistakes in 
transcription and translation ("Jehova" is a good example). Then, too, the 
people in the Bible made human mistakes, such as Saul acting on the belief 
that David was trying to overthrow the throne.

Sorting out all this, and other mechanical oddities, is the province of 
scholars and theologians.


>
> If you examine the Scriptures carefully, you will discover that the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3:16).

Believers, maybe. I know of no scholars who've said the Bible is factually 
perfect.

Of course Paul never met Jesus (in the flesh) and was a Greek, not a Judean. 
What has always puzzled me, is what the heck Paul was doing in that part of 
the world anyway?
```
