```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:ua1mj.57463$Mu4.16153@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
>
> Now, prove me wrong. Just honestly go on record right here that, if you
could
> see real and unquestionable proof that the Bible is absolutely true and
reliable,
> you would be willing to accept Jesus and submit your life to God.
>
> So, open minded or not?  I'm betting you will obfuscate, change the
subject or
> otherwise avoid the issue. :-)
>
> Anybody else want to take the challenge and demonstrate you are really
open,
> and only waiting for evidence? How about you, Alistair? :-)
> --
> Judson McClendon       judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)

Nice try, Judson.  Anybody who is honest and of a truly open mind would
readily agree that if there's real and unquestionable proof they'd accept
your theology.  I would!

The problem is that your committment requires faith.  To the rest of us,
truth is something that works whether we want it to or not.  Pi = 3.14159...
no matter what we'd like it to be, and no matter that we've been assured by
someone important that it's 3 (including some US state legislations that
have tried) and no matter how much faith we have in that person.  If Jesus
Christ comes tomorrow in all his glory and tells me that Pi = 3, then I will
have to check it out.

If you really expect or would like others to agree with you, you're going to
have to present your propositions and let us  test them according to our
ideas of truth.  If they work as you say, then you win.  I have prayed, most
sincerely, twice in my life.  Result: nothing at all.  I have seen
innumerable examples of people desperately calling on God to save them - in
the face of a flood or an avalanche or a volcanic eruption - and I'd bet
that GWB prayed that Hurricane Katrina would miss - again, nothing.  I
repeat - your ideas have to work whether people want them to or not before
sceptics will agree with you.

PL
```

"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> The problem is that your committment requires faith.

No, the problem is that non belief *also* requires faith, which Pete denied,
and why I posted my challenge. My whole point is that *any* belief system,
including secular humanism and athiesm, are matters of faith. Pete's response
will clearly show if he is truly moved *only* by evidence.

> If you really expect or would like others to agree with you, you're going to
> have to present your propositions and let us  test them according to our
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> sceptics will agree with you.

My challenge wasn't meant to persuade Pete to my point of view, but to
demonstrate that he also has faith, in his secular belief system.

My broader point being that people don't believe because they don't want
to believe; not because there is "no evidence." My challenge to Pete should
also serve to help illustrate that fact.

On Thu, 24 Jan 2008 14:46:21 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>No, the problem is that non belief *also* requires faith, which Pete denied,
>and why I posted my challenge. My whole point is that *any* belief system,
>including secular humanism and athiesm, are matters of faith. Pete's response

People of faith say this a lot, but they never convince others of it.


>My challenge wasn't meant to persuade Pete to my point of view, but to

they really are believers.    They usually come back with comparisons
with stuff like not believing in Santa Clause.

- **Message-ID:** `<e26fc8af-2f29-47b3-bb9e-08308da84c59@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com> <2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <lhrep3tkarppnc6fc4plu0vj0nfbuueoc2@4ax.com> <CuKlj.78589$L%6.21482@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5vr57sF1np9flU1@mid.individual.net> <ua1mj.57463$Mu4.16153@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Ur3mj.25709$3b7.10642@newsfe23.lga> <pO6mj.57612$Mu4.30146@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
On 24 Jan, 20:46, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> No, the problem is that non belief *also* requires faith, ...
> including secular humanism and athiesm.

I have no religious faith. I do believe that the process of science
will prevail and wipe out the last vestiges of superstition (which is
what religion is). It is a regret of mine that I will not live to see
that day.

As to belief/faith: it is very difficult to believe in anything at
all. The human being perceives, cogitates and reacts to stimuli. Those
stimuli are past perceptions (it takes a measurable amount of time for
the stimulus to pass along nerve fibres and for a response to be
observed) which means that no human is perceiving the current moment
in time. So, do you still exist? You can not ever be certain of that.

Further, although we have debated in this group artificial
intelligence (perhaps artificial stupidity would be a better concept)
and the simulation of mice and men's perecrania and found the current
technology lacking, there is nothing to indicate that we are not,
ourselves, simulations. Cut me and I bleed. Or do I? Am I just
imagining it? What is reality? You subscribe to one version and I
subscribe to another and they are not the same. We are both hedging
our bets.
```

> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> that day.

Sounds like Voltaire. He said in a hundred years Christianity would cease
to exist. Last time I checked, there was a Bible organization using his old
house. God does, indeed, have a sense of humor.

> As to belief/faith: it is very difficult to believe in anything at
> all. The human being perceives, cogitates and reacts to stimuli. Those
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> our bets.

Well, my point is that we both have faith in our belief systems. Neither
of us are 100% totally objective evidence driven in our beliefs. The vast
majority of non-believers are unwilling to admit this, because they much
prefer to insist that they don't need faith, they have "evidence," while
insisting believers are somehow less valid because "they need faith."
And that is absolute rubbish! Atheists are just as faith based as believers,
and in fact, are believers in a religion: Atheism. Atheism is a man-made
"god" just as they insist believers have created.

On 24 Jan, 23:47, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> house. God does, indeed, have a sense of humor.

Yes, he created humans (malformed unstable evolutionary prototypes)
and has allowed us to poison our nest.

>
> > As to belief/faith: it is very difficult to believe in anything at
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well, my point is that we both have faith in our belief systems.

All I said was that science will prevail.

> Neither
> of us are 100% totally objective evidence driven in our beliefs.

How is any belief in science less than 100% objective?

> The vast
> majority of non-believers are unwilling to admit this, because they much
> prefer to insist that they don't need faith, they have "evidence,"

You're confusing a belief in a (religious) concept with a belief in a
verifiable and reproducible experimental result. No atheist can have
evidence that god does not exist. All we have is the refutation of
superstitions. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.

You have failed to provide irrefutable evidence for a deity. On the
balance of probability there is no god.

> while
> insisting believers are somehow less valid because "they need faith."

Believe in your refutable and redundant concepts if you wish. The only
valid faith is that which can (for the observer) be experimentally
proved. Clearly, especially as you have taken such a hiding here, your
faith is very real to you. I would argue that your standard of proof
is not as high as mine and I would not be satisfied by your
experiences.

> And that is absolute rubbish! Atheists are just as faith based as believers,
> and in fact, are believers in a religion: Atheism. Atheism is a man-made
> "god" just as they insist believers have created.

There is no hint of divinity or deity about atheism. Atheism is not a
religion.
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> and has allowed us to poison our nest.

God created humans without flaw or sin, but gave them free will to choose
good or evil. Humans chose to sin and "poison our nest." (good phrase,
by the way. :-)

>> > As to belief/faith: it is very difficult to believe in anything at
>> > all. The human being perceives, cogitates and reacts to stimuli. Those
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> How is any belief in science less than 100% objective?

You can prove that 100% objectivity to my satisfaction by answering the
challenge. Would you believe and submit to God if evidence you yourself
considered unquestionable and deniable were presented to you that the
Bible is true? So, 100% objective, evidence based, or not? Not! :-)

>> The vast
>> majority of non-believers are unwilling to admit this, because they much
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> experiences.

The premise you use to argue that is that my beliefs are "only" founded
"on faith," whereas yours are founded on "evidence". If evidence is your
only criteria, then your answer to my challenge should be clear. But you
will not answer my hypothetical question because evidence is not the
*real* issue for you.

>> And that is absolute rubbish! Atheists are just as faith based as believers,
>> and in fact, are believers in a religion: Atheism. Atheism is a man-made
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> religion.

A religion doesn't require a divinity. In the place that believers put God,
you put atheism. Believers expect to go to Heaven after death because of
their beliefs; you expect to cease to exist at death because of your beliefs.
Believers take comfort in their belief in God, you take comfort in your
belief you won't have to face God. It is your religion. :-)
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:Zgpmj.60043$vt2.57545@bignews8.bellsouth.net...
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

…[102 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't get the point of all this. Everthing is based to some degree on 
faith.  We know that our senses only give us limited information about 
reality. We have faith in them because they seem to allow us to build useful 
maps about reality, but the map is not the territory. Even scientific belief 
requires faith.  Science does not prove things true beyond all doubt. 
Scientific truth is just a set of laws and propositions that have not yet 
been proved false or in need of modification. As I have mentioned here 
before, Newton laws were confirmed time and time agin for hundreds of years 
but that did not make them true. A day came when situations were found where 
they were inaccuarte and therefore had to be modfied.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com> <2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <lhrep3tkarppnc6fc4plu0vj0nfbuueoc2@4ax.com> <CuKlj.78589$L%6.21482@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5vr57sF1np9flU1@mid.individual.net> <ua1mj.57463$Mu4.16153@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Ur3mj.25709$3b7.10642@newsfe23.lga> <pO6mj.57612$Mu4.30146@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <e26fc8af-2f29-47b3-bb9e-08308da84c59@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <_r9mj.59464$vt2.11049@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <367c1edf-8f32-4ca0-b481-a76bda5dd4c4@p69g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <Zgpmj.60043$vt2.57545@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <13pl0em41m10od4@corp.supernews.com>`

```


"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13pl0em41m10od4@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
…[121 more quoted lines elided]…
> modfied.

Not quite, Charlie. Newton's laws were/are accurate (so much so, that NASA 
still uses them to calculate trajectories for space probes.)

They just weren't complete. There was a creeping shift in the orbit of 
Mercury which they did not account for. At first (because nobody was 
prepared to challenge Newton) it was believed that this was down to the 
difficulty of accurate observation, Mercury being so close to the sun. It 
was only when Einstain explained the way in which Mass warps space and 
affects anything passing through it (like a small planet), that the anomaly 
could be cleared up. (Better observation during the 20th century showed that 
Mercury behaved exactly as Einstein had predicted, and the shift in its 
orbit was indeed caused by the warping of space in the immediate vicinity of 
the sun.)

Newton was right. So was Einstein. The story is still not finished...

Whether religious belief follows the same model or not is yet to be seen. It 
is certainly true that whether Mankind believes in God or not, the Universe 
will continue to go about its business, just as it did before we ever 
arrived, and just as it will continue to do, long after we are gone.

Pete.
```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[142 more quoted lines elided]…
> Then my recollection of phics must be wrong. I thougth that they did not apply to bodies moving close to the speed of light.

Newton was an extremely cautious and meticulous scientist. From the top
of my head (and not checking my copy of The Principia) IIRC, Newton used
terms something like M(rest) and M(vel), indicating he was allowing for
the possibility that mass might vary with velocity, though he had no data
for it. If my memory is accurate, then Newton was absolutely correct, and
the relativity equations basically supplied the missing mass adjustments
Newton already allowed for. For anyone but Newton, such a thing would be
virtually unthinkable.

IMHO, Newton was not only unquestionably the greatest scientist who ever
lived, but very likely the greatest intellect who ever lived. Who else,
with the information he had, could have done The Principia in 18 months?
And consider, he first did the proofs geometrically, then translated them
to analytical proofs. In 18 months. With a quill pen. Boggles the mind.
```

- **References:** `<5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com> <2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <lhrep3tkarppnc6fc4plu0vj0nfbuueoc2@4ax.com> <CuKlj.78589$L%6.21482@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5vr57sF1np9flU1@mid.individual.net> <ua1mj.57463$Mu4.16153@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Ur3mj.25709$3b7.10642@newsfe23.lga> <pO6mj.57612$Mu4.30146@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <e26fc8af-2f29-47b3-bb9e-08308da84c59@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <_r9mj.59464$vt2.11049@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <367c1edf-8f32-4ca0-b481-a76bda5dd4c4@p69g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <Zgpmj.60043$vt2.57545@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <13pl0em41m10od4@corp.supernews.com> <5vvj5sF1ok3ljU1@mid.individual.net> <asymj.60326$vt2.41321@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <13pme9ggt17km32@corp.supernews.com> <600thgF1oj17qU1@mid.individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:600thgF1oj17qU1@mid.individual.net...
>

<snip>

>>>>> I don't get the point of all this. Everthing is based to some degree 
>>>>> on faith.  We know that our senses only give us limited information 
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."

From "A Brief History of Time" by Stephen Hawking, page 10:

"A physical theory is always provisional, in the sense that it is only a 
hypothesis: you can never prove it. No matter how many times the results of 
experiments agree with some theory, you can never be sure that the next time 
the result will not conntradict the theory.  .....  In practice what often 
happens is that a new theory is devised that really is an extension of the 
previous theory. For example, very accurate observations of the planet 
Mercury revealed a small difference in its motion and the predictions of 
Newton's theory of gravity. Einstein's general theory of relativity 
predicted a slightly different motion from Newton's theory. The fact that 
Einstein's predictions match what was seen, while Newton's did not, was one 
of the crucial confirmations of the new theory.  However we still use 
Newton's theory for all practical purposes because the difference between 
its predictions and those of general relativity is very small in the 
situations thate we normally deal with."

So I think my statement that there are situations where Newton's laws are 
inaccurate is correct. Newton's law of gravity does not correectly predict 
the motions of Mecury. You as they are correct just not complete. 
Incomplete, inacurate whaterver it is a minor quibble.
```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

Sorry, I wasn't referring specifically to your post. I've just seen
so many posts where people make statements implying that Einstein did
to Newton what Copernicus did to Ptolemy, I appreciated Pete's post
setting it straight. As Pete pointed out, Newton wasn't "wrong," he
was simply "incomplete" for some situations he had no way to know
about. As Pete alluded in his last line, there is certainly more to
come. Who knows what may come out of quantum mechanics in the next 50
years? A unified field theory would be nice. Perhaps even a Theory of
Everything. Even God says pretty much anything is possible (Genesis
11:6). :-)
```

On 25 Jan, 17:47, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> by the way. :-)

I was thinking of rats when I used that phrase.

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Belief in a god is subjective. Any belief in science is objective. The
difference is that one can be tested but the other can not.


> You can prove that 100% objectivity to my satisfaction by answering the
> challenge. Would you believe and submit to God if evidence you yourself
> considered unquestionable and deniable were presented to you that the
> Bible is true? So, 100% objective, evidence based, or not? Not! :-)

I think that I have already said:
1.  I have already undergone that challenge and found there is no
evidence to support your case;
2.  I have challenged you repeatedly to put up the evidence or to shut
up. Neither of which you have complied with;
3.  If there were undeniable and irrefutable evidence for god (there
is not) then I would believe. Unfortunatley for you, there is no
undeniable irrefutable evidence and you have failed to live up to your
challenge and have not provided any evidence to date. So

PUT UP OR SHUT UP.

>
>
> The premise you use to argue that is that my beliefs are "only" founded
> "on faith," whereas yours are founded on "evidence".

My belief system is founded on verifiable evidence. Your belief system
is founded on superstitions dating bach more than 2000 years. Whereas
I stand on the shoulders of giants you stand on the backs of those who
prostrate themselves before an unknowable unseeable omniscient
omnipotent omnipresent ghost perceived only by few (of dubious
sanity).

> If evidence is your
> only criteria, then your answer to my challenge should be clear. But you
> will not answer my hypothetical question because evidence is not the
> *real* issue for you.
>

How many times do I have to say PUT UP OR SHUT UP?


> >> And that is absolute rubbish! Atheists are just as faith based as believers,
> >> and in fact, are believers in a religion: Atheism. Atheism is a man-made
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> you put atheism.

Atheism is not a religion. All religions to my knowledge have deities
or ghost-like spirits and mythologies which do not bear examination.

> Believers expect to go to Heaven after death because of
> their beliefs; you expect to cease to exist at death because of your beliefs.

Wrong. I expect to cease to exist because there is nothing thereafter.
I do not expect to cease to exist because I believe that I will cease
to exist. Subtle difference.


> Believers take comfort in their belief in God, you take comfort in your
> belief you won't have to face God. It is your religion. :-)

That is like the alcoholic muslim who's behaviour results in his
sister's death. He accepts that god made it so, so he does not accept
any responsibility for his own behaviour. Unlike people promised an
afterlife I accept responsibility for my own actions. I also recognise
that this world would be a better place without the lies and
hypocrises of religions (christianity and Islam to name but two).


> --
> Judson McClendon       ju...@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -
```

That's not what I'm saying at all. I'm simply trying to establish that athiests
are faith based just as believers are faith based. Athiesm is a religion to
athiests, just as Christianity is a religion to Christians. Athiests deny this
because they want to say "we believe in evidence while believers only
have faith." The fact is that both have faith in their belief systems, beyond
total objective fact based analysis.

The fact that you don't and won't proves my point, because if evidence
were the only factor, clear undeniable proof would be decisive. Since it
isn't, then there must be something else - faith also. Q.E.D.

```
On 24 Jan, 23:57, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >> So, open minded or not? I'm betting you will obfuscate, change the subject or
> >> otherwise avoid the issue. :-)
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> athiests,

Nonsense. I don't call the faithful to prayer nor do I do missionary
work in darkest Manchester. There is no injunction upon me requiring
my spreading the word. How can atheism be a religion if I can not
prove this world exists?

> just as Christianity is a religion to Christians. Athiests deny this
> because they want to say "we believe in evidence while believers only
> have faith."

OK, where is your evidence? The bible is a book. It contains
misspellings (see Red Sea for Reed Sea), false injunctions (see the
version that said THOU SHALT SIN), omissions (where is the gospel
according to Mary and why was it left out?). Face facts, it is a
poorly edited anthology of science fiction.

> The fact is that both have faith in their belief systems, beyond
> total objective fact based analysis.

My faith in science stems from the total failure of christianity,
mainstream religions and esoteric faiths to explain the world, provide
a suitable model of the universe or indeed even a decent morality
system. In my life I have only ever once come close to experiencing
any religious feelings, and that was at Karnak in Egypt. Amun rocks.

>
> If I'm wrong, you or Pete can easily prove it by accepting my challenge.

Your challenge is so yesterday. We both accepted it as children and
found it wanting. I have no interest in revisiting those uncertain
times as the continual self-doubt it engenders does nothing postivie
for me or the world. Personally, I would rather worship Amun. Now
there is a deity that one can believe in.

> The fact that you don't and won't proves my point, because if evidence
> were the only factor,

Pete and I (I hope he forgives me for putting words into his mouth as
his wrath is far-reaching and mightily strong) have bothe been there,
seen it, done it, got the t-shirt.

> clear undeniable proof would be decisive.

There is no clear undeniable proof. Every proof that you have provided
so far has been refuted by people better than myself.

Since it
> isn't, then there must be something else - faith also. Q.E.D.
>
> Anybody note all the squirming and side stepping? See? I told ya. :-)
> --

Yes, you are guilty of that.
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> On 24 Jan, 23:57, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> >> So, open minded or not? I'm betting you will obfuscate, change the subject or
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> prove this world exists?

One's religion is one's belief system about God, the supernatural and
other related issues. Your belief system on those things is atheism. Your
belief in no god is as much your religion as my belief in God.

>> just as Christianity is a religion to Christians. Athiests deny this
>> because they want to say "we believe in evidence while believers only
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> poorly edited anthology of science fiction.

I suspect I have spent far more time studying the Bible than you (my
apologies if this is wrong) and I do not see the Bible that way at all.
I just posted a detailed description of my view of the Bible and
textual issues in another message, so I won't repeat it here.

>> The fact is that both have faith in their belief systems, beyond
>> total objective fact based analysis.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> any religious feelings, and that was at Karnak in Egypt. Amun rocks.

You accept current scientific theories because they fit what you want
to believe. No one has proven evolution, for example, but you except
long chains of reasoning based on partial evidence as truth, because
that's what you find satisfying. If you have no internal bias as to what
is truth, then please prove that by answering my simple "yes" or "no"
challenge. If you are willing to state that, given proof sufficient to meet
your criteria, you would be willing to believe God and submit your
life to Him, then I'll accept that and apologize, right here. :-)

>> If I'm wrong, you or Pete can easily prove it by accepting my challenge.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> there is a deity that one can believe in.

Ah, more obfuscation and side stepping. :-)

>> The fact that you don't and won't proves my point, because if evidence
>> were the only factor,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> seen it, done it, got the t-shirt.

Great! Then you won't mind putting the answer down right here for all
of us to see?

>> clear undeniable proof would be decisive.
>
> There is no clear undeniable proof. Every proof that you have provided
> so far has been refuted by people better than myself.

I'm not offering a proof. I'm offering you a clear and decisive way for
you to prove your statements about your motivations by answering a
simple "yes" or "no" hypothetical question. But you can't answer that
question without revealing your true motivations, which is why you don't. :-)

> Since it
>> isn't, then there must be something else - faith also. Q.E.D.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, you are guilty of that.

Sorry, but that's a really lame attempt at misdirection. It's pretty clear
who is dancing around the issue here; I'm staying dead on target. :-)
```

```
On 25 Jan, 18:06, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
> One's religion is one's belief system about God, the supernatural and
> other related issues. Your belief system on those things is atheism. Your
> belief in no god is as much your religion as my belief in God.
>

This is only true if you accept the widest and loosest definition
provided for the word religion:

<Quote from Wikipedia>
A religion is a set of common beliefs and practices generally held by
a group of people, often codified as prayer, ritual, and religious
law. Religion also encompasses ancestral or cultural traditions,
writings, history, and mythology, as well as personal faith and mystic
experience. The term "religion" refers to both the personal practices
related to communal faith and to group rituals and communication
stemming from shared conviction.
</Quote>

A better definition is that a religion requires a belief in a
spiritual entity and a god-head. Atheism is not a believer in either
and does not qualify as a religion, in my book. If you wish to persist
in your misrepresentation of atheism then you will accomplish nothing
except bolstering your own poorly founded beliefs.

> >> just as Christianity is a religion to Christians. Athiests deny this
> >> because they want to say "we believe in evidence while believers only
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> apologies if this is wrong) and I do not see the Bible that way at all.

Reading the bible and studying it are not the same.

>
> You accept current scientific theories because they fit what you want
> to believe.

I accept science because I see that on the balance of probabilities it
is more likely to be correct than religious views.

> No one has proven evolution, for example,

Not true. There is no point in debating this point with some one who
believes that the world started 4000 years before christ was born.

>
> I'm not offering a proof. I'm offering you a clear and decisive way for

The only thing that you have managed to do is to raise my ire and my
blood pressure.
```

></Quote>

An occasionally useful definition that could include "communism" or
"patriotism".

>A better definition is that a religion requires a belief in a
>spiritual entity and a god-head. Atheism is not a believer in either
>and does not qualify as a religion, in my book. If you wish to persist
>in your misrepresentation of atheism then you will accomplish nothing
>except bolstering your own poorly founded beliefs.
```

```


"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:02389aef-ef98-4650-81ac-321cfda9c9af@x69g2000hsx.googlegroups.com...
> On 24 Jan, 23:57, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> >> So, open minded or not? I'm betting you will obfuscate, change the 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>> have faith."

That is such a load of bollocks it really isn't worth responding to, 
however, I'll give it one last shot...

 Judson, you haven't spent time around atheists, you haven't read any 
literature by informed atheists, you really have no idea what atheists 
"believe".

I'm an atheist. I DON'T say: "we believe in evidence while believers only 
have faith."

Why don't I?

1. I am not looking for a contest between my belief system and anyone 
else's. I leave that to Evangelists like yourself. I really don't care what 
believers believe, or how or why they believe it. It is of passing interest 
only, just as all beliefs and opinions can be of passing interest. My 
interest level raises when I see what I consider to be "dangerous" in a 
belief system, but beyond that I practice complete tolerance.

2. Of course I believe in evidence. And I use the intelligence our species 
has evolved over three million years to evaluate that evidence and arrive at 
a conclusion about it. Whether or not believers do this is of no consequence 
to me whatsoever; it isn't a contest.

3. Whether I have "faith" or not is simply not relevant. I have arrived at 
the conclusions I have, using the processes I have described here in other 
posts. I certainly have "faith" in my own evaluation, but that is NOT the 
same thing as religious "faith".

You may be qualified to speak about what atheists believe and the processes 
we employ, after you have done a lot more research.

Get back to me after you've read "The God Delusion" by Professor Richard 
Dawkins, for example. It won't change YOUR mind, but it MAY give you some 
insight into MINE.

You simply are not currently qualified to present the atheist position, and 
therefore fall back on the antagonistic devices I would expect from street 
preachers who shoot their mouths off to the passing populace downtown on 
Saturday night: "You are all wicked", "You'll all burn in the Lake of Fire", 
"If you don't have religion you must have faith in 'no religion' ", "I 
challenge you to accept Jesus as your Lord, right now!" Frankly, I expected 
more...


>
> OK, where is your evidence? The bible is a book. It contains
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> total objective fact based analysis.

You have consistently failed to show that as a "fact". Stating it doesn't 
make it so.

>
> My faith in science stems from the total failure of christianity,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My faith in Science has nothing to do with the failure of other approaches, 
although I can understand thinking people being repelled by religion and 
looking for answers elsewhere.

My "faith" in Science is NOT the "faith" of a religious believer; it is the 
faith that the scientific method, properly applied, gives correct answers. A 
"faith" based on principles that are axiomatic. Simple as that.

>>
>> If I'm wrong, you or Pete can easily prove it by accepting my challenge.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> for me or the world.

Well said, Alistair. I agree.


>Personally, I would rather worship Amun. Now
> there is a deity that one can believe in.

Assuming one has not yet outgrown the need to HAVE a deity... :-) I like 
personal responsibility; don't blame God (ANY God...) for your personal 
shortcomings and inadequacies, take responsibility for your screw-ups and 
work on fixing them. This process leads to growth, and that leads to wisdom 
and contentment. (A valuable spin-off is that it also makes the world a 
better place for all of us...)

>
>> The fact that you don't and won't proves my point,

You have no point. You have a pointless blast of rhetoric that signifies 
nothing.

>> because if evidence
>> were the only factor,
>
> Pete and I (I hope he forgives me

Excuse the mid-sentence interruptions, Alistair.  I don't forgive you 
because I see "forgiveness" as a supercilious action between a superior and 
an inferior, not the action of equals. However, I have absolutely no problem 
with the words you correctly placed in my mouth on this occasion... :-)

> for putting words into his mouth as
> his wrath is far-reaching and mightily strong)

(You got that right... :-))

> have bothe been there,
> seen it, done it, got the t-shirt.
>
Indeed.

>> clear undeniable proof would be decisive.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Anybody note all the squirming and side stepping? See? I told ya. :-)

Yes, I noted a fair bit.

The only side-stepping has been by you. You're still trying to sneak in Free 
Will as the reason we are rotten, and completely side-stepped the issue that 
this would mean God is NOT omniscient, just as ONE example (there are 
many...)

I have been as direct as possible in my responses.

Readers can and will draw their own conclusions.

Pete.
```

can prove the existence of a god which would require personal
experience on my part.

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:ua1mj.57463$Mu4.16153@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> amused. :-)

Well, my therapist says as long as I hang on to Mr. Twinky, I'll be fine...
>
> You have made many statements here showing serious bias, even hostility,
> against things the Bible clearly teaches.

My "hostility" is not against teachings. (Anybody's... I respect the right 
of people to examine teachings and draw their own conclusions.)

I get testy when someone presents their own interpretation of a teaching as 
the ONLY possible one, and then proceeds to press their conviction, ignoring 
all evidence to the contrary.

>Assuming for the sake of argument
> that God exists and the Bible is true, you have a priori rejected the
> principles established by your Creator and Judge.

As interpreted by whom? You? Anyway, why would I assume ("for the sake of 
argument") something that I KNOW is not true?

Such an argument would be pointless, and we don't pursue pointless arguments 
in CLC....Ooops...never mind.


> Reality doesn't depend on
> your understanding or consent to be.

It certainly does in my Universe.

>But when have you ever made the slightest
> hint of anything like "If I could see real evidence, I would be willing to
> accept Jesus and submit my life to God?" None, zip.

When have I ever said: "If I could see the slightest evidence for the 
divinity of the Buddha, I would join a monastery?"

or...

"If I could see real evidence, I would be willing to accept the Tooth Fairy 
and submit my life to The Spaghetti Monster?"

Pointless statements. Best avoided.

>You are clearly not open
> to or interested in that proposition, as witnessed by any number of 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> to God.

Not so. I spent 30 years searching for such evidence. My conclusions have 
been reached after evaluating what that search turned up.

>
> Now, prove me wrong. Just honestly go on record right here that, if you 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> you would be willing to accept Jesus and submit your life to God.

I don't need to prove you wrong; you are doing an excellent job of that all 
by yourself...

No. I don't dance to your tune, and if there REALLY was some supernatural 
power that exists, I wouldn't want to close my options by opting for such a 
narrow sect as Christianity.

No deal.

>
> So, open minded or not?  I'm betting you will obfuscate, change the 
> subject or
> otherwise avoid the issue. :-)

Just like I always do?

Pete.
"I used to write COBOL... now I can do anything."
```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
>>
>> As I pointed out in another post, people believe what they want to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> it or not...

To the Jew and Christian the goals are the same: Belief in a higher power 
and leading a righteous life.

One rabbinic sage opined that an agnostic who decided to follow all the 613 
commandments as an experiment would eventually conclude these rules could 
not be the product of human intellect and, therefore, by following the 
rules, he would come to believe in a higher power.

Similarily, for whatever reason, an athiest accepted Christ into his heart, 
he would naturally try to lead a Christ-like existence.

Both end up at the same place: Sincerity of belief and a life of good works.
```

> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

Franky this sounds like just the kind of idea that some human would come up 
with to manipulate others. Do you think God wants to be loved or feared? To 
me the second death is to be permanently cut off from a loving God. Those 
who suffer this would not be happy in heaven so it more merciful for them to 
suffer a second i.e.  permanent death.  They and sin will cease to exist.

Burning forever in a lake of fire also seems cont\\rary to the laws of 
physics.

Beyond that do you really want to worship any being who tortures, whether 
eternally or otherwise.  Even most supposedly sinful humans would not do 
that kind of reprehensible thing. Such a being should be opposed not 
worshiped, even at the risk of ending up in hell.
```

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> be loved or feared?

Apparently both. See 2 Corinthians 7:1 and 1 John 5:2, only two of many,
many examples.

> To me the second death is to be permanently cut off from a loving God. Those who suffer this would not be happy in heaven so it 
> more merciful for them to suffer a second i.e.  permanent death.  They and sin will cease to exist.
>
> Burning forever in a lake of fire also seems contrary to the laws of physics.

Who says the lake of fire is in this universe, and therefore subject to the
laws of physics? Remember, death and hell will be thrown in there, as
well as multitudes of fallen spirit beings like Satan. Hardly things you
could build a physical container to hold. Personally, I believe "lake
of fire" is describing something we probably have no concept of, and
"lake of fire" is just the closest you could get in human language, and
probably conveys the idea clearly enough. According to the Bible, it
is the spirit world that is real and permanent, and this world that we
see is like a shadow that will pass away.

> Beyond that do you really want to worship any being who tortures, whether eternally or otherwise.  Even most supposedly sinful 
> humans would not do that kind of reprehensible thing. Such a being should be opposed not worshiped, even at the risk of ending up 
> in hell.

Well, if the Biblical account of those things is accurate, then it wouldn't
be a "risk", it would be a "certainty." But remember, if the Bible is true,
then all one has to do to avoid that fate is accept God's gift of salvation
in Jesus Christ. Eternal bliss vs. eternal torment; our choice. God gives
us the freedom of choice to accept or reject Him. We just don't get to
choose the consequences of those choices; God made that decision
Himself.
```

They would be so miserable in heaven that they prefer death.  There is some 
line of poetry about preferring to rule in hell than serve in heaven 
(Dante?). I am not sure about my interpretation, but I prefer to think that 
whatever God decides and does is done out of mercy and love.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CKlmj.59944$vt2.12169@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com> <2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <13pfkvlm2vft2b5@corp.supernews.com> <6xSlj.78824$L%6.26596@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <13pice5be2e4kcc@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
>
> Eternal torment seems inconsistent with a God of love.  Sin mere death alone would seem to wipe out sin and sinners anthing more 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> think that whatever God decides and does is done out of mercy and love.


Love is not God's only attribute. He is also holy, just and righteous, and
those attributes demand purity and dealing with sin. God could have simply
consigned all of humanity to destruction and satisfied those attributes, but
to also satisfy His love, He provided a way of escape by taking the penalty
of our sin upon Himself, in the form of Jesus Christ. To my mind, the act
of the sovereign God of the universe humbling Himself to be born as a human
being, suffering persecution and even death, in order to pay the penalty for
the sins of a rebellious humanity He created is staggering. And what does
God get in return? Just read some of the posts made here recently. Not just
rejection, but outright contempt. And people question why a holy, just and
loving God, after unrepentant sinners openly show contemptuous disregard
for that incredible sacrifice God made to pay for their sins, would consign
them to eternal punishment?

Sorry, but I see no inconsistency there. What I see is incredible restraint
and patience, that a Holy God puts up with such conduct from creatures who
only live, breathe and exist because His wills it, in order that those who
will follow Him have a chance to be born, live and accept His Amazing Grace.
Even more amazing is that God is ready, even eager, for those same people
who have such contemptuous attitudes toward Him now, to repent and accept
His offer of complete pardon, paid for with the blood of His Son, Jesus
Christ. That's why God commands believers to tell others the Gospel (good
news) of what He has done for us, even at the cost of persecution. Amazing
Grace, indeed!
```

> Beyond that do you really want to worship any being who tortures, whether 
> eternally or otherwise.  Even most supposedly sinful humans would not do 
> that kind of reprehensible thing. Such a being should be opposed not 
> worshiped, even at the risk of ending up in hell.

So, even if it's true, it's not right?  I see phrases like yours (and 
others on this thread), and can't help but think that you're only 
focusing on one side of the story.  God doesn't want people to go to the 
lake of fire any more than I do.  That's why He made such a simple way 
out, that's free!  He did something that we could never do, and says 
"Here it is - take it."

It's a battle for souls, and God is "playing fair" by not controlling 
the choice of man.  He's "played His hand," so to speak, and so has the 
other side.  Some will be convinced one way, others will be convinced 
the other way.  Any way you slice it, sin cannot enter heaven - and that 
only leaves one other eternal resting place.
```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:pICdnUfgU4vN8QTanZ2dnUVZ_vGinZ2d@comcast.com...
> Charles Hottel wrote:
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> choice of man.

But why have a "battle for Souls" at all?  A Supreme Being MUST win so 
what's the point in playing? (He isn't Supreme?)

So, Free Will gets exercised and God doesn't know the outcome, before it is 
exercised? (For some reason this smacks of Quantum Physics... :-))

(Then He can't be omniscient).

If He doesn't want us to go to the Lake of Fire, why create such a Lake at 
all?

If a dictator said: "Everyone loyal to me gets this 'Get out of Jail Free' 
card; everyone else gets tortured to the point of death then revived and 
tortured again, ad infinitum..."   Should we be grateful for the 'Get out of 
Jail Free' option?

If the conditions attached to the issuance of the 'Get out of Jail Free' 
card were complete enslavement and acceptance of the wishes of the Dictator, 
wouldn't some people find this unacceptable and opt for the torture...?

(It never ceases to amaze me that throughout history, human beings about to 
be burned at the stake and offered the chance to recant, have refused to do 
so. There is a nobility in the human spirit that has nothing to do with 
pride, stubbornness, or belief. A lot more people would believe in God if He 
behaved a bit more honourably...)

>He's "played His hand," so to speak, and so has the other side.  Some will 
>be convinced one way, others will be convinced the other way.  Any way you 
>slice it, sin cannot enter heaven - and that only leaves one other eternal 
>resting place.
>

So there we have it.

A Supreme Being who isn't supreme, indulging in games that are a battle, 
with us as pawns, and if we lose we burn forever.

High Stakes.

And you want me to believe that this whole contrivance was not created by 
Man :-)?

Pete.

> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This completely vindicates Howard's position. (As much as the ravings of a 
cave dwelling lunatic can vindicate anything). It establishes that the Lake 
of Fire is not the end (as you might expect), but is a synonym for "Eternal 
torture".

>  Then I saw a great white throne and Him who sat on it, from whose
>  face the earth and the heaven fled away. And there was found no
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>  lake of fire.

Where the writer has already established "they will be tormented day and 
night forever and ever"...
>
> One might assume that only some who are cast into the lake of fire
> will be tormented forever, but the Bible gives no support for this
> position.

No, the Ramblings of "St. John the Divine" make it quite clear that ANYONE 
who goes in there gets tortured forever...

Anyway, another, (possibly more important) point...

It says that judgement will be according to the works of each individual. 
Yet, elsewhere in the NT it says that faith is the important thing and NOT 
works (lest any man should boast) (Ephesians 2:9)

So, we have Psychotic Paul telling us no amount of good works will buy us 
into Heaven, and John (having retired for his afternoon nap to his cave on 
Patmos, probably after a good skinful of wine and/or a few of the mushrooms 
growing on the wall...) telling us we better do something good, 'cos that's 
what we're gonna be judged on...

A man has a few drinks, falls asleep, in the first century, and predicts 
what has been argued to be the fall of the Western Roman Empire and a number 
of other things. Two thousand years, later people are still arguing about 
what he meant, and frightening the faithful into compliance with images of 
Lakes of Fire, eternal torture, and such.

Can a 21st Century man really buy this stuff?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

- **References:** `<atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com> <2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5vr51kF1nt06dU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> Anyway, another, (possibly more important) point...
>
> It says that judgement will be according to the works of each individual. Yet, elsewhere in the NT it says that faith is the 
> important thing and NOT works (lest any man should boast) (Ephesians 2:9)

There's no contradiction. Our "work" of believing on Jesus Christ as our
Savior (John 6:29) is "accounted" to us as righteousness (Romans 4:5).
Ephesians 2:9 is contrasting trying to earn salvation by doing good works,
with accepting salvation by God's grace through faith in Jesus. Believing in
Jesus doesn't "earn" our salvation; our faith is the way we receive God's
free gift of salvation. That's why Jesus (John 6:29) said that the only "work"
God absolutely required of us is our faith.

According to Joseph Good (one of the world's top experts on Judaism, highly
respected and welcomed among Jewish scholars in Israel, though he is not
Jewish, and is a Christian www.hatikva.org), the Jews never believed that the
sacrifices they made absolved their sins. Good says they believed, as do
Christians, that the sacrifices were a picture of how sins are forgiven,
(substitutionary sacrifice) and that their sins would in actual fact be
absolved by the Messiah. They made those sacrifices in faith, believing the
Messiah would take away their sins when He came. From cover to cover, the
Bible always teaches that what God requires of humans is faith in God. Good
works are desirable, even necessary (Peter says if we don't have works it
means our faith is dead), but don't buy us anything toward salvation. Jesus
did all of that. The absolute requirement is to have faith in what Jesus did
for us.
```

As I understand it, when a person dies s/he goes to heaven or to hell (or to
purgatory if a Catholic).  In all cases the destination is permanent,
eternal.

If the wages of sin are death, meaning obliteration of the self and
consciousness - again, eternally - then why worry?  You won't suffer because
there will no longer be a "you".

But then of course there's resurrection on judgement day when the decision
will be made.  Therefore death is at most a temporary oblivion.

I'm glad I don't have to justify any of this!

PL

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> to what you do, not what someone else does.

This raises for me an interesting, (and extremely pertinent) question:

WHY would a loving God, who created us, want/need to judge us?

My experience has been that a judgemental attitude and nature serves no 
useful purpose at all in improving things. As this judgement occurs at the 
end of a life, there is no possible opportunity to learn from it, or change 
things.

If God were REALLY loving, (then he wouldn't be Judgemental anyway, but, for 
the sake of this argument...), He would judge us in the middle of our lives 
and show us where we needed to buck our ideas up. (Maybe a bit like the 
ghosts of Christmas in Dickens' "A Christmas Carol"). At least there would 
be a chance for improvement and growth.

As He doesn't, we can only assume that this Judgement serves some OTHER 
purpose than helping us to be better.

Is it to reward the ones who toed the party line, and dispose of those who 
didn't?

If so, God DOESN'T love those who choose to question or disobey Him. He DOES 
love those who give blind obedience or "choose" the "right" path.

He's not all-loving then.

Is it to frighten future generations into compliance and submission?

He's a bully, then.

If he needs to bully people it is because he is not all-powerful. (Bullies 
enforce their will because they realise they are not all-powerful. (often 
they are subject to bullying by powers higher than themselves). The 
recognition of this inadequacy makes them misuse the power that they DO 
have.)

Given that He is omniscient, he knew how each of us was going to turn out 
from the moment we were conceived.  If He didn't, and Free Will enables us 
to change the result, then He isn't omniscient.

So now we have inklings that some of the "generally accepted" perceptions of 
God, certainly as promoted in the Church propaganda, are demonstrably NOT 
so.

He isn't omniscient.

He isn't loving.

Summarising, the need for Judgement tells us the following about God:

1. He isn't all-loving.
2. He isn't all-knowing.
3. He isn't all-powerful.

According to Kant's famous syllogism, EITHER He isn't omnipotent OR HE is 
uncaring, also.

Not quite the God the Church would have us believe in, is it?

But then, there is a certain type of person who WANTS a God of Vengeance, a 
God who will wreak havoc on the Unbelievers, a terrible, awesome, Lord of 
Hosts who you would be well advised to be with, rather than against...

Fine. (The fact that God would want a host of intimidated followers shows He 
isn't very smart, either...)

If you have a personality predisposed to judgement, punishment, and being on 
the side that will win every battle, Christianity may be for you...:-)

But then you may have some problems reconciling gentleness and 
lovingkindness with the more brutal aspects.

The Teachings of Jesus are completely incompatible with the actions of God, 
as described in the Old Testament.

And yet Christians (even in this forum) have no problem with quoting the Old 
Teastament to support their arguments.

We are complex creatures... :-)

Most religions have warlike and gentle aspects to them. This is because they 
were invented by Man and fulfill the needs of Man. The God(s) they present 
are acceptable to the multifaceted nature of Men so that both the dark and 
lighter sides can be represented, with individuals predisposed to one way or 
the other finding what they need in the Scriptures.

It doesn't do to examine the concepts too closely, or to apply logic and 
reason to what are primarily emotional responses arising from deep-seated 
needs.

If the Judgement of  souls is accepted as part of the creed that a Christian 
must believe, it raises many more questions than it answers.

Pete.
```

On Wed, 23 Jan 2008 10:20:30 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Most religions have warlike and gentle aspects to them. This is because they 
>were invented by Man and fulfill the needs of Man. The God(s) they present 
>are acceptable to the multifaceted nature of Men so that both the dark and 
>lighter sides can be represented, with individuals predisposed to one way or 
>the other finding what they need in the Scriptures.

Religions are effective at telling people that their way of life is
Right.    And when life gives us lemons - they can take comfort that
they will be rewarded and/or others will be punished.

The problem occurs when people believe this with enough fervor that
they accept as right and proper for others to suffer now.    After
all, whatever we do to the infidels is nothing compared to what God or
Allah will allow to be done to them.    Torture's OK.   Destruction's
OK.   It's all part of God's (or Allah's) plan so that my way of life
wins.    Those people who thought they were true believers a century
ago weren't really - they had it wrong.   But now I've got it right,
God has intended from the very beginning that His True Way is the Only
Way - and it's coincidentally my way.

Accepting Torture as right or necessary can't be evil if God does it.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> Summarising, the need for Judgement tells us the following about God:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> uncaring, also.

This line of logic assumes that we *are* omniscient.  Otherwise, how 
would we know what is caring and what isn't?  Can you think of something 
bad that happened in your life?  Can you look back, years removed from 
it, and see some good that has come into your life because of it?

It's kind of like those pictures I saw on the 'net somewhere.  They 
looked like naked body parts, because you could only see one part of it. 
  When they showed the entire un-zoomed picture, you could see that what 
looked like a butt was actually someone's folded arm, a breast was 
actually an orange.  (Don't ask where that site is - this was a long 
time ago!  ;> )

Realizing that *we* may not be able to see the whole picture is key. 
(Of course, if one doesn't think there *is* a "whole picture" to see in 
the first place, then this argument wouldn't really convince them.)  :)
```

On Mon, 21 Jan 2008 05:07:31 -0800 (PST), Alistair
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>On 21 Jan, 02:48, LX-i <lxi0...@netscape.net> wrote:
>> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>I was always under the impression that unbaptised babies go to hell.

In Catholicism, the Church can only entrust them (unbaptised babies)
to the mercy of God, as she does in her funeral rites for them.
Indeed, the great mercy of God who desires that all men should be
saved, and Jesus' tenderness toward children which caused him to say:
"Let the children come to me, do not hinder them" (Mk 10:14; cf. 1 Tim
2:4), allow us to hope that there is a way of salvation for children
who have died without Baptism. All the more urgent is the Church's
call not to prevent little children coming to Christ through the gift
of holy Baptism.

Regards,

On Sun, 20 Jan 2008 19:48:39 -0700, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>But, for those of us that believe it, it certainly explains the behavior

Why is it about obedience?

...

>There's also a concept in Scripture called the "age of accountability." 
>  It's not a magic number, per se, but it's the age at which they have

much as becoming indoctrinated enough to know.

But if you believe the baby that grows up in a Hindu family in a Hindu
culture who accepts their values, is good, but hasn't accepted Jesus
Christ for their "get out of Hell free" card - is damned - then what's
the difference between that child and an infant from our culture?

Maybe the difference is that you don't have to look at them in the eye
and say they will be tortured.

are, generally, not successful people.

>>  It's not a magic number, per se, but it's the age at which they have 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> much as becoming indoctrinated enough to know.

So the presence of false definitions means that even the correct one 
does not exist?

> But if you believe the baby that grows up in a Hindu family in a Hindu
> culture who accepts their values, is good, but hasn't accepted Jesus
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and say they will be tortured.

You love that torture word.  It's not torture, it's punishment. 
Throughout the Bible, Old and New Testament, there are records of people 
who were justified by faith.  I didn't make up the punishment, and I 
don't want *anyone* to have to endure it.  But this whole "tortured" 
line (pardon the double entendre) is getting really old.
```

off shoots who deny that original sin exists, others who absolve Eve
of all sin (BTW, she is a Babylonian storm goddess. Quite fitting
seeing as Yahweh is a Midionite storm god) and blame Adam and still
others who say that they could not have sinned because god knew they

unnamed one. The first refused to lie beneath Adam (saying she was his
equal) and goodness knows what happened to the second.

```
On 21 Jan, 17:00, SkippyPB <swieg...@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
> On Mon, 21 Jan 2008 05:04:09 -0800 (PST), Alistair
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> >of all sin (BTW, she is a Babylonian storm goddess.

Sorry got that wrong; she was a serpent goddess.

>
> >Did you know that Eve was Adam's third wife? after Lilith and an
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Eve was created in the Garden of Eden to be the wife of Adam, as he
was lonely. A Jewish book called The Alphabet of Ben Sira entered
Europe from the East in the 6th century A.D. In it Lilith, not Eve,
was Adam's first wife, created at the same time and from the same
dust. Claiming to be thus created equal, she refused to sleep or serve
"under him." When Adam tried to force her into the "inferior"
position, she flew away from Eden into the air, where she copulated
with demons, conceiving hundreds more each day. God sent three angels
after her, who threatened to kill her brood if she refused to return
to Adam. But she did refuse. So God made Eve from Adam's rib to be his
"second wife."

The Alphabet of Ben Sira Midrash goes even further and identifies a
third wife, created after Lilith deserted Adam, but before Eve. This
unnamed wife was purportedly made in the same way as Adam, from the
"dust of the earth", but the sight of her being created proved too
much for Adam to take and he refused to go near her.
```

basis that your God is all things to all people and is whatever they want 
Him to be. Organise rallies and prayer meetings and whip up the hysteria. If 
anyone complains that their prayers weren't answered, just tell them it is 
the work of Satan and they must try harder and contribute more money.

Make sure the target group is not comprised of the sharpest tools in the 
box; people who can read and write are less likely to become adherents. Then 
spread paranoia that the world is in a mess because the people outside the 
group are evil, and God (or "Charlie" as the chosen ones are allowed to call 
Him) wants us to acqire nuclear weapons and destroy the Babylon that is the 
world of commerce. We must do this quickly because the evil infidels are 
trying to suppress and discredit us. (No-one in the cult is allowed to read 
newspapers or access other news sources, because this i sthe work of the 
Devil and the infidels are deliberately manipulating the media anyway...)

Then you, as the leader of the Cult of Charlie go into retreat and are not 
seen or heard from again. The legends tell that Charlie took you up to 
Heaven to be with Him. The Cult falls into disarray without your guiding 
hand, and most of the members are arrested and incarcerated while trying to 
get into a nuclear facility...

You, of course are sitting on a beach in Queensland sipping cocktails at 
your favourite 5 star retreat, surrounded by your adoring concubines who 
have been told that this is all an illusion but must go on while Charlie 
deals with the real world of evil.

You keep this up until the Swiss Bank account where the cult's contributions 
have been deposited, runs dry.

Then...

...Get a group of people, decide how you want God to behave, and start a new 
(tax free) religion. Evangelize it on the basis that your God is all things 
to all people and is whatever they want Him to be. Organise rallies and 
prayer meetings and whip up the hysteria. If anyone complains that their 
prayers weren't answered, just tell them it is the work of Satan and they 
must try harder and contribute more money.

and so on...

Pete.

of entertainment and social value in churches.   The bigger dangers
are when they elect officials who give their beliefs lip service then
steal from them.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** tim Josling <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au>
- **Date:** 2008-01-15T10:35:16
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13op334bhs2p0cc@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<13o9hivfs712a8f@corp.supernews.com> <L06hj.44163$Mu4.29579@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <17d66e6b-41d9-467f-ae4c-1bc9629490f8@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <AMahj.69384$L%6.54404@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <13ocasvqbc8ak39@corp.supernews.com> <3Fqhj.30190$L27.1835@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5uns9tF1j30lfU1@mid.individual.net> <dqOhj.51297$N67.44049@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <5uqg38F1jhl04U1@mid.individual.net> <378go3tnhko793u45ebi4snptotcr90tik@4ax.com> <d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 14 Jan 2008 07:45:19 -0700, Howard Brazee wrote:

> On Fri, 11 Jan 2008 20:14:46 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> does evil or good doesn't mean that his whole group is evil or good.

I hesitate to mention this but here goes... in one of John Bradshaw's
books he quotes a study about the nature of religious adherence. This was
done in the US from memory and did not seem to have any particular agenda
as far as I could see. The study found religious people were mostly in
three broad categories. The impact of religion on behaviour varied by
category. I think a lot of confusion about the impact of religion on
behaviour stems from failure to distinguish different sub-groups of
adherents.

Convenience:

Those who professed religion for reasons of advance or convenience. "In
this town you can't get ahead if you don't belong to xxx:. These were about
40-45% of all religious people. Religion did not affect these people's
behaviour much, unless people were watching. Some of these people were
decent people and other were not. Whether people behaved badly depended
mostly on what type of people they were. I would probably put a number of
politicians in this group. Can there really be only 1 atheist out of 550
members of congress?

Comfort:

Those who found that religion gave them a comfortable feeling that they
were good people, or that they would not really die, or that life is not
meaningless, or that their life is not a pointless farce. This is the group
that by and large attends church on Sunday and behaves as usual the rest
of the week. By and large, these people did not much change their
behaviour as a result of their religion. Again, about 40-45% of the flock
are in this category.

Whether this lot behaved badly depended largely on their capability for
self-deception. People in this group can tend to believe "We're good
people so what we do must be good". This can make them blind to the bad
things they are doing. Perhaps some of those currently involved in
authorising torture and detention without trial may be in this group.

True believers:

These are about 5-10%. There are subcategories here too. Some are
dogmatic and do not question or work on their beliefs in any meaningful
way. Others do examine themselves more rigorously and thoughtfully.

Whether these people do bad things is dependent on how detached from
"normal evident concrete reality" they are. For some true believers, the
religious beliefs can become more real than the evidence of their own
eyes. These are the ones of whom Richard Dawkins noted that religion can
make good people do bad things.

Others are more aware of the limits of human knowledge and the extent of
human fallibility, and temper their enthusiasm with some pragmatism.
Possibly burning a witch to death may save her from eternal damnation, but
maybe I'm not sure enough to actually do it.


It would be interesting to do a similar analysis of non-believers. I would
suspect some just don't care about other people's opinions, some feel they
can succeed without playing the game, some just don't think the evidence
stacks up, others have never thought about it. 

Some people don't have much of a spiritual impulse. I remember reading
about Temple Grandin (who has Aspergers syndrome) in the book "An
anthropologist on mars". She does did not understand what people are
talking about when they stand at the top of a mountain at sunset and feel
something special or spiritual. Perhaps that sort of ultra-geek /
semi-aspergers brain does not have much room for religion for some reason.
There do seem to be a lot of geeks who espouse atheism. In my experience
geeks are very honest people (generally) so maybe it's partly that fewer
of them are fake adherents than in the general population.

Tim Josling
```

On Wed, 16 Jan 2008 11:20:00 +1300, Pete Dashwood wrote:

> That's fine, until somebody decides that everyone not in the Group is
> evil... then the trouble starts.
> 
> Pete.

One explanation (among many) for the existence of religion is that as man
gained the capacity for rational thinking, this conflicted with the need
to do irrational things...

Such as settle down and have kids -> Thus love

Such as work for the benefit of the team not just the individual.

Man's great strength with language and intelligence is to work in powerful
teams for common goals. Often, this is in the context of conflict with
other groups who were competing for the same food, territory and women.
There was a need to have something that bound the group together,
something that clearly distinguished "us" from "them".

Religion would be a possible solution to this problem. If so, this would
explain why something which is supposed to be about love and harmony and
peace is so often associated with conflict between groups.

Tim Josling
```

<Alistair.J.L.Maclean@googlemail.com> wrote in message 
news:10e1510a-aa43-4629-be1c-90a6a4a5128d@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
On 15 Jan, 22:20, "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:
> "tim Josling" <tejgcc_nos...@westnet.com.au> wrote in message
>
…[124 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -

So...if you don't get emotional about sunsets should we exorcise you
from our midst?

Pete: Alistair, really... Read it again... I certainly DO get emotinal about 
sunsets and other things. Yesterday I was in a laboratory where they are 
raising kiwi birds. It takes several days for a chick to get out of the egg 
and I was priveleged to be in the right place at the right time and saw one 
completing the hatching process. The experience affected me quite deeply. 
This tiny little unique bird fighting its way into the world, a symbol of 
our nation, brought tears to my eyes... I got choked up and was as close to 
embarrassed as I have ever been, until I realised everyone else in the room 
was similarly affected...:-) There's nothing wrong with getting emotional; 
as long as you don't let yur emotions hurt other people.

Mind you, I can't argue that I shouldn't be exorcised from your midst, 
anyway... :-)

Pete.
```

On 15 Jan, 10:35, tim Josling <tejgcc_nos...@westnet.com.au> wrote:

> It would be interesting to do a similar analysis of non-believers. I would
> suspect some just don't care about other people's opinions, some feel they
> can succeed without playing the game, some just don't think the evidence
> stacks up, others have never thought about it.

You missed a category here: those who do not believe because they have
personal experience which causes them to lose faith in a 'loving' god.
My grandmother died (painfully) of cancer when my mother was 10 years
old. Result: my mother does not believe that  a loving god would allow
such pain and misery and hence can not bring herself to believe in
god.

>
> Some people don't have much of a spiritual impulse. I remember reading
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> something special or spiritual.

My last near-spiritual experience was when walking between the pyla at
the temple of Karnakh. There are places and views that can engender
spiritual feelings even in atheists.

> Perhaps that sort of ultra-geek /
> semi-aspergers brain does not have much room for religion for some reason.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Dunno 'bout that. I'm a geek and an atheist. My atheism grew from
being told, at the age of 11, how the miracles really were not all
they were cracked up to be. From there to opening my eyes during
prayer (where was Jesus? he certainly wasn't walking around near me
when I was praying. Kids can take things so literally) And finally to
becoming hooked on science, space and the natural world.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** tim Josling <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au>
- **Date:** 2008-01-17T06:21:12

Robert wrote:
>
> How about when US patriotism is used in defense of torturing and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> religion.

I don't think "patriotism" is the rationale for torturing or killing. 
Exigent circumstances seems more likely.

But how is doubling the size of the prison at Guantanamo immoral? In my 
view, we've got to have somewhere to put unlawful enemy combatants. Absent 
enlarged accommodations at Guantanamo, the choices seem to be:

1. Kill them where they stand.
2. Incarcerate them where they're captured (Iraq, Afghanistan, etc.).
3. Release them.
4. Put them on parole, with ankle-bracelets, or
5. Incarcerate them on U.S. soil.

Just yesterday, a UK minister proposed implanting RFID chips under the skin 
of criminals so monitoring them while on parole could be facilitated. The 
single reason for so proposing this is to relieve overcrowding at UK 
prisons. He apparently shares your view that additional prison space is out 
of the question.
```

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

can usually be done in 2-3 days. (Source: personal experience.) After that, put them in
POW camps that follow the Geneva Convention. Invite the Red Crescent/Red Cross to inspect.

will simply cut the bracelet off and disappear.

```
Robert wrote:
>>
>> I don't think "patriotism" is the rationale for torturing or killing.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to see the enemy brutalized.

Not that there's anything wrong with that.

>
> Doing so undermines US moral authority. It makes us look no better
> than the bad guys.

"What do you care what other people think?" by Richard Feynmann.

>
>> But how is doubling the size of the prison at Guantanamo immoral?
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Invite TV cameras (without violating prisoners' dignity or privacy).

I agree it can be done in two or three days with vigorous interrogation 
techniques.

As for putting them in POW camps, they are *NOT* POWs. There are no Geneva 
Conventions or protocols that govern unlawful enemy combatants (UEC). The 
3rd Geneva Convention defines "lawful enemy combatant" as one who meets all 
of the following criteria:

1. Wears a distinctive uniform or insignia visible at a distance
2. Carries weapons openly
3. Adheres to a responsible chain-of-command
4. Comports his activities in accordance with the rules of war

Plus a few others, such as masters of vessels, war correspondents, medical 
personnel, construction workers... Those who do not meet the tests of 
"lawful" combatants are deemed "unlawful."

In the past, unlawful enemy combatants (spys, saboteurs, guerrillas, 
fifth-columnists, etc.), have been treated somewhat peremptorily by 
virtually all nations. Our first UEC encounter, Major Andre, was hanged by 
order of George Washington shortly after being caught.

As for inviting outside observers to document the conditions, it better 
serves a deterrent purpose for potential terrorist to imagine the worst. 
"Three hots and a cot" is a pay raise to some.

>
> As for those who refuse to play nice, outsource them to the lowest
> bidding country in the Middle East. We can't afford to build SuperMax
> in Iraq.

We've tried that. It's called "rendition."
```

>order of George Washington shortly after being caught.

The Geneva Conventions were amended in 1977 to recognize guerrillas as combatants. 

Countries equip fighters with uniforms and chains of command. Our enemies in Iraq aren't
fighting for a country, they're fighting for a religion and/or culture. They are
guerrillas, which means their weapons are invisibility (hiding in the population),
surprise and mobility (no fixed bases). 

The best way to fight people like that, IMO, is to beat them at their own game. Be more
mobile, more stealthy, get behind their lines and ambush THEM; destroy their logistical
pipelines; get the common people on OUR side.

>As for inviting outside observers to document the conditions, it better 
>serves a deterrent purpose for potential terrorist to imagine the worst. 
>"Three hots and a cot" is a pay raise to some.

If we killed them when caught, none of them would surrender. They'd fight to the death.
Offering humane treatment weakens their resolve by encouraging surrender.
```
