>
>> I have not seen you make this argument, but he implication of your post 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Invalid reasoning.

No it isn't. It is observable fact. You just refuse to accept it. (Do you 
wonder why I won't present hard evidence in support of OO... :-)?)

>Result doesn't have to be absolute for there to be a
> cause. Firing a gun at someone's head may not kill them, but if it does
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Already invalidated above.

Only in your mind.

>Result need not be absolute for there to be
> a cause. Simply influencing probablity is quite sufficient. That's a real
> no-brainer, Tim. :-)

You certainly seem to using no brain to demonstrate it... :-)

>
>> The "cause" of the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> make it up and claim it as fact? ;-)

Er... it was YOU who claimed that God created the Universe by being outside 
it...
>
>> The takeout is that the idea of "first" cause has nothing to do with
>> causality and the attempt to use causality in this way is bogus.
>
> Again, invalid reasoning on faulty (actually missing) premise.

Only invalid reasoning here is yours...
>
>> 3. Even in physics there is no need for a first cause. Each event can be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Physics has nothing to say about things external to the universe.

That's right. Physics is based on real observable phenomena and rightly 
recognises that events outside a frame of reference do not affect that frame 
of reference.

>Supposing
> a series of (obviously and provably) connected events "just happened" 
> without
> any cause is, on the face of it, preposterous.

As has been noted here on a number of occasions, with examples, by myself 
and others, there is nothing "preposterous" about it (other than the 
consideration that most of what happens in Quantum Physics is "preposterous" 
when considered in the light of our perception...). Nevertheless, 
"preposterous" or not, these effects DO occur. It IS a wave AND a particle, 
photons DO go through both slits simultaneously unless you don't want them 
to, the cat is neither alive nor dead until you open the box, and simply 
observing what is going on affects the results. There is much more here than 
we perceive and it has nothing to do with God. It simply reflects our, as 
yet, imperfect understanding of the Universe. But we are getting there, and 
the requirement to make some Supreme Being responsible for it all, 
diminishes as our understanding increases.

I like the story in Genesis. The fruit of the Tree of Knowledge is indeed 
powerful. One day (when we have acquired the necessary skill and 
understanding), we'll tell God to sod off and build our own garden...


>Mention physical principles,
> then throw them out the window by fiat with no basis in observable fact? 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sorry Tim, I fail to see any revelance to the discussion.

Is that a problem with his argument or with your ocular perception?

>
>> No-one knows why the universe is as it is and not some other way. I would
>> love to know, but these sorts of arguments don't help.
>
> If God created it, He knows.
And if He didn't, then He doesn't. So what?

> If you a priori chuck that explanation out
> window, as you blithely did physical principles above, and it's the 
> correct
> one, then you'll never know, will you? :-)

And if it is NOT the correct one, continued investigation will lead to 
better understanding of it, so he WILL know, won't he? :-)

Guess which horse I'm backing...

Pete.
```

of the non-existance of either god or Satan.

- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <5ug8jsF1hf0vaU1@mid.individual.net> <31563764-0489-40cd-8b99-a26901a37575@k39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <r8Mgj.43576$Mu4.6530@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> Some people interpret Revelation 12:4 to mean that Satan subverted one
> third of the angels to revolt against God.

Interesting. To the Jew, Satan is a good guy.

"Satan" (literally, "the accuser") appears only four times in the Hebrew 
scriptures. Once in a Psalm (where he sits on the right hand of God), once 
in 1st Kings where he delivers a message from God to David, and throughout 
the Book of Job (I forget the 4th). In Job, while Satan torments and does 
nasty to the main character, Satan's actions are directed by God and Satan 
does nothing without permission.

The Christian, on the other hand, has found rascals throughout the Old 
Testament and denominated many of them as Satan by another name. To the 
Christian, the serpent in the Garden of Eden is Satan in a snake-suit. To 
the Jew, the serpent is a serpent.

Oh well.
```

On Jan 9, 3:40 am, Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> On 8 Jan, 03:22, "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the non-existance of either god or Satan.

I think that  'existence' or 'non-existence' is the wrong question. In
Caananite mythology (or exagerated ancestor history) El had several
offspring. One was Jehovah another was Baal. It is entirely possible
that these were actual people where Jehovah went on to set up his own
WarLorddom with   the hebrews and Baal Zebub (or Baelzebub) was a
neibouring WarLord with whom they competed.

Yoiu mnay also note that Jehovah was one of the Elohim (or 'House of
El').
```

The Bible itself has examples of this: Joseph, the husband of Mary, was the
son of Jacob (Mark 1:16); also the son of Heli Luke 3:23).    And if you
follow the geneaologies back to Abraham, they are wildly different in every
respect, most crucially in the number of generations.

PL

"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> The Bible itself has examples of this: Joseph, the husband of Mary, was the
> son of Jacob (Mark 1:16); also the son of Heli Luke 3:23).    And if you
> follow the geneaologies back to Abraham, they are wildly different in every
> respect, most crucially in the number of generations.

Those accounts are generally understood to be genealogies of Mary and
Joseph, which would, of course, be different. Though Joseph was not
Jesus' physical father, he stood in the position of a father to the household,
and God apparently chose to establish that genealogy as well.

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-09T15:50:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ykbhj.4492$M24.261@newsfe17.lga>`
- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <5ug8jsF1hf0vaU1@mid.individual.net> <31563764-0489-40cd-8b99-a26901a37575@k39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <207583ce-21d4-42ea-abc7-7ba352a75b1f@i72g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <s_ahj.7519$q36.2315@newsfe14.lga> <C7bhj.69398$L%6.15737@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`

```
That's one of the two usual counter-arguments.  But how does Joseph get into
Mary's genealogy?  And that isn't what the two verses say, anyway.

(IIRC, the other argument is approximately that Jacob and Heli were
brothers; that one of them died - the elder brother - and that the next
brother assumed responsibility for his children, as was the custom.  I have
read elsewhere of that custom and have no reason to suspect that the
accounts are wrong.  But the Bible doesn't say that Jacob and Heli  were
brothers so it's an unwarranted assumption at best.  And it seems to me that
when such cases occurred there would have to be some way of referring to the
two different sets of kids, for matters of primogeniture, inheritance,
etc.).

PL

- **Message-ID:** `<78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <5ug8jsF1hf0vaU1@mid.individual.net> <31563764-0489-40cd-8b99-a26901a37575@k39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <207583ce-21d4-42ea-abc7-7ba352a75b1f@i72g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <s_ahj.7519$q36.2315@newsfe14.lga> <C7bhj.69398$L%6.15737@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6cb69101-c093-4bcf-92db-63a48c570d3c@l32g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>as the word of god, is evidence for his inconsistency too.

I won't admit that, I would be dishonest if I did, and I'll tell you why.
I do not expect you to except what I'm about to write, but it is the only
answer I can give you, and I know as surely as one can know anything that
it's true. Perhaps it will at least help you understand why I believe as
I do, even if you do not.

Unlike yourself Alistair, I have spent considerable time on both sides of
this argument. I was 27 years old when I accepted Jesus. I avidly read
science fiction from a very early age, subscribed to Scientific American
from the time I was 10, studied physics and math at university, and was
thoroughly inculcated in the humanism you espouse. I had never studied
the Bible, and like you, it did not make much sense to me when I read any
of it. It never made sense to me that Christians would connect verses
from different parts of the Bible and put them together to make a point.
It seemed to me that one could do the same thing from a dictionary or
encyclopedia (or any other source) to make any point you like.

What I could not see, what I did not know, and could not realize, is that
the Bible *cannot* be understood *through the intellect alone*, any more
than color can be understood through the intellect alone. Unless a person
has color sense, you can describe color to them until your tongue falls
out, and they *will not get it*. You either can see color, in which case
explanation is unnecessary, or you can't see it, and explanation is
useless. No amount of study would permit even a Newton or an Einstein to
know color, without color vision.

I did not come to Christ because I believed the Bible; I hadn't read much
of it. I asked Jesus into my life because I had a lot of serious problems
in my life at the time, and was desperate for any help I could get. But,
once I did ask Jesus into my life, things began to change, a lot. When a
person accepts Jesus as Savior God, sends His Holy Spirit to live inside
them. This Presence is real and tangible to the Christian. You can clearly
sense the presence of the Holy Spirit. And as promised in the Bible, the
Holy Spirit opens your understanding to the Bible, just like having color
vision opens color to you. I began to have an enormous appetite for the
Bible, and read it avidly every day. I began to see that the Bible really
is one, integrated, completely consistent work. I remember the astonishment
I felt when I first realized that I could now *see* why various Scriptures
could be taken from different places in the Bible to make a single point.
It is because the Bible *is* one whole consistent work, and when the Holy
Spirit gives one the understanding of it, it isn't arbitrary at all.
Studying the Scriptures, I can see things myself, that are the same things
Christians in other parts of the world also see. It is amazing to meet
Christians from different cultures; it's like meeting family or dear old
friends you didn't know you had. Some people, particularly non-believers,
usually only accent the differences; but the commonality of belief and
worldview is astonishing. Like different paintings by the same painter.

Over time, as I studied the Bible and some Christian history, I learned
that there are fewer things in the Bible that are criticized as error by
non-believers today, than in the past. Over time the Bible is increasingly
attested by other sources to be correct, not the reverse. No purely human
book would exhibit this characteristic. If the Bible were purely human,
therefore fallible, then in such a large work, over time the number of
known fallacies would increase, not decrease. For example, until (IIRC)
circa 1930, the only known reference to Sargon II, king of Assyria, was
Isaiah 20:1 "In the year that Tartan came to Ashdod, when Sargon the king
of Assyria sent him, and he fought against Ashdod and took it...". But
about 1930 they dug up Sargon II's palace, along with his 100,000 volume
library, and now it's thought that Sargon II may have been the greatest
king Assyria ever had. Oh, and in that 100,000 volume library they found
in greater detail the same account as in Isaiah 20:1. Again and again the
Bible has been shown historically correct through further archeological
discoveries. It was once thought that the Gospels presented contradictory
chronologies of Jesus life, but that has been shown to be false, since
chronologies have been developed that are consistent with the Gospel
accounts.

Are there passages in the Bible that are difficult to reconcile? Yes,
there are. There are several reasonable explanations for this. For one
thing, we do not have the original manuscripts, and scribal errors and
changes have definitely been introduced. For another, as shown in the
apparent Gospel chronology problems, our knowledge is incomplete. As we
continue to study the Bible, more and more of the seeming contradictions
are resolved, as one would expect in a genuine divinely inspired book.
In addition, there have been a lot of "jewels" uncovered in the digging
done to resolve the apparent contradictions. So many that theologians
have suggested God may have intentionally permitted those problems just
to promote deeper digging in some areas.

It is also true that the documentary evidence for Biblical events is
vastly greater than for any other events of comparable antiquity. For
example, there is more evidence of Jesus crucifixion and resurrection
than that Julius Caesar ever existed. There are vastly more manuscripts,
and they date from much closer to the actual events, than any other
historical events of comparable antiquity. For example, there are only
(IIRC) nine extant copies of Caesar's "Gallic Wars", and the oldest
dates over 900 years after the events. There are literally tens of
thousands of ancient Biblical manuscripts, and some of the New Testament
manuscripts date from about 30 years after the events.

(Aside: anyone interested in a legal defense of Jesus crucifixion and
resurrection should read a little book "The Testimony of the Evangelists"
by Simon Greenleaf. Check out Greenleaf's credentials while your at it.)

So, the only thing I, or any other Christian, can do to answer critics
such as yourself is tell you what we know from experience. What you do
with that information is up to you. You can either believe it, accept
Christ as your Savior, and you will see and experience what we see
and experience. Millions have chosen that road. Or you can continue to
reject our testimony, and you won't ever see it, or experience what we
do. Many more millions have chosen that road. The choice is yours, and
that's the bottom line. :-)
```

It is obvious (to those of us that 'see' that) that you have

for it to appear as if it was a logical argument.

that it is a satisfactory way of going about making an argument.

that we don't.  You "see" the pot of gold, we only see the rainbow
(and know how it is formed).

From the Christian standpoint, any other religion (except Judaism) is
either rejection of God (e.g. atheism) or worshiping a false god. The
Bible says such false gods are actually demons, evil spirit beings.
These spirit beings can influence people, though not like you see in
the movies. I've known several people who were deeply into the
occult before accepting Jesus, and every one of them insists that
things such as "channeling demons" is absolutely real.

On Jan 15, 5:48 am, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> things such as "channeling demons" is absolutely real.

In my view every religion is correct only in so far as it claims that
all the others are wrong, misled, or simply deluded.

Yes, "channeling demons" is exactly as real as "accepting Jesus", or
being abducted by aliens, or many other alledged supernatural
activities, or at least they obviously _seem_ to be real. But, as I
say, I can do that too, the difference is that I know that it is all
entirely my brain centres being fed by my own endorphins. But that is
because I haven't been fed some stories about what it is, nor promised
some SantaClausist nonsense that requires certain things to be
accepted uncritically.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<uR2jj.66142$rc2.52922@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<207583ce-21d4-42ea-abc7-7ba352a75b1f@i72g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <s_ahj.7519$q36.2315@newsfe14.lga> <C7bhj.69398$L%6.15737@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6cb69101-c093-4bcf-92db-63a48c570d3c@l32g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <8offo31afaa6brlaleceh07cnrgus3dft9@4ax.com> <k1Qhj.51338$N67.8724@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <tctmo31b0g4irm0qrr1v1g63efepkkm83u@4ax.com> <DnMij.50469$Mu4.455@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v25k0F1kh7qcU1@mid.individual.net> <13op3dk345dme85@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> an authority on much at all.

They should be a pretty good authority on their own experiences, and what
they've witnessed. The people I'm talking about aren't "flakes", in any
sense of the word. The stereotype of "occult people" being similar to what
you see on the movies is as wrong as many movie stereotypes are. Sure,
there are people with multi-colored hair, 50 face piercings, and all that.
But for every one of those, there are ten who could work in the cubicle
next to you for years, and you would never suspect their involvement in
the occult. The people I'm talking about are like that. Unless they told
you, you simply wouldn't know or suspect. One of them was an attractive
woman who attended the same church as I did. She was as sweet and gentle
a person as you could imagine; the picture of a gentle, cultured and
respectable wife. But years before she had been studying to be a witch.
Had she not told me herself, I would never have suspected. She said that
when she was around crowds, such as in a shopping mall, she could sense
the evil spirits in others walking past who were in the occult.

One of them was one of the two most intelligent people I have ever known
personally. Just for kicks, let me relate something he told me. I knew
this guy very well, because he and I were partners in a computer business
for several years. He had told me that years before he had gotten involved
in the occult for a while, and had once seen objects moving through the
air on their own. I knew him to be truthful, or I would hardly have gone
into business with him. He had made reference to some event that had scared
him, and caused him to abandon the occult and move here from California.
But he hadn't told me what that event was. We were having lunch one day and
he decided to tell me about that event. He had hardly started when he began
to visibly tremble and went ashen. He was obviously terrified. It took him
several minutes to regain his composure, and he never mentioned it again.
This guy was mentally tough, and had faced death on several occasions. I'm
certain he could have witnessed a murder or other horrible event and been
completely casual about it. Take this as you will, but as I said, this was
one of the two most intelligent people I ever met, well educated in the
sciences, a computer professional, skeptical, tough and hard nosed; the
antithesis of gullible.

I've known several such people, and they all relate consistent details about
the occult. Primarily that it's real and it's dangerous.

Further, there are certain "psychological" disorders that are thought by many
mental health professionals to be demonic oppression, rather than just mental
illness. Multiple personality disorder is a major one. I've discussed this
with several practicing clinical psychiatrists and psychologists who agree.
My daughter minored in psych, and one of her professors, also a practicing
psychiatrist, believes the same thing. That's not the "official position",
of course. But for those who actually deal with people suffering from such
problems, there are aspects that just can't be explained as a pure mental
disorder.

But, I'm just posting this FYI. I'm not really interested in debating the
existence of demons. :-)
```

```
On 15 Jan, 13:49, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "tim Josling" <tejgcc_nos...@westnet.com.au> wrote:
> The people I'm talking about are like that. Unless they told
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> respectable wife. But years before she had been studying to be a witch.

There is a difference between the kind of occultist who could loosely
be described as a Satanist (and they can be nice people too) and a
practitioner of Wicca (which is a valid and acceptable religion pre-
dating christianity). Unfortunately witches tend to be tarred with the
same brush as Satanists much as most christians are tarred by the
fundamentalist brush.


> I've known several such people, and they all relate consistent details about
> the occult. Primarily that it's real and it's dangerous.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> illness. Multiple personality disorder is a major one.

I have a friend who suffers from paranoid-schizophrenia. His condition
was, until recently, exacerbated by heavy use of skunk. I can state
categorically that, even though he hears voices telling him to kill,
he is not possessed by any demons. Aside from the debatable point that
I do not believe in demons, ghosts or ghouls, my discussions with him
and my reading about recent research into schizophrenia leads me to
believe that what is happening is a mild dissociative behaviour
(similar but not quite as severe as the out-of-body experiences people
claim).

If any mental health professional starts talking about demons then
they should be struck off or lose their licence to practice. There is
no room for quackery in medicine. Demonic possession is only sloppy
thinking by these professionals.

> I've discussed this
> with several practicing clinical psychiatrists and psychologists who agree.
> My daughter minored in psych, and one of her professors, also a practicing
> psychiatrist, believes the same thing.

He should be summarily dismissed and his department closed.

> That's not the "official position",
> of course. But for those who actually deal with people suffering from such
> problems, there are aspects that just can't be explained as a pure mental
> disorder.

Then they should acknowledge their ignorance and restrain from
practising quackery.

>
```

Similar things were said about the doctor in England who kept telling

taking 4 antibiotic pills. If the demons are real, it isn't "quackery". Do
you have proof they aren't? Do you thing demons are stranger than
some of the things coming out of quantum mechanics? There are some
very strange things in this universe, and we've probably barely scratched
the surface.

On Wed, 16 Jan 2008 17:37:44 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> <Alistair.J.L.Maclean@googlemail.com> wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> surface.

Quite true. The test I apply to medical theories is: can you cure the
condition using your theory? Theories of demonic possession have not, and
yes I have checked, resulted in satisfactory treatments.

I'm not a slavish adherent of tranditional medicine though. The fact that
there is a movement in medicine called "evidence-based medicine" is
eloquent that traditional medicine is a long way from where it should be.
Diagnostic processes are also very haphazard. As Peter Norvig pointed out,
even simple AI programs outperform experienced clinicians in diagnosis.
Specialisation means that often no-one can see the big picture. Etc.

I think the ulcer guy & gal were Aussies actually:

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barry_Marshall


Tim Josling
```

On 16 Jan, 23:37, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> <Alistair.J.L.Macl...@googlemail.com> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> And your objective proof of this is...

Proof:
1. There is no supernatural. Until you can conclusively prove the
existence of so much as one angel, demon or god then this must stand.
2. Therefore there can be no demons.
3. All people I have met or heard of who advocate the existence of
angels, demons or gods are flawed personalities.
4. I asked my friend about his voices. He confirms that they are his
voice (tones, etc.) and entirely within his head. He does admit that
there are nutters who claim to hear god but he denies that such voices
are a manifestation of angels, demons or god.

>
> > Aside from the debatable point that
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> people from the 1970s that ulcers were caused by bacteria.

So what? At least science and medicine eventually proved his claim
correct. No religion is able to prove its' claims correct.

> In the '90s
> my dad, who had suffered from ulcers for decades was cured by
> taking 4 antibiotic pills. If the demons are real, it isn't "quackery". Do
> you have proof they aren't?

Do you have proof that they are real? Don't trawl out the clap-trap
from the bible. That is merely superstitious nonsense and you should
realise that there is precious little evidence to verify the bible and
certainly none to verify the existence of gods, demons, angels, satan,
whatever. There is no more evidence for them than there is for the
flying spaghetti monster.

> Do you thing demons are stranger than
> some of the things coming out of quantum mechanics?

To paraphrase you: quantum mechanics is a theory and not proven fact.
I take quantum mechanics and parallel universii with a pinch of salt.
Quantum mechanics is only a model for the nanoscopic level of the
universe.

> There are some
> very strange things in this universe, and we've probably barely scratched
> the surface.

And the strangest is how can people wedded to their bibles denounce
and disparage science and medicine (and the processes thereof) whilst
benefiting from the advances made by science.

People wedded to the bible should retire from society and live like
the Amish should (or would if they weren't so busy breaking their own
rules).

>
> >> I've discussed this
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> clinical practice makes that certain. Right.

First, my previous comments are based on personal experience and
observation of a schizophrenic.

Second, in discussions with my niece (FIRST CLASS HONOURS GRADUATE
FROM LEICESTER UNIVERSITY IN PSYCHOLOGY):

a. any psychiatrist or psychologist or doctor who ascribes demon
influences to a schizophrenic is (in her words) "highly
unprofessional, disrespectful towards their patient and judgemental".
She agreed that such a "professional" should be removed from their
post and barred from practice.

b. she referred me to wikipedia which carries an article on
schizophrenia which she considers to be current and highly
informative. She also recommended following the Julian Jaynes link
with regard to the bicameral functioning of the brain, the corpus
callosum (which joins the two sides of the brain) and also to read
about dissociative disorders.

c. my niece is sick of studying schizophrenia as it is a subject that
was covered several times during her degree.

d. she finally added that I should mention that electro-stimulation of
parts of the brain can induce the demon voices in the minds of
schizophrenics. I pointed out that you would see that statement as
support for your antiquated position and as proof that demons exist
and that the bible is truely the consistent word of god. She laughed.
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> In physics "theory" doesn't mean what it does in popular culture.   A
> theory is a mathematical model that matches (to some degree) observed
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> arguments.

Then riddle me this.  Why does the *law* of entropy (order to disorder, 
things slowing down) apply to every visible phenomena on the planet, yet 
we're supposed to believe that, via evolution, the world is getting more 
complex and more refined?

As I've said before, I guess I just don't have enough faith to believe 
in evolution.  (Of course, I'm speaking of it as an explanation for the 
origin of life, not the English word meaning "to gradually change from 
one thing to another".)
```

Entropy applies to closed systems. The input of energy - and thereby

system as a whole becomes more disordered. The sun is running down.

this would not have been pointed out?

This should be taught at bible colleges so people don't embarrass
themselves.

```
tim Josling wrote:
> On Thu, 17 Jan 2008 17:56:45 -0700, LX-i wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> system as a whole becomes more disordered. The sun is running down.

That's a pretty sketchy explanation, especially considering the universe 
itself is a closed system.  Everywhere else is going to disorder, but 
we're so lucky to be on the one relatively small piece of it that's 
perfecting itself.  I don't buy it.

> Do you really think that if evolution contradicted the laws of physics
> this would not have been pointed out?

I think evolution contradicts a lot of laws, but that's just me...

> This should be taught at bible colleges so people don't embarrass
> themselves.

The entire scientific process is observe, hypothesize, test.  Evolution 
can't be verified at step 1, much less steps 2 or 3.  Yet we're just 
supposed to blindly believe that that's the way it happened, because 
it's an explanation that *didn't* come from a religious book?  I may be 
simple-minded - I just can't believe that.
```

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

You were the one talking about entropy. Perhaps you should educate
yourself about it.

Consider this analogy. You as an individual grow larger and stronger
during your first 20 or so years. Does this contradict the laws of
physics? No, because of the input of energy in the form of food.

Similarly the ecosystems of the earth grow larger, more complex and
more diverse because of energy input from the sun. Overall, entropy grows,
when you take the sun into account.

tim Josling wrote:
> On Thu, 17 Jan 2008 20:33:59 -0700, LX-i wrote:
> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> when you take the sun into account.

So "light from the sun" is the magic potion that makes this happen? 
That just doesn't compute.  Is Mars going from disorder to order too? 
 From what I've studied, Venus is a case study in disorder.

I've seen children grow, and even though they may grow larger, many of 
our organs begin wearing down right away.  We can *try* to put things in 
order (see my previous post to Howard), but end the end, it's futile. 
Hearts will wear out.  Lungs will wear out.  Joints will wear down 
cartilage to nothing.  Hips will break.  It's a part of life.

As someone who believes Creation (which, I realize, gives me a premise 
with which you disagree), and believes that humans are God's crowning 
creation - if this is how *we* work, and how every other animal and 
plant works, why would we believe that the earth ages any differently?
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:sQRkj.54931$Mu4.15363@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> is very disturbing,

No it isn't and you are generalizing (again).

I've covered here several times how, as an atheist, I  will be perfectly 
happy to be called to account before God's Great Judgement Seat if I am 
actually wrong about Him... I don't find this in the least bit disturbing 
and neither do most of the atheists I know. You are projecting how you think 
we SHOULD feel (given your belief basis) and that is nothing like reality.

Have you thought aboiut about what impact it would have on your life, if YOU 
are wrong? :-) (No, it is inconceivable isn't it?)

I don't have "faith" in evolution; it doesn't require "faith" to examine the 
facts and see that somethig is obviously so...

Only where the "facts" are seriously disputable, or the Authority on which 
something is based is contradictory (like the Bible), does "faith" become a 
pre-requisite to belief.



> thus a strong vested interest in keeping that faith,

Not at all. I really don't mind either way...:-)

> at all costs to rationality; the very thing they accuse believers of. :-)

I don't "accuse" believers of anything.

Please make sure that when you are generalizing about "unbelievers" you 
include me in that group, and ensure that your generalization fits all of 
us...

Otherwise your observations are really of little value... :-)

Pete.

On Mon, 21 Jan 2008 08:38:52 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> torment.

Another old argument. And equally invalid to the first cause argument
which you raised previously.

There is no credible evidence of an afterlife and no known way of knowing
anything about it, even if there is such a thing. Making essentially random
assumptions about it does not get you any expectation of a better outcome
there.

Perhaps there is eternal torture there for those who uncritically swallow
unfounded beliefs... who knows?

We do know, pretty well for sure, that people burned at the stake for
heresy, or blown up by religion-inspired suicide bombers, die horrible
deaths.

We know for a fact that religious dogmatism has held back the advance of
knowledge by thousands of years.

Tim
```

On Thu, 17 Jan 2008 20:33:59 -0700, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>
>That's a pretty sketchy explanation, especially considering the universe 
>itself is a closed system.  Everywhere else is going to disorder, but 
>we're so lucky to be on the one relatively small piece of it that's 
>perfecting itself.  I don't buy it.

I haven't measured whether the universe is becoming more disordered or
more ordered.    But I have measured that one small part - my desk -
is becoming more ordered, and sure enough it is more ordered today
than it was yesterday.

So there are examples of subsets of the universe becoming more
ordered.
```

```
On 18 Jan, 03:33, LX-i <lxi0...@netscape.net> wrote:
> tim Josling wrote:
> > On Thu, 17 Jan 2008 17:56:45 -0700, LX-i wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> perfecting itself.  I don't buy it.

That is your entitlement. You might like to consider that the universe
is expanding and cooling. With infinite expansion then the universe
will eventually freeze. Physical laws do not prevent localised order
as appears on earth. My limited understanding of Chaos Theory leads me
to believe that the physical world will order itself where it is
possible to do so, and has done so.


>
> > Do you really think that if evolution contradicted the laws of physics
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Darwin wrote about the origin of species and not about the origin of
life. Speciation is observable in the natural world. There is a gull
which ranges globally. It originally has a black back but at the
extreme range in one direction it is white and in the other covered
with more black. Although each extreme can interbreed with the
midrange bird the extreme colour varieties do not interbreed at the
extremes of their ranges. That is speciation in the making. Other
examples are where populations of plants or animals become
geographically isolated and substantially changed compared to the
original (see Darwin's finches).
```

```
On Thu, 17 Jan 2008 17:56:45 -0700, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Howard Brazee wrote:
>> In physics "theory" doesn't mean what it does in popular culture.   A
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>complex and more refined?

There is conservation of information. What appears to be entropy is information transfer
from physics to biology.

Frederick Kantor, in Information Mechanics, derived many laws of physics from the premise
that information is physical. John Wheeler, a well known physicist, agreed when he said,
"It is not unreasonable to imagine that information sits at the core of physics, just as
it sits at the core of a computer".  

This leads me to think the universe is god's method of reproduction. Evolution is the
purpose of life. Saying that god created man "in his own image" is like saying a strand of
DNA is the image of a person, or an egg is the image of a chicken. It's true at one level,
but shouldn't be taken literally.
```

On Thu, 17 Jan 2008 17:56:45 -0700, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Then riddle me this.  Why does the *law* of entropy (order to disorder, 
>things slowing down) apply to every visible phenomena on the planet, yet 
>we're supposed to believe that, via evolution, the world is getting more 
>complex and more refined?

Every where I look, I see things moving towards order.   Writing this
post is putting things into order.   Making my bed puts things in
order.   Putting my book on the shelf puts things in order.

How can you say that every visible phenomena on the planet does the
what is counter to what I observe?    Don't you observe things coming
to order?

Of course, putting things in order does require energy.   My bookcase
requires outside energy to become more ordered.    But the planet
Earth is receiving outside energy.    (That may be God - or it may
just be the sun)
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Thu, 17 Jan 2008 17:56:45 -0700, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> order.   Putting my book on the shelf puts things in order.

But if things weren't moving from order to disorder, would you *need* to 
make your bed, or put books on a shelf?  Just because an isolated 
incident increases order on a small scale doesn't mean the big picture 
isn't winding down.

> How can you say that every visible phenomena on the planet does the
> what is counter to what I observe?    Don't you observe things coming
> to order?

I observe a constant struggle between humans and nature to maintain 
"order" - which, interestingly, is quite unnatural.  Grass doesn't grow 
itself in a single, 2" pattern across a lawn, then stop growing in 
observance of the walkway.  We have to mow it and trim it for it to look 
like that.

I have a 2-year-old - our house is *always* moving to disorder!
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Sun, 20 Jan 2008 16:07:26 -0700, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> with what we see.   

You must have more faith than I, then.  I simply cannot believe that the 
*ordered* complexity that I see in the world just "happened" to be that 
way.  Sure, you could "happen" to throw a deck of cards in the air and 
have it land with the cards in order and suit.  But, the fun thing about 
odds is that every time you do it, the odds are the same.  And for the 
life of me, I cannot understand how someone ridicules *my* faith, then 
in the next breath talks about how this world just "happened" to be.
```

On Thu, 24 Jan 2008 20:43:58 -0700, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>> Theory says that the universe is winding down, but any argument that
>> says a small part of the universe cannot become ordered doesn't agree
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the next breath talks about how this world just "happened" to be.

No, I didn't throw it up in the air.   I sorted the cards by hand. And
sure enough they got ordered.    It didn't just "happen to be", it
took an input of energy.

You may argue that the universe itself is becoming ordered by input of
energy from outside of it (God).    That's reasonable.   I haven't
observed the whole universe to test this theory though.

But to claim that nothing becomes more ordered as the basis of any
discussion is contrary to observation.    Some things do become more
ordered.    Credit this to God if you wish, but don't claim it doesn't
happen.
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:6zwjj.54677$_m.48824@bignews4.bellsouth.net...
> <Alistair.J.L.Maclean@googlemail.com> wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> And your objective proof of this is...

No demon has been identified or named; no demon has manifested itself in any 
way that could ONLY be the effect of a demon (and not caused by habitual 
drupg abuse), and the symptoms described are part of a well understood 
pattern of mental illness which requires no more complex explanation. (Your 
good buddy: William of Ockham... but of course, you only apply his teaching 
when it suits you... :-))

>
>> Aside from the debatable point that
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> people from the 1970s that ulcers were caused by bacteria.

Simlar things may have been said, but that is where the similarity ends. 
There is no similarity between describing physical, quantifiable phenomena 
like bacteria, and non-physical, unquantifiable things like demonic 
possession, so the argument is lost.


>In the '90s
> my dad, who had suffered from ulcers for decades was cured by
> taking 4 antibiotic pills. If the demons are real, it isn't "quackery".

But they are not, so it is.

>Do
> you have proof they aren't?

It is not incumbent on us to provide proof they don't exist. It is incumbent 
on you to provide proof that they DO.

So far what you have provided can be explained by normal natural science.

>Do you thing demons are stranger than
> some of the things coming out of quantum mechanics?

There are indeed some strange effects in Quantum Mechanics, but they are 
only strange in the context of the world we perceive and live in (which is 
NOT the Quantum world). The fact that science is currently investigating 
some very complex and strange areas of the Universe, (and the knowledge 
being gathered is increasing logarithmically), does not open a cornucopia 
for every crackpot, weird, way out, drug besotted, moronic theory to be 
located there.

Mediaeval superstition is best left in the Middle Ages.



>There are some
> very strange things in this universe, and we've probably barely scratched
> the surface.
>

Notwithstanding Hamlet's sage injunction to Horatio, it is very likely that 
the things as yet undreamt of, will turn out to be explicable by rational 
and logical processes rather than irrational and illogical ones.  I know 
which horse I'd rather back...


>>> I've discussed this
>>> with several practicing clinical psychiatrists and psychologists who 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> clinical practice makes that certain. Right.

You don't need a Doctorate or extensive clinical practise to see that a man 
holding and teaching such beliefs should not be doing so. He probably does a 
lot more good than harm in the other areas of his practice, and he is 
pprobably sensible enough to realise that he should not raise his profile 
regarding his occult beliefs. For those reasons he is still in practise.

Using your reasoning, if he honestly believes in demonic possession, why 
hasn't he published a paper, backed by case studies, on it? Then he could 
see if other physicians have encountered the same phenomena, with only the 
same possible explanation?.

He doesn't, because he knows full well, there is no solid evidence, the view 
is subjective, the clinical establishment would laugh him out of court, 
before having him struck off.

Yet you expect us to accept this man's unsupported opinion as evidence for 
occult phenomena, when there really is no irrefutable or even repeatable or 
measurable evidence for this.

And then you challenge us to prove it isn't so... :-)

Get real.

Pete.
```

>

Not so.  If you make an assertion it's up to you to prove it, not up to
someone else to disprove it (although if they can they certainly ought to).
That someone can't refute you doesn't mean you're right.  And if you fall
back on the old argument that it's impossible to prove that something DOES
NOT exist (which has always struck me as intellectual laziness and
unscientific), then set to and prove that it CANNOT exist.  Or do a reductio
ad absurdum.

PL
```

> On 15 Jan, 13:49, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>> the occult. Primarily that it's real and it's dangerous.

Given that they all sunbscribed to it, it would be surprising if they didn't 
all share this conclusion. By definition, if they didn't believe in the 
occult, then they wouldn't be occultists.

That doesn't make it real or dangerous.

They are deluded, and may change their views in later life. Many occultists 
(you're not the only one who has firends or acquaintances who dabble in this 
rubbish) do so in response to much more basic, real and primitive drives 
than possession by so-called demons. It may be rebellion against authority, 
the Church, parents or family, or simply the chance to get some titillation 
and gratuitous sex...

The point is that there are logical explanations which are much more likely 
than the the postuation that the occult is real or dangerous.

However, if it weren't for the occult, Hollywood would be deprived of a 
source of serious revenue... :-)

>>
>> Further, there are certain "psychological" disorders that are thought by 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

Amen!

>

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

knowledge should not be allowed to do so if they are preaching unscientific 
nonsense, based on their personal delusions. It is quite disturbing to think 
that this is happening in the USA, in the 21st century.

It needs to be jumped on early, and hard.

Pete.

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> forever.   Too bad they weren't born in the Right families.

The Bible says that those who have not heard God's word will be
judged accordingly. Also, Jesus said that the people hearing Him
had guilt because they saw and heard Him, but did not believe.
The Bible clearly teaches that people are judged according to the
light they are given. You try to portray God as some oblivious and
uncaring force that has no compassion, a powerful nitwit. That is
simply your own petty view. The God portrayed in the Bible, the
God known by millions of Christians, is a wise and compassionate
God, Who does not desire that anyone should "perish". Jesus
willingly took our punishment, out of love. Have you ever shown
that kind of love, Howard, to voluntarily suffer death and torture
to save someone else? Yet you twist it unrecognizably in order to
assign blame to God.
```

On Tue, 15 Jan 2008 10:56:05 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>The Bible says that those who have not heard God's word will be
>judged accordingly. Also, Jesus said that the people hearing Him
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>assign blame to God.

Not every Christian believes in an omnipotent being who allows every
non-believer to be tortured beyond all understanding forever and ever
without hope of parole.    In fact fewer and fewer do.   But the
number of such believers is significant.

And not every Christian believe that He would allow even one person to
be so tortured.    Some believe non-believers will mercifully be
allowed to die.    Still, the number of people who believe that He
would allow such is significant.

What you or any other Christian believes is a minority belief of what
all Christians believe.  

Still, the "belief" requirement tends to be very similar to saying "My
kind of people will be rewarded for thinking the way I think", which
seems to be human nature.

In real life there are lots of levels of belief and of opportunity.
```

Christians don't define how Christianity should work; God does. It is
clear that, since the first century, there have been times when there were
lots of people following Christ, and times when there were few. Looking
at the Middle Ages, it's difficult to see many true Christians at all. After
some revivals during the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number
of Christians grew dramatically. Right now, and for several decades,
the church has been doing a pretty crummy job. I don't have a single
Christian friend who wouldn't say the same thing. Many contemporary
Christians have speculated this might be the "falling away" mentioned
in 2 Thessalonians 2.

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> So how do you know your version of Christianity is God's?

From study of God's Word, the Bible, and listening to the direction of
the Holy Spirit. That is the only standard.

>   Others
> have been absolutely sure that their version was Right.   They backed
> their decisions by careful readings of the scriptures but came to
> different conclusions.   And now you believe they weren't True
> Christians.

There are areas of disagreement between equally dedicated Christians,
to be sure. But when such disagreements involve more than relatively
trivial matters of doctrine, one or both has departed from the Bible.
This usually appears in a couple of ways. One is putting some Bible
passages above others, without reconciling them as one whole truth.
If God is Who the Bible declares Him to be, then He won't contradict
Himself. Anytime someone looks at two passages as contradictory,
they haven't yet understood them. The other is putting human judgment
equal or above God's Word. Jesus took the Pharisees to task for this.
An example of this is when Galileo was persecuted by the Roman
Church for heresy when he merely said he saw mountains on the Moon.
Such a statement in no way contradicts anything in the Bible! But
human church authorities decided "God did not make anything that
wasn't 'perfect', so what Galileo says is heresy." One can only suppose
the nitwits never looked at the moon and saw the varying shades of
brightness, clearly indication the Moon isn't a billiard ball. This is what
happens when humans supplant God's Word with their own thinking,
even if they do it "in the name of" God. The visible organization called
"the church" was created by man, not God. The real Church is not
visible, but the collection of all true believers in Christ.

Many people have become disgusted with Christianity, because of
experiences they've had with church organizations and human doctrines.
I am equally disgusted with many of them myself.
```

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:6f8jj.44477$L27.14090@bignews9.bellsouth.net...

> the church has been doing a pretty crummy job. I don't have a single
> Christian friend who wouldn't say the same thing. Many contemporary
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

2:2 or 2:3?

Doesn't the Bible also say somewhere that no man shall know when the Lord is
coming?

Speculation on this topic, as you very well know, Judson, has been going on
for centuries and has been completely futile. Just for interest (I was
staying with my evangelical-church brother whose library has virtually
nothing but Christian works) I read a book by Hal Lindsay - can't remember
the exact title but it's to do with the coming Armageddon based on the
Revelations - his idea is that St. John time-travelled to the 21st century
AD - right now! and saw an atomic war in progress, then went back to his own
time and wrote down what he could remember.  Lindsay says he has been
studying the Bible in a Spirit-believing way for 40 years or more.  His
conclusion is that Jesus will arrive in the next 50 years or so.  Another
one is "Cracking the Apocalypse Code" by Gerard Bodson - by using the Kabala
and gemiatric methods he concludes that it will happen in 2043, no sooner,
no later.  The book is almost completely a waste of paper.

Now either of these guys may be right, or "many contemporary Christians" may
be right; but the likelihood is low, based on results so far.  What is
important is that all these people regard themselves as good Christians and
all of them are using the same book as their authority.  And you may be
right, Judson - that the Bible is beyond human understanding - which would
explain why these people come up with such different conclusions.  To me
that implies that there's no use trying to predict the future based on the
Bible.

It would seem to me to be more worth while to try to live a good life as
defined by the golden rule than to try to justify it by recourse to such an
authority.

PL
```

"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2:2 or 2:3?

The "falling away" is mentioned in 2 Thessalonians 2:3: "Let no one deceive you
by any means; for that Day will not come unless the falling away comes first,
and the man of sin is revealed, the son of perdition..."

Note: That Day refers to the Tribulation Period immediately before the Second
Coming of Christ, and the man of sin refers to the Antichrist, a charasmatic
world leader who will claim to be God.

> Doesn't the Bible also say somewhere that no man shall know when the Lord is
> coming?

Jesus Himself said "no man shall know the day or the hour." He also chastized
the Pharisees for not recognizing the seasons (e.g. the time when Christ came).

(Aside: About 10 years ago Jewish Hebrew scholors translating first century
manuscripts discovered that the expression "No man shall know the day or the
hour" was a first century idiom for the Jewish feast Rosh Hashanah. This was
likely because, unlike any other Jewish feast, the date technically wasn't
known until the day it started, and also unlike other Jewish feasts, it lasts
two days. So, Jesus could have been saying His return would be on some
Rosh Hashanah. In His life, Jesus fulfilled all the Spring Feasts on the very
day. Rosh Hashanah is a Fall Feast. When you study the Jewish feasts, the
parallels between Rosh Hashanah and Christ's Second Coming are significant.
Also, Rosh Hashanah is announced by the "last trump" of 30 trumpet blasts
commemorating the previous 30 days.)

> Speculation on this topic, as you very well know, Judson, has been going on
> for centuries and has been completely futile. Just for interest (I was
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bible.

I have spent many years studing prophetic Scripture, and I agree, trying to
set dates for the Second Coming is not likely to be productive. What I believe
the Bible teaches is that God's people should be discerning of the times in
which they live. Like most contemporary Christians who are serious about Bible
study, I believe it is very likely that we are living in the last generation
before Christ's Return. The main reason for this is the regathering of Israel,
prophesied by Moses 3500 years ago. The event is unparallelled in history. An
ethnic people was dispersed through the world, yet retaind their ethnic and
national identity for most of 2,000 years, then regathered, reestablishing a
sovreign nation, even their original language, as Moses prophesied. Absolutely
astonishing. This event, more than any other, seems to mark these times as the
very last days before the return of Christ. However, every generation since
Christ has thought He would return in their lifetimes, so it's not wise to be
dogmatic. The "falling away" I mentioned is only said to happen before the
Second Coming and the appearance of the Antichrist. The Bible doesn't say how
long before.
```

<Alistair.J.L.Maclean@googlemail.com> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>on us?

Because it is my wish that you would also have forgiveness for your
sins. I do not want to see you or anyone else be thrown into the lake
fire for eternity. If I were drowning near you, and you had a life
saver, would you throw it to me? I believe you would. Why would I not
want to see you saved from a much worse fate than drowning? The fact
that you and others see that attitude as bad says a lot about what's
in your hearts, and why forgiveness is necessary. God sent Jesus to
save people from that horrible fate. The condemnation comes from
rejecting that loving sacrifice. What would you say if Christians,
having received such a precious free gift from God, keept it from you
and failed to tell you about it? Worse than not throwing a life saver
to a drowning person. But there's no cooercion, no force, no "jihad"
if you don't accept it.

You apparently hate even the concept of God because of how people have
dealt with you. As the Bibls says, the human heart is deceitful and
wicked. But instead of blaming people for not obeying God and being
completely honest with you and good to you, you blame God. Jesus died
on a cruel Roman cross *for you*, just as much as He did for any other
human. Nobody ever showed as much love for you as Jesus has. He made
it possible for you to be reconciled with God, to live in eternal
freedom and joy in God's presence. All you have to do is accept it.
But you don't have to, you won't be forced to, because God gave you
free will. :-)

----- Original Message -----
From: Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
> You apparently hate even the concept of God because of how people have
> dealt with you. As the Bibls says, the human heart is deceitful and
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't see how you can put such unquestioning trust in God.  We KNOW he can
be deceitful.  Consider the story of Abraham and Isaac: if Abraham knew that
God would relent at the last instant and release him from having to offer
his son Isaac as a burnt offering, the story has no meaning at all: Abraham
must have absolutely sure that he would have to do it.  Therefore God had to
deceive him.

We also KNOW that God is capable of "heads I win tails you lose" behaviour.
Read the account in Exodus and you'll find over and over that Pharoah gave
in but God hardened his heart and made him refuse again (Exodus 10:1: And
the Lord said unto Moses, go in unto Pharoah: for I have hardened his heart
and the heart of his servants, that I  might shew these my signs before
him).  Now what's the point of making someone do something then punishing
them for it?  Especially after they've agreed voluntarily to do what you
want?

PL
```

tlmfru wrote:
> 
> I don't see how you can put such unquestioning trust in God.  We KNOW he can
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> deceive him.

God didn't deceive him - He asked him to do something, and then stopped 
him before he got through with it.  It was a test of faith - Isaac was 
the son promised to Abraham and borne of his wife when she was well past 
childbearing age.  Did Abraham have enough faith to believe that, if God 
told him to sacrifice this promised son, that He would provide another, 
so as to fulfill the promise he had been given that his seed would be as 
numerous as the stars?

It had nothing to do with deceit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>> tlmfru wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> However, given that God knew what the outcome would be anyway, was such a test necessary?

When God "tests" people, it isn't for His benefit, but for people. God
was establishing, for everyone who came after, how strong Abraham's
faith was. A central doctrine of the Bible is that God "accounted"
righteousness to Abraham because of his faith. It was important for his
faith to be documented by such a test. It would hardly make sense for
the Bible to stake so much on Abraham's faith, then not record anything
significant later generations could see to know how strong his faith was.
Apparently, the most important thing God is looking for in people is
their willingness to trust Him. It's not an easy thing.
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<5vch9dF1m6g8nU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <s_ahj.7519$q36.2315@newsfe14.lga> <C7bhj.69398$L%6.15737@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6cb69101-c093-4bcf-92db-63a48c570d3c@l32g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <8offo31afaa6brlaleceh07cnrgus3dft9@4ax.com> <k1Qhj.51338$N67.8724@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <tctmo31b0g4irm0qrr1v1g63efepkkm83u@4ax.com> <DnMij.50469$Mu4.455@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <giopo3tac3o2p7nj9hqc15kvat0t2drdts@4ax.com> <zA5jj.44409$L27.15584@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <8223b353-670d-41ac-9d97-078e16a0007c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <mayjj.23822$m6.23695@newsfe18.lga> <frCdnX5Mgf8efhPanZ2dnUVZ_oimnZ2d@comcast.com> <5vch9dF1m6g8nU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:frCdnX5Mgf8efhPanZ2dnUVZ_oimnZ2d@comcast.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> I think that each kid has value.

I do too - but I'm not Abraham.  :)  In fact, today is "Sanctity of 
Human Life Sunday" here (well, probably world-wide at Southern Baptist 
churches everywhere).  True, this request was very outside the normal 
request that God would make (in the Bible, God *never* required human 
sacrifice), but it did come from Him nonetheless.

I would have trouble making such a decision today, simply because God 
doesn't come speak to me in an audible voice.  I'd probably think that I 
was being deceived.  :)

>> It had nothing to do with deceit.
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> if you did?  Who is insecure here?

The important part wasn't the outcome, it was the process.  God didn't 
need to test Abraham, but Abraham needed to go through that trial, to 
strengthen *his* (Abraham's) faith.  It's easy to say "Sure, God, I'll 
give my life for you!"  It's quite another thing to be faced with the 
reality.

Think of it like physical exercise.  Your doctor may tell you to walk 
for 20 minutes a day.  As far as the doctor is concerned, *he* gets no 
benefit from your walking - from a professional standpoint, he could 
probably care less.  (From a personal standpoint, you'd hope that he 
cared, of course.)  But, by you walking 20 minutes a day, *you* get the 
benefit.

Another benefit from this was increased confidence.  You can think ahead 
and decide some things, but until you're actually faced with making a 
tough decision, you can't say with 100% certainty what you would do. 
Going through this gave Abraham the confidence he would need later in 
his life.

God may be demanding at times, but He's not insecure.  :)
```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:ucudnRm8_ufjRA7anZ2dnUVZ_rSrnZ2d@comcast.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> it did come from Him nonetheless.

Daniel, I read all of this response and it is good and well reasoned.  Are 
you quite sure that God has never required Human sacrifice? What about the 
false prophets? Adulterers (stoned to death)... you can argue that these are 
not "sacrifices" per se but punishments for misdeeds. Nevertheless, I'm not 
convinced that God has not REQUIRED the death of Human Beings on certain 
occasions.

(I'm probably a lot softer on "punishment" than people were 3500 years 
ago...:-))
>
> I would have trouble making such a decision today, simply because God 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> The important part wasn't the outcome, it was the process.

While I understand the point you're making here, I still can't see that such 
an awful test was necessary as a "confidence builder" :-) It is one Hell of 
a way to motivate somebody... :-)


>God didn't need to test Abraham, but Abraham needed to go through that 
>trial, to strengthen *his* (Abraham's) faith.

Why? Why was it necessary for Abraham to have such "faith"? Why does God 
require such "faith"?


>It's easy to say "Sure, God, I'll give my life for you!"  It's quite 
>another thing to be faced with the reality.

Agreed. But most of us would make no such promise if we really didn't mean 
it...

>
> Think of it like physical exercise.  Your doctor may tell you to walk for 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> But, by you walking 20 minutes a day, *you* get the benefit.

That's not the same thing at all, but I catch your drift and will let it 
pass... :-)
>
> Another benefit from this was increased confidence.  You can think ahead 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> God may be demanding at times, but He's not insecure.  :)

You made a good case. No further cmment at this point... :-)

Pete.
```

calculated by other events. Sarah was 90 when she gave birth to Isaac, and 
127 when she died. Her death coincided with the putative sacrifice of Isaac.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

- **References:** `<19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6cb69101-c093-4bcf-92db-63a48c570d3c@l32g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <8offo31afaa6brlaleceh07cnrgus3dft9@4ax.com> <k1Qhj.51338$N67.8724@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <tctmo31b0g4irm0qrr1v1g63efepkkm83u@4ax.com> <DnMij.50469$Mu4.455@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <giopo3tac3o2p7nj9hqc15kvat0t2drdts@4ax.com> <zA5jj.44409$L27.15584@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <8223b353-670d-41ac-9d97-078e16a0007c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <mayjj.23822$m6.23695@newsfe18.lga> <frCdnX5Mgf8efhPanZ2dnUVZ_oimnZ2d@comcast.com> <5vch9dF1m6g8nU1@mid.individual.net> <ucudnRm8_ufjRA7anZ2dnUVZ_rSrnZ2d@comcast.com> <06a8a738-9358-4539-9384-112ffccdeab3@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13p9cf3r2k0ff9a@corp.supernews.com> <566664b8-5090-424d-ab9e-ecda626b1fb9@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:566664b8-5090-424d-ab9e-ecda626b1fb9@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com...
> On 21 Jan, 14:53, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
>> Alistair wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> I guess there must be some reward (longevity) for blind faith.

Faith does not have to be blind.  Jesus was not against giving evidence for 
faith.  He did not say anything against Thomas for not believing until he 
could feel the nail holes etc.  Of course not everyone could experience this 
and thus they had to accept it or not accept it  on the basis of faith.  I 
believe the left brain wants evidence before believing and the right brain 
(emotion) more readily accepts things that align with emotion.  I want to 
believe that I will be reunited with my loved ones so that emotion makes me 
want to believe in that.  It makes me feel better.  If my faith is misplaced 
I do not see what harms it does, and in fact it can have a great positive 
effect on my mental outlook.  However I do think that in other cases I need 
to be wary of this kind of faith as it can mislead me and lead to hurt.  How 
many people want to believe in their partner's fidelity so much that they 
are blind to evidence of infidelity? How many preachers and/or manipulators 
use information about peoples deep emotional desires to  obtain followers or 
to extract money etc.?

As far as my left brain has been able to acertain there is no scientific 
evidence that proves that God exists.  I wish that there was such evidence. 
I do not understand why God does not provide us that evidence that 
certainty. There are a lot of things such as the beauty of nature etc. that 
many accept as evidence but it is not to the level of proof required by the 
scientific method.  Such evidence is always subject to interpretation by the 
individual and that interpretation is greatly influenced by emotion and 
emotion can easily mislead.  In the Old Testament God spoke directly to some 
but it doesen't seem to occur at all now.  It would be nice if we all could 
get an inspiring lecture from Him from time to time that gave us some real 
proof.  I want to believe that there is a loving God that cares for me and 
for us all, but I also need to be aware of how that could leave me open to 
possible manipulation by people with an agenda.  If I have a belief that is 
not proven as fact, then there is always some probablity that it could be 
wrong.  If there is new evidence that contradicts my belief then I need to 
be willing to reevaluate that belief.  I need to know how certain I am about 
each belief.  I need to know what I believe and why I believe it.  If  I do 
not really understand these things about my beliefs then I am more open to 
manipulation.  If I do understand them then I am less likely to fall for the 
72 virgins scam and I am less likely to follow a leader who wants me to 
murder or to do something else incompatible with my beliefs.
```

```


"I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13pd7071501q989@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> have a great positive effect on my mental outlook.

That's a very important point and one of the reasons I don't go through life 
expressing my personal belief to all and sundry (except in this forum, where 
I'm really just defending what I believe against bigotry, and anyway, it 
wasn't I who started the religious posts... :-))

The only saving grace I can see in religious faith, is that it CAN bring 
great comfort to many people and can definitely help in hard times. Like you 
say, no harm is done... BUT, this is only true at a personal level. Once the 
"organization" comes in with the dogmatic party line about what will and 
will not be believed, (and any questioning is dismissed as the work of 
Satan) things start to deteriorate, and if this progresses to the point 
where anyone not in the group is sinning against God and must be punished, 
then it becomes dangerous.

I see no need to evangelize atheism; the people capable of having it will 
embrace it anyway, and those who can't, won't. I'm OK with this; the number 
of people embracing or rejecting it has no influence whatsoever on what I 
believe.

I respect totally your right to your personal belief, Charles, and I would 
defend that right, whether I agree with you or not.

> However I do think that in other cases I need to be wary of this kind of 
> faith as it can mislead me and lead to hurt.  How many people want to 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to extract money etc.?

It's certainly true that humans have demonstrated a great propensity ot 
believe what we want to believe... :-)
>
> As far as my left brain has been able to acertain there is no scientific 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> now.

I suspect He found such conversations boring and gave up on them... :-)

>It would be nice if we all could get an inspiring lecture from Him from 
>time to time that gave us some real proof.

A lecture? Why not a conversation?

Many religious people would counter that God speaks to them every day... On 
the corner of my street there is a Salvation Army church (I think they do a 
great job and support them as much as I can) and they have a little board 
outside the church designed to catch the eye of people passing down the main 
road. I am always amused at the little aphorisms they post there and 
mentally formulate my own rejoinders to them. "If you feel detached from 
God, who moved?"  (that would be all us retinas... :-) no wonder the 
faithful aren't seeing so well...). Point is that one of the signs of a 
"healthy" relationship with God, is a feeling of closeness and ability to 
converse with Him. (there is a name for such a state in Pyschology; it's 
called "delusional"... :-))

> I want to believe that there is a loving God that cares for me and for us 
> all, but I also need to be aware of how that could leave me open to 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> about each belief.  I need to know what I believe and why I believe it.

You summed it up perfectly in that last sentence. That is the very nub of 
"belief". Even attempting to come to grips with that can make you feel 
better. You may never get completely satisfactory answers, but knowing you 
gave it your best shot, provides a degree of satisfaction. The alternative 
is to accept a complete set of someone else's answers, by "faith". For an 
enquiring mind, that it is not an attractive option. :-)

>If  I do not really understand these things about my beliefs then I am more 
>open to manipulation.  If I do understand them then I am less likely to 
>fall for the 72 virgins scam and I am less likely to follow a leader who 
>wants me to murder or to do something else incompatible with my beliefs.

From reading the above, I reckon you're in very good shape, Charlie... :-)

Pete.
```

>
> I too have had unpleasant experiences with organized religion.  They use guilt and other things to manipulate people for various 
> purposes especially to get money or obtain follwers.

Same here. Jesus Christ created the Church, but men created church
organizations. I believe the problem is not organization per se, but that
with organization come positions of authority, and people are drawn to
them for reasons having nothing to do with God. The Church has
always been at it's purest and best when it is under persecution. I think
the reason is that there is no reason to be involved then unless you're
the "real deal," to the point of being willing to suffer for it. Persecution
drives away the chaff, leaving the wheat.

>Christ did not create any church. That was the work of the disciples.

Not according to the words of Jesus in Matthew 16:18. If the Church
were created by humans, it would be no greater than any other purely
human work. Humans are the "living stones" used by Jesus, but Jesus
builds and maintains the Church, and God's Holy Spirit breathes life
into it. The Church is not visible to the eye; it is the collection of true
believers in Jesus Christ, and man did not create or build it, any more
than bricks "build" a house.

If you're talking about church demoninations, then yes, those are
created by men, which is one of the big problems with them. But when
you see the word "church" in the Bible, it is not talking about human
church denominations, but the collection of true believers above. There
is no Biblical basis for human sectarian churchs; the reverse in fact (see
1 Corinthians 3).
```

- **References:** `<19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6cb69101-c093-4bcf-92db-63a48c570d3c@l32g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <8offo31afaa6brlaleceh07cnrgus3dft9@4ax.com> <k1Qhj.51338$N67.8724@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <tctmo31b0g4irm0qrr1v1g63efepkkm83u@4ax.com> <DnMij.50469$Mu4.455@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <giopo3tac3o2p7nj9hqc15kvat0t2drdts@4ax.com> <zA5jj.44409$L27.15584@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <8223b353-670d-41ac-9d97-078e16a0007c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <mayjj.23822$m6.23695@newsfe18.lga> <frCdnX5Mgf8efhPanZ2dnUVZ_oimnZ2d@comcast.com> <5vch9dF1m6g8nU1@mid.individual.net> <ucudnRm8_ufjRA7anZ2dnUVZ_rSrnZ2d@comcast.com> <06a8a738-9358-4539-9384-112ffccdeab3@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13p9cf3r2k0ff9a@corp.supernews.com> <566664b8-5090-424d-ab9e-ecda626b1fb9@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13pd7071501q989@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
>
> Faith does not have to be blind.  Jesus was not against giving evidence for faith.  He did not say anything against Thomas for not 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> with my beliefs.

I enjoyed reading your post Charles. :-)

If you examine the Bible account of the times when God made His presence
obvious, it did not produce faith, love for God, or obedience. During the 40
years Israel followed Moses in the Sinai desert, God's presence was visible
and undeniable. They got into the desert by walking across the dry bottom of
the Red Sea, seeing the water heaped up on both sides like walls, and watched
as the water cascaded down on the Egyptian army, killing them to the last man
and horse. Every single day, except the sabbath, they picked up food (manna)
from the ground. Manna spoiled if kept overnight, except when kept overnight
for the sabbath. At any time they could look at the sky and see God's presence
in the form of a protective cloud by day and a glowing cloud by night. When
the cloud moved, they moved. Often they saw God come in to Moses' tent in the
form of a cloud, and Moses came out glowing like a space alien, which scared
everybody so much they asked Moses to wear a bag over his head. There were
also many other visible evidences, like the Shekinah Glory, which the people
saw go into the Tabernacle. On the occasions when the high priest entered the
Holy of Holies, they always tied a rope around his leg, because if he went in
there without proper sacrifices, they had to drag his dead body out with the
rope. When moving the Arc of the Covenant, if anyone touched it, even by
accident, they were instantly struck dead. There were absolutely no athiests
among those people in those days; they *knew* God was real. But during that
time, the people constantly complained and there were (IIRC) ten rebellions.
There is something perverse in the human heart that, when it does not want to
believe, it will ignore or "explain away" any evidence, and believe what it
wants.

Jesus said the most important thing is to love God with everything you have
(Matt 22:37). Apparently, when God makes His presence visible and obvious it
just doesn't produce that in rebellious humanity. I suspect that is one reason
why God does not make Himself more visibly obvious. I also suspect that those
40 years when God was clearly visible were (in part) to demonstrate that fact.
```

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
