- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2Slj.78806$L%6.56686@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <8offo31afaa6brlaleceh07cnrgus3dft9@4ax.com> <k1Qhj.51338$N67.8724@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <tctmo31b0g4irm0qrr1v1g63efepkkm83u@4ax.com> <DnMij.50469$Mu4.455@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <giopo3tac3o2p7nj9hqc15kvat0t2drdts@4ax.com> <zA5jj.44409$L27.15584@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <8223b353-670d-41ac-9d97-078e16a0007c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <mayjj.23822$m6.23695@newsfe18.lga> <frCdnX5Mgf8efhPanZ2dnUVZ_oimnZ2d@comcast.com> <5vch9dF1m6g8nU1@mid.individual.net> <ucudnRm8_ufjRA7anZ2dnUVZ_rSrnZ2d@comcast.com> <06a8a738-9358-4539-9384-112ffccdeab3@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13p9cf3r2k0ff9a@corp.supernews.com> <566664b8-5090-424d-ab9e-ecda626b1fb9@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13pd7071501q989@corp.supernews.com> <LuJlj.78555$L%6.52619@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <q5Qlj.10318$M24.4790@newsfe17.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> places they'd already seen.

They weren't lost. Where did you get that idea? They probably knew where
they were all the time. What happened was (see Numbers, chapters 13 & 14),
about six months after they left Egypt, God brought them to a place called
Kadesh, near Canan the "Promised Land," prepratory to entering there. They
first sent in people to scout out the land. When the scouts returned, they
all gave glowing reports of the richness of the land. But all the scouts
except two said the people were very large and too powerful to conquer.
Because of this, the people refused to enter Canan. God said (very loose
paraphrase) "Okay, but you will wander in the desert until every person
here who refused to enter Canan is dead." That they did, and it took 40
years. All that time they stayed under the visible cloud God provided.
When the cloud moved, they moved; when the cloud stayed, they stayed. So
God determined when they (following the cloud) finally got there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

```
There was a missionary to the natives in what would become Canada, named
Jean-Paul Brebeuf.  He was captured and tortured by one group - the Hurons,
I think, but that's not important;  nice things like having a red-hot knife
thrust through his foot.  After he got away or was released, he decided that
God had been punishing him (Brebeuf) for not loving God enough, and
professed himself pleased with the experience and the lesson and loved God
even more.  He was later killed by the natives.  Apparently he was an
arrogant sod.

The point here is that being convinced of something past the point of
reason, clinging to it at all costs, leads to extreme irrationality if not
madness.  "God may be demanding ... " sounds to me like that sort of
thought.

Frank Yerby, in the notes to his novel "Judas my brother" (a marvellous
story if you can withstand the emotionally exhausting way in which it's
written) comments that theology seems to him to be a form of insanity.  I'd
say he's on to something.

PL


Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:06a8a738-9358-4539-9384-112ffccdeab3@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com...
On 20 Jan, 23:30, LX-i <lxi0...@netscape.net> wrote:

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Or maybe god is just a big tease?
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Just the reverse. The human brain is wired to search for cause-and-effect. 
Were it not, humans would have become extinct a very long time ago.

comes after...). To NOT search for these things is evidence sufficient of 
disease or defect.
```

```
On 17 Jan, 00:10, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> <Alistair.J.L.Macl...@googlemail.com> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> sins.

I have no sin. What does it look like? Can I use it to buy food? Sin
is an outmoded religious concept that has no place in the modern world
except where religious figureheads wish to justify their evil
practices.

> I do not want to see you or anyone else be thrown into the lake
> fire for eternity.

I won't be. Nor will I gain 70 virgins.

> If I were drowning near you, and you had a life
> saver, would you throw it to me?

No. I would be in the water too and I'm not a strong swimmer.

> I believe you would. Why would I not
> want to see you saved from a much worse fate than drowning?

Don't my personal human rights come in to this? Don't I have a right
not to be saved? Why is it that religious groups spend a lot of their
time trying to save souls (another outmoded concept)? Leave people be.

> The fact
> that you and others see that attitude as bad says a lot about what's
> in your hearts, and why forgiveness is necessary.

Stupid first then bad. Also outmoded.

> God sent Jesus to
> save people from that horrible fate. The condemnation comes from
> rejecting that loving sacrifice. What would you say if Christians,
> having received such a precious free gift from God, keept it from you
> and failed to tell you about it?

Nothing more than I would say to the Mormons, Jehovahs, Amish, ....

> Worse than not throwing a life saver
> to a drowning person. But there's no cooercion, no force, no "jihad"
> if you don't accept it.

Coercion and force may be subtle: you'll roast on a spit in hell for
all eternity if you don't believe....

>
> You apparently hate even the concept of God because of how people have
> dealt with you.

Nothing to do with other people and how I have been treated. I hate
the concept of god because I see no evidence for it's veracity but I
see a lot of evidence for how that concept (in one or another of the
many variants of the gods delusion) has caused misery and hate and
been used to justify actions that no sane human would normally
countenance.

> As the Bibls says, the human heart is deceitful and
> wicked. But instead of blaming people for not obeying God and being
> completely honest with you and good to you, you blame God.

There is no god. There is a delusion. If I were a tad more cynical I
would probably suggest that there is a conspiracy by the Illuminati to
keep people believing in god so that they will be more easily ruled.
Certainly, up to the end of the second world war the god delusion held
the masses in check.

> Jesus died
> on a cruel Roman cross *for you*,

Did I ask him to do so? No. Then why should I be grateful that a self-
deluding Jew with a megalomaniacal attitude deliberately placed
himself in such a place and time as to be arrested, tortured and
murdered by the Romans? The man wanted fame. How else could his
actions be justified. That is a rhetorical question, I do not require
an answer.

> just as much as He did for any other
> human. Nobody ever showed as much love for you as Jesus has. He made
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> free will. :-)

Bunkum. Sorry Judson but this is tosh.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net...
> <Alistair.J.L.Maclean@googlemail.com> wrote:
>>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> free will. :-)

I believe you are doing what you are doing here from pure and decent 
motives, Judson.

That doesn't mean I won't jump on it when I consider it becomes dangerous, 
or simply tiresome, but, I do accept you are not one of the bad guys, and 
your intentions are generous...

I have a number of Christian friends who have expressed concern for my 
mortal soul and they do so out of kindness.  (Some of them even pray for me; 
I don't believe it has done me any harm :-))

For me the problems with your argument are the ambivalences in it, the 
fundamental position that people are evil (I believe exactly the opposite), 
and the fact that it is based on scripture which is, to say the least, 
"inconsistent", despite your protestations to the contrary.

Personally, I do not "hate even the concept of God" or even the concept of 
Religion.  (Although I confess, I'd be happy if the world got over it and 
proceeded without the need for a "spiritual crutch")

What I hate is bigotry, dogma, superior priests, "elders", or preachers who 
claim the right to interpret scripture because I am not capable of doing so 
myself, and anything Religious that advocates torture or death for not 
believing,  for questioninf, or thinking differently from the "Party Line".

Outside of that, I have no problem with it... :-)

Pete.
```

```
On Fri, 18 Jan 2008 13:44:46 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Sat, 19 Jan 2008 09:11:37 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
><dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>or The Bible or Free Enterprise or their Computer Language.


  Only the individual who has come to terms with his self can have a
  dispassionate attitude toward the world. Once the harmony with the
  self is upset, and a man is impelled to reject, renounce, distrust or
  forget his self, he turns into a highly reactive entity. He cannot
  stand apart, poised and self-sufficient, but has to attach himself
  wholeheartedly to one side or another.

  Faith in a holy cause is to a considerable extent a substitute
  for the lost faith in ourselves.

  A man is likely to mind his own business when it is worth minding.
  When it is not, he takes his mind off his own meaningless affairs by
  minding other people's business. .. In running away from ourselves
  we either fall on our neighbor's shoulder or fly at his throat.

-- Eric Hoffer
```

```
On Jan 12, 6:59 am, "Judson McClendon"

> It is also true that the documentary evidence for Biblical
> events is vastly greater than for any other events of
> comparable antiquity.

While there may be some 'documentary evidence' for many biblical
events that  does not indicate that Jehovah was anything more than a
local warlord that was deified by his followers, as was the style of
the time.

> For example, there is more evidence of Jesus crucifixion and resurrection
> than that Julius Caesar ever existed.

That is complete crap. There is _vastly_ more evidence for Julius
Caesar than the purely annecdotal 'crucifixion and resurrection'.

The several tons of manufactured 'pieces of the true cross' are only
evidence of the gullibility of believers.

> There are vastly more manuscripts,
> and they date from much closer to the actual events,
>  than any other historical events of comparable
> antiquity.

While there are many manuscripts that have been preserved they do not
'prove' divinity in any way.

The Mormons believe that god gave gold tablets to Joseph Smith. The
Rastafarians believe that Rastas was/is god and can point to their
writings which support this. Hindus have their Rigveda which predates
any Jewish writings.
```

```
> vastly greater than for any other events of comparable antiquity. For
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

There is no point in engaging in discussions about the results achieved (or
not) by faith as it satisfies no-one and results only in hardening of
positions.  Therefore I will just challenge you on the above paragraph.  In
a previous long disputation in this ng, you made the same claim; I
challenged you then to substantiate it and you didn't.  So let's try again:
I know of the Bible and the history by Josephus as more-or-less contemporary
writings.  To make such a statement you must know of a source that lists
said documents apart from these two: what is it?  (After all, somebody
responded to my repeated proddings about a list of expanding glaciers;
surely you can do the same!)  This is a part of your explanation that ought
to be subject to fairly objective analysis - so I urge you to give us your
references.

Respectfully,
PL
```

```
On 11 Jan, 17:59, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> Unlike yourself Alistair, I have spent considerable time on both sides of
> this argument.

I, too, have spent considerable time on both sides. I started to
question the dogma of christianity when I was 11 years old but did not
become faithless untill I was at least 18. During that period I spent
some time studying christianity (I was forced to at school and still
despise the system that allows Jews and Muslims to opt out but doesn't
allow the same rights to atheists and agnostics) and other religions
(I rather like Buddhism but just can not bring myself to believe in a
spirit or miniature white elephants). Around the age of 21 I more or
less gave up on religion, seeing it as one of the worlds' forces for
evil but did not declare myself to be an atheist until well after
hitting 40.

> I was 27 years old when I accepted Jesus. I avidly read
> science fiction from a very early age, subscribed to Scientific American
> from the time I was 10, studied physics and math at university, and was
> thoroughly inculcated in the humanism you espouse.

I don't think that I have ever advocated humanism. I consider it an
ugly term. I just think that the world would be a better place if we
ditched superstition (I know people who claim to have seen demons. I
don't know what they had been smoking at the time)

> I had never studied
> the Bible, and like you,

The bible makes perfect sense to me. It contains religious tracts
which detail the perceived history of the world (as seen by people
from around 4000 years ago who could not possibly have been able to
see the world for what it really is). As a historical document it is a
wonderful work; albeit one that is inconsistent (two accounts of
creation, inconsistent genealogies, improper translation (the Red Sea
was the Reed Sea), contradictory accounts of the crucifixion of
Christ, highly selectively edited (see the Agnostic Gospels) and
propaganda). As an historical document it details about three weeks
worth of Christ's life leaving questions as to where he was for the
rest of his life. I presume you know of the Kashmiri tradition that
Christ was in fact a wondering Indian scholar?

> it did not make much sense to me when I read any
> of it. It never made sense to me that Christians would connect verses
> from different parts of the Bible and put them together to make a point.

It is called extrapolation. That is how the injunction to farmers to
not cast seed upon stoney ground can be interpreted by narrow-minded
bigots as an injunction against homosexuals. Humans are great at
twisting stories to suit their own ends.

> What I could not see, what I did not know, and could not realize, is that
> the Bible *cannot* be understood *through the intellect alone*,

To believe the bible, verbatim, requires only blind faith. I have
encountered many people who were committed fundamentalist christians
and they had one thing in common: deeply flawed personalities. I'm
sorry if that hurts.

> any more
> than color can be understood through the intellect alone.

As one who is colour blind and has visual impairments to boot, I have
wondered how colour can be accurately described in a meaningful
fashion to people who perceive only grays. Answer: specify the
wavelength of the light. No other description has any meaning.

> Unless a person
> has color sense, you can describe color to them until your tongue falls
> out, and they *will not get it*. You either can see color, in which case
> explanation is unnecessary, or you can't see it,

An object has form (eg a cube). It's properties and form can be
described and so too can the colour property of an object. If one were
blind such a property would have little meaning but would still allow
categorisation of the object according to the colour property.


> I did not come to Christ because I believed the Bible; I hadn't read much
> of it. I asked Jesus into my life because I had a lot of serious problems
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> them.

I have no doubt but that Muslims will say the same about their
religion. Only one of you can be right. As both religions perceive
much the same it is logical to conclude that neither system is right.

> This Presence is real and tangible to the

self-deluding

> Christian. You can clearly
> sense the presence of the Holy Spirit.

Can it be measured or perceived by others? What properties does it
have? Is your experience the same as others of your faith? ....

I don't need nor do I require answers to these questions. I merely
wish to point out that your experience, although real to you, is
subjective and is no more real to another person as the ability to
perceive colour is to a blind man. I can describe colour with
reference to the real world but your experience is not verifiable.

> And as promised in the Bible, the
> Holy Spirit opens your understanding to the Bible, just like having color
> vision opens color to you.

I have already pointed out that my colour vision is imperfect. Perhaps
your interpretation of the bible and acceptance of Christ's spirit is
similarly marred?

> I began to have an enormous appetite for the
> Bible, and read it avidly every day. I began to see that the Bible really
> is one, integrated, completely consistent work.

You are not paying attention to the writings in this newsgroup.
Inconsistencies in the bible have been documented briefly here but
more information is available elsewhere. The bible is not one
integrated consistent work. It is a collection of scriptures that by
omitting other scriptures (see the Agnostic Gospels) have been brought
together to represent the views of the Roman Catholic church of about
400 ad.

> I remember the astonishment
> I felt when I first realized that I could now *see* why various Scriptures
> could be taken from different places in the Bible to make a single point.

Extrapolation to prove any point you care to make.

> It is because the Bible *is* one whole consistent work, and when the Holy
> Spirit gives one the understanding of it, it isn't arbitrary at all.
> Studying the Scriptures, I can see things myself, that are the same things
> Christians in other parts of the world also see.

There is no astonishment in that. Nazis the world over see the same
consistent message in Mein Kampf.

> It is amazing to meet
> Christians from different cultures; it's like meeting family or dear old
> friends you didn't know you had. Some people, particularly non-believers,
> usually only accent the differences; but the commonality of belief and
> worldview is astonishing.

How many different christian groups are there? Let me count the ways:
Roman Catholic
Greek Orthodox
Russian Orthodox
Anglican
Baptist
Methodist
Anabaptist
Seven Day thingummybobs (sorry can't remember their ending)
Calvinist
Lutheran
Christian Scientists
Plymouth Brethren
ad infinitum ad nauseum.

> Like different paintings by the same painter.
>
> Over time, as I studied the Bible and some Christian history, I learned
> that there are fewer things in the Bible that are criticized as error by
> non-believers today, than in the past.

You mean that archaeologists are able to confirm some of the
historical context of the writings. Confirming a few points (such as
the Battle of Khadesh) does not make much in-road into the host of
inaccuracies found within the bible.

Could you tell me one thing: why do christians go to the wrong
Bethlehem to visit the "manger" wherein christ was allegedly born?

> Over time the Bible is increasingly
> attested by other sources to be correct, not the reverse.

No, some references are confirmed. Other archaeological evidence is
deliberately hidden because it would deny the bible (see the Naked
Archaeologist series).

> No purely human
> book would exhibit this characteristic.

Try the Koran. Try the hieroglyphics on the temple of Karnakh. Try the
Hindu scriptures. Try the Sikh scriptures. If your faith is the only
true faith then these others must be purely human books and yet they
too are being proven correct.

> If the Bible were purely human,
> therefore fallible, then in such a large work, over time the number of
> known fallacies would increase, not decrease.

If the bible were purely the word of god then it would have been
written with one voice/hand and would be consistent in manner
throughout. Bible scholars (better christians than yourself)
acknowledge that the individual books are the written works of a
number of scholars with some books exhibiting the hand of more than
one person in their texts.

> For example, until (IIRC)
> circa 1930, the only known reference to Sargon II, king of Assyria, was
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> accounts.

Really? You have already shown how christians are prepared to take
unrelated texts within the bible and extrapolate them to prove/
disprove/form the basis of.. some other belief. My recollection of the
gospels is that they are contradictory and most certainly not
contemporary.

>
> Are there passages in the Bible that are difficult to reconcile? Yes,
> there are. There are several reasonable explanations for this. For one
> thing, we do not have the original manuscripts, and scribal errors

Surely the word of god can not be mistranslated? And remember, the
bible is a consistent whole but now you are saying that it is
difficult to reconcile passages (or in other words inconsistent).

> and
> changes have definitely been introduced. For another, as shown in the
> apparent Gospel chronology problems, our knowledge is incomplete. As we
> continue to study the Bible, more and more of the seeming contradictions
> are resolved, as one would expect in a genuine divinely inspired book.

Or perhaps, science is able to show us the small kernel of truth
underlying some of the bible's historical base.

> In addition, there have been a lot of "jewels" uncovered in the digging
> done to resolve the apparent contradictions. So many that theologians
> have suggested God may have intentionally permitted those problems just
> to promote deeper digging in some areas.

Just as god planted dinosaur skeletons in the rocks to encourage
palaeontology?

>
> It is also true that the documentary evidence for Biblical events is
> vastly greater than for any other events of comparable antiquity.

The Hindu scriptures document events that pre-date the biblical events
by millennia.


> For
> example, there is more evidence of Jesus crucifixion

To whit:
1. The bible;
2. A (singular) reference to Jesus in Roman documents;
3. References in Jewish documentation to christ.

Three weeks worth of a man's life. Not much to show for 33 years on
the earth.


> and resurrection

No evidence at all. Unless you mean the footprints in Kashmir
(complete with misplaced nail holes).

> than that Julius Caesar ever existed.

Codswallop.

> There are vastly more manuscripts,
> and they date from much closer to the actual events, than any other
> historical events of comparable antiquity.

I wouldn't count multiple copies of the same document. As for the
vastly more manuscripts: which ones?

> For example, there are only
> (IIRC) nine extant copies of Caesar's "Gallic Wars", and the oldest
> dates over 900 years after the events.

Don't we count the contemporary sculptures of Caesar? His conquests?

> There are literally tens of
> thousands of ancient Biblical manuscripts, and some of the New Testament
> manuscripts date from about 30 years after the events.

40 years at least. Unusually long-lived these apostles for a
persecuted sect. And I do not recall reading that any of the gospels
had been written by the idividuals whose names they bear.

>
> (Aside: anyone interested in a legal defense of Jesus crucifixion and
> resurrection should read a little book "The Testimony of the Evangelists"
> by Simon Greenleaf. Check out Greenleaf's credentials while your at it.)

Testimony of the Evangelists? That is like giving a thief the right to
re-write all witness statements bearing evidence against himself.

>
> So, the only thing I, or any other Christian, can do to answer critics
> such as yourself is tell you what we know from experience.

One man's subjective experience is another man's hallucination.

> What you do
> with that information is up to you. You can either believe it, accept
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that's the bottom line. :-)

No contest. Rejection it is then.
```

```
On Jan 8, 3:07 pm, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> On Tue, 1 Jan 2008 14:55:52 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[68 more quoted lines elided]…
> evolution.

You miss the point entirely. Changes _are_ evolution.  The question
would be whether a particular change is due to 'sexual selection' or
'natural selection' or 'mutation' or 'genetic variation' or all of
these.

If you want to attribute it to some supernatural space pixie then you
will never be able to make sense of the world.


> >Today the birds are the only descendants related to dinosaurs, and the
> >large animals are mammals.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> were warm or cold blooded and whether some (all) laid eggs.

That is correct. It appears that many dinosaurs were warm blooded at
least to some degree. The evidence is that some had many details that
are in birds today. Certainly most dinosaurs laid eggs, as birds still
do.

Some fish also are warm blooded, such as Tuna, in that they can
generate heat and regulate this.

Some types of snakes lay eggs, some have live young. I don't know of
any dinosaurs that didn't lay eggs but it is possible.

What was your point ?

> I don't
> follow the issues closely enough to evaluate the snippets I have heard
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> able to bring about the creation and modify it.
Well, there's a cop-out if ever I saw one.


> >> Both explanations require
> >> assumptions which may have absolutely no basis in fact.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the explanations are reasonable but can be completely off the mark.

What is it that you think are 'assumptions' and 'extrapolations' ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

```
Fur, a mammalian feature can be found on duck-billed platypii but on
no other "reptile". It is possible that fur could have evolved on dbps
separately to mammals (convergent evolution) but it is more likely
that the pre-mammalian feature of incubating eggs externally was
retained whilst the dbps evolved a minor route change to the nerve
passage.
```

- **References:** `<846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <40e02849-5e7d-43df-b50a-813ababa8253@p69g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <QFDgj.20304$m6.6912@newsfe18.lga>`

```
On Mon, 7 Jan 2008 23:15:04 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:


>The current opinion on duck-billed platypuses is that they are reptiles with
>mammalian features.  This is decided on the basis of how some nerve passes
>through the skull (I'm not making this up!)

There is a whole lot of DNA research on monotremes i.e. platypus and echidna (4 species).
They are unique in having 10 sex chromosomes (9 for echidna). No other animals have more
than 4, the vast majority have 2. Only plants have numbers close to 10. 

Mammals have an XY system in which the male determines the sex of offspring.  Birds have a
ZW system in which the female determines the sex. Platypuses are like mammals in that the
male determines the sex, XYXYXYXY being male and XXXXXXXXXX being female. However, at the
other end of 4 out of 5 X chromosomes are bird-like Z sex-determining genes, and
distributed up and down the chromosomes are more bird genes than mammal genes. 

Reptiles use X/Y, Z/W and a third system that is temperature sensitive. On close
examination, reptilian sex genes are different from mammals and birds. Monotremes do not
have any reptile-like sex genes. Their X genes also do not look like those of therians
(marsupials, rodents), which is where they are usually classified. For more on this see:
http://genomebiology.com/2007/8/11/R243

Another research report says platypus genes look close to those of a marsupials (opossum,
kangaroo), but not to rodents. 
http://www.pubmedcentral.nih.gov/articlerender.fcgi?tool=pubmed&pubmedid=9720117

The reason for so much interest is that monotremes are like living fossils. They have
evolved much slower than other animals, thus give a glimple of what mammal and bird genes
looked like during the early Jurassic, 160 million years ago, rather than the late
Jurrassic, 140 MYA, when marsupials appeared.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T08:01:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zmLgj.43554$Mu4.31094@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <40e02849-5e7d-43df-b50a-813ababa8253@p69g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> You miss the point entirely. Changes _are_ evolution.

Not necessarily. The ability to adapt to changing conditions could have
been built in by an intelligent Creator, quite different from any evolutionary
process. We do the same thing in programs that are specifically designed
to accomodate to various conditions. Such design pardigms would be
purposely limited in scope, unlike "evolution", which is uncontrolled and
unlimited, by definiton. E.g. a "horse type" creature might adapt into
Clydesdales, poneys and donkeys, but never into a dog or giraffe.
```

```
On Jan 9, 3:28 am, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> On Mon, 7 Jan 2008 19:46:30 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz>
> wrote:

> The change between egg laying and carrying in the womb is drastic.

No, it is progressive. Some types of snakes have made part of that
change. Even some types of fish and of insects have live young.
Marsupials are one intermediate form where the egg hatches internally
but the carrying is external with milk glands.

> The
> change between single cell reproduction and sexual reproduction is
> drastic.

No, it is progressive, evolutionary. Single cell organisms will also
merge which will give genetic diversity.

> In both cases finding a plausible transition is difficult.
>

Only if you don't know how to look.


> >If you want to attribute it to some supernatural space pixie then you
> >will never be able to make sense of the world.
>
> You should read more science fiction.

Fantasy makes more sense ?
```

```
>> People like everything to fit into nice neat categories.   The

I wasn't talking about you specifically.  I like to put things into
categories, it makes them easier to understand - or it makes them
easier to think I understand them.    It can be useful to recognize
that things don't fit into categories as nicely as we would like.

This is obvious in such categories as race and religion.   But it even
applies to whether something is a computer language, or life kingdoms.
```

```
In article <13kuv5hdahco9bc@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>> This was the immediate cause of the late rupture and present revolution.

[snip]

>> --end quoted text
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>been referring to something other than the Civil War.

Mr Smith, Vice President Stephens begins his speech with 'I was remarking 
that we are passing through one of the greatest revolutions in the annals 
of the world. Seven States have within the last three months thrown off an 
old government and formed a new.'

Secession from the Union began on 24 Dec 1860 (South Carolina) and by 9 
Feb 1861 six other states joined and formed the Confederate States of 
America.  I do not believe that there were too many other 'latest 
ruptures' which Mr Stephens might have been addressing; if you have 
evidence that might support that view it could be interesting to see.

DD
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:ed98l3hri5djdsnpqdmf3i4id0q4bjdq84@4ax.com...
> And even for most of Christian history - marriage was not a secular
> institution, it was a sacrament that was the responsibility of the
> church, not the state.

Maybe we should return to that. Five bucks says if there were no secular 
benefits associated with "marriage" all the acrimony regarding "committed 
partner" benefits would be moot.

Why did the state have to create this institution we call "marriage," 
anyway?  This is an honest question: since most states DO have such an 
institution, I assume there must be a reason for it; but damned if I can 
figure out just what that reason might be.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-12-03T10:39:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5f8l3hdmk95t4ik7mo7ler1ddp31uu78q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <d015e273-f1c9-4148-8fb5-ad2d8e0f5868@f3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <q5X3j.48587$eY.12642@newssvr13.news.prodigy.net> <abl0l3plq5ve0dqnhavromn9b4gg6n8agv@4ax.com> <p532l3ha34428nk6g3muv6lptbgjodrepv@4ax.com> <ed98l3hri5djdsnpqdmf3i4id0q4bjdq84@4ax.com> <NNV4j.2927$NY.1432@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com>`

```
On Mon, 3 Dec 2007 10:03:39 -0600, "Michael Mattias"
<mmattias@talsystems.com> wrote:

>> And even for most of Christian history - marriage was not a secular
>> institution, it was a sacrament that was the responsibility of the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>figure out just what that reason might be.

I've read that one big reason in the U.S. was to counter polygamy. But
politicians like power, so secular marriages aren't limited to LDS
strongholds.

The state does have some interest in divorce - but Lee Marvin's
palimony case indicates that marriage isn't necessary for alimony -
and it certainly isn't necessary for child support.

The right to not testify against one's spouse is a place where legal
marriage makes a difference.

The other big non-government difference is in insurance.

But the biggest difference is in how the state runs things.   Social
Security is supposed to be about investment (although "double dippers"
must not be investors).    So how come the wife who supports the
husband for 40 years before they divorce and he re-marries doesn't get
the lion's share of his Social Security spousal benefit?

Get the state out of the marriage business altogether.   Let me choose
my social security beneficiary - if that happens to be my infirmed
mother or brother or sister, that's fine.   My marriage contract might
say it needs to be my spouse - but that should be a private contract
enforceable as such.

And if my church allows divorce (which has a much bigger impact on
marriage than gay marriages would), and yours doesn't.   We need to
take up our disagreements with our Church.   We don't need to push our
sacrament's definition on each other.
```

```
On Mon, 03 Dec 2007 11:49:10 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Mon, 03 Dec 2007 12:41:53 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>one that is easily fixed.

I think it would be very difficult to define 'couple filing jointly' without some kind of
marriage standard. What about a person in their twenties living with single parent? What
about true roommates? 

Compute your federal income tax with and without a spouse to see how much the deduction is
worth. Assume she earns zero. Last time I did it, the difference was $6K, or about $500
per month.
```

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:up89l31us6q8nmufqegf6q31341tsaoj2b@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 03 Dec 2007 11:49:10 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> per month.

I know that the (US) Supreme Court addressed the issue of polygamy in the 19th 
century, but I have NEVER understood how they reached their conclusion(s) - 
especially as in that case (with Mormons) it was a religious marriage that they 
were saying would NOT be recognized by the State (and was in fact "illegal").

Personally (and for obvious reasons), I would say that either:

A) Civil unions (between any number of "consenting adults" should be an 
established type of "contract" that is all that the government recognizes (and 
for which "public benefits" are tied)

     OR

B) Marriage (if it is a "government" institution) should not be allowed to 
discriminate based on "color, creed" or SEX or NUMBER of participants or 
anything else for which "discrimination" is considered against the "public 
good".

  ***

If "marriage" is allowed to "discriminate" (based on "most common" - but not 
universally accepted - public religious or moral attitudes - then it should NOT 
be something that the government uses for taxing, benefits, or any other 
"government" activity)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<13lb236stve91ba@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <d015e273-f1c9-4148-8fb5-ad2d8e0f5868@f3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <q5X3j.48587$eY.12642@newssvr13.news.prodigy.net> <abl0l3plq5ve0dqnhavromn9b4gg6n8agv@4ax.com> <p532l3ha34428nk6g3muv6lptbgjodrepv@4ax.com> <ed98l3hri5djdsnpqdmf3i4id0q4bjdq84@4ax.com> <NNV4j.2927$NY.1432@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <a5f8l3hdmk95t4ik7mo7ler1ddp31uu78q@4ax.com> <2aj8l31h7qvnir7enkumk6s5a3l49rk88c@4ax.com> <drj8l31v6c2aivsnmaa2a0n514kcucu537@4ax.com> <up89l31us6q8nmufqegf6q31341tsaoj2b@4ax.com> <O145j.161807$bj2.116558@fe08.news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>
> Personally (and for obvious reasons), I would say that either:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> benefits, or any other "government" activity)

Slight correction: I don't think it's against the law to HAVE more than one 
spouse, I think it's against the law to MARRY more than one spouse. The 
difference is non-trivial.

Suppose someone comes to this country from another where plural marriages 
are permitted. Is he required to disavow or divorce one of his wives? 
Suppose a citizen of the US (with an existing wife) travels to a country, 
where plural marriages are permitted, and he meets another citizen of the US 
and marries her. They both return to the US. The man now has two wives, but 
what crime (if any) did he commit?

Now imagine a really tough one: What if the man's religion specifically 
commands that he take a second wife? I'm not making this up. In Judaism, 
there is a requirement that a man marry his deceased brother's wife. This 
commandment has been set aside in Ashkenasic Jewry (Europe), but not in the 
Sephardic tradition (North Africa). Here is a direct conflict between 
religion (constitutionally protected) and ordinary secular law. It would 
seem the Constitution would trump convention.

I think this issue will get more and more attention in the future. Everybody 
knows a two-paycheck marriage barely makes ends meet; maybe a three-paycheck 
marriage is the answer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Marriage (was OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-12-04T20:49:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cotbl31c6dks1hv5lni8m4pu233788on98@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<abl0l3plq5ve0dqnhavromn9b4gg6n8agv@4ax.com> <p532l3ha34428nk6g3muv6lptbgjodrepv@4ax.com> <ed98l3hri5djdsnpqdmf3i4id0q4bjdq84@4ax.com> <NNV4j.2927$NY.1432@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <a5f8l3hdmk95t4ik7mo7ler1ddp31uu78q@4ax.com> <2aj8l31h7qvnir7enkumk6s5a3l49rk88c@4ax.com> <drj8l31v6c2aivsnmaa2a0n514kcucu537@4ax.com> <up89l31us6q8nmufqegf6q31341tsaoj2b@4ax.com> <O145j.161807$bj2.116558@fe08.news.easynews.com> <13lb236stve91ba@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Tue, 4 Dec 2007 11:03:39 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>Suppose someone comes to this country from another where plural marriages 
>are permitted. Is he required to disavow or divorce one of his wives? 

The extra spouses will not receive permanent residency permits. They are usually brought
in via employment or student visas. Ditto for same-sex partners, including the first one.

Permanent Partner Immigration Acts, which give same-sex partners the same privilege as
opposite-sex, have been introduced in Congress every session since 2000, by Rep. Jerrold
Nadler (D-NY). They haven't made it out of committee. 

Ironically, a foreign worker (e.g. H1B) can bring a same-sex partner to the US more easily
than a citizen can, with a B2 visa. The B2 was originally intended for non-spouse
dependents such as aging parents. It now explicitly includes same-sex partners. A citizen
cannot get one for his partner, it's only for temporary workers. 

>Suppose a citizen of the US (with an existing wife) travels to a country, 
>where plural marriages are permitted, and he meets another citizen of the US 
>and marries her. They both return to the US. The man now has two wives, but 
>what crime (if any) did he commit?

Polygamy is illigal in only a few places, left over anti-Mormon laws, and seldom
prosecuted. But under the Defense Of Marriage Act (DOMA), passed 1996:

1.The Federal government does not recognize polygamous or same-sex marriages.

2. States are not required to recognize them, but not prohibited either.

Twenty-six states have constitutional amendments saying the same, and another twenty
states have statutory DOMAs. Some of those states allow same-sex civil unions, which they
carefully avoid calling marriage. 

>Now imagine a really tough one: What if the man's religion specifically 
>commands that he take a second wife? I'm not making this up. In Judaism, 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>seem the Constitution would trump convention.

The US Supreme Court defined tests for a law's constitutionality re First Amendment
freedom of religion in Lemon v. Kurtzman, 403 U.S. 602 (1971), commonly called the Lemon
Test.

   1. The government's action must have a legitimate secular purpose;
   2. The government's action must not have the primary effect of either advancing or
inhibiting religion;
   3. The government's action must not result in an "excessive government entanglement"
with religion.

Several objections to MOMA have been presented to the Supreme Court on other grounds, not
religious. The Court declined to consider any of them. The most serious (IMO) claims that
marriage is a fundamental right protected by the Due Process Clauses of the Fifth and
Fourteenth Amendments. This was established by the Supreme Court in Loving v. Virginia,
388 U.S. 1 (1967), in which the Court said, "Marriage is one of the "basic civil rights of
man," fundamental to our very existence and survival...." The counterargument is that
there's no law against same-sex unions, therefore no one has been deprived of life,
liberty or property ... without due process. 

In my opinion, denial of tax deductions and social security benefits (among 1,000 others
listed below) IS a deprivation of property, and denial of privileged communication between
spouses DOES violate the Fifth Amendment on self-incrimination. The GAO compiled this list
of 1,138 Federal government policies that treat married people differently from single
people. 
http://www.gao.gov/new.items/d04353r.pdf

>I think this issue will get more and more attention in the future. Everybody 
>knows a two-paycheck marriage barely makes ends meet; maybe a three-paycheck 
>marriage is the answer. 

There's no law against it now, you just can't claim the second spouse as a dependant.

Keep in mind that for every bigamy (three person family) there is a corresponding one
person family.
```

```
On Tue, 04 Dec 2007 03:43:42 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>"government" activity)

I agree with William Klein's personal A OR B and the follow-on (name
given for clarity).  Unfortunately, in Canada there wasn't clear
thinking so the number 2 is sacred.  Also the situation becomes messy
in terms of definition of "civil union/marriage" because of
responsibility relationships and how does the union get broken (or
partially broken/added to where the number of participants can be
greater than 2).  The problems can be worked out but they haven't even
been thought about by most legislative bodies.  I also wonder how we
get from polygamy being acceptable in David's time to being at least
frowned upon by the time of Acts.  Understanding that might lead us to
believe that there is a practical reason or government interest in
limiting the number of people in an intimate relationship to 2.  Also,
should presumed sexual intimacy be a requirement for civil union in
order to obtain the governmental benefits of that union.  Should
siblings be able to have a civil union without a sexual union?
```

```
On Sun, 16 Dec 2007 22:27:40 -0400, Clark F Morris
<cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:

>Also the situation becomes messy
>in terms of definition of "civil union/marriage" because of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>been thought about by most legislative bodies.  

Contract law has existed for a long time.   And implicit contracts for
common-law marriages and parental support outside of marriage are old
too.

>I also wonder how we
>get from polygamy being acceptable in David's time to being at least
>frowned upon by the time of Acts.  Understanding that might lead us to
>believe that there is a practical reason or government interest in
>limiting the number of people in an intimate relationship to 2.  

Or more likely, there is a political reason.

>Also,
>should presumed sexual intimacy be a requirement for civil union in
>order to obtain the governmental benefits of that union.  Should
>siblings be able to have a civil union without a sexual union? 

IMHO, yes.   Keep the state out of my bedroom.   Let me pick my
infirmed brother or mother or neighbor as the beneficiary of my Social
Security Insurance.
```

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
>
> Why did the state have to create this institution we call "marriage,"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In the minds of the majority, society would be better off. It was thought 
that marriage would lead to stability and responsibility - that society 
would benefit as a whole from marriage. It's not a new concept - marriage 
has been promoted - and codified into law - for at least 3,000 years.

This concept can be seen in other contemporary areas, compulsory education, 
tax deductions for owning your own home, vaccinations, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-12-03T11:59:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0uj8l3hl2rhbd2ceo0e7ehdcaibfo7iuii@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <d015e273-f1c9-4148-8fb5-ad2d8e0f5868@f3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <q5X3j.48587$eY.12642@newssvr13.news.prodigy.net> <abl0l3plq5ve0dqnhavromn9b4gg6n8agv@4ax.com> <p532l3ha34428nk6g3muv6lptbgjodrepv@4ax.com> <ed98l3hri5djdsnpqdmf3i4id0q4bjdq84@4ax.com> <NNV4j.2927$NY.1432@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <13l8fuei17ak2d5@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 3 Dec 2007 11:41:37 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>In the minds of the majority, society would be better off. It was thought 
>that marriage would lead to stability and responsibility - that society 
>would benefit as a whole from marriage. It's not a new concept - marriage 
>has been promoted - and codified into law - for at least 3,000 years.

Mostly it hasn't been codified into secular law.   But it has survived
quite nicely as cannon law or even not codified as a sacrament.    The
state didn't take over marriage because it wanted more people married
- or was there a spike in marriages when the state took over?    I
very much doubt it.

>This concept can be seen in other contemporary areas, compulsory education, 
>tax deductions for owning your own home, vaccinations, etc. 

At the turn of the 20th century, French authorities were concerned
that they were not getting enough cannon fodder for their army - so
they created a propaganda campaign promoting motherhood in a country
where child rearing was often farmed out to the countryside.    

But I haven't read that governments used their control over marriages
to increase the percentage of people who got married.    I suppose it
might work in a state with a very strong church - for those who
weren't religious.

But in the United States, it is easy to find someone non-religious who
has no government job to marry you - he does have some paperwork to
perform to make it legal.    Turn that paperwork from a license to a
contract instead.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:sk7bl3tcdptjvkssllf9curb4m5bsumsn9@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 04 Dec 2007 11:52:50 -0500, SkippyPB
> <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Other legal partnerships exist with the same kind of issues.

Estate planning? I think you call those documents "wills."

Anyway, for all the legal stuff either you can "marry" or execute mutual 
durable power of attorney documents. (And for all I know the legal fees for 
that would be less than the cost of a "marriage" license).

My best guess as to why the state got into the marriage business is that is 
was always considered a Good Thing for a Nation-State to have a larger 
population.... and since the majority of citizens/subjects don't procreate 
except when "married" it made sense for the nation-state to encourage 
procreation by encouraging "marriage."

Then again, I am someone who honestly believes women are born with a 
"shopping" gene, so perhaps I might lack a tad of credibility on "things 
factual."

No, really, I do!  I think it's a vestige of our days as hunter-gatherers. 
While men were the primary hunters, women were the primary gatherers; to be 
a good gatherer, selecting the most nutritious roots and berries required 
the gatherers be able to distinguish "Good Value" from "Bad Value" among 
large collections of things which look pretty much alike.

And which of the gatherers and their families survived to become our 
great-great-great {yadda, yadda more greats] grandparents? Those who HAD the 
ability to make good nutritional value selections!

It's not really such a great leap from "selecting the best nutritional value 
from a bunch of roots which look more or less the same"  to "selecting the 
best fashion value from a bunch of blouses which look more or less the 
same," is it?

MCM
(And don't anyone even try to tell me today's women don't hunt and gather!)
```

```
news:ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> secession.)

I've never come across this view before and find it interesting. My 
knowledge of American history is the "received" one that we learn in school.

<Anecdote - skip this if you don't like stories>
(Learning History in New Zealand is a pretty major task: (depending to some 
extent on which schools you attend, although the basic curriculum is set 
Nationally) we study Europe from the Congress of Vienna in 1815 until 1939, 
taking in the unification of Italy (Garibaldi), the unification of Germany 
including the Franco-Prussian War, The Austro-Hungarian Empire, Metternich 
and Bismarck, the fall of the Ottoman Empire, the Crimea and Balkans, the 
history of France. Then we do  Ancient history (Stone Age, Bronze Age, Iron 
Age, Babylon, Egypt, Greece, Rome), Australia, New Zealand, (both, 
thankfully pretty short :-)), Great Britain from 1066 to 1815 (after which 
it is included in "Europe 1815 - 1939"), and, USA (The Revolution, the War 
of 1812, the Civil War, specific figures like Washington, Lincoln, Patrick 
Henry, John Paul Jones, and even Benedict Arnold... :-) We don't cover, 
except at the most superficial level, the system of government, although, of 
course, the Constitution and the Declaration of independence are covered in 
detail. Average Kiwis who have reached University Entrance level have a good 
and wide knowledge of the World around them. We spend a lot of time 
acquiring it. You can imagine how mortifying it was for me the first time I 
visited the US (30 years ago) and met people who thought NZ was part of 
Australia (another State; that may well happen in 50 years but definitely 
not yet...), "The Sydney Harbour bridge runs from Sydney to Auckland" - it's 
1400 miles... (based on the very dubious logic that the Bay Bridge runs from 
San Francisco to Oakland, and "Oakland" sounds like "Auckland".... :-))... 
now I have become resigned to it and understand that Americans have no 
reason to find out about much outside America... The best example of this 
was the following:

I stopped for gas at a service station on Ventura Boulevarde.

The attendant was courteous (as I find most Americans to be, definitely more 
so than in Europe) and was intrigued by my accent.

Attendant: "So, where you from?"

Me: "New Zealand".

Attendant (face lights up): "Aw, Man, I'd love to go there...".

Me (joking): "You guys don't need to go anywhere. You have it all, right 
here on Ventura...".

Attendant (looks around, then quite serious...): "Yeah, you're right..."

That little gem has stayed with me for some time. Maybe the attendant went 
on to become a movie star and achieve the American dream (I really hope 
so...), maybe he flies to NZ in his own private jet, but I think it is 
unlikely. Either way, I'm no longer offended when people of any nationality 
show ignorance about my country. The worry is that if they start to find out 
and start coming here, we might not be able to enjoy it as we currently do 
:-) Seriously, we welcome visitors and Tourism is now in the top five growth 
industries for NZ.

Just finally on that, in passing, David Beckham and the LA Galaxy arrived 
here yesterday. After watching the powhiri (traditionally Maori "welcome" 
which is pretty intimidating for people not familiar with it), and the 
subsequent press conference, I can tell you they are excellent Ambassadors 
for America. They seem to be genuinely enjoying their visit and NZ is loving 
to have them.

</Anecdote - skip this if you don't like stories>

If what you say is true (and I'm not saying it isn't, Judson; just that I 
honestly don't know...) then surely, the history texts should reflect this 
important point? If such a terrible and bloody conflict could have been 
avoided, isn't there a responsibility for future generations to be aware of 
that?

>
> The Constitution decreed that, in presidential elections, the states would 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> computers.

In principle, it makes sense today. The problem is if States are allowed to 
juggle who is elegible. If that happens you get the empowered ruling class 
simply making sure that only "one of us" can represent them. (It happened in 
Great Britain right up until the 20th Century). That means that everyone 
else is then disenfranchised and the whole idea of a Democratic "level 
playing field" goes out the window.


>Because the states select and send the electoral delegates, they decide how 
>the delegates are to be selected, including who can vote for them.

That was my original point. They shouldn't be allowed to. One simple rule: 
"Everyone not in Jail gets to vote". (OK, you can exclude the mentally 
ill...but only if it is certified by three doctors... :-))

(Being the first country in the world to give women the vote (may have been 
a mistake, but we've learned to live with it :-)), we are pretty passionate 
about Democracy... here's a quote from "The right to Vote": 
http://www.elections.org.nz/study/history/right-to-vote.html

"In 1893, after a long and dramatic struggle, the right to vote was granted 
to all adult women. By that time it was widely accepted that the franchise 
was a right of citizenship, and that therefore all adult citizens should be 
able to take part in elections (with some exceptions, such as prison inmates 
and the mentally ill). "

By the time British women achieved the right to vote, NZers had had it for 
20 years; by the time American women got it (1920), nearly 30 years.)

Forgive my lack of in-depth understanding, but don't Federal Laws override 
State Laws PRECISELY so that individual States (who might be tempted to pass 
local legislation to discriminate against certain sectors) cannot do so?

If a State passes legislation which is seen to be against the spirit of the 
Union or violates the Constitution, doesn't that get overridden at Federal 
level?

What's the point of a Union if it's principles and the values for which it 
stands can be eroded by individual members of it?

>In modern times, political parties nominate delegates who are sworn to vote 
>for a particular candidate. In times past, the ballots only had the names 
>of the delegate candidate, not the person running for office. Eventually 
>the candidate's names were added along with the delegates. IIRC, in 
>Alabama, the delegate's name may no longer appear.

I don't understand this at all. Sorry.

It's too complex. There should be names on a paper. Everybody who is not in 
jail should privately mark the paper against the name they want to vote for. 
Everybody gets one vote (it can be easily controlled to ensure they do).
>
> Many people (including me) believe the Constitution would be better if 
> amended so presidential elections are by straight popular vote.

We call that "First past the Post" (FPP) It was the basis of our elections 
up until 1996. In 1993 we had a referendum and decided that Proportional 
Representation would be a fairer way to ensure that everybody (including 
minorities) was represented in Paliament. Pure PR was not suitable for us, 
so we adopted a system called Mixed-Member Proportional (MMP) based on a 
system developed and shown to work well in Germany. In effect, it means that 
people vote for specific candidates (as in FPP) and also for the Parties 
they favour. Any party receiving 5% of the vote will be allocated seats in 
Parliament (even though none of their specific candidates may have won their 
local area (electorate)).  In the 1996 elections MMP proved to be a winner 
and we have been using it ever since. (We have Natonal Elections every 3 
years. It used to be 5 years but we found that, once elected, parties tended 
to "coast"  and were not driven to perform during the latter part of their 
mandate. Three years keeps everyone on their toes and ensures performance is 
maintained.)

I'd agree that for the office of President, it should be FPP across the 
nation, without regard to the size, wealth, or population of States.


>Senate and House elections would probably still be determined by the 
>states, similarly to now. The problem with a Constitutional amendment is 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>people want, or wasting the whole process by voting it down to avoid that.

Ah, an unwieldy Bureaucracy will always undermine even the most well defined 
Democracy. It's almost as if we overwhelm ourselves with paper and process 
and lose sight of what is really important.

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> learn in school.

Whoever said that history is always written by the victors was dead on.
The best way to abolish slavery would have been to pass legislation that
combined financial incentives for freeing slaves with penalties for keeping
them, that would be applied progressively over a period of several years,
ending with manumission of all slaves. I believe this is pretty much what
the southern states proposed circa 1860. The fact that it was voted down by
the northern block states says more than anyone's opinion or comments about
the actual intent at the time.

Even if nothing at all had been done to free the slaves, the Industrial
Revolution was making slavery uneconomical. Combined with the pressure from
those who wanted to abolish slavery for moral reasons, slavery would have
been abolished within a fairly short period of time anyway, as evidenced by
the proposed legislation above. Then some 750,000 Americans wouldn't have
died. Killing 750,000 to free 4,000,000, and laying waste to a big chunk of
North America. Even if the reason had been purely to free the slaves, what
a terrible way to do it, under the circumstances. Nothing to brag about,
any way you slice it.

What bothers me is that liberals never seem to learn from their mistakes.
In the late 1970's a huge hydroelectric dam project in north Georgia was
stopped because it was said to endanger a "rare" small fish called the
Snail Darter. Hundreds of families incomes was trashed and many millions of
dollars was wasted, and the dam wasn't built. In the 1990's a serious DNA
study of the fishes in North American rivers proved that the Snail Darter
is a very common fish, only differing slightly in color from many other
varieties of the same species. Anyone think we could use more environment
friendly hydroelectric power these days?

In the 1970's the EPA decided that, since exposure to asbestos can be a
health risk, that all asbestos in existing building must be removed post
haste at all costs. Billions of dollars was spent in a futile attempt, and
even the EPA admits the effort was a disaster, exposing many thousands of
people to asbestos dust caused by the asbestor removal process, saying it
would have been much better to just leave the asbestos where it was, and
deal with it as necessary. Nice to know that ... now.

It isn't that the liberals necessarily choose bad goals, it is that they
are blind to the widespread and sometimes disasterous repurcussions of
implementing their "solutions", and do not accept the limitations of
their current knowledge and understanding. They get an idea in their
heads, and become blind to everything else.

Can we spell "G-L-O-B-A-L W-A-R-M-I-N-G?" May God protect us from such
"benefactors". Yes, I know, I'm about to be bombarded with tons of
"pseudo evidence" (long chains of "reasoning" based on partial science,
the sum of which can't be directly experimentally verified, and which
everyone will know is wrong in 10 or 20 years) to prove Global Warming
is "real" and definitely caused by humans. Yes, I've seen the phenomenon
before. The Snail Darter, mandantory asbestos removal, "Exxon Valdez
destroys environment" (two years after, biggest salmon catch ever, and
www.labor.state.ak.us/research/trends/apr06reg.pdf), "Forest fires are
always bad, prevent them at all costs" (it's now known that small forest
fires every few decades are beneficial for the forest, because they keep
underbrush down, otherwise the uncontrolled underbrush permits a huge
devastating uncontrollable fire that destroys the forest in a hundred
years or so {e.g. Yellowstone Park}, and they "renew" the living habitat
for many forest animals) and "Coming New Ice Age" from just 20 years ago
(Google "coming new ice age 1980's"), to name but a few.
```

```
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
would have recovered eventually but it would have taken much longer
before the wildlife had recovered.

Seeing as the environment is of no concern to you (I presume you have
an insurance policy with God) perhaps we should sit back and allow oil
producers to pollute with impunity. Hell, let us allow China and the
US to pollute our atmosphere, let us allow Russia (and the USA) create

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

because their prayers will be heard rather than because they are out
there doing something to improve the world.

>
> >> and "Coming New Ice Age" from just 20 years ago
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Since humans have existed in one form or another for 7.5 million years
the cheeky answer (since you can not prove me wrong on this) is QUITE
PROBABLY.

However, to be fair (and I do not know why I am doing this) much of
the climate fluctuations were as the result of earth axis wobbles and
other causes, not human activity. The extent of global warming due to
natural causes (fires, wobbles, volcanic activity, methane releases,
etc.) can be tracked and calculated. The difference between the
observed and calculated warming can only be due to human activity
(unless you want to throw Satan into the equation).

>
> The point is that many of the same people who were touting New Ice
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> them to be robots, perfectly toeing the P.C. line? :-)

In a universe where the creator knows all, past, present and future,
there can be no free will. Children have a habit of circumventing
authority and doing what they want to do anyway.
```

```
On Fri, 30 Nov 2007 06:46:29 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>What bothers me is that liberals never seem to learn from their mistakes.
>In the late 1970's a huge hydroelectric dam project in north Georgia was
>stopped because it was said to endanger a "rare" small fish called the
>Snail Darter. Hundreds of families incomes was trashed and many millions of
>dollars was wasted, and the dam wasn't built.

This isn't a matter of "liberals" - it is a matter of laws.    People
from all political leanings pass laws that don't have room for the
difficult-to-define "common sense".    Part of this is because they
don't think - part of it is because it is politically inconvenient to
put in exceptions - and part of it is because they see the long
history of the powerful of finding ways to say "this law doesn't apply
to what I want to do".    (Even the Constitution doesn't apply)

So what are the courts to do?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-02T16:28:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13l6cbq7sfl87bb@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<5qu3apF11a14mU1@mid.individual.net> <xpH2j.3877$_%4.1885@newsfe15.lga> <Qd2dnR0dGcO-btHanZ2dnUVZ_hadnZ2d@comcast.com> <syk3j.4023$KK1.2672@newsfe24.lga> <d5ork3l3ndq0951hu2ie1v85sbdpfsjvam@4ax.com> <13kru8ae0i51o59@corp.supernews.com> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <mub0l31c083h5ttcheseht71vf64htkb9a@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> This isn't a matter of "liberals" - it is a matter of laws.    People
> from all political leanings pass laws that don't have room for the
> difficult-to-define "common sense".

One is always surprised when common sense triumphs.

In the wake of 9/11, a committee was put together to formulate a plan for 
Air Traffic Control in the event of a repeat - who was to have authority to 
issue an ATC-ZERO (all planes grounded), etc.

The committee met, took testimoy, heard experts, reviewed the sequences of 
events on that fateful day and issued its recommendation.

Do nothing.

That's right. The committee said that those on the ground with decades of 
experience could make an ad hoc decision better than any pre-formulated 
300-page regulation!

I recall a sequence from the TV-movie "911" that went something like this:

ATC Chief: "Anybody know how much we have aloft, real world?"
Voice 1: "About 4,000"
Voice 2: "4215, chief."
[pause]
ATC Chief: "Okay. This shit stops now. I want evere plane on the ground at 
the nearest airport. Make it happen."
```

- **References:** `<5qu3apF11a14mU1@mid.individual.net> <xpH2j.3877$_%4.1885@newsfe15.lga> <Qd2dnR0dGcO-btHanZ2dnUVZ_hadnZ2d@comcast.com> <syk3j.4023$KK1.2672@newsfe24.lga> <d5ork3l3ndq0951hu2ie1v85sbdpfsjvam@4ax.com> <13kru8ae0i51o59@corp.supernews.com> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga>`

```
On Fri, 30 Nov 2007 12:42:49 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>Can we spell "glaciers melting all over the world, the Arctic ice cap
>melting so quickly that it may be gone in 20 years, the Antartic ice fields
>floating away to sea"?  Can we come up with a reason other than global
>warming??

Most of the leaders who doubted global warming a decade ago not only
accept its reality but also accept that man has contributed to it.
That doesn't mean they do or even should accept some of the costs that
go along with some of the proposals to lessen its rate.

Some scientists have pointed to the climate changes over the centuries
due to irrigation - which used to be limited to a few parts of the
world, but which are now of global scale.   Roads, highways, farming -
and possibly over-fishing do change the environment.   Compared to
them, controlling CO2 is cheap & easy - but is it sufficient?

It may be that any long term solution that doesn't involve population
control will require a much wealthier world.
```

```
I interpreted your post as casting doubt upon global warming happening at
all.

And while it certainly can happen without our help, you're just being
stubborn if you contend that - two centuries of spilling heat, carbon
dioxide, pollutants of all sorts, chemicals such as nitrogen and phosphorus
which cause natural growths to run away, into the environment - have had
nothing to do with it.

What's really, really dumb is to pretend that carbon/emission credit trading
is going to do anything to solve the problem.  (You haven't said this, so
you needn't respond if you don't want to).  Book-keeping, which is what this
is, won't do anything.  As it is done now, emission credits are just a
license to pollute.  If there was true political courage anywhere in the
world, polluters would be fined so heavily that they couldn't afford NOT to
clean up.  Especially if they couldn't pass it along to their customers.

PL
```

```
On Sat, 1 Dec 2007 09:12:52 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

BS.  Volcanic eruptions can enhance global warming by adding CO2 to
the atmosphere. However, a far greater amount of CO2 is contributed to
the atmosphere by human activities each year than by volcanic
eruptions. T.M.Gerlach (1991, American Geophysical Union) notes that
human-made CO2 are dwarfed the estamated global release of CO2 from
volcanoes by at least 150 times. The small amount of global warming
caused by eruption-generated greenhouse gases is offset by the far
greater amount of global cooling caused by eruption-generated
particles in the stratosphere (the haze effect). Greenhouse warming of
the earth has been particularly evident since 1980. Without the
cooling influence of such eruptions as El Chichon (1982) and Mt.
Pinatubo (1991) greenhouse warming would have been more pronounced.

Mt.Pinatubo erupted in the Philippines on June 15, 1991, and one month
later Mt. Hudson in southern Chile also erupted. The Pinatubo eruption
produced the largest sulfur oxide cloud this century. The combined
aerosol plume of Mt. Pinatubo and Mt. Hudson diffused around the globe
in a matter of months. The data collected after these eruptions show
that mean world temperatures decreased by about 1 degree Centigrade
over the subsequent two years. This cooling effect was welcomed by
many scientists who saw it as a counter-balance to global warming.


>Remember "Humans are unquestionably destroying the ozone layer and we're
>all going to be fried!" a short while back? Maybe that's why it is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What planet have you been living on?  When it was recognized that
"humans" were primarily responsible for the ozone layer depletion,
nearly every country passed laws to limit the amount of flurocarbons
put into the air via aerosols beginning in 1978.  Most other countries
gegan shortly after that.  Other, less universal, actions include
abatement of fluoroform greenhouse gas emissions from HCFC
manufacturing, process changes to eliminate trifluoromethylsulfur
pentafluoride emissions from electrochemical fluorination and ceasing
manufacture of perfluorooctanyl sulfonate compounds due to their
persistence in human tissue.  

The size of the hole in the ozone layer in October 1998 was three
times the size of the USA, larger than it had ever been before. In
autumn 2000, the hole in the ozone layer was at its largest ever.
Observers had hoped that its 1998 level was due to El Niï¿½o and would
not be exceeded.

In April 1991, satellite data from the US space agency NASA revealed
that the ozone layer had depleted by 4ï¿½8% in the northern hemisphere
and by 6ï¿½10% in the southern hemisphere between 1978 and 1990. Between
1989 and 1996 the ozone layer over the Arctic diminished by around
40%. The ozone layer above the USA was reduced by 12.6% in 1993; that
above the UK was reduced by almost 50% in 1996. By September 1996,
levels in the upper atmosphere had reached a record low.

In 1987, an international agreement called the Montrï¿½al Protocol was
established. The protocol was one of the first global environmental
treaties and banned the use of chemicals responsible for ozone damage,
such as chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) in aerosols and refrigerants (see
ozone depleter). Data collected since then by scientists using
instruments in satellite and ground-based devices showed that some
degree of success has been established in slowing down the rate of
ozone depletion in the upper atmosphere. Since 1994, ozone depletion
rates have fallen from 8% to 4% per annum. However, even at current
rates, the ozone layer will not be healed until 2050.

>But this discussion is pointless. Lemmings are going to do their thing,
>regardless. Just don't expect me to jump with them. :-)

Then you are part of the problem and not the solution.

Regards,

If you never did, you should. 
These things are fun and fun is good."

Steve
```

```
On 1 Dec, 20:25, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
> In article <3c487264-e5e4-4e5d-a5a8-0bc3e080e...@r60g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

How about the increase in air travel (which contributes a cooling
effect by sulphate aerosols)?

Declining contributions from the Sun: (from Wikipedia) "estimated that
the Sun may have contributed about 45-50% of the increase in the
average global surface temperature over the period 1900-2000, and
about 25-35% between 1980 and 2000."

Increased volcanic dust emissions.

Reductions in cosmic rays (cosmic rays contribute to cloud formation).

Go to http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_warming and read it for
yourself.
```

```
Alistair wrote:
>
>> and a host of other natural sources, common sense should tell us
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> (because the scientists hidden agenda is not your hidden agenda).

Majority vote in determining truth is the venue of the liberal arts 
(English, History, Cello), not science. It is totally irrelevant how many 
scientist support or deny a claim.


>
> Again a falsehood spread by yourself. It is a fact that humans are
> causing global warming. You only have to look at the Hockey Stick
> Curve or the Keeling Curve to see that.

An investigation of the "Hockey Stick" hypothesis shows that Mann 'cooked 
the books' (pun intended) and that his conclusions are considerably more 
equivocal than he originally claimed (i.e., he lied).
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hockey_stick_controversy


>
> We don't look to God for the truth either.

Perhaps you should:

"[His Holiness Pope Benedict XVI] ... suggested that fears over man-made 
emissions melting the ice caps and causing a wave of unprecedented disasters 
were nothing more than scare-mongering.

"The German-born Pontiff said that while some concerns may be valid it was 
vital that the international community based its policies on science rather 
than the dogma of the environmentalist movement. "

http://www.dailymail.co.uk/pages/live/articles/news/worldnews.html?in_article_id=501316&in_page_id=1811&ito=1490
```

```
tlmfru wrote:
> I interpreted your post as casting doubt upon global warming
> happening at all.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> environment - have had nothing to do with it.

Yes, but natural growths soak up the CO2, giving off Oxygen. It averages 
out.

>
> What's really, really dumb is to pretend that carbon/emission credit
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The biggest gain in fighting pollution is improved technology, not draconian 
laws, shell-games involving carbon, or changes in lifestyle. We have the 
cleanest air (in the U.S.) we've ever had and we got it through increased 
technology, not through trying to power our society from sunbeams or eating 
free-range chickens.

People just don't apply one of the "quality control thinking" processes, 
such as "what then." For example, it we get rid of automobiles, and go back 
to a simpler time, what will we do with all the horse shit?
```

```
In article <13l6e6iden87h8d@corp.supernews.com>,
HeyBub <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

[snip]

>We have the 
>cleanest air (in the U.S.) we've ever had and we got it through increased 
>technology, not through trying to power our society from sunbeams or eating 
>free-range chickens.

Ummmm... it just might, possibly, have been that the technology that made 
this cleaner air possible was spurred by the Air Pollution Control Act of 
1955 (authorising research), the Clean Air Act of 1963 (authorised a 
Federal program and more research), the Air Quality Act of 1967 
(authorised enforcement), the Clean Air Act of 1970 (established 
standards), the Clean Air Act Amendments of 1977 and the Clean Air Act 
Amdnements of 1990... see http://www.epa.gov/eogapti1/module7/caa/caa.htm

Then there were the CAFE standards of 1975 (enacted by Congress as part of 
the Energy Policy Conservation Act) and their associated penalties, see 
http://www.nhtsa.dot.gov/cars/rules/cafe/overview.htm .

But... that cleaner air is only a technological coincidence, right?

DD
```

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:9qa8l35qrctl8q0js22v0q8qsifc3kabp4@4ax.com...
> >>
> >> People just don't apply one of the "quality control thinking"
processes,
> >> such as "what then." For example, it we get rid of automobiles, and go
> >> back to a simpler time, what will we do with all the horse shit?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The bloom is already coming off bio-fuels: the government of Quebec recently
backed away from further funding of crop-based (i.e., corn etc.) ethanol due
to the costs of fuel, fertilizer and land taken out of food production
required to raise bio-fuel input; they are going to explore waste products
like wood chips to see if that'll be economically and environmentally
sustainable.  There are quite a few articles indicating dubiety, as well.

Bio-fuel will work fine, though, as long as it's consumed by bio-machines,
like horses!

PL
```
