```
> blooms. Neither is any good for the environment.

CO2, plants do in fact use oxygen part from that.  The obvious experiment
would be to try to raise plants in a pure CO2 atmosphere.
```

```
On Thu, 6 Dec 2007 20:43:17 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>
>Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>would be to try to raise plants in a pure CO2 atmosphere.

Plants cannot live in pure CO2, they breathe oxygen at night, but they grow faster in an
atmosphere with higher CO2. That's why greenhouses burn natural gas to increase CO2 to
1,000 ppm, three times the ambient level.
```

```
Alistair wrote:
>
> Plants do produce more oxygen than they consume (and they produce
> methane as well) but eutrophication chokes rivers; algal blooms are
> frequently toxic (all algae produce chemicals which leech into the
> environment).

A larger point you're making is that the consequence may be more damaging 
that the original problem.

Suppose we take all the cars off the road - that would certainly address the 
CO2 "problem."

But:
1. Where are we going to get enough horses? and
2. What will we do with all the horse shit?

There are no proposed solutions to "global warming" that are more benign 
than doing nothing.
```

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:B0i4j.6679$k27.1089@bignews2.bellsouth.net...
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >> "SkippyPB" <swieg...@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
> >>
> >> > Oh yeah Judson.  Pull that bag over your face and ignore the
evidence.
> >> > You must be related to Seantor Inhofe.
> >>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> trials, not from vastly complex and disputed hypothesis.

Well, no! It has never be proven that smoking tobacco
causes cancer. In particular, it has never been shown
how the cancer develops, only that it does. Without
showing how the cancer develops, the claim that
"smoking causes lung cancer" is but another example
of the logical fallacy, "post hoc, ergo propter hoc".

The similarity between "smoking causes lung cancer"
and "global warming" seems to be that those who
believe there is overwhelming evidence that "X causes
Y" want X stopped without having to show how "X
causes Y".
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:5refb0F142e70U1@mid.individual.net...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
[snip]
> >> > And smoking tobacco doesn't cause cancer either, does it Judson?
> >>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick?

I am not certain how that question relates to the obligation,
of those making scientific claims, to establish credible scientific
evidence; but yes, I do smoke and, when I first saw the U.S.
mandated warning on cigarette packages in 1969, I found the
claims to be not credible. Having since examined some of the
scientific evidence, I have found no reason for me to change
my mind. ["Experimental Production of Carcinoma With
Cigarette Tar", Ernest L. Wynder, Evarts A. Graham, and
Adele B. Croniger, June 8, 1953, is presented often as proof
that "smoking causes lung cancer". While the experiment did
find the presence of "carcinogenic factors in cigarette smoke",
it never determined how the cancer developed.]
```

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> it never determined how the cancer developed.]

I.e.: Yes. :-)

It relates to the bias you may or may not have toward the subject,
Rick. In science you always want to know why, but it is equally
scientific to prove that something is, as long as the proof is
experimental/hands on/actual tests, not hypothesis only which we
can't test or duplicate, as is the situation in global warming. If
you want to go on believing that smoking tobacco does not cause
cancer, that is your prerogative. :-)
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:82v4j.10371$_m.4999@bignews4.bellsouth.net...
[snip]
>  In science you always want to know why, but it is equally
> scientific to prove that something is, as long as the proof is
> experimental/hands on/actual tests, not hypothesis only which we
> can't test or duplicate, as is the situation in global warming.

It seems to me that the current state of research into cancer
causation is at the same level as "Spontaneous Generation";
that is, meat is to maggots as [some] chemicals are to cancer.
One can "cause" maggots by leaving meat to putrify and one
can "cause" cancer by painting the backs of in-bred rats with
a chemical; both are valid experiments but neither reveals the
truth.
-----
Spontaneous Generation, or abiogenesis, ancient theory that
certain lower forms of life generate spontaneously from
inorganic substances. This view went uncontradicted until
1660, when Italian physician and poet Francesco Redi
disproved the prevailing belief that the maggots of flies
generated spontaneously in putrefying meat exposed to air.
Later work, in 1768 by Italian naturalist Lazzaro Spallanzani
and in 1836 by German naturalist Theodor Schwann, further
disproved the theory. In the 1860s French chemist and
microbiologist Louis Pasteur found that airborne particles
could grow in a suitable broth, and German botanist Ferdinand
Julius Cohn named these particles, which he believed to be
plants, bacteria.

Encarta� 98 Desk Encyclopedia � &  1996-97 Microsoft
Corporation. All rights reserved.
-----
Medicine, 1668

Italian physician-naturalist Francesco Redi, 42, disproves the
notion of spontaneous generation. He shows that no maggots
will develop in meat, no matter how putrified it may be, if it is
covered with a thin cloth to protect it from flies that will lay eggs.
But most people will continue for centuries to believe that
maggots are products of spontaneous generation rather than of
fly larva (see Needham, 1748; Pasteur, 1859).

The People's Chronology is licensed from Henry Holt and
Company, Inc. Copyright � 1995, 1996 by James Trager.
All rights reserved.
-----

And, apparently, most people will continue to believe that cancer
is the product of [some] chemicals rather than of the factor that
causes the cancer to develop. <g>
```

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message 
news:13l45rqdb179nd4@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> evidence;

Sometimes, particularly when the evidence may not be definitive, or certain 
bodies of evidence may seem contradictory, the viewpoint of the person 
presenting a case needs to be factored in before it is possible to make a 
proper assessment as to the value of the presented case.

>but yes, I do smoke and, when I first saw the U.S.
> mandated warning on cigarette packages in 1969, I found the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks.

I should state that for around 15 years of my life, I also smoked. When I 
quit (nearly 30 years ago, although I still enjoy the odd cigar with a 
decent brandy, but never in the house :-)) I was smoking over 40 a day. I 
enjoy good food and good wine far too much to go back to it (it diminished 
my sense of taste by at least 50%), never mind being able to breathe and 
enjoy water sports like snorkelling

I do believe cigarette smoking causes cancer so I read your very logical 
piece with interest.

The fact that there may be no scientific explanation as to how cancer 
develops, does not lead me to believe it is OK to smoke. There is enough 
statistical correlation with smokers and lung cancer to persuade me that the 
chances of getting lung cancer are greatly increased by perpetual smoking. 
(However that isn't why I quit; I was just feeling ill...)

(I don't need to know exactly how being hit by a truck will kill me; the 
statistics show that when a person is impacted by a truck moving at pace, 
the odds are greatly in favour of the truck. I therefore try and avoid 
contact with moving trucks...)

Finally, I would stress that I don't make judgements about other people 
smoking (unless their smoke is irritating me) and feel that what they do is 
none of my business. I only asked you because it has a bearing on the weight 
I would give to your argument. Had you said you don't smoke I would have 
been more sure that your selective facts were fairly selected without bias. 
As you do smoke, I have to conclude that your facts have been selected to 
support your case, rather than from a position of neutrality.

Pete.
```

```
>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> Unlike global warming, tobacco was proven to cause cancer from
actual
> >> >> trials, not from vastly complex and disputed hypothesis.
> >> >
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sometimes, particularly when the evidence may not be definitive, or
certain
> bodies of evidence may seem contradictory, the viewpoint of the person
> presenting a case needs to be factored in before it is possible to make a
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> statistical correlation with smokers and lung cancer to persuade me that
the

> (However that isn't why I quit; I was just feeling ill...)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> smoking (unless their smoke is irritating me) and feel that what they do
is
> none of my business. I only asked you because it has a bearing on the
weight
> I would give to your argument. Had you said you don't smoke I would have

I am not certain what you intend by "your facts ... selected to
support your case, rather than from a position of neutrality."
I responded to "tobacco was proven to cause cancer from
actual trials" by disputing "proven" then later identified a
report often cited as "proof". These are not, as far as I can
tell, my facts and my dispute is in opposition to their case.
What do you think my case is?

As for neutrality, I began my study January 1, 1992, with
the single notion that "smoking causes lung cancer" was
something my research had to confirm. I ended my study
February 24, 2003, in utter shock and disbelief at how
weak the case was. If I seem to lack neutrality now, it is
because of what I found and didn't find during those
eleven years and not because I smoke.
```

- **References:** `<vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <g743l3hfg6bsm31vlmqt1bdna9mvlaos2b@4ax.com> <Axg4j.19049$mb.16643@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <a6e9111c-5320-43ce-b8f7-b4f5c328001c@e1g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <B0i4j.6679$k27.1089@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <13l3fsdh7v9t361@corp.supernews.com> <5refb0F142e70U1@mid.individual.net> <13l45rqdb179nd4@corp.supernews.com> <5rfjv4F14eiukU1@mid.individual.net> <13l6o5up348vh75@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 2 Dec 2007 20:48:20 -0500, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[110 more quoted lines elided]…
>eleven years and not because I smoke.

Other studies that found the same weakness have been censored.

1. The World Health Organization sponsored a very large (n=2,000) case-control study on
second-hand smoke, known as Boffetta. When results came out wrong i.e. it did not cause
increased risk to innocents, WHO dropped its sponsorship, tried to stop publication, and
issued a statement saying the opposite of its own study's finding. 

Even the report's abstract contradicts its own finding when it says "ETS exposure during
childhood was not associated with an increased risk of lung cancer (odds ratio [OR] for
ever exposure = 0.78; 95% confidence interval [CI] = 0.64- 0.96)." The correct
interpretation of that statistic, if one were neutral, is that childhood exposure has a
protective effect. 
http://jnci.oxfordjournals.org/cgi/content/abstract/90/19/1440)

2. The American Cancer Society sponsored a large (n=35,000) 40 year cohort study on
second-hand smoke, known as Enstrom & Kabat. When it appeared the results were coming out
wrong, ACS dropped its sponsorship. When Enstrom & Kabat sought to publish, no journal
would touch it. It took four years to get the report published in BMJ. That sparked a
firestorm of ad homina against the authors. Enstrom, one of the foremost epidemiologists
in the US and a brave man, wrote this in reply to his critics. 

"Owing to the charged atmosphere surrounding the issue of passive smoking, our paper
provoked strong reactions on bmj.com. The most disturbing reactions have come from the
enforcers of political correctness who pose as disinterested scientists but are willing to
use base means to trash a study whose results they dislike. They have no qualms about
engaging in personal attacks and unfounded insinuations of dishonesty rather than judging
research on its merits.1 The resulting confusion has misled many readers and diverted
attention from the facts of the study."

http://www.bmj.com/cgi/content/full/327/7413/504

"In recent months the campaign against me has escalated because of the actions of
activists who dislike my research and want me silenced. First, the Regents of the
University of California are now considering a University-wide ban on tobacco industry
funding, as summarized in a January 26, 2007 Science news article [5]. Hopefully, this ban
will never be implemented because it would have a chilling effect on academic freedom and
would make virtually impossible the type of research that I published in my BMJ paper.
Second, the University had to conduct an investigation of ï¿½scientific misconductï¿½
allegations against me contained in October 12, 2006 and January 24, 2007 letters from the
ACS. Fortunately, a March 22, 2007 letter from the University about this investigation
completely exonerated me and concluded that the ACS allegations ï¿½provide no evidence of
scientific misconduct.ï¿½ This investigation was discussed in a March 30, 2007 commentary
entitled ï¿½Enstrom Cleared of Scientific Misconduct Charges; American Cancer Society Owes
Him An Apologyï¿½ [6].

This campaign is not going to silence me and is not going to stop me from doing honest,
high quality epidemiologic research. On the contrary, this campaign is going to help me
get out the message that my BMJ results are entirely consistent with other US
epidemiologic evidence relating ETS to mortality. One major piece of this evidence is the
199-page 1995 Emory University dissertation, ï¿½Environmental Tobacco Smoke and Lung Cancer
Mortality in the American Cancer Societyï¿½s Cancer Prevention Study IIï¿½ [7]. This
dissertation was approved by Michael Thun, the top ACS epidemiologist. The dissertation
abstract concluded: ï¿½This study found no evidence of an association between self-reported
ETS and lung cancer risk among nonsmokers. However, using spousal smoking habits to assess
exposure, we found ETS is only weakly, and not statistically significantly, related to
lung cancer risk among nonsmoking women in seven years of follow-up of the CPS II cohort.ï¿½
A second major piece of evidence is the new Western New York State study, published in the
October 9, 2006 Archives of Internal Medicine [8]. This study found ï¿½After adjustment for
covariates, exposure to secondhand smoke was not significantly associated with an
increased risk of myocardial infarction.ï¿½ Indeed, if all peer-reviewed epidemiologic
evidence is fairly and fully evaluated, the relationship between ETS and lung cancer and
coronary heart disease mortality in the US is very weak and is consistent with my
findings."

http://www.sourcewatch.org/index.php?title=James_E._Enstrom_in_his_own_words
```

```
http://www.sourcewatch.org/index.php?title=James_E._Enstrom_in_his_own_words

For the benefit of those who didn't spend the time to research
the claim "smoking causes lung cancer", the explanation for
the results, above, regarding ETS is:

1. Dr. Wynder's experiment showed that the residue of
    tobacco smoke, making contact over an extended period
    with mouse skin, resulted in cancer.

2. The cilia lining the lungs and throat prevents the formation
    of the residue of tobacco smoke.

For those who smoke, it takes about twenty years for the
residue to begin to form. It would be rare for a non-smoker
to have any formation of residue; but such a formation might
occur in a non-smoking spouse, given enough time.

Simply stated the survey results confirm: no residue--no cancer.

This "no residue--no cancer" does not fit the model for
chemical-causation of cancer; there being many chemicals
where simple exposure is sufficient to lead to the development
of cancer. The implications are huge because, if tobacco
smoke does not fit the model for chemical-causation, there is
no current scientific explanation to support the claim "smoking
causes lung cancer".

Thus, neither WHO nor ACS can accept the survey results
without also accepting the possibility that long-standing claims
about smoking are in error.
```

```
This smoking issue reminds me of some articles I've read about how
many expensive studies have been done to try to get rid of obesity
(sometimes in school districts) - without success.

It seems to be quite acceptable to condemn fatties, after all it's
their choice (even though their separated-at-birth twin is just as
fat).
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:801d95d7-eb25-40b8-b579-23d9ff881331@f3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com...
> On 2 Dec, 02:23, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
[snip]
> > I am not certain how that question relates to the obligation,
> > of those making scientific claims, to establish credible scientific
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> carinogen) would help you?

H'm, I believe I could help epidemiologists, so, no, in my
opinion, a repeat of the experiment would be of no value
to either of us.

> Or pehaps you would need a detailed
> analysis of the chemical pathways permitting the carcinogen to enter
> the cell and cause said damage?

Unnecessary.

> Don't you place any emphasis on the association of a carcinogenic
> chemical with a damaged cell?

I would not use the term "carcinogenic chemical" (it implies
the relation "chemical causes cancer") and prefer the term
"carcinogenic factors" (which implies nothing more than an
association that may lead to cancer).

I will say that my emphasis involves the association between
carcinogenic factors in chemicals and damage to cells.

> I am tempted to ask whether you would
> remove all three forms of asbestos from a building without protective
> equipment because the exact mechanism by which asbestos causes lung
> cancer has not been proven?

Not that I would be involved in such activities; but, if I
were, I would chose to wear protective clothing because
of the dust and dirt and not because of any fear of
mesothelioma.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0m34l3pqd3pr7mabe2h4eofml4jku947pl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <g743l3hfg6bsm31vlmqt1bdna9mvlaos2b@4ax.com> <Axg4j.19049$mb.16643@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <a6e9111c-5320-43ce-b8f7-b4f5c328001c@e1g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <B0i4j.6679$k27.1089@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Sat, 1 Dec 2007 12:49:17 -0600, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>trials, ...

Hundreds of attempts to induce cancer in animals by exposing them to tobacco or smoke have
ALL FAILED. Such trials on humans are not permitted on ethical grounds.  What we have is
statistical correlation provided by epidemiology, which reports smokers got lung cancer
more often than nonsmokers. 

That experiments do not work on animals suggests a confounder unique to humans, such as
diet. Animals don't eat Big Macs or deep fried Twinkies. If epidemiologists were inclined,
they'd find exposure to professional wrestling and supermarket tabloids also correlates
with lung cancer. The confounder is poverty, thus the high fat diet of poor people. 

What most reports don't tell you is that dose/response forms an inverted V. One pack a day
smokers get the most lung cancer; three pack a day smokers are back to the nonsmoking
baseline. If smoking caused lung cancer, dose/response would at least be linear, more
likely exponential. The explanation is that one pack a day smokers are (statistically)
poorer than average; three pack a day smokers are high earners. 

There is a mathematical techinque for discounting confounders, assuming you know what they
are. You do a linear regression between the confounding variable and the dependent
variable, then apply the correlation coefficient to the independent variable. Careful
reading of smoking studies shows they do just that for irrelevant confounders such as
occupation and geography, but carefully 'accidentally overlook' the one that matters.

Why would scientists do that? Because of financial incentives. Lay people see antismoking
as a religious cause. Scientists, being objective and unemotional, see it as a mortgage
payment. Big Pharma either turned them into or exploited their inclination to be whores.
```

- **References:** `<5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <g743l3hfg6bsm31vlmqt1bdna9mvlaos2b@4ax.com> <Axg4j.19049$mb.16643@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <a6e9111c-5320-43ce-b8f7-b4f5c328001c@e1g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <B0i4j.6679$k27.1089@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <0m34l3pqd3pr7mabe2h4eofml4jku947pl@4ax.com> <gbv4j.10375$_m.2156@bignews4.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
>> Why would scientists do that? Because of financial incentives. Lay people see antismoking
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> what you say above and the scientists who are pushing global warming? :-)


Well yes.  I stopped smoking nicotine, and started smoking pot as I read 
it.  It is becoming more and more amusing, and I think the two facts are 
related.

It's been a while since I smoked a doob on a Sunday morning, and I've 
missed it.  Back when I had a roomy, we use to always sit at the front 
window, smoke a doob, and sorta do a review of the week. Always on 
Sunday morning.  We could watch the regular congregation of the corner 
church arrive and depart, and use it to time cooking our breakfast. 
Called it "going to church", even if the two of us are low-life, 
dope-smoking criminals who would be as likely to walk down the street 
naked as enter a church.

Thats a valuable thing to be able to do, with a friend, and know that 
you will get honest, and likely real feelings back, even if you do not 
agree. The other view-point is as real as my own.

I think we have become a bunch of fuddie-duddies. We argue like pompous 
old men, bickering politely, while the world just-keeps-on-happening. 
And I am not sure that what is happening is me, or me and you, or 
everybody else. But I do not like it a lot.  I think it sucks(the 
current world, that is), in fact, but I also think I can improve it. 
Just maybe. So I am going to keep trying.

I get the impression that you do not want anything changed, ever.  What 
exists is perfect, or god is imperfect, one or the other, and because 
the latter is intolerable, the former must be true.  That is, because 
you believe in God, you also believe that you must accept the world as 
it is.

I'm far more existential than that. I believe the world is what we 
perceive it to be, and we alter our perceptions consciously. That means 
we create our own worlds, literally. That implies that nothing is 
certain, unless we can get enough people to believe it so.

Donald<--A old hippie Cobol coder.
```

```
On Sun, 2 Dec 2007 13:49:23 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>Wow, Donald, that is almost the opposite of that I think. :-) Personally,
>I believe that the world was created perfect, but the sin and corruption
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

If the word was perfect, and part of the world was man - then man was
perfect as well.

Each generation sees that the change is for the worse - but it doesn't
take much studying of history to see that the world was a lot worse
before.     Of course, we must define what criteria we are using.

Here are some proposed criteria:
1.   Likelihood of being killed by other people.
2.   Likelihood of starving.
3.   Opportunity to get educated.
4.   Freedom to leave an oppressor.
5.   Freedom to have a religion other than your king's.
...

Add your own criteria and then put figures on them to demonstrate that
things are worse today than in previous generations.    We don't have
figures back at creation - but certainly we can extrapolate from, say
a thousand years ago.
```

```
On Sun, 2 Dec 2007 03:48:07 -0600, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote:

>> Why would scientists do that? Because of financial incentives. Lay people see antismoking
>> as a religious cause. Scientists, being objective and unemotional, see it as a mortgage

than will, truth or money. It takes organization to marshall money and political forces,
to keep  troops pointed in the same direction rather than bickering with each other.

side.  I don't know the answer.
```

```
On Mon, 3 Dec 2007 00:51:04 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>
…[73 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.

I'm with you on those points Pete.  I quit smoking almost 7 years ago
but I should have quit much, much sooner.  I was a pack and half a day
smoker and I quit cold turkey.  However, the damage to my lungs was
already done.  I know suffer from emphysema.  My lung capacity has
been decreased by 30%.  I wont get any better, but now that I don't
smoke, it won't get any worse.

I don't know what some people have been reading, but there is plenty
of scientific proof that smoking can cause lung cancer.  It certainly
causes emphysema COPD and other illnesses.

Looking at statistics from just the UK,  smoking kills around 114,000
people in the each year.

Of these deaths, about 42,800 are from smoking-related cancers, 30,600
from cardiovascular disease and 29,100 die slowly from emphysema and
other chronic lung diseases.

Cigarettes contain more than 4000 chemical compounds and at least 400
toxic substances.

When you inhale, a cigarette burns at 700ï¿½C at the tip and around 60ï¿½C
in the core. This heat breaks down the tobacco to produce various
toxins.

As a cigarette burns, the residues are concentrated towards the butt.

The products that are most damaging are:

Tar, a carcinogen (substance that causes cancer);
Nicotine is addictive and increases cholesterol levels in your body;
Carbon monoxide reduces oxygen in the body;
Components of the gas and particulate phases cause chronic obstructive
pulmonary disorder (COPD).

The damage caused by smoking is influenced by:

The number of cigarettes smoked;
Whether the cigarette has a filter;
How the tobacco has been prepared. 

Saying that smoking doesn't cause cancer is ludicrous based on all the
available scientific evidence.

Smokers are more likely to get cancer than non-smokers. This is
particularly true of lung cancer, throat cancer and mouth cancer,
which hardly ever affect non-smokers.  Check out the statistics from
the CDC or anyother reputable health organization.  The proof is in
the pudding, as they say.

The link between smoking and lung cancer is clear.

Ninety percent of lung cancer cases are due to smoking.

If no-one smoked, lung cancer would be a rare diagnosis - only 0.5 per
cent of people who've never touched a cigarette develop lung cancer.

One in ten moderate smokers and almost one in five heavy smokers (more
than 15 cigarettes a day) will die of lung cancer.


Your proabability of smoking and not getting some kind of lung related
disease is about the same as winning at a slot machine.  

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"From there to here, from here to there, 
funny things are everywhere. 
If you never did, you should. 
These things are fun and fun is good." 
--- Dr. Seuss
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

- **References:** `<5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <g743l3hfg6bsm31vlmqt1bdna9mvlaos2b@4ax.com> <Axg4j.19049$mb.16643@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <a6e9111c-5320-43ce-b8f7-b4f5c328001c@e1g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <B0i4j.6679$k27.1089@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <0m34l3pqd3pr7mabe2h4eofml4jku947pl@4ax.com>`

```
Well, you guys that smoke: you absolutely have the right to believe or
disbelieve the evidence and do what you like to your own bodies.  But does
that give you the right to make others share the experience?  I hate being
around smokers: I have to contend with the ash and casually dropped butts, I
have to take the stink on my clothes home, and I REALLY don't want to
breathe the air that's saturated with smoke, first- or second-hand.  I have
the right to go anywhere public that smokers go.   I can get my smoking
friends to sit down-wind from me.  But that does me no good at all in a
group of any size.  If smokers have a right to smoke, then they also have an
obligation to protect non-smokers from the results of their habit.

PL
```

```
1. Evidence that tobacco caused cancer was in the public domain but

2. Global warming is a fact (you have said so yourself).

3. The fact is that human activity is contributing to that warming.

4. The only question is whether the rise in global temperatures by the
next century will be limited to 1-2 degrees C or as high as 11 degrees
C as one model predicts.

ecosystems, cause a rise in sea levels and deprive many island peoples
of their homes and livelihoods. Of course, as Judson doesn't live on
an island with a max elevation of less than one metre, Judson isn't
concerned about such matters.
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> writing?

I have maintained from the beginning that we *don't know for certain* if global
warming is happening, and even if we did, we *don't know for certain* that
humanity is causing it. I never said that either one was absolutely not happening.
It is the global warming proponents who have no doubts, which in and of itself
is alarming, considering the uncertainty of the evidence. It is this absolute,
unshakable, blind belief, when the evidence doesn't warrant it that distresses me
most. Such attitudes are the antithesis of what the scientific attitude should be.

And please don't rate on about belief in God. The situations are completely
different. If I believe in God and there is no God, then I am no worse off;
I will die and never know it. If you don't believe in God, and He does exist,
you are in trouble that makes global warming look like a picnic, for eternity.

The situation with global warming is different. If you global warming advocates
are wrong, a vast amount of money will have been wasted and a lot of draconian
laws will have been passed, damaging personal freedom (a vastly important thing)
for no reason and no benefit. A total waste. But if global warming is happening,
we will know for certain at some point and will deal with it then. If global
tempratures have increased only one degree in a century, we aren't going to be
fried in another 50 years, or even 100 years, if it takes that to know. And it
would be expected that by then we would have better technology to deal with it
if necessary. If we blow ourselves up in a war in the meantime, it becomes moot.

> As good a time as any. Except when it is too late.

The same could be said of imprisoning a person who you think is "probably guilty"
of serious crimes. You take drastic, vastly expensive action only when you know,
not when there is doubt.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net...
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
> not when there is doubt.

Ding! Ding! Ding!

...And the winner is... NOT the Global warming lobby!

The above is a persuader as far as I'm concerned. I accept that the case for 
Global warming is definitely not made.

Yes, I saw "An inconvenient truth", yes, I live in a place that is extremely 
sensitive about the environment, yes, I have many friends who are strong 
advocates of the case for Global Warming, but in this instance at least, I 
think Judson makes a valid case.

Nevertheless, whether the GWists are right or wrong, we should consider the 
possibility that there might be something in their argument and seek further 
evidence. While that evidence is being obtained, it wouldn't hurt for all of 
us to be aware of our actions, environmentally. Plant trees, recycle 
garbage, don't litter, don't rape the environment. If you enjoy fishing and 
hunting, observe quotas, and don't take more than you and your friends and 
neighbours can eat. (point being: if the quota says 50 and you can only use 
40... take 40.)

It is fair to say that an alarm has been rung.

We should not ignore it, but neither should we panic...

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
> We should not ignore it, but neither should we panic...

Thanks, Pete. I was beginning to think this whole debate was pointless;
that no one was going to be swayed. :-)

I agree with your assessment of what we should be doing. It is only the
drastic steps which may not be warranted, that I oppose.

I live in an area that was second (in the U.S.) only to Pittsburg, PA in
steel production for a long time, and there used to be a lot of strip
mining here for the raw materials. Such vast devastation of the environment
was unconscionably stupid and reckless. The EPA has done a lot of good, and
I applaud that. But in it's zeal has also done a lot of harm. To my mind,
the danger on both sides is when you become too zealous and unrestrained in
your thinking; absolutely certain and *have no doubt that you know exactly*
what should be done, when our knowledge is *always* incomplete. Stop. Think.
Realize our limitations. Use reason and common sense, commodities all too
lacking in today's world. But above all, act with humility, not hubris, when
contemplating things with vast cost and sweeping consequences that could be
wasted, damaging or even devastating. I see none of these attributes being
displayed by the global warming alarmists.
```

```
waiting a bit.
```

```
In article <t8qil3ll1tpc76rffun1n7n7oekmupjs2n@4ax.com>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>On Fri, 07 Dec 2007 15:32:21 GMT, spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
>wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Glacier melts for one 

In some areas. In other places, some glaciers are growing. 

>- but there is a large body of work showing
>damage.   But scientists get fame and fortune by proving the
>establishment wrong.   Get the evidence that they are all wrong (even
>GWB has accepted that they are not wrong) - that fame and fortune will
>be yours.

Sorry, you missed the point. "Damage" in this analogy is not simple warming, 
but *human-caused* global warming.
>
>>>I think a better analogy is fixing the crack in the dam.   We can wait
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>So if we didn't create it, it's not our problem, right?

That's where the 'crack in the dam' analogy breaks down: the dam is a human 
artifact, and thus (presumably) repairable by humans, regardless of the cause 
of the crack. But if we didn't create global warming, then it's a natural 
process -- and there isn't any reason to suppose that we *can* do anything 
about it.

>But what would you accept as evidence?

For starters, data showing that temperature increases *follow* the human 
activity that is alleged to cause them. Data so far shows that the bulk of the 
temperature increase in the last century and a quarter came *before* the human 
activity that is alleged to have caused it.
```

```
On Fri, 07 Dec 2007 15:57:17 GMT, spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
wrote:
>>Glacier melts for one 
>
>In some areas. In other places, some glaciers are growing. 

The difference isn't even close.

>>- but there is a large body of work showing
>>damage.   But scientists get fame and fortune by proving the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>but *human-caused* global warming.

Sorry, you missed the point.   We don't accept what nature gives us
just because it is natural.    It doesn't matter how global warming is
caused if we don't want those changes.

>>>>I think a better analogy is fixing the crack in the dam.   We can wait
>>>>to make sure that the leak will, in fact break the dam, we can wait
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>about it.

If we didn't create polio, then it's a natural process -- and there
isn't any reason to suppose that we *can* do anything about it.

Or not.

>>But what would you accept as evidence?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>activity that is alleged to have caused it.

What would you accept as evidence that global warming exists?

The evidence that there are particular causes is complex enough that
we cannot do the math in this forum.   Unfortunately, this means that
the proposed solutions also require similar studies and math.   I've
seen some studies saying that irrigation is a huge cause here - but
it's one with no cheap solution.

But those who fight the proposed solutions have been virtually united
in denying that global warming exists and that if it does exist, it
isn't our fault.    To most of us this looks like a quasi-religious
argument, not a rational argument.   It appears to be something you
don't want to hear, so you fight it.
```

```
In article <rosil31gd1msue1vnit25tq7jmtk30nli2@4ax.com>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>On Fri, 07 Dec 2007 15:57:17 GMT, spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
>wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>The difference isn't even close.

In any event, it's irrelevant to the question of whether the very slight (0.6 
degrees C since 1880) warming is a natural process, or the result of human 
activity. The planet has, in the past, been much warmer than it is now.
>
>>>- but there is a large body of work showing
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>caused if we don't want those changes.

Sorry, you missed the point. If it's a natural process, it's very unlikely 
that anything we do, or don't do, is going to have much effect on it.
>
>>>>>I think a better analogy is fixing the crack in the dam.   We can wait
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or not.

No, actually, it's a life form, not a natural process. There's plenty of proof 
that human activity can cause the extinction of other species. 
>
>>>But what would you accept as evidence?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>What would you accept as evidence that global warming exists?

That's not relevant, really -- if the earth is getting warmer as the result of 
natural climate cycles, there probably isn't a damn thing we can do about it, 
and our efforts are much better directed at figuring out how to cope with it, 
rather than in futile efforts to stop the unstoppable.

>The evidence that there are particular causes is complex enough that
>we cannot do the math in this forum.   Unfortunately, this means that
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>don't want to hear, so you fight it.

Actually, I'm the one here who is insisting on a rational argument, 
specifically that effects must *follow* their causes, rather than preceding 
them. The majority of the slight warming that has occurred so far came 
*before* the majority of the human activity that is alleged to have caused it. 
To continue to insist that said activity is the cause of something that 
occurred half a century previous is, to use your phrase, "not a rational 
argument."
```

- **References:** `<5uA4j.7739$N67.5951@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9b4dda98-ff62-4625-a812-1c7a28bd2876@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Y5_5j.9900$k27.6555@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <t3pil3dte4p1d1ahff9e0u69mbf0n7aljj@4ax.com> <SHd6j.3145$Vq.1205@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <t8qil3ll1tpc76rffun1n7n7oekmupjs2n@4ax.com> <e3e6j.3148$Vq.1673@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <rosil31gd1msue1vnit25tq7jmtk30nli2@4ax.com> <xWe6j.79635$Um6.12957@newssvr12.news.prodigy.net> <fp0jl3d6tl78alcul4t71du3ns6l8urshv@4ax.com> <F5g6j.3169$Vq.2594@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com>`

```

Doug Miller <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message
news:F5g6j.3169$Vq.2594@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com...
> >Of COURSE we can make changes in nature.   That's what we do!
>
> Evidence, please, that humans can influence climate...
>


Clear-cutting forests leaves them open to erosion; erosion changes water
drainage patterns; drainage patterns affect climate.

Clear-cutting forests changes the albedo of the area, which changes the
amont of heat reflected back into the atmosphere, which affects the climate.

Clear-cutting forests, in particular rain forests, reduces the amount of
rain that falls in the area.  That affects climate.

Air pollution in cities and industrial areas alters the nature of the
atmosphere, locally and with diminishing effects with distance.  That
affects the climate.

Altering the temperature of the oceans - by changing the colour of given
areas due to waste being added - affects the climate.

As human beings are part of the environment - of which the climate is just
one manifestation - EVERYTHING they do affects it.

PL
```

```
On 7 Dec, 16:55, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
>
> In any event, it's irrelevant to the question of whether the very slight (0.6
> degrees C since 1880) warming is a natural process, or the result of human
> activity. The planet has, in the past, been much warmer than it is now.
>

Whether the planet can naturally increase temperature globally is
neither here nor there. What is important are some facts:

1. The global temperature is increasing (even in the Antarctic).

2. Humans consume 400 years worth of solar energy in every year by
burning fossil fuels.

3. CO2 is a significant green house gas. There are others and there
are refrigerant gases too.

4. Better minds than ours have considered the the problem and
pronounced humanity guilty.

5. More scientists believe in God than deny global warming.


>
> Actually, I'm the one here who is insisting on a rational argument,
> specifically that effects must *follow* their causes, rather than preceding
> them. The majority of the slight warming that has occurred so far came
> *before* the majority of the human activity that is alleged to have caused it.

Have you considered that there has been a period of global cooling in
the last 50 years due to reduced solar effects?

> To continue to insist that said activity is the cause of something that
> occurred half a century previous is, to use your phrase, "not a rational
> argument."

That is not what anyone is saying. Few have cottoned on to the fact of
a period of global cooling since 1950. View the article on wikipedia
on global warming for further information.

> --
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -
```

```
On Fri, 07 Dec 2007 16:55:57 GMT, spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
wrote:

>In article <rosil31gd1msue1vnit25tq7jmtk30nli2@4ax.com>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>>On Fri, 07 Dec 2007 15:57:17 GMT, spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

And it has been a hell of lot colder too during the Ice Age.  The
planet was warmer many thousands of years ago when it was still in its
formative stagees.  The .6 C that you so proudly keep posting is
inaccurate.

The IPCC (Intergovermental Panel on Climate Change - a UN agency
tasked with studying these things) found that the global average air
temperature near the Earth's surface rose 0.74 ï¿½ 0.18 ï¿½C (1.33 ï¿½ 0.32
ï¿½F) during the last 100 years.  The panel also found that most of the
observed increase in globally averaged temperatures since the mid-20th
century is very likely due to the observed increase in anthropogenic
greenhouse gas concentrations".  Natural phenomena such as solar
variation combined with volcanoes probably had a small warming effect
from pre-industrial times to 1950 and a small cooling effect from 1950
onward.  These basic conclusions have been endorsed by at least 30
scientific societies and academies of science, including all of the
national academies of science of the major industrialized countries.

Increasing global temperatures will cause sea level to rise, and is
expected to increase the intensity of extreme weather events and to
change the amount and pattern of precipitation. Other effects of
global warming include changes in agricultural yields, glacier
retreat, species extinctions, and increases in the ranges of disease
vectors.

Remaining scientific uncertainties include the amount of warming
expected in the future, and how warming and related changes will vary
from region to region around the globe. 

But one thing is certain.  It is happening, much of it is man made and
it can be slowed if not altoghter eliminated.


>>>>- but there is a large body of work showing
>>>>damage.   But scientists get fame and fortune by proving the
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Before the Industrial Revolution, levels of CO2, the principal
greenhouse gas, stood at around 280 parts per million (ppm).

Today, CO2 concentrations are around 380 ppm and are rising between
two and three ppm per year as big energy-gobbling countries, such as
China and India, pursue their economic rise. 

There's your man-made "crack".

>>If we didn't create polio, then it's a natural process -- and there
>>isn't any reason to suppose that we *can* do anything about it.
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>argument."

Regards,

         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

were just napping."
---Rita Rudner
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Steve
```

```
On Sun, 9 Dec 2007 17:19:29 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Doug Miller" <spambait@milmac.com> wrote:
>> SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>not possibly* be 0.1 or -0.2! Absolutely amazing. :-)

That is because the .14 difference is within the .32 plus or minus
variance.  And besides, NASA has come to the same conclusions as the
IPCC did (see the link I provided in a previous post).  Here's a brief
synopis from NASA.

A computer model developed by the Goddard Institute was used to
simulate climate from 1880 through today (2007). The model included a
more comprehensive set of natural and human-made climate forcings than
previous studies, including changes in solar radiation, volcanic
particles, human-made greenhouse gases, fine particles such as soot,
the effect of the particles on clouds and land use. Extensive
evaluation of the modelï¿½s ability to simulate climate change is
contained in a companion paper to be published in Climate Dynamics.

The authors use the model for climate simulations of the 21st century
using both ï¿½business-as-usualï¿½ growth of greenhouse gas emissions and
an ï¿½alternative scenarioï¿½ in which emissions decrease slowly in the
next few decades and then rapidly to achieve stabilization of
atmospheric CO2 amount by the end of the century. Climate changes are
so large with ï¿½business-as-usualï¿½, with additional global warming of
2-3ï¿½C (3.6-5.4ï¿½F) that Hansen (Dr. James Hansen, of NASA's Goddard
Institute for Space Studies, New York) concludes ï¿½ï¿½business-as-usualï¿½
would be a guarantee of global and regional disasters.ï¿½

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
```

```
In article <b8b02bb2-48f8-48ed-b20a-dd7d20044d59@y5g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>So how do you explain the observations? 

I don't have an explanation. And I don't have to, either: the people who need 
to produce an explanation are the ones who insist that the observations 
demonstrate a human cause, since that hypothesis proposes that the effect 
preceded the cause.

>And, since the instruments
>used were not as accurate as modern ones, what do you believe the real
>variations have been over the last 100 years and what was the 'true'
>level of CO2 one hundred years ago?

I have no idea. And neither does anyone else -- which is kinda the point: 
we're being asked to make massive changes on the basis of speculation.
```

```
radically is as yet impossible to say, although there are some indications
that such is the case, as for example the drying up of the northern part of

assuming I can find the text.
```

```
>Well, I have a 1932 Collier's National Encyclopedia.  I couldn't find an
>entry specifically for global temperature changes, but the article on
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Sure -- read up on the Eocene epoch, for instance.

Here's a start:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eocene

Brief except:
"The Eocene global climate was perhaps the most homogeneous of the Cenozoic; 
the temperature gradient from equator to pole was only half that of today's, 
and deep ocean currents were exceptionally warm.[2] The polar regions were 
much warmer than today, perhaps as mild as the modern-day Pacific Northwest; 
temperate forests extended right to the poles, while rainy tropical climates 
extended as far north as 45 degrees latitude. The difference was greatest in 
the temperate latitudes; the climate in the tropics however, was probably 
similar to today's.(Stanley, 508)"
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F%z7j.8341$3J5.7988@newsfe20.lga>`
- **References:** `<%6w4j.7323$N67.2695@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <24585871-8019-42c5-b327-09e7ca7b6b38@s36g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <5uA4j.7739$N67.5951@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9b4dda98-ff62-4625-a812-1c7a28bd2876@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Y5_5j.9900$k27.6555@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <t3pil3dte4p1d1ahff9e0u69mbf0n7aljj@4ax.com> <SHd6j.3145$Vq.1205@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <t8qil3ll1tpc76rffun1n7n7oekmupjs2n@4ax.com> <e3e6j.3148$Vq.1673@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <rosil31gd1msue1vnit25tq7jmtk30nli2@4ax.com> <xWe6j.79635$Um6.12957@newssvr12.news.prodigy.net> <9IL6j.951$m6.324@newsfe18.lga> <iTZ6j.31588$Pv2.6649@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <fA%6j.2400$3b7.480@newsfe23.lga> <VK17j.31637$Pv2.5644@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <5Af7j.14$E01.6@newsfe22.lga> <Vhg7j.77078$YL5.15122@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net>`

```
As an honest person must, I looked up the sites Doug mentions and a bunch of
others.  It's true what he says: they all state that since about 1860 global
temperatures have risen by about 0.75 degrees.  I was surprised: I had an
idea it was more like 2 degrees.

But leaving it at that is somewhat misleading.  Temperatures did start
rising, very slowly, about 1860.  Then there was a period of little change,
and a period of slight cooling.  But ALL the charts I looked at showed that
starting in about 1970 or 1980, there began a sustained rise in temperature,
at a much quicker rate than any time in the period; this accounts for .4
degrees or more of the observed total increase.

Doug is right, to a point: 0.75 degrees isn't very much - apparently,
anyway.  But that much of a change, calculated world-wide, represents an
enormous amount of energy.  All the sites I looked at agree that there will
be further increases of from 1.1 to 6.5 degrees (C) in the next 100 years.
That's a further enormous-squared amount of energy in the atmosphere.  A
simple extrapolation of the last 30 years would indicate an increase of 1.2
degrees; and there's no guarantee that the rate won't accelerate.

Very little comfort can be taken in the bald statement that temperatures
have risen only 0.75 degrees since 1860.  Most of that rise has come in the
last 30 years and it shows no sign of stopping.  There's no question that
global warming is happening.  The only questions are: the rate at which it's
happening, will the rate change, and how long is the process likely to last?
Far better now to start looking at alternatives than to wait until the
crisis is upon us.

Judson doesn't like the idea because it may cost trillions of dollars: who
will take all that money?  There's money to be made here; that should make
the whole thing more attractive to the Judsons of the world.

PL
```

- **Message-ID:** `<n1ltl35jlsrrcfi1l2925p33dp6dal5c5o@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<t8qil3ll1tpc76rffun1n7n7oekmupjs2n@4ax.com> <e3e6j.3148$Vq.1673@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <rosil31gd1msue1vnit25tq7jmtk30nli2@4ax.com> <xWe6j.79635$Um6.12957@newssvr12.news.prodigy.net> <9IL6j.951$m6.324@newsfe18.lga> <iTZ6j.31588$Pv2.6649@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <fA%6j.2400$3b7.480@newsfe23.lga> <VK17j.31637$Pv2.5644@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <5Af7j.14$E01.6@newsfe22.lga> <Vhg7j.77078$YL5.15122@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net> <F%z7j.8341$3J5.7988@newsfe20.lga>`

```
On Tue, 11 Dec 2007 11:44:08 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>But leaving it at that is somewhat misleading.  Temperatures did start
>rising, very slowly, about 1860.  Then there was a period of little change,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>degrees or more of the observed total increase.

This doesn't necessarily indicate that we did something significantly
different around 1970 or 1980.    When climate has changed rapidly in
the past it has been after slow changes get far enough to push the
rate of change over the top.    Ocean currents have accounted for most
of these, although desertification has done it as well.   The big
changes have occurred when the ice melts enough  (or freezes enough)
to change the currents between the Atlantic and the Arctic.

If it is our actions which have been sufficient to trigger this
increase, then controlling CO2 emissions could very well be too
little, too late.    Whether or not it is our actions that caused it,
any actions we can make to ameliorate the effects are likely to
require much bigger costs than the politicians are talking about.
```

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> As an honest person must, I looked up the sites Doug mentions and a bunch of
> others.  It's true what he says: they all state that since about 1860 global
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

Actually, I am much more concerned about the draconian laws affecting
personal liberty than about the money. Historically, it's much easier and
more likely for governments to take away liberties than to restore them.

As I've said before, the climate is constantly changing, and a modest
increase or decrease in temperatures over several decades is probably
par for the course. We just do not have accurate historic data for long
enough back to know for certain. There are a plethora of factors that
affect climate, and practically none of them are well understood.
Certainly the whole is poorly understood, at best. At current state of
technology, it is not possible to absolutely guarantee it will or will
not be raining in any given place even 12 hour later, all the time. Some
times and places, yes you can, but not all places all the time. Such
unpredictability makes a mockery of claims that we can know for certain
what will be happening one or two years later, to say nothing of 50 or
100 years later. It's hubris to the point of absurdity. That claim of
"certainty" is a Big Lie so vast that it's apparently believable to some
who would reject a similar but lesser lie. Nobody questions that short
term weather forecasting is that uncertain, because it is demonstrated
to us all the time. But when you stretch out the time frame, that quick
negative feedback is missing, giving the mistaken impression that your
new hypothesis is correct, because there is no visible proof to the
contrary. But the important word here is "yet".

But, could the GW alarmists be right? Sure, they could be right. But they
could be wrong too, and I think that just as likely. And they have to be
right and not wrong in a whole series of claims for it to make sense for
us to take such big and expensive measures. Consider this:

1. Global warming must be true

2. We must be causing or seriously aiding it, or at least the massive
   efforts we undertake must be effective

3. The sum of the long term effects must be seriously negative. (No using
   the great verdant areas currently too far north for agriculture to
   feed ever increasing populations or for living space, stuff like that.
   Have you ever thought about how many people could live in Siberia and
   Northern Canada if it were warmer there? Who knows the true potential?)

4. The increase in tempratures and the cumulative effects must be more
   than linear, otherwise in 100 years we won't notice the additional .6
   degree difference.

5. There cannot be currently unknown effects, such as we have seen many
   times before in nature, that will limit or completely reverse the
   effects over time (e.g. increase in game population -> increase in
   predator population -> reduction in game population -> reduction in
   predator population). The ecology is amazingly self regulating, even
   to cleaning up our messes, over time. And we don't begin to know all
   that may be out there.

6. The consequences of the efforts we make cannot be worse than those for
   us doing nothing, or far less.

If they are wrong in *any one* of the above assumptions, the laws we pass
and the money we spend could be completely wasted. Even assuming a 1:2
probability of each of the above being true, the probability that *all*
of them are true is 1/2 * 1/2 * 1/2 * 1/2 * 1/2 * 1/2 = .015625 or 1:64.
Even if every one of them had an 80% probability of being true, the odds
that all would be true is .262144, slightly better than 1 in 4. The odds
of #5 is clearly unknown. And not one of the above items can be verified
through direct observation or experimentation. Not good betting odds.

And I am not reassured when they make claims that I am absolutely certain
cannot be proved, based on data that is far less reliable and accurate
than they claim. When people start claiming they can *know with certainty*
the average global tempratures 1000 years ago to .1 degree by looking at
tree rings or ice cores, it's all I can do to laugh out loud. Now, how many
millions of 1000 year old tree rings and ice core samples did you say you
processed to .1 degree accuracy from the great deserts, the 3/5 of the
Earth's surface covered by oceans and the great plains, to know this? :-)

If I was approached by someone, showing the same characteristics as the GW
alarmists, who wanted to sell me something, their demeanor alone would
throw up a big red flag. When people make claims of certainty when you know
their evidence couldn't possibly justify certainty, that's another big red
flag. And when these same people want you to make very serious personal
sacrifices, and pay them truckloads of your money, based on their "there
can be no doubt" completely unverifiable hypothesis, it's time to "get out
of Dodge". :-)
```

```
On Tue, 11 Dec 2007 13:58:37 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:


>Actually, I am much more concerned about the draconian laws affecting
>personal liberty than about the money. Historically, it's much easier and
>more likely for governments to take away liberties than to restore them.

Laws limiting us from spewing our wastes into public places are quite
old.    Are you objecting to this whole body of laws?   Or objecting
to clean-air laws?   Or just CO2?    And whose liberties are being
taken away?   Personal liberties (taxing car users)?   Corporate
liberties (taxing smokestack)?


>But, could the GW alarmists be right? Sure, they could be right. But they
>could be wrong too, and I think that just as likely. 

I don't think the likelihoods are equal.

>And they have to be
>right and not wrong in a whole series of claims for it to make sense for
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>and the money we spend could be completely wasted. 

Switching from a society that changes the composition of the
atmosphere to one that attempts to keep the status quo in our
atmosphere is not bad stewardship - even if global warming isn't true.
The ecology will adapt - it has a history of doing so, with species
dying off or suffering greatly.    Letting nature handle our mistakes
is expensive.

What are the bad consequences that you are afraid we might have by
having cleaner emissions?    That the costs will grow to be a
noticeable percentage of what we are spending in Iraq?   (You can use
that as an example of the "establishment being wrong").
```

```
On Tue, 11 Dec 2007 14:53:12 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>> That the costs will grow to be a
>> noticeable percentage of what we are spending in Iraq?   (You can use
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>attacks that may have been made on citizens here? Answer: we don't know.

Let's say we are not nurturing a breeding ground for terrorists.  (I
don't believe that for a minute).    We are in there for a different
reason than was stated at the start.    The establishment was wrong
back then, and we have spent lots of lives and money - for what you
state is a good cause anyway.

If we spend some money (not war-like money) on cleaning up our
emissions, and it turns out that CO2 wasn't the global warming problem
we thought it was - we could point out other advantages that result
from closer monitoring of emissions world wide.   (China)     The cost
of being wrong isn't in human lives, it isn't in destruction of
properties, it isn't in possibly making the problem worse.    Just
spending some money to keep the atmosphere closer to the way we got
it.
```

```
In article <d4d48409-df9f-44b5-a729-d5268a415a63@l1g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>It is certainly true that the earth has been warmer in the past (it
>was molten rock at one stage) and it is also true that at warmer
>periods it did have significant life inhabiting it. 

Yes, indeed it did. The Eocene Epoch is one such period.

>It has also been
>colder.

And that is, of course, exactly my point: periodic warming and cooling -- to 
much greater extents than what has been observed in the last century -- is a 
normal cycle for the planet, that has been going on for much longer than human 
beings have been around.
```

_(reply depth: 37)_

```
In article <Br6dndnruMqfHvfanZ2dnUVZ_tHinZ2d@golden.net>, donald tees <donaldtees@execulink.com> wrote:
>Doug Miller wrote:
>> In article
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>were not around is not that desirable to the rest of us humans.

Only if you think that the climate we have right now necessarily must be the 
best possible, or the only one that could possibly be suited to humans. In any 
event, there's no reason to believe that we have the ability to alter a 
natural cycle that's been continuing for millions of years, one that started 
long before we arrived -- and will continue long after we're gone.
```

```
Doug Miller <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message
news:ztxdj.27478$4V6.10170@newssvr14.news.prodigy.net...
> >> >
> >> >> Sure there is -- this is alleged to be a global effect. There's no
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >Here's something that's within the reach of current technology.  You
may or
> >> >may not know that there's a huge inflow of water from the Atlantic to
the
> >> >Mediterranean.  So: let's block the strait of Gibraltar.  (Great for
the
> >> >land transportation and tourist industries).  That will have
>>gigantic<<
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >If you will specify which of my sentences you think need butressing - I
will
> >determine if it's worth my while to do so.
>
> Specifically the claim that blocking Gibraltar "will have >>gigantic<<
effects
> on globabl conditions."
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>         Doug Miller (alphageek at milmac dot com)


A couple of consequences ought to be obvious:

1) the circulation patterns of water in the Atlantic would be changed.  It's
possible that the Gulf Stream would be shifted north a bit.  This would
affect the climates of England and northern Europe.  In any case there would
be changes.

2) The Mediterranean, over a long period of time, would become shallower due
to the excess of evaporation over input (from the Nile, the Black Sea and
other sources).  It would become saltier and a large area of sea-bottom
would become dry.  This in turn would change the flow of the lower reaches
of all the rivers that empty into the Med.

I doubt that you'll find many discussions of this anywhere.  You might find
the reverse, because there was a time when the Med. was in fact not
connected to the Atlantic.  Whatever: I offer it as an example of how human
activities could change the climate in a global context.

PL
```

```
Mr. Miller,

Your response is exactly what I expected.  You don't care to think about the
factual part of the discussion and resort instead to nitpicking, to
obfuscating about the meaning of words.  I don't know what you would
consider "gigantic" or "global" (since users of your style of argument are
careful to refrain from committing themselves to any definable stand which
could be rebutted) but an event which would have effects from Iceland east
to Mesopotamia, and from Iceland south to North Africa - qualifies as
gigantic so far as I'm concerned.  I haven't got the energy just now to
calculate just what percentage of the earth's surface that comes out to: but
I'll hazard 5%.  My mistake in this is trying to find ONE cause which would
be big enough to impress you: I should have stuck to the obvious: that while
any given human activity affects perhaps only a tiny portion of the global
environment, adding them all up affects most of the world.

How do you feel about nuclear winter?

PL
```

```
In article <HAQdj.3626$M24.1008@newsfe17.lga>, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>Mr. Miller,
>
>Your response is exactly what I expected.  You don't care to think about the
>factual part of the discussion and resort instead to nitpicking, to
>obfuscating about the meaning of words. 

Not at all. You claimed that closing off Gibralter would necessarily have 
gigantic effects on a global scale. You have failed to show that the effects 
would be either gigantic or global.

> I don't know what you would
>consider "gigantic" or "global" 

While "gigantic" is perhaps a relative term, the meaning of "global" is 
clearly defined and widely understood. Widely understood, that is, except 
by you, as you seem to think that "part of northern Europe" and "global" are 
synonymous.

>(since users of your style of argument are
>careful to refrain from committing themselves to any definable stand which
>could be rebutted) but an event which would have effects from Iceland east
>to Mesopotamia, 

Oh, so Mesopotamia is part of northern Europe now? Or are you changing your 
claim again?

Either way, it doesn't change the facts that:
a) the range of effects you claim is *not* global
b) you have failed to provide any evidence for your claim. [Hint: get a 
dictionary and compare the definitions of "assertion" and "evidence". You seem 
to have the two confused.]

>and from Iceland south to North Africa - qualifies as
>gigantic so far as I'm concerned. 

Previously, you said "England and northern Europe." Please make up your mind.

Also please note that "Iceland south to North Africa" still isn't global.

> I haven't got the energy just now to
>calculate just what percentage of the earth's surface that comes out to: but
>I'll hazard 5%.  

I'll accept that as a rough approximation. Will you accept that 5% of the 
globe isn't anywhere close to all of it, or even most of it?

>My mistake in this is trying to find ONE cause which would
>be big enough to impress you: I should have stuck to the obvious: that while
>any given human activity affects perhaps only a tiny portion of the global
>environment, adding them all up affects most of the world.

No, your mistake in this was in making a claim for which you have no 
supporting evidence, and attempting to compensate for your failure to 
substantiate it by repeatedly changing the claim.
>
>How do you feel about nuclear winter?

I fear that about as much as I fear global warming, the bogeyman, little 
green men from Mars, the Loch Ness Monster, chupacabra, and other 
equally mythical things.
>
>
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I don't know how relevant you will find this, but you might want to look at:
    "Climate Control Requires a Dam at the Strait of Gibraltar"
at
    http://www.agu.org/sci_soc/eosrjohnson.html
which says (in part)
    " If it is validated by the onset of ice-sheet growth in Canada and cooling 
in northern Europe, a partial dam at the Strait of Gibraltar could be 
constructed to limit the outflow and reverse the climate deterioration, thus 
holding off the next ice age."
```

- **References:** `<t8qil3ll1tpc76rffun1n7n7oekmupjs2n@4ax.com> <e3e6j.3148$Vq.1673@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <rosil31gd1msue1vnit25tq7jmtk30nli2@4ax.com> <xWe6j.79635$Um6.12957@newssvr12.news.prodigy.net> <9IL6j.951$m6.324@newsfe18.lga> <iTZ6j.31588$Pv2.6649@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <d4d48409-df9f-44b5-a729-d5268a415a63@l1g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <orgaj.317$6%.167@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <Br6dndnruMqfHvfanZ2dnUVZ_tHinZ2d@golden.net> <Cuvaj.31013$lD6.28940@newssvr27.news.prodigy.net> <ll2lm3t6m2p2kklqqt9alj8h8turb10rqe@4ax.com> <g%waj.56256$eY.46049@newssvr13.news.prodigy.net> <anzaj.8161$E01.1643@newsfe22.lga> <ZoRaj.84271$YL5.40055@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net> <gZjdj.4378$R55.130@newsfe13.lga> <ztxdj.27478$4V6.10170@newssvr14.news.prodigy.net> <gKEdj.9352$E01.1837@newsfe22.lga>`

```
On Dec 30, 5:00 pm, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> Doug Miller <spamb...@milmac.com> wrote in message
>
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> be changes.

While you are correct that there is a surface inflow from the Atlantic
to the Med, there is also an outflow at depth through the straits. The
outflow is of denser, saltier water.

You haven't provided any mechanism that would 'shift the gulf stream'.
Nor have you provided any affect that might occur. The asserted
'changes' may not exist at all, or may balance out, or may be in any
direction.


> 2) The Mediterranean, over a long period of time, would become shallower due
> to the excess of evaporation over input (from the Nile, the Black Sea and
> other sources).

Yes, the med does evaporate and the water become saltier and denser.
It then sinks and creates an outflow back into the Atlantic, which you
speculation would also block.

> It would become saltier and a large area of sea-bottom
> would become dry.  This in turn would change the flow of the lower reaches
> of all the rivers that empty into the Med.

While those would be a consequence of a lower level in the Med, you
haven't actually shown that a lower level would actually result.

> I doubt that you'll find many discussions of this anywhere.  You might find
> the reverse, because there was a time when the Med. was in fact not
> connected to the Atlantic.  Whatever: I offer it as an example of how human
> activities could change the climate in a global context.

There is no 'human activity' attempting to block the strait, so it
shows nothing at all.

If the strait were blocked it would be because the African plate is
moving north lifting up the alps and creating the volcanoes and
earthquakes in Italy, Greece and Turkey, and could eventually reach
Spain, but at the same time the Rift Valley is opening due to the same
movement and this could split the red sea to refill the med.

None of which is 'human activity'.
```

```
On 30 Dec 2007, 04:00, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> Doug Miller <spamb...@milmac.com> wrote in message
>  So: let's block the strait of Gibraltar.  (Great for
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Armed with a degree in Marine Biology (which is not Oceanography but
did include that subject matter but knowledge has no meaning in this
group) I would be interested in knowing how blocking off the Med would
cause the Gulf Stream to deviate. The Mediterranean Outflow Current
would cease to exist and would cease to warm the NW coast of Scotland
(where Mediterranean species of plankton can be found). That would
cause a drop in land temperatures in Scotland perhaps reducing it to
the same range as experienced in Canada.

> 2) The Mediterranean, over a long period of time, would become shallower due
> to the excess of evaporation over input (from the Nile, the Black Sea and
> other sources).  It would become saltier and a large area of sea-bottom
> would become dry.

Previously the Med has dried out (there are huge salt deposits being
mined in (I think) Sicily (but it might be Sardinia).

> This in turn would change the flow of the lower reaches
> of all the rivers that empty into the Med.

They would still empty in to the Med. And dry up.
```

```
On 21 Dec 2007, 15:58, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
> In article <anzaj.8161$E01.1...@newsfe22.lga>, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is also a hotter denser more saline current which flows out of
the Mediterranean, turns northwards and surfaces off of the NW coast
of Scotland. The net result is that the Med is more saline than the
Atlantic. The Med has also, in Doc's golden hayze, dried up completely
in the past.
```

- **References:** `<t3pil3dte4p1d1ahff9e0u69mbf0n7aljj@4ax.com> <SHd6j.3145$Vq.1205@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <t8qil3ll1tpc76rffun1n7n7oekmupjs2n@4ax.com> <e3e6j.3148$Vq.1673@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <rosil31gd1msue1vnit25tq7jmtk30nli2@4ax.com> <xWe6j.79635$Um6.12957@newssvr12.news.prodigy.net> <9IL6j.951$m6.324@newsfe18.lga> <iTZ6j.31588$Pv2.6649@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <d4d48409-df9f-44b5-a729-d5268a415a63@l1g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <orgaj.317$6%.167@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com>`

```
On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 21:55:40 GMT, spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
wrote:

>And that is, of course, exactly my point: periodic warming and cooling -- to 
>much greater extents than what has been observed in the last century -- is a 
>normal cycle for the planet, that has been going on for much longer than human 
>beings have been around.

Yep.   And floods and turning forests to deserts have been happening
for much longer than human beings have been around.    That doesn't
mean that humans today don't contribute to floods and deforestation.
And it doesn't mean that humans today can't change our behavior that
creates human caused floods and deforestation.

Just because something happens naturally doesn't mean humans don't do
it too.   And just because an undesirable effect was caused by nature
doesn't mean that humans can't do things to make the results better on
us.
```

```
On 7 Dec, 15:57, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
> In article <t8qil3ll1tpc76rffun1n7n7oekmupj...@4ax.com>, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> >On Fri, 07 Dec 2007 15:32:21 GMT, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> In some areas. In other places, some glaciers are growing.

That would be because the melt water drains to the base of the
glaciers and lubricates the ice as it moves across rocky substrates.
Result - the glacier moves faster than the leading edge melts, extends
and may appear to grow. The crunch is how much snow is being deposited
at the head of the glacier? In Greenland the snow is being deposited
at a slower rate and the glaciers are shrinking away from the valley
sides. The surface snow that remains is darkening because of pollution
and that leads to an increase in melt rate. So which glaciers do you
know of which are actually increasing in size AND volume?

>
> >- but there is a large body of work showing
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> but *human-caused* global warming.

As long as you can deny the human aspect you can justify ignoring
global warming. Even GWB admits that global warming is happening. I
suspect that the Ayatollahs in Iran would point out that nuclear power
plants will mitigate the human contribution to global warming (it
won't by much).

>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>*no* evidence that human activity is causing the planet to get warmer.

What evidence have you that we are not driving global warming? Is a
90% certainty that humans are contributing to GW not enough?
Statistically, 95% would be a clincher.

>
> >There may be *no* evidence that that dam crack was created by people.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> activity that is alleged to have caused it.

The data goes back to approx 1750 and shows a steady increase in
temperature compared to expected natural causes since the industrial
revolution.


>
> --
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's time to throw all their damned tea in the harbor again.
```

```
On Sun, 09 Dec 2007 22:14:22 GMT, spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
wrote:

>>Have you any accurate data to support your hypothesis? Have you any
>>inaccurate data to support your hypothesis?
>
>Another logical fallacy. The burden of proof lies with those making claims, to 
>show them to be true, not on others to show them to be false.

Both sides are making claims.    And while majority doesn't make right
in science, those who disagree with the majority of the scientific
papers on the topic need good evidence before they will be taken
seriously by other scientists.

You can take any scientific issue - the more famous scientists get
correspondence all the time from people who have "theories" why
science (and the evidence) is wrong.   And the Internet makes it easy
for us to discover a lot of these "theories".
```

```
more interested in the evidence behind the motivations of those who espouse

mass-movement phenomenon. Suggested reading: "The True Believer" by Eric 
Hoffer.
```
