###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-13T10:11:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yQc8j.21333$_m.13782@bignews4.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<5uA4j.7739$N67.5951@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9b4dda98-ff62-4625-a812-1c7a28bd2876@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Y5_5j.9900$k27.6555@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <t3pil3dte4p1d1ahff9e0u69mbf0n7aljj@4ax.com> <SHd6j.3145$Vq.1205@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com> <t8qil3ll1tpc76rffun1n7n7oekmupjs2n@4ax.com> <13luanphu2aqm02@corp.supernews.com> <7e50m31ephslmi9lb2q6gipv35dum6ql3o@4ax.com> <s0Z7j.13354$k27.4976@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <okj2m3d5omjon0re0vci588hqrqkptb4m2@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> walk...

The English word "control" means to direct and determine the outcome.
To "have control of X" or "be able to control X" means 'X' won't get out
of your ability to direct it. Your "control" intent may be only to limit 'X',
but if 'X' can overwhelm your efforts, you do not "control X". Floods
absolutely can overwhelm our efforts, without doubt.

It is too presumptuous a word for forces of nature. One can "control"
a computer mouse, or (for the most part) "control" an automobile, but
we don't "control" floods. We can "reduce" the probability of flooding
in limited areas by better drainage, levees, etc. But we don't truly
"control" floods, even small ones, to any degree in all locations. We
attempt to control murder, illegal drug use and other crimes, but even
there we only mitigate it, we do not "control" it, or there would be
none.

Knowing you, Howard, you're likely to say that an automobile can also
overwhelm our efforts to control it, so I'll preempt that. A skilled
driver should be able to keep complete control of a vehicle, unless
a *qualitively different* problem occurs, such as mechanical failure
or error by another driver. A big flood is a only quantitively
different from a small flood, not qualitively different.
```

```
http://epw.senate.gov/public/index.cfm?FuseAction=Minority.Blogs&ContentReco
rd_id=c9554887-802a-23ad-4303-68f67ebd151c
>

Which particular article?  I looked over the whole first page and checked
several sub-pages but I could not find support for that assertion.

> >
> >>> I think a better analogy is fixing the crack in the dam.   We can
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> The consequence may be our problem, but the fix certainly isn't. If there
is
> significant global warming and if it is caused by factors beyond our
control
> (such as increased radiation from the sun), then trying to overcome an
> otherwise natural process is futile. Might as well stop volcanoes, tidal
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Evidence of what? Global warming? or the "cause" of global warming? I'm
much
> more interested in the evidence behind the motivations of those who
espouse
> a global warming disaster. Right now, I'm leaning toward Flouridation.
>

Your mind is obviously made up.  Can you honestly say that you are more
objective than the "believers"?  That "your science" is more scientific than
the science of the believers?  Are you sure that your motivations are
impeccable?

I should imagine that each of the articles cited at this site could be
countered, one-for-one, by the opposite view.

Most of what I read at this site seemed to be devoted more to proving that
man-made causes aren't in play than that global warming isn't happening.
That may be true.  And the examples of warmer temperatures within the reach
of written history are quite correct.  I don't see, though, that they are
relevant to the situation we have today; they happened and then the climate
changed again.  Our climate is changing, period.  I don't see how anyone can
argue that man-made inputs to the climate have NO effect; everything we do
affects the environment and the real problem is how much the environment can
take before it morphs into something we can no longer accept.

I'll add that I agree 100% with the idea that carbon/emission credits
trading is a fraud.  Book-keeping will not solve any physical problem.
Credits are a license to pollute, nothing less.

> No, the cause of "Global warming" is a political, religious, and
> mass-movement phenomenon. Suggested reading: "The True Believer" by Eric
> Hoffer.
>


That works both ways, doesn't it?

PL
```

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-07T20:20:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Wh6j.42515$_H4.38198@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <78254d1e-eccf-4954-929b-dbb123c4cddb@a39g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <5reg6pF112ofgU1@mid.individual.net> <%6w4j.7323$N67.2695@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <24585871-8019-42c5-b327-09e7ca7b6b38@s36g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <5uA4j.7739$N67.5951@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9b4dda98-ff62-4625-a812-1c7a28bd2876@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Y5_5j.9900$k27.6555@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <t3pil3dte4p1d1ahff9e0u69mbf0n7aljj@4ax.com>`

```
I haven't replied to a lot of this thread.  As someone who is NOT a scientist, I 
have been quite convinced that the overwhelming scientific evidence is that 
Global Warming is happening and that it is caused (at least in part) by human 
actions that could be modified.

However, this note is just to comment on the "wait until we are POSITIVE" 
approach.

As someone with AIDS - who has been HIV positive since early in the 1980's, let 
me remind people about how long it took to "convince" the general public that 
this was a general health issue that needed action.  There is a LOT of question 
about how much "cheaper" (and better) in the long run things would have been if 
the US federal government had taken it more seriously in 1981-1985 - when many 
people reported that the "scientific evidence of a general epidemic" wasn't well 
established (or at least not well enough established).  This was a time when 
many people thought that this was a "gay thing" (and couldn't figure out the 
poor Haitians - and the cases in sub-Sahara Africa weren't even talked about - 
if they were diagnosed).

IMHO, "costly solutions" to POSSIBLE (probable) problems now OFTEN cost less 
(and work better) than "final solutions" after ALL possible evidence is in.

I don't think that I will convince anyone else of this view - and this is 
definitely a "generalization" for which counter-examples exist.  However, I did 
think it was important to express my opinion (based on personal impact from 
those who wanted to wait for more evidence.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Fix it now or later (was OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-07T16:15:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3Cj6j.21209$rc2.20148@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <5reg6pF112ofgU1@mid.individual.net> <%6w4j.7323$N67.2695@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <24585871-8019-42c5-b327-09e7ca7b6b38@s36g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <5uA4j.7739$N67.5951@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9b4dda98-ff62-4625-a812-1c7a28bd2876@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Y5_5j.9900$k27.6555@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <t3pil3dte4p1d1ahff9e0u69mbf0n7aljj@4ax.com> <0Wh6j.42515$_H4.38198@fe08.news.easynews.com> <huejl3dr7ggmim1t2smvoqrelqft8taaqf@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> At the risk of being on-topic... Y2K.

Not a good parallel. There was no doubt that the vast majority of date
sensitive software using 2 digit year would fail on Y2K, which included
the vast majority of business software. The fact could be demonstrated
to any imbecilic doubter by setting the date forward on the computer,
running the software and watching it crash. You could also remediate a
given piece of software and test it to verify it was working.

Global warming is very different. The questions on global warming are
if it's even happening, whether or not humans contribute to it if it is,
and whether or not we can do anything to make a difference. There is no
experimental verification that it is happening, or that any proposed
efforts to address it would work either. Compared to Y2K, it's like a
black cat in a coal cellar at midnight: spending vast amounts of money
and making vast, sweeping changes to our personal freedoms, without
knowing if there is even a need, and without knowing if what your doing
will help. That's the problem when you can't experimentally verify a
hypothesis - you just don't know for certain. Period.
```

```
On Mon, 10 Dec 2007 11:08:29 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>>>Global warming is very different. The questions on global warming are
>>>if it's even happening, whether or not humans contribute to it if it is,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>You can only reason with those for whom reasoning is a faculty. :-)

The hard part of this discussion is that we all *know* that (for
various values of "my"), "My minority belief is reasonable, and
shouldn't be compared with that crazy's minority belief".

For the moment, I will hold off on scientific arguments - and note
that more and more scientists and people who rely on scientists for
their data are accepting that global warming is significant and that
our share in its cause is significant.    Since science has had a very
good record of producing results that we can see in our life, it is
easy to accept that their research is likely to be correct.   

This means we require some more evidence from those who disagree with
the establishment, especially from people who have demonstrated that
they are more concerned with issues other than the scientific method.
```

```
wrote:

>>For the moment, I will hold off on scientific arguments - and note

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Politics, the Internet, Public Opinion, and most arguing do not
constitute science.    Policy changes need to not be as irrational as
Mr. Spock and be surprised when people act like people.

>>Since science has had a very
>>good record of producing results that we can see in our life, it is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>they are more concerned with issues other than the scientific method.

Well GWB has stated that he believes in global warming.   While this
doesn't mean that the President's scientific advisors are correct, it
means that the global warming crowd has the high ground.   Those
working against the establishment will have to work harder - getting
better evidence and more workable theories.

Einstein had to work against the tide as well, and he won.   But he
really did back up his argument with well done theory that was
testable and was tested with a high degree of accuracy.  

>
>I couldn't agree more. We should start by demanding more evidence from AlGore.

Hollywood has a tendency to have inaccuracies in anything it touches -
even things that are generally true.    Gore isn't a scientist.
Persuade the scientists first.
```

```
Science operates on consensus a lot of the time. Peer review is the
means of enforcing consensus and keeping science from wandering off on
absurd unverifiable claims.

>

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Those assertions were based on the simple observation that the current
temperatures were between the maxima and minima observed (by means
that some here dismiss as inaccurate/unprovable/blah blah blah) in
recent history and no scientist was able to state whether we were
going into another ice age or still coming out of the previous ice
age.

>

>
```

```
> Science operates on consensus a lot of the time. Peer review is the

Consensus can not be reached until after the reviewed
information is available to a group, as a whole. Using peer
review as a "means of enforcing consensus" goes by the
name censorship!

It seems to me that peer review is useful for preventing
errors of commission; however, peer review can not prevent
errors of omission!
```

```
On Mon, 10 Dec 2007 11:46:57 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Mon, 10 Dec 2007 11:08:29 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
><judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>they are more concerned with issues other than the scientific method.

In the 1980s, most scientists and nearly all popular press reports told us acid rain was a
pending catastrophe. They said the problem would be solved if we reduced sulphur emissions
by 50%. We did better, we reduced them 65%, at a cost of billions. Seventeen years later,
acid in lakes hasn't gone down. The problem was not caused by smokestack emissions. 
Here's an abbreviated story about Ed Krug, a scientist who almost single handedly changed
the beliefs of the scientiic community BY PRESENTING FACTS. His reward was a ruined
career.

------ quotation -------------
Krug's problems began, in a sense, in the late 1970s. Scientists in the United States,
Canada, and Scandinavia became alarmed at what they believed was massive environmental
degradation caused by sulfur dioxide-laced rain that came from coal-fired power plants.
The media followed with hundreds of apocalyptic stories, such as "Scourge from the Skies"
(Reader's Digest), "Now, Even the Rain is Dangerous" (International Wildlife), "Acid from
the Skies" (Time), and "Rain of Terror" (Field and Stream).

In 1980, the EPA declared that acid rain had acidified lakes in the northeastern United
States a hundredfold since 1940, and the National Academy of Sciences predicted an
"aquatic silent spring" by 1990, declaring in 1981 that the nation's number of acid-dead
lakes would more than double by 1990.

In response to these concerns, Congress in 1980 commissioned an interagency governmental
study--National Acid Rain Precipitation Assessment Project( NAPAP)--to document the damage
acid rain was causing to lakes, rivers and streams, aquatic life, forests, crops, and
buildings.

Krug came to NAPAP in 1981 while working with the Connecticut State Agricultural
Experiment Station. As a soil scientist, he was to examine the effects of acid rain on
soils, lakes, and streams. Like most of his colleagues, he believed that his experiments
would document that acidic precipitation was doing real damage to the environment.

The popular theory among acid-rain scientists in 1981 was the mineral titration theory,
which said that acidic soils, which are common in many regions of the country, have little
buffering capacity against acid rain. In other words, soils that are acidic because of
organic matter cannot absorb and neutralize acid rain as do alkaline (or lime-bearing)
soils.

Because much of the soil in the Northeast and eastern Canada is acidic, many scientists
simply assumed that acid rain ran off directly into streams and lakes and made them
acidic. Unless acid rain was stopped, the problem would intensify and more and more lakes
and streams would become unfit for fish and other aquatic life.

The theory seemed convincing when one realized that many Adirondack lakes that are now
dead had held plenty of trout and other fish at the turn of the century. As coal-burning
utility plants sprang up, spewing sulfur dioxide into the air, scientists thought, acid
rain had slowly choked these lakes to death.

These scientists held that Adirondacks lakes such as Woods Lake were acidic because of
acid rain. Samples of its surface water found the lake to have a pH factor of 5.0, which
is too acidic to support fish and other life. But scientific models based on the mineral
titration theory predict that eliminating half of the acidity of rain could raise the pH
level to a more-neutral and life-supporting 6.0 over the next 50 years.

Advocates of drastic reduction of acid rain also held that the sulfur dioxide in
precipitation was destroying U.S. forests. The acid, they claimed, stripped the soil of
necessary nutrients, damaged the forest canopy, and leached into water tables metals found
naturally in the soil.

These latter claims were refuted early on by NAPAP research. Scientists found no evidence
of damage to forests (with the exception of less than 0.1 percent of the red spruces found
in the Southern Appalachian highlands). Nor did they find that it was causing soil
minerals to leach into the water table. And in those forests that were dying, scientists
found that insects and various plant diseases were causing the damage. Indeed, there was
some evidence that acid rain acts as a mild fertilizer.

But Krug's research went even farther, raising disturbing questions about what were then
accepted scientific theories. Specifically, Krug began to question whether acid rain even
contributed to lake and stream acidity at all.

At that time, much of the scientific community, holding to the mineral titration theory,
believed that lakes and streams that were located in watersheds dominated by acid soils
would only increase in acidity if acid rain fell on them. In 1983, however, Krug published
an article in the prestigious journal Science that showed that acid rain might have almost
nothing to do with acid lakes.

The article, co-authored by Charles R. Frink, was entitled "Acid Rain on Acid Soil: A New
Perspective." It was the only invited article by a NAPAP scientist to be published in
Science, and it was very controversial, not just because it challenged scientific
theories, but also because of its implications for U.S. policy makers.

The article examined acidic soil in watersheds and challenged the belief that landscapes
"act merely as net sinks for acid rain." Krug and Frink looked at historical land-use
patterns in the United States, Canada, and Scandinavia and concluded that existing soil
chemistry was as important or more important to the pH of a lake or stream as acid rain.
To perhaps oversimplify, it is acid in the surrounding plants and soil, not that in the
rain, that causes acid lakes.

Krug and Frink noted that acidity in lakes and streams had positive correlations with land
use, a point verified by the EPA in 1989. Core samples taken from the bottom of many
Adirondack lakes show increased acidity in the recent past but also show they were acidic
and fishless before European settlement. Krug noted in his article "Fish Story," published
in Heritage Foundation's Policy Review, that the translation of the Iroquois word
adirondack is "bark eater," and history has shown that many of these lakes and watersheds
failed to provide fish and game for the Indians.

Trout survived better in the Adirondacks around the turn of the century than in earlier
times because of extensive slash-and-burn logging of that area. Eliminating the acid
vegetation caused the soil to become more alkaline (a high pH), reducing the acid flowing
into lakes and streams. In turn, the lakes became more hospitable to fish. After "forever
wild" legislation stopped the logging in 1915, the watersheds reverted to acid soils and
vegetation, and the lakes became acidic again.

Krug also found that Florida, not the Northeast, has the largest number of acidic lakes,
despite the fact that its rainfall is much less acidic than rain in the Northeast. He also
studied lakes in Australia and New Zealand that had a pH well below 5.0 despite the fact
that acid rain did not fall in their watersheds. Acid soils and vegetation caused lake
acidity, Krug found. On the other hand, the Ohio Valley, which has the nation's most
acidic rain, has no acid lakes or streams.

In their summary, Krug and Frink wrote, "Thus, the interactions of acid rain, acid soil,
and vegetation need to be carefully examined on a watershed basis in assessing benefits
expected from proposed reductions in emissions of oxides of sulfur and nitrogen.

In other words, they implied that drastically cutting back emissions from coal-fired
plants might not have any significant effect on lake acidity. For example, Woods Lake,
even hundreds of years ago when "pure" rain fell in its watershed, was very acidic. Thus,
the mineral titration model's prediction that reducing acid rain would cut the acidity in
Adirondacks lakes was wrong. The generally accepted theory, while seemingly impressive,
violated a basic rule of science: It could not make an accurate prediction.

Krug's findings were significant because they seemed to refute what would become the basis
for acid-rain legislation. They also set him apart from many other scientists in NAPAP,
some of whom believed in mineral titration and some of whom simply did not wish to offend
the EPA, the lead agency in NAPAP.

But the cause of acidic lakes was one of the few areas of controversy within the NAPAP
scientific community. Most of the studies either refuted or seriously questioned nearly
every apocalyptic claim made by environmentalists in the government, and when NAPAP
released its Interim Report in 1987, many in Congress and the media were not ready for its
message.

The assessment concluded that acid rain was not damaging forests, did not hurt crops, and
caused no measurable health problems. The report also concluded that acid rain helped
acidify only a fraction of Northeastern lakes and that the number of acid lakes had not
increased since 1980. The assessment also agreed that acid rain hampered visibility in the
eastern United States.

The report ignited a firestorm of protest. Rep. James Scheuer (D-NY), chairman of the
House Subcommittee on Natural Resources, Agriculture Research, and the Environment, said
the assessment was "intellectually dishonest" and badgered NAPAP witnesses before his
committee. Environmentalists belittled the document because it came from the Reagan
administration. They were especially angry at J. Lawrence Kulp, whom Reagan had appointed
NAPAP director.

Scientists, however, generally endorsed the study. Documents from the International
Conference on Acid Precipitation in 1988 show participants agreed with most of NAPAP's
conclusions almost unanimously. In fact, the scientists from Canada agreed with Krug on
the important watershed acidification theory, which was partly at odds with the Interim
Assessment. In other words, NAPAP's conclusions were scientifically correct, if not
politically correct.

When James Mahoney became NAPAP director in 1988, he assured Scheuer's subcommittee that
he "would not subscribe...at this time" to the view that acid rain would not harm any more
Northeastern lakes. Three years later, he would subscribe to that position on 60 Minutes.

NAPAP was ready to release a final findings document in 1989. Under congressional mandate,
the document was supposed to guide priorities for the Clean Air Act. But the EPA, now led
by Bush appointee and zealous environmentalist William Reilly, refused to approve it.
After much revision, the EPA finally allowed the document to be released on July 27,
1990--long after Bush, who in his 1988 presidential campaign had promised to be the
"environmental president," signed the new law.

The Findings Document differed little from the Interim Assessment. An exhaustive,
worldwide scientific search said acid rain was an environmental nuisance, not a crisis.
The much-feared "silent spring" had not arrived.

After authorizing nearly $600 million for the NAPAP study, Congress refused to hear the
good news. One committee met to examine the results, but only Sen. Daniel Patrick Moynihan
(D-NY) appeared at the public hearing.

By the time the findings were released, Krug was out of favor with the EPA and out of
NAPAP. His research only compounded the government's political problems, and no one in the
EPA, Congress, or the national media wanted to listen to him, even though his theories had
by 1990 *become the scientific consensus.*

While Krug's studies have gained worldwide recognition, they may also have robbed him of
his livelihood. Gary Stensland, a NAPAP scientist for the Illinois State Water Survey who
worked with Krug on many projects, notes, "Ed had a big effect on NAPAP. They did a lot of
research on things that he said should be looked at. They [NAPAP] often didn't give him
credit." Krug, he says, "has not benefitted professionally" from his discoveries.

Krug's work gained him an entry in Who's Who in Science and Engineering for 1991. His
Science article is the third most-cited in scientific literature on acid rain. But since
that article was published, Krug has been unable to obtain research grants. "You would
think that the agency [EPA] would have solicited him," says Stensland. "It sure looks
peculiar."

The EPA funds most soil research, and after 1983, that agency wanted nothing to do with
Krug. Krug sought--but did not receive--competitive grants. The EPA did not approach him
for research even though scientists with lesser qualifications received funding.

While Krug was working for the Illinois State Water Survey in 1986, the EPA gave a grant
for soil research to a team at the University of Illinois. "These scientist did not have
my qualifications," Krug says, "and I really knew things were bad when they came to me and
asked me to show them how to do the chemical analysis."

In 1985, the EPA advertised for a soil scientist at its Corvalis, Oregon, station. Krug
applied. After he scored 99 out of 100 in the civil service exam, the EPA suddenly
informed him that the position had been canceled. "They later advertised for an
ecologist," Krug recalls.

The U.S. Department of Energy, however, gave him a grant to publish a lengthy assessment
of acid-rain theories. The assessment went through peer review and was widely praised upon
its publication in 1989. Erik Eriksson of Sweden, considered to be the father of acid-rain
theories, sent DOE an unsolicited letter commending Krug's report as "most welcome for
sifting evidence from the sea of loose speculations often found."

........

Kroft [60 Minutes] asked Krug about a then-recent New York Times story that claimed acid
rain had turned forests in the Appalachia's into "ragged landscapes of dead and dying
trees."

Krug replied, "I don't know where they got that from. It appears to be another assertion,
unsubstantiated...We do not see that occurring."

Kroft also interviewed David Hawkins, a former EPA administrator who is now with the
Natural Resources Defense Council. Hawkins simply dismissed the NAPAP study as irrelevant,
stressing that the NRDC had always ignored NAPAP and had concentrated, instead, on policy
making based upon the assumption that acid rain was an environmental disaster. "The
environmental community has spent almost no effort attempting to even monitor the progress
[of NAPAP]....We have been working on trying to get legislation in Washington."

A. Alan Moghissi, a former scientist with the EPA, says that Hawkins paid little attention
to scientific research while he was with the government. "My experience with
EPA...indicated strong disagreement between Hawkins and the scientists within EPA," wrote
Moghissi in a letter to Krug.

......

The EPA's performance on acid rain--and how it dealt with a respected scientist who told
the truth--is not comforting when one considers how important the federal government now
is in funding scientific research and how politicized current environmental issues such as
global warming and depletion of the earth's ozone layer have become. One NAPAP scientist,
who for obvious reasons wishes to remain anonymous, warns that in the future the EPA will
not go through the pretense of research and debate: "There is no NAPAP for global
warming." 
http://www.sepp.org/Archive/controv/controversies/epavskrug.html
```

```
warming is a hoax? It's because they would be committing career suicide.
```

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

```
Acid in lakes has gone down.  Here's a study done in the UK:

http://www.ezilon.com/information/article_13789.shtml

And in North America (with other consequences):

http://environmentalresearchweb.org/cws/article/research/31962

http://www.nature.com/news/2005/050810/full/news050808-10.html


About Ed Krug.  He did not deny that acid rain was a problem.  He
stated it was not a catastrophe.  That was in 1992.  I'd like to know
what he things today had the EPA and other agencies not acted.  How
much worse does a problem have to get?


<snip>

at:

http://www.sepp.org/Archive/controv/controversies/epavskrug.html
```

```
On Tue, 11 Dec 2007 11:51:52 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>On Mon, 10 Dec 2007 22:02:03 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.ezilon.com/information/article_13789.shtml

Notable by omission are pH readings. 

>And in North America (with other consequences):
>
>http://environmentalresearchweb.org/cws/article/research/31962

"Because the dissolved organic carbon in such systems is largely made up of organic acids,
the pH of the waters may not rise as sharply as might be expected from the decreasing
levels of acidifying sulphates."

In plain English, acidity hasn't gone down.

>It was reduced in Canada but is still a problem in Ontario.  See:
>
>http://www.nature.com/news/2005/050810/full/news050808-10.html

Nor has it gone down in Ontario.

>About Ed Krug.  He did not deny that acid rain was a problem.  He
>stated it was not a catastrophe.  

More importantly, he said it wasn't caused by man. Therefore, reducing smokestack
emissions would not affect water acidity. The evidence shows he was right. Here is one of
the few reports that admits the truth:

"Results show that pH has remained fairly stable since 1982,"
http://www.menv.gouv.qc.ca/air/pre_acid/noranda-en/noranda.htm#acidification

>That was in 1992.  I'd like to know
>what he things today had the EPA and other agencies not acted.  How
>much worse does a problem have to get?

A paper he wrote in 2003 says reduction of sulphur emissions screwed up the soil in the
UK:

"ï¿½Ironically, decreased [atmospheric] S inputs in recent decades have resulted in S
deficiency in many agricultural crops in Europeï¿½ (Knights et al., 2000, p. 1867).
For the United Kingdom, S deficiency was determined to be a problem in 33 percent of its
soils. If the government 2003 target for S emissions is met, S deficiency is predicted to
be a problem for 50 percent of United Kingdom soils (McGrath and Zhao, 1995)."

http://www.sws.uiuc.edu/pubdoc/CR/ISWSCR2003-02.pdf
```

```
On Fri, 07 Dec 2007 20:20:13 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I haven't replied to a lot of this thread.  As someone who is NOT a scientist, I

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

Public opinion on AIDS changed when it was announced (without evidence) that AIDS could be
transmitted by 'normal' (vaginal) sex between white middle class people. In other words,
'If it doesn't affect my kind, to hell with it. If it does affect my kind, cost is no
obstacle.'

I heard one third of South Africans have AIDS. They must be doing it with monkeys<ha ha>. 

What's all this talk about global warming? It snowed here in Chicago yesterday, and the
forecast is for more this weekend. I can see with my eyes that the globe isn't warming. Al
Gore must be making it up. 

We don't understand why the rest of the world calls us Ugly Americans. Better adjectives
would be dilletantish and short-sighted. 

Want to get rich quick? The Federal Reserve will hold a summit conferences at Jackson Hole
next Tuesday, 12/11. The topics will be sub-prime mortgages, which everyone knows is a
problem, and lack of liquidity in the secondary banking market (e.g. commercial paper).
The solution to both is turn on the printing presses. The effect will be cheapening the
dollar. The feds see that as a benefit, they can repay 30% of government debt with cheaper
dollars. For the rest of us, it means higher prices at Wal-Mart and Toyota dealers. I
recommend buying call options on Euros, Yen or Pounds before Tuesday. You'll make 500%
within a month.
```

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> (snip) 
> Well OK then lets take Jusdon's idea and don't anything until all the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> -oOO--(_)--OOo-

From:
http://www.enchantedlearning.com/subjects/dinosaurs/mesozoic/jurassic/

Quote:

GEOLOGY AND CLIMATE DURING THE JURASSIC
The Jurassic period is named for rock strata found in the Jura 
Mountains, which are located between France and Switzerland.

There was no polar ice during the Jurassic, so the sea levels were 
higher than they are now. The climate was warm. Early in the Jurassic, 
the continents were jammed together into a supercontinent (Pangaea), 
making much of the inland area dry and desert-like. Towards the middle 
of the Jurassic, when Pangaea began to break up, there were vast 
flooded areas, temperate and subtropical forests, and coral reefs. The 
extensive water moderated the strong seasonality so that by the end of 
the Jurassic there was less seasonality than we have now.

***end quote***

As John Maynard Keynes once said, in the long run we are all dead 
anyway.  The planet's climate is not my responsibility.  It's already 
been much warmer than it is now.  I've got better things to worry about.

With kindest regards,
```

```
SkippyPB wrote:
>
> Well OK then lets take Jusdon's idea and don't anything until all the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> it.

I've never seen an iceberg or a coral reef. I think I saw a polar bear once. 
(I have, however, seen pictures of all of them.) If they disappear, wouldn't 
directly affect me at all (I don't think).

Some of the proposed "solutions" would, however, affect me and millions of 
others.

For example, curtailing CO2 in the atmosphere. CO2 accounts for three 
one-hundredths of one percent of the atmosphere. If all the MASSIVE 
suggested changes in human endeavor were implemented, the best we could hope 
for is, what, TWO one-hundredths of one percent?

Can anyone, anywhere, demonstrate or even claim that an atmospheric change 
of one one-hundredths of one percent of anything in the atmosphere will have 
any effect on the environment? Man, the atmosphere varies by a hudred times 
that on a daily basis (i.e., the percentage of water vapor rises 
dramatically when the fog rolls in).
```

```
things as practicing safe computer practices.

best.   But that doesn't absolve us of our need to be active stewards.
```

```
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net...

>
> I do believe we should be good stewards of the earth. And part of that
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

HYPOTHETICAL SITUATION: global warming is occurring and the level of the
oceans worldwide will increase by ten meters by the end of this century.
The rise will be slow until about 2075 and then will accelerate
exponentially.

In this scenario, the need for movement of populations and protection of
seacoast cities won't become apparent until after 2080 or so.  At that time
it will still be possible to get all the protective actions done but at a
cost of ten times what it would have been in 2070.  Every year of delay
doubles the cost and by 2090 it'll be too late.

In this scenario, the evidence for global warming and the rise in ocean
levels in the early years of the century is not conclusive although the
argument becomes more and more one-sided as the studies continue.

Question based upon this scenario: would it not be smarter to start planning
and to make small changes in the early years?  Granted it would cost money
but orders of magnitude less than waiting until the situation was unarguable
would cost.  And the changes would be minimally destructive.

In Winnipeg, where I live, there was built in 1914-1919 an aqueduct to bring
water for the city from a source 95 miles away.  A certain influential group
of itizens and businessmen derided it as being totally unnecessary and a
waste of money since the city would never ever need as much water as it
could carry.  Well, nearly 90 years later the aqueduct is still running
without having been upgraded while the city's population has about
quadrupled and industry has grown even more.  If it had been built thirty
later than it was I doubt if the city could have afforded it.  Likewise,
after the disastrous flood of 1950, the Premier of the province, Duff
Roblin, started construction of a floodway around the city.  This was also
mocked as being overkill and was referred to as Duff's Ditch.  Since 1950,
it has prevented at least a dozen minor floodings of the city and at least
five more disastrous ones: in 1997, during the flood that overwhelmed Grand
Forks and other cities, the floodway JUST held back the waters (there's a
very good book about it, "A Red Sea Rising").   That was a 150-year flood
and now the floodway is being upgraded to handle a 500-year flood.  It may
never happen; a 150-year flood may not ever happen again.

But in both cases, a decision was made to handle a problem and to do it in a
manner that far exceeded the anticipated worst cases.  (Now if only the city
could bring itself to build a rapid-transit system!)

Things can be done now that will cope with huge things in the future and
cost less and cause far less disruption than waiting until the need is on
us.

PL
```

```
On Tue, 4 Dec 2007 10:03:52 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>> Taking action early is so much cheaper than taking action later that
>> it is a very cost effective insurance.
>
>Only when the action is needed. Taking action that isn't needed, or is
>useless or ineffective, is always a waste.

We had this discussion about insurance about a week or two ago.    It
was about coding with practices designed to handle unlikely, but
expensive events.     But I also have disaster insurance in my
personal life.   By many standards, I am underinsured - but I do have
some insurance.

If the disaster doesn't arrive, by your definition, my insurance was
wasted.    Trouble is, I don't have the advantage of hindsight when I
make my plans.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mAf5j.29558$K27.8885@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <5reg6pF112ofgU1@mid.individual.net> <%6w4j.7323$N67.2695@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <bbs5l3djeu8ps745rrfmfig6o3pvilmv7c@4ax.com> <13l8hpi1emiek57@corp.supernews.com> <shk8l359l2f2fnk24mpdd74i0flr56lv4u@4ax.com> <wJ_4j.10474$vt2.196@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <vju8l3pc9q2sjd5e6chcrg0eeb1ouq1oj8@4ax.com> <e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <ojsal3lcqva2rblocpu1j0i9ttginqc44f@4ax.com> <zTe5j.29535$K27.8144@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <unval39cv9mesa8olijo8etk1imb0vnvjc@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> make my plans.

It is an absolute certainty that things for which insurance would be helpful
do happen to people, and we do know that insurance would help in those
situations. Though you may not know if it is going to happen to you, you
do know it *will* happen to some people, perhaps you. Global warming is
very different, because we *do not* know that global warming is happening,
we *do not* know if we are causing/exacerbating it, and we *do not* know
if what we could/would do about it will help, even if the above are true.

I.e.: the situations are not parallel.
```

- **References:** `<ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <5reg6pF112ofgU1@mid.individual.net> <%6w4j.7323$N67.2695@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <bbs5l3djeu8ps745rrfmfig6o3pvilmv7c@4ax.com> <13l8hpi1emiek57@corp.supernews.com> <shk8l359l2f2fnk24mpdd74i0flr56lv4u@4ax.com> <wJ_4j.10474$vt2.196@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <vju8l3pc9q2sjd5e6chcrg0eeb1ouq1oj8@4ax.com> <e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <ojsal3lcqva2rblocpu1j0i9ttginqc44f@4ax.com> <zTe5j.29535$K27.8144@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <unval39cv9mesa8olijo8etk1imb0vnvjc@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> If the disaster doesn't arrive, by your definition, my insurance was
> wasted.    Trouble is, I don't have the advantage of hindsight when I
> make my plans.

Right. It's a question of degree of loss vs. degree of probability. The cost 
to insure your house against destruction by stampeeding elephants is 
probably not a big number. But however small it is, it is a waste. But 
suppose that cost was HUGE; wouldn't your rather put your money down against 
a more likely event?

Cost per life saved per year can be calculated. For example, putting a smoke 
detector in every home would be less than $0.01. Requiring seat belts in 
school busses would cost $2,800,000 per life saved per year.

This means that for the money the seat belt advocates propose be spent, MANY 
more lives could be saved by universal use of smoke detectors.
```

```
HeyBub wrote:
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>> If the disaster doesn't arrive, by your definition, my insurance was
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> more lives could be saved by universal use of smoke detectors. 

People don't like thinking of humans in economics terms.  "If it saves 
just *one* life, isn't it worth it?"  Well, quite frankly, *no*.  This 
is also what some charity workers tell themselves so they can feel good 
about not seeing results.  (This isn't a slam against all charities - 
most are good.)

Thomas Sowell wrote an article about this phenomenon following the Utah 
mine cave-in, and the subsequent doubling of the death toll by adding 
rescue workers who probably shouldn't have still been there.

http://www.townhall.com/columnists/ThomasSowell/2007/08/28/no_trade-offs
```

```
that are much more difficult to address though.
```

```
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:nJj5j.29681$K27.24464@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
>
> And nowhere have I suggested we should not be taking steps to protect
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Judson McClendon       judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)


Your bunk-o-meter may be pretty risible but your "faulty generalization"
generator is equally active.  Since you attribute non-objectivity to the
global warming community, would you claim that the other side IS objective?

There was a little article in the Winnipeg Free Press about a year ago
entitled "Tests defy greenhouse warming theory".  It was by Dennis T. Avery
who formerly was with the US State Department, where he won an award called
the National Intelligence Medal of Achievement (sic!  you'll see why in a
moment).  He is the co-author of a book called "Unstoppable Global Warming -
every 1500 Years".  Here's part of the article: "John Cristy ... recently
led a team that digitized the old manual temperature records AND ADJUSTED
FOR ANY CHANGE that could have altered the individual station records:
location, instruments, paving, etc.  The ADJUSTED record says that the San
Joaquin's minimum summer-fall temperatures have risen about 3 degrees C
since 1910".  (Emphasis added).

Now, tell, me, if anybody alters his raw data to support his predetermined
conclusion, exactly what is the conclusion worth?  If I were allowed this
sort of liberty I could probably prove that the Atlantic Ocean is nowhere
more than 600 feet deep ...  And note that even after his fiddling he admits
that a temperature rise has indeed happened.  I would like to know waht the
unaltered figure show ...  Would you describe this as objective, or even
science?

Anyway, Judson, objectivity and poor science on either side of the debate
notwithstanding, the fact remains that the glaciers and the icecaps are
melting.    (LX-i denies this and urges us to google it - well, I did and at
a quick glance couldn't find any articles to support him - give us one or
two, LX-i, so you can be sure I'm not being selective).  There is no other
realistic cause than global warming.  It may be part of a natural trend, to
be sure, but that doesn't alter the reality.

PL
```

- **Message-ID:** `<dVj5j.4075$Dt4.1559@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<5reg6pF112ofgU1@mid.individual.net> <%6w4j.7323$N67.2695@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <bbs5l3djeu8ps745rrfmfig6o3pvilmv7c@4ax.com> <13l8hpi1emiek57@corp.supernews.com> <shk8l359l2f2fnk24mpdd74i0flr56lv4u@4ax.com> <wJ_4j.10474$vt2.196@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <vju8l3pc9q2sjd5e6chcrg0eeb1ouq1oj8@4ax.com> <e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <qu1bl3lv680hik0k5a5oc1ltp73cb5i8hs@4ax.com> <CHh5j.29624$K27.21873@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1obbl3hpi01afdqsfve73cajppefq9u9js@4ax.com>`

```
In article <1obbl3hpi01afdqsfve73cajppefq9u9js@4ax.com>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>You have espoused waiting until the evidence isn't just sufficient to
>persuade the vast majority of environmental scientists.  

Please note that "the vast majority" is irrelevant when discussing science. 
Science depends on evidence, not consensus. And in the case of so-called 
global warming, the science isn't there:

Fact 1: Average global temperature has risen by approximately zero-point-six 
degrees Celsius since 1880.

Fact 2: About three-fourths of that slight increase occurred *before* 1940.

Fact 3: The bulk of the human activity (fossil fuel consumption) alleged to be 
the cause of this slight increase occurred *after* 1940.

There's the real "Inconvenient Truth" for the global warming hypothesis: the 
effect preceded its alleged cause by more than half a century.

> I'm not sure
>what criteria would convince you, nor how long you think is
>appropriate to wait before taking action.   I do know that if they are
>right, waiting will make the consequences much more expensive.    

Trouble is, there is *no* evidence that human activity is the cause of the 
slight warming that has occurred so far, and thus no reason to believe that 
any changes in human activity will affect that small trend. 

Meanwhile, Mars is also getting warmer.

And this planet has been much, much warmer in the past than it is now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-04T20:34:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13lc3hbq9ter59e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<5reg6pF112ofgU1@mid.individual.net> <%6w4j.7323$N67.2695@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <bbs5l3djeu8ps745rrfmfig6o3pvilmv7c@4ax.com> <13l8hpi1emiek57@corp.supernews.com> <shk8l359l2f2fnk24mpdd74i0flr56lv4u@4ax.com> <wJ_4j.10474$vt2.196@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <vju8l3pc9q2sjd5e6chcrg0eeb1ouq1oj8@4ax.com> <e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <qu1bl3lv680hik0k5a5oc1ltp73cb5i8hs@4ax.com> <CHh5j.29624$K27.21873@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1obbl3hpi01afdqsfve73cajppefq9u9js@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> You have espoused waiting until the evidence isn't just sufficient to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> that are much more difficult to address though.

Ignorance is not evidence.

Radical changes in lifestyle or immense diversion of resources for something 
only assumed to exist is equivalent to a nationwide manhunt for Bigfoot or 
calling out the armed services to investigate crop circles.

Every dollar diverted to CO2 remission, every hour of every researcher spent 
on an amorphous quest, is a dollar or an hour diverted from a cure for 
cancer, production of wealth, world peace, or new uses for chicken fat.

The earth is probably (though not assuredly) getting warmer. To attribute 
this warming to something that makes up only 0.03% of the atmosphere is 
simply incredible on its face. Then to assert that we can do anything that 
would sufficiently affect this 0.03% is doubly unbelievable. Now even if it 
is unbelievable, it may still be true, but it is up to the proponents of the 
idea to prove the assertion. It is simply silly to rearrange society on the 
chance that man affects CO2 which then affects warming.
```

```
On Tue, 4 Dec 2007 20:34:25 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>Howard Brazee wrote:
>>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>cancer, production of wealth, world peace, or new uses for chicken fat.

Excess CO2 is self-correcting. Rising water and increased rainfall accelerates rock
weathering. The extra calcium freed up combines with atmospheric CO2 to form calcium
carbonate (calcite). In 250,000 years, climate will be back to normal. We'll just have
more limestone and gypsum.
```

- **References:** `<bbs5l3djeu8ps745rrfmfig6o3pvilmv7c@4ax.com> <13l8hpi1emiek57@corp.supernews.com> <shk8l359l2f2fnk24mpdd74i0flr56lv4u@4ax.com> <wJ_4j.10474$vt2.196@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <vju8l3pc9q2sjd5e6chcrg0eeb1ouq1oj8@4ax.com> <e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <qu1bl3lv680hik0k5a5oc1ltp73cb5i8hs@4ax.com> <CHh5j.29624$K27.21873@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1obbl3hpi01afdqsfve73cajppefq9u9js@4ax.com> <13lc3hbq9ter59e@corp.supernews.com> <pl8cl3pclp7kq9hnjcesmr74vuqqbnb68s@4ax.com>`

```
On 5 Dec, 04:10, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Tue, 4 Dec 2007 20:34:25 -0600, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> >Howard Brazee wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -

Acid rain does dissolve rock formations. It also poisons biological
systems. The CaCO3 rarely gets a chance to precipitate out in oceans
as it is rare at depth and is recycled by being reabsorbed into the
biological systems. Excess CO2 is not self-correcting because it is
not being taken out of the system by a 'sink'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-12-06T22:01:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oldhl3lbugjek1imqib2ri4kg2p046gh4p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<shk8l359l2f2fnk24mpdd74i0flr56lv4u@4ax.com> <wJ_4j.10474$vt2.196@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <vju8l3pc9q2sjd5e6chcrg0eeb1ouq1oj8@4ax.com> <e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <qu1bl3lv680hik0k5a5oc1ltp73cb5i8hs@4ax.com> <CHh5j.29624$K27.21873@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1obbl3hpi01afdqsfve73cajppefq9u9js@4ax.com> <13lc3hbq9ter59e@corp.supernews.com> <pl8cl3pclp7kq9hnjcesmr74vuqqbnb68s@4ax.com> <f15c6e97-0826-477d-b943-c62869bb6da6@j44g2000hsj.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 6 Dec 2007 14:49:33 -0800 (PST), Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>On 5 Dec, 04:10, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>cancer, production of wealth, world peace, or new uses for chicken fat.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>systems. 

Acid rain has almost nothing to do with CO2. Rain water is naturally acidic due to
carbonic acid, created by CO2. The phrase acid rain refers to sulphur and nitrogen
compounds in water.

>The CaCO3 rarely gets a chance to precipitate out in oceans
>as it is rare at depth and is recycled by being reabsorbed into the
>biological systems. Excess CO2 is not self-correcting because it is
>not being taken out of the system by a 'sink'.

If there were no natural sinks, Earth would now be uninhabitable. The natural sinks are
oceans, forests (when expanding), carbonate salts such as limestone, and soil. Problem is,
they're slow. They are not absorbing CO2 as rapidly as humans are releasing it. A few
hundred thousand years after we stop, they'll restore the atmosphere to normal.

Limestone is captured (sunk) CO2. About 10% of the world's rocks are limestone. 
A small percentage of CaCO3 went through marine organisms before their shells became
limestone deposits. Limestone also removes the sulphur in acid rain. That's how gypsum is
created.
```

- **References:** `<e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <qu1bl3lv680hik0k5a5oc1ltp73cb5i8hs@4ax.com> <CHh5j.29624$K27.21873@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1obbl3hpi01afdqsfve73cajppefq9u9js@4ax.com> <13lc3hbq9ter59e@corp.supernews.com> <pl8cl3pclp7kq9hnjcesmr74vuqqbnb68s@4ax.com> <f15c6e97-0826-477d-b943-c62869bb6da6@j44g2000hsj.googlegroups.com> <oldhl3lbugjek1imqib2ri4kg2p046gh4p@4ax.com> <592644b6-44a5-4fb2-a2c2-d764d1ec6c12@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sat, 8 Dec 2007 07:41:56 -0800 (PST), Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>On 7 Dec, 04:01, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Thu, 6 Dec 2007 14:49:33 -0800 (PST), Alistair
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>...dissolving in water which contributes to acid rain.

Acid rain is similar to global warming in that both are environmental 'emergencies'
claimed to be caused by man and solvable by regulation. The US EPA has been monitoring
every lake and stream in the Northeast since 1977, as well as capturing rain samples. One
of the programs is called The National Atmospheric Deposition Program (NADP). A picture of
its collector is here:
http://www.epa.gov/region1/eco/acidrain/measurement.html

In 1990 US Congress amended the Clean Air Act to solve the acid rain problem by requiring
sulphur scrubbers on smokestacks and low sulphur diesel fuel in trucks. Canada passed a
similar law. The effort cost private industry billions of dollars. AFTER low sulphur fuel
was mandated, O rings in truck fuel injectors started failing. Initially, government
environmentalists denied it was happening. Within a few years, every fuel injuector had to
be rebuilt. If activists are so good at predicting the future, why didn't they know O
rings would fail?

Seventeen years after the law passed, sulphur emissions have indeed been reduced to one
third the starting level, and continue falling. Here is a chart:
http://www.epa.gov/region1/eco/acidrain/trends.html

Notable in its absence from the EPA report are any measurements of acid in rainwater or
lakes. They showed a picture of their collection equipment; why don't they report the
results? The answer is, because acid levels have not declined much. In some places they
declined 10%; in most places they didn't decline at all. On the west coast, where the
culpret is nitrogen rather than sulphur, acid rain got worse. 

For the environmentalists' answer,  see "Why the Acid Rain Problem Is Not Solved" at 
http://www.cleartheair.org/relatives/18480.pdf
In summary, it says "Greater emission reductions will be needed to support recovery."
Just what we need -- MORE of the solution that isn't working.
```

```
news:13lc3hbq9ter59e@corp.supernews.com...
> The earth is probably (though not assuredly) getting warmer. To attribute
> this warming to something that makes up only 0.03% of the atmosphere is

> would sufficiently affect this 0.03% is doubly unbelievable. Now even if
it
> is unbelievable, it may still be true, but it is up to the proponents of

> idea to prove the assertion. It is simply silly to rearrange society on

> chance that man affects CO2 which then affects warming.

>

represent?  And what % of the atmosphere was composed of fluorocarbons?  How
much of the atmosphere is composed of sulphur dioxide after a really good
volcanic eruption?  The role that carbon dioxide plays in climate is very
well established.  You're really, really tilting agisnt the wrong windmill
here.

PL
```

- **References:** `<5reg6pF112ofgU1@mid.individual.net> <%6w4j.7323$N67.2695@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <bbs5l3djeu8ps745rrfmfig6o3pvilmv7c@4ax.com> <13l8hpi1emiek57@corp.supernews.com> <shk8l359l2f2fnk24mpdd74i0flr56lv4u@4ax.com> <wJ_4j.10474$vt2.196@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <vju8l3pc9q2sjd5e6chcrg0eeb1ouq1oj8@4ax.com> <e605j.10519$vt2.3178@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <qu1bl3lv680hik0k5a5oc1ltp73cb5i8hs@4ax.com> <CHh5j.29624$K27.21873@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <1obbl3hpi01afdqsfve73cajppefq9u9js@4ax.com> <13lc3hbq9ter59e@corp.supernews.com> <3004ab71-9401-44da-af70-8f080aaad01b@a39g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <FM%5j.9950$k27.5845@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mO46j.3048$c%6.1622@newsfe21.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> Judson - just out of curiosity, what would you consider to be objective
> proof (testable facts) that global warming is happening?

What would you consider to be objective proof (testable facts) that you
had a fever? :-)

Unambiguous measurements of a temperature increase would be a good start.
As it is, the .6 degree increase claimed to have happened over the last
100 years can be answered by a number of very likely possibilities that
are ignored or poo-pah'ed by the global warming alarmists. Let me list
just a few points, but they are more than sufficient to completely refute
the arguments made to support global warming.

1. The increase is smaller than the resolution of the vast majority of
instruments used to measure temperature outside the laboratory (e.g. by
agencies such as weather bureaus), especially prior to the last few
decades.

(Only after weather satellites were orbited in the late 70's were
widespread measurements available to .1 degree. Before then, it is
impossible to know what widespread tempratures were to that precision.)

2. A large number of such instruments have been increasingly encroached
upon by cities and their incumbent "heat bubbles".

(This could easily cause an apparent increase in overall temperatures.)

3. Most of the increase supposedly happened in the first half of the
century, but most of the "green house gas" emissions were in the latter
half.

(If there is a correlation, it is backwards. It would be hard to imagine
a scenario with a bigger and more obvious "red flag" than this. It is
more logical to infer from this that humanity's release of "green house
gases" actually mitigates global warming.)

Note that the above points are *not* hypothesis, but verifiable facts,
the reverse of "evidences" given to support global warming, which are
all based on series of hypothesis and inferrences. In true science, any
number of inferrences or hypothesis should be trumped by one conflicting
fact. This clearly demonstrates that global warming alarmists are more
concerned about their agenda than the science, and is another big and
obvious "red flag".

Global weather patterns are constantly changing. We simply do not have
*precise measurements* of global tempratures even 100 years ago, to say
nothing of 1000 or 5000 years ago. Even if you could say with certainty
that global tempratures have risen a whole degree in 100 years, there
is no *precise temprature history* to show for certain whether that is
a significant change or not. For all we know from direct observation, a
one degree variation over a century may be insignificant or less than
average.
```

```
accurate instruments can be discounted.

2. A large number of such instruments have been increasingly

greater than had been measured?

 century, but most of the "green house gas" emissions were in the

explanation of that effect.

4. Global weather patterns are constantly changing. We simply do not
have *precise measurements* of global tempratures even 100 years ago,
```

```
On 9 Dec, 22:13, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
> In article <cc5c26f5-84a4-42ce-bf13-281530b92...@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Arte you incapable of doing a search and checking the link for
yourself?

>
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> And on and on and on.

You are on dodgy ground bringing the hurricanes into the argument.
Hurricanes are subject to a cycle and the current crop of hurricanes
are merely the peak of that cycle. I am not aware of any evidence to
support the view that the current crop of hurricanes (or their
severity) is related to GW. Indeed there is evidence that there are
statistically no more hurricanes nor any more severe hurricanes than
would naturally occur at this point in the cycle.

However, warmer oceans would be expected to lead to more frequent and
more severe hurricanes.

>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Lack of response noted)

So you would rather that humanity killed itself off than took action
to resolve the problem?

No answer required. I have a friend who is happy to throw his rubbish
over the fence rather than spend seconds putting it in the trash bin.
You and he are very much alike.

>
> --
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -
```

```
Alistair wrote:
.
>>
>> Yep. The mark of the "true believer" -- everything is confirmatory.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> more severe hurricanes.

As it turns out, recent evidence (like this week) says ocean temperature is 
only half the story. Hurricanes depend more on temperature DIFFERENCE 
between the ocean an the troposphere. In the last three years, the oceans 
have been warmer, but so have the winds aloft, resulting in fewer 
hurricanes.

>>
>> (Lack of response noted)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The answer is yes. Now pay attention:

It's not about the weather, it's about the money. China, India, Pakistan, 
Russia, and others want the U.S. and Europe at an economic disadvantage so 
their economies can compete. To achieve this end, they have enlisted 
millions of hapless drones to drone on about the end of the world and that 
the only fix is for American school children to walk to school and everyone 
switch to renewable resources (like whale oil).

China, Russia, et al have insisted they be exempted from environmental 
concerns and that the U.S. (for example) curtail its economic engine. If a 
coal fired power plant is so vile, why doesn't China stop building them 
(they are opening at least one coal-fired plant per week)?

The fix - if there is one - will depend on technology because this world 
can't be run off  sunbeams. And technology wins. In the past year, the U.S. 
has reduced its green-house gas emissions by over 6% while Europe (for 
example) has increased their by almost 3%.

Calvin Coolidge said: "When you see ten troubles rolling down the road, if 
you don't do anything, nine of them will roll into a ditch before they get 
to you."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-12-19T14:47:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7u3jm3lojeek96ud788gq2ciudj5366m3l@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<FM%5j.9950$k27.5845@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mO46j.3048$c%6.1622@newsfe21.lga> <hua6j.20955$rc2.18261@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <09769a73-b042-4b2b-8020-a9480c9cb68d@i12g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WHz6j.5038$fl7.2876@newssvr22.news.prodigy.net> <cc5c26f5-84a4-42ce-bf13-281530b929cc@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <_LZ6j.31582$Pv2.11079@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <c984dece-921c-4175-8a04-09fb4d23ba69@l1g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <13mj2gk5o5qfhd9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 15:16:11 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:


>> However, warmer oceans would be expected to lead to more frequent and
>> more severe hurricanes.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>hurricanes.

The wild card in this analysis is currents.   A slight current change
can make a huge difference.


>It's not about the weather, it's about the money. China, India, Pakistan, 
>Russia, and others want the U.S. and Europe at an economic disadvantage so 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>switch to renewable resources (like whale oil).

Which may be why GWB got on board.   He wants treaties and programs to
cost competitors of the U.S. at least as much as the U.S.

>China, Russia, et al have insisted they be exempted from environmental 
>concerns and that the U.S. (for example) curtail its economic engine. If a 
>coal fired power plant is so vile, why doesn't China stop building them 
>(they are opening at least one coal-fired plant per week)?

They have a bit of power to do this as long as the U.S. is perceived
as the bad guy here.   But that can change if the best statesmen work
from a position of strength.

>The fix - if there is one - will depend on technology because this world 
>can't be run off  sunbeams. And technology wins. In the past year, the U.S. 
>has reduced its green-house gas emissions by over 6% while Europe (for 
>example) has increased their by almost 3%.

Even if it does nothing about global warming, there are huge
advantages for the Wealthy countries in making this a technology race.

>Calvin Coolidge said: "When you see ten troubles rolling down the road, if 
>you don't do anything, nine of them will roll into a ditch before they get 
>to you." 
>

Sure enough.   But how many troubles does it take to get run over? The
wise person finds a way to use that trouble (or those troubles) for
gain.
```

```
An interesting idea which I had not considered. On reflection, I would have 
thought that the CIPR (China, India, Pakistan, Russia) economies you mention 
ALREADY have the US and Europe at a disadvantage, and these economies are 
not only competing, but winning... This conclusion is based on the consumer 
goods I see available for sale in shops here, which come from these 
economies, as opposed to consumer goods which come from the USA or Europe. I 
realise this is a narrow view, but nevertheless, it doesn't look like they 
have anything to fear when it comes to "competing"...

To achieve this end, they have enlisted
> millions of hapless drones to drone on about the end of the world and that 
> the only fix is for American school children to walk to school and 
> everyone switch to renewable resources (like whale oil).
>
:-)

> China, Russia, et al have insisted they be exempted from environmental 
> concerns and that the U.S. (for example) curtail its economic engine. If a 
> coal fired power plant is so vile, why doesn't China stop building them 
> (they are opening at least one coal-fired plant per week)?

I'm sure natural attrition will curtail this. Sooner or later they will run 
out of coal...
>
> The fix - if there is one - will depend on technology because this world 
> can't be run off  sunbeams. And technology wins.

I would agree that our best hope for the future lies in technology. However, 
I would get very uncomfortable were the US to lose it's technology lead, and 
there are indications that some of the nations which have been playing 
"catch up"are now trying to take that lead. Korean scientists doing genetic 
experiments (which may possibly be banned in the US) produced cats that glow 
under UV light last week. While there may (or may not...) be a need for 
glowing pussies, the point is that they have the technology and 
understanding to produce them.

Today it's a cat that glows, tomorrow it may be cold fusion, or star 
travel...

>In the past year, the U.S. has reduced its green-house gas emissions by 
>over 6% while Europe (for example) has increased their by almost 3%.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to you."

Er... whatever happened to Coolidge...?

Pete.
```

```
Evidently you're incapable of posting a link that would support your claim.

>> >> [snip]

…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>are merely the peak of that cycle. 

Try again. The last two years have seen far *fewer* hurricanes than normal, 
not exactly the peak of the cycle.

>I am not aware of any evidence to
>support the view that the current crop of hurricanes (or their
>severity) is related to GW. Indeed there is evidence that there are
>statistically no more hurricanes nor any more severe hurricanes than
>would naturally occur at this point in the cycle.

That, however, hasn't stopped a lot of hysterics from blaming them on GW. 
Guess you weren't paying attention during the summer of 2005, or you would 
have heard them.

Then when 2006 had no major hurricanes, they blamed *that* on GW, too.
>
>However, warmer oceans would be expected to lead to more frequent and
>more severe hurricanes.

And yet, that isn't happening. Obviously something's amiss with the 
expectation.

>> >> >4. Global weather patterns are constantly changing. We simply do not
>> >> >have *precise measurements* of global tempratures even 100 years ago,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>to resolve the problem?

a) there is any problem at all that needs to be resolved
b) we had anything to do with causing it
c) we are able to anything about it
before taking any action.
>
>No answer required. I have a friend who is happy to throw his rubbish
>over the fence rather than spend seconds putting it in the trash bin.

Back to the ad hominem arguments, I see...
```

```
Alistair wrote:
> 
> No answer required. I have a friend who is happy to throw his rubbish
> over the fence rather than spend seconds putting it in the trash bin.

I despise littering.  However, I don't separate my garbage either. 
Someone mentioned it earlier, but technology has a way of compensating 
for humans' lack of desire to change their ways.  Already, our current 
"recycling" program doesn't demand separating paper from plastic from 
metal - because there is technology to separate them.  It's a small leap 
to run *all* trash through a sorter, that pulls that stuff out without 
humans having to have two trash cans.  (15 years ago, when I worked for 
a restaurant, we had this thing that we ran the food waste through as we 
dumped it into the trash - it caught metal and prevented silverware from 
being thrown away.)
```

```
HeyBub wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
>> Alistair wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> It was a corporal who eye-balled everything you scrapped off your tray. 

Heh - I don't think that would have gone over with the folks that worked 
at this restaurant.  They didn't even like it when the kitchen manager 
told them to do something - and he was the nicest guy in the world. 
Now, I could understand them not liking the general manager; he had 
little patience for slackers.  I never had any trouble with him, but 
then again, I like giving my employer a good value for his money.  :)

<anecdote>
Speaking of the kitchen manager - I even helped him move when he got a 
job.  His wife had seizures from time to time, so she couldn't drive.  I 
drove she, his son, and their puppy to a hotel in the city where they 
were moving to, then caught a cab to the bus station and took a bus back 
up.  (I learned that a 3-hour layover between 0300 and 0600 in Atlanta, 
GA is *not* my idea of a fun time!)  He was there to pick me up, then he 
drove the truck down, towing his car.  (Of course, I had also helped 
load that truck the day before.)
</anecdote>
```

```
To measure true average global tempratures precisely, you have to have
*A LOT* of precise instruments covering the whole area.

> 2. A large number of such instruments have been increasingly
> encroached upon by cities and their incumbent "heat bubbles". Didn't
> someone already address this issue in this thread by referring to a
> scientist who adjusted for encroachment and concluded that GW was
> greater than had been measured?

How do we know his "adjustments" are correct, unless (once again) by
experimental verification? Answer: we don't.

What I'm wondering is, what are you going to say in several years when
there's not a shred of doubt that global warming was a hoax?  After
all, many of the same people crying "global warming!" now were crying
"new ice age!" 20 years ago. Did I miss the new ice age? :-)
```

```
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

I mentioned the studies by John Cristy et al concerning long-term
temperature changes in the San Joaquin Valley; he adjusted the historical
readings since 1910 "... for any change that could have altered the
individual station records...".  My point was the one that Judson is making:
his "adjustments" were completely unjustifiable by any scientific standard.
And even so, he found that the temperature had increased by 3 degrees C (5.4
degress F) since 1910.

My bigger point that was, if persons in favour of the global warming
hypothesis are guilty of bad science, so are those on the other side.

And I'm still waiting for the list of glaciers and ice fields that are
expanding!!!!!

PL
```

```
On Dec 21, 2:43 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> tlmfru wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.iceagenow.com/List_of_Expanding_Glaciers.htm

That may be 'a list' but it is nonsense. The Abel Tasman, for example
has been retreating at its face for the last 25 years. When I visited
there a few decades ago, the car park had been made at the face, now
the face is half a mile away from it.

I note that it concentrates of the 'head', the start of it up the
mountain.  Glaciers are rivers of ice, the snow up the mountain either
stays in place if it is cold enough or flows into the 'river' if it it
pushed by sufficient weight or warms up so that a lesser weight can
make it move.

The fact that the head has grown shows that the snow and ice at the
top of the mountain has warmed up. The retreating of the face shows
that the bottom of the mountain has warmed up.
```

```
On 7 Dec, 17:31, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> > Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> to follow Him? And if you don't think that, why don't you believe Him?

This is too easy. Why would a minor Midianite deity be concerned with
the works of man? Christ was a MAN of his times and was clearly
influenced by the religious theories of his (superstitious) time.
Hence his belief in a god, in heaven and hell (even the Catholic
church has doubts about whether purgatory and hell exists. If it
denies those places then it must also deny the existence of heaven).
The Bible (the Word of God in the eyes of some) was written by humans
and has been adulterated over the centuries (remember the one that
said 'Thou shalt sin'?) hence the existence of different variants of
the texts.
```

```
On 3 Dec, 18:13, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> SkippyPB wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> dramatically when the fog rolls in).

CO2 in increased concentrations (currently 377 parts per million) can
lead to an increase in productivity of botanical systems. However, it
also increases the acidity of rain and of the oceans, both leading to
biota reductions. A decline in concentrations of CO2 may lead to a
reduced botanic production on land due to reduced availability of CO2
but may increase oceanic production. Swings and roundabouts.

Water vapour is also a greenhouse gas contributing more to the
greenhouse effect than CO2 and is likely to increase as the oceans
warm up.
```

```
On 1 Dec, 18:46, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
> I have maintained from the beginning that we *don't know for certain* if global
> warming is happening,

Therefore, YOU can not be certain that it is not happening.

> and even if we did, we *don't know for certain* that
> humanity is causing it.

All data shows that the most significant increase in global CO2 and
temperature has occured with increasing human population and
particularly the INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION. Coincidence? I think not.

> I never said that either one was absolutely not happening.
> It is the global warming proponents who have no doubts, which in and of itself
> is alarming, considering the uncertainty of the evidence.

There is no uncertainty to the evidence nor to the conclusions that
the scientists have drawn from the evidence. Any uncertainty exists in
your mind. I rant on about your religious beliefs because I view that
those beliefs are significant contributing factors in your ostrich-
like approach to this issue.

> It is this absolute,
> unshakable, blind belief, when the evidence doesn't warrant it that distresses me
> most.

Ditto re your religious beliefs and your posture on global warming.

> Such attitudes are the antithesis of what the scientific attitude should be.
>
> And please don't rate on about belief in God. The situations are completely
> different.

Quite right. One is based on evidence and the drawing of conclusions
from said evidence and the other is based on blind ignorance in the
absence of any evidence.

> If I believe in God and there is no God, then I am no worse off;
> I will die and never know it. If you don't believe in God, and He does exist,
> you are in trouble that makes global warming look like a picnic, for eternity.

No trouble - THERE IS NO GOD.

>
> The situation with global warming is different. If you global warming advocates
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> we will know for certain at some point

We know for certain NOW!

> and will deal with it then.

How much evidence do you need before you will change your views? Do
you need the Simpson-esque hand of God to come down from the skys and
order you to change?

> If global
> tempratures have increased only one degree in a century, we aren't going to be
> fried in another 50 years, or even 100 years,

The current standpoint has it that if we do not start doing something
now then in 20 (and most certainly 50) years time it will be too late
to reverse the trends and prevent the forthcoming environmental
catastrophe. I believe that those who see global warming as a gentle
increase in temperatures over time are deluding themselves. I foresee
a tipping point which will have icecaps melted and temperatures
increased non-linearly; a viewpoint which is not ruled out from the
range of possibilities for the future.

As an aside, I presume you are happy to have the massive influx of
immigrants that will come to the Americas looking for land to live on
after their countries are turned into deserts?


> if it takes that to know.

And still the nay-sayers will cry "Falsehood!" We still have people
alive who believe the earth is flat, despite the evidence to the
contrary.

> d it
> would be expected that by then we would have better technology to deal with it
> if necessary.

The Republican viewpoint: "We don't need to do anything now because in
50 years we will have technology save us".

>If we blow ourselves up in a war in the meantime, it becomes moot.

Unlikely to happen.

>
> > As good a time as any. Except when it is too late.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -
```

```
In article <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
>All data shows that the most significant increase in global CO2 and
>temperature has occured with increasing human population and
>particularly the INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION. Coincidence? I think not.

Of course it's not a coincidence -- because it's not true.

There are NO reliable records of GLOBAL temperatures prior to the late 19th 
century, for three reasons: first, early thermometers were not accurate enough 
to allow valid comparisons with modern data; second, record-keeping was spotty 
and inconsistent; and third, temperature measurements were neither made nor 
recorded *globally*.
```

```
In article <edc2bf5a-c795-4202-92d0-2a4f46705c58@w40g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>On 2 Dec, 15:43, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
>> In article
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>isotopes in marine snail shells.

You can *infer* temperature from those things, but you can't *measure* it. And 
the global-warming Chicken Littles would have us make massive changes in every 
facet of life on the basis of an inference. I'd rather have actual
```

```
I am sure of myself and , when TIMFRU invents a time machine, he can

brigade wrong. I won't hold my breath.

Judson, I realise that my belief in GW is as strong as your scepticism
but is founded in observable fact and not in ignorance or scepticism.
I do not think it is too late to save the world but I do think that,
without support from the entire population of the world, I will be
able to sunbath outside my flat in 2050 on christmas day with the
waves lapping around my feet on the beach (currently 200 feet above
sea level).

As for name calling? Calling you a worshipper of a minor Midianite
storm god is an accurate observation. Look up where Yahweh came from
on Wikipedia for yourself and you will see what I am getting at. By
the way, It was a novel by James Branch Cabell that appraised me of
that one.
```

```
isn't subject to controlled experiments.  I simply can't understand the

floating away, hundreds of square miles at a time.

withdrawing!
```
